# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Question 1: Define Data Communication. List the five components of a data communication system. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer:**

Data communication is the process of transferring digital data between two or more devices through a transmission medium.

The five components are:
1. **Message** — the information to be communicated
2. **Sender** — the device that sends the message
3. **Receiver** — the device that receives the message
4. **Transmission Medium** — the physical path (cable or wireless)
5. **Protocol** — the set of rules governing communication

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between Simplex, Half-Duplex, and Full-Duplex communication modes with one example each. `[KTU University Exam – June 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Mode | Direction | Simultaneous? | Example |
|------|-----------|---------------|---------|
| **Simplex** | One-way only | No | TV broadcasting |
| **Half-Duplex** | Both ways, alternating | No | Walkie-talkie |
| **Full-Duplex** | Both ways, parallel | Yes | Telephone conversation |

In simplex, the sender can only transmit; the receiver cannot reply. In half-duplex, both devices can transmit and receive, but not at the same time — they must take turns. In full-duplex, both devices can transmit and receive simultaneously.

---

## PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Long Answer with Internal Choice)

### Question A: Comprehensive Question on Concepts and Terminology

**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]`**

**a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the five components of a data communication system. Describe the three criteria for evaluating network effectiveness. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Explain the three categories of networks (LAN, MAN, WAN) with their characteristics. List and explain any two physical topologies with diagrams. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution

**Five Components of Data Communication (with block diagram):**

A data communication system consists of the following five components:

1. **Message** — The information (text, audio, video, etc.) that needs to be transmitted from one device to another.
2. **Sender** — The device that originates the message and sends it. Examples include a computer, mobile phone, or workstation.
3. **Receiver** — The device that receives the message sent by the sender. Examples include a computer, printer, or server.
4. **Transmission Medium** — The physical path over which the message travels from sender to receiver. It can be a **guided medium** (twisted-pair cable, coaxial cable, fiber optic) or an **unguided medium** (radio waves, microwaves, infrared).
5. **Protocol** — A set of rules agreed upon by both sender and receiver that governs the data communication process. It defines the format, timing, sequencing, and error-control rules.

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    msg["MESSAGE"]
    proto["PROTOCOL"]
    sender["SENDER"]
    medium["TRANSMISSION MEDIUM"]
    receiver["RECEIVER"]

    msg --> sender
    sender --> proto
    proto --> medium
    medium --> receiver
```

**Three Criteria for Network Effectiveness:**

1. **Performance** — Performance is measured using **transit time** (the time taken for a message to travel from source to destination) and **response time** (the time between an inquiry and the corresponding response). Performance is influenced by the number of users, the type of transmission medium, the efficiency of software, and the capabilities of hardware.

2. **Reliability** — Reliability is measured by the **frequency of network failures**, the **time taken to recover** from a failure (downtime), and the network's **robustness** during catastrophic events such as natural disasters or power outages.

3. **Security** — Security involves protecting data from unauthorized access, ensuring the **privacy** of sensitive information transmitted over the network, and implementing proper **authentication and authorization** mechanisms to verify the identity of users and devices.

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

**Three Categories of Networks:**

| Category | Full Form | Geographic Span | Speed | Example |
|----------|-----------|-----------------|-------|---------|
| **LAN** | Local Area Network | < 5 km (room to building) | High (100 Mbps – 10 Gbps) | Office network, College lab |
| **MAN** | Metropolitan Area Network | 5 km – 100 km (city) | Moderate | Cable TV network, Bank branches in a city |
| **WAN** | Wide Area Network | > 100 km (country/world) | Lower per link | Internet, Satellite communication |

**LAN** connects computers and peripherals within a small geographical area, typically owned by a single organization. **MAN** covers a city or metropolitan area and may be owned by a single organization (e.g., a municipal corporation) or a consortium. **WAN** spans large geographical areas and is typically composed of multiple smaller networks owned by different entities. The **Internet** is the largest WAN.

**Physical Topology 1: STAR Topology**

In a star topology, all devices are connected to a central device called a **hub** or **switch**. All communication passes through the central node.

- **Advantages:** Easy to install, easy to add new devices, failure of one node does not affect others, simple troubleshooting.
- **Disadvantages:** The central hub is a single point of failure — if it fails, the entire network goes down.
- **Use case:** Most modern home and office networks (Wi-Fi routers, Ethernet switches).

**Physical Topology 2: BUS Topology**

In a bus topology, all devices are connected to a single shared communication line called the **backbone cable**. Data is transmitted in both directions along the cable.

- **Advantages:** Simple, inexpensive, requires less cabling than star or mesh.
- **Disadvantages:** A break in the backbone cable disrupts the entire network; performance degrades under heavy traffic; difficult to troubleshoot.
- **Use case:** Older Ethernet networks (10BASE2, 10BASE5).

**Physical Topology 3 (Bonus): MESH Topology**

In a fully connected mesh, every device is connected to every other device by a dedicated point-to-point link. For **N** nodes, the number of links required is $\frac{N(N-1)}{2}$.

- **Advantages:** Extremely reliable — multiple redundant paths ensure data delivery even if several links fail.
- **Disadvantages:** Very expensive due to the large number of cables and I/O ports required.
- **Use case:** Internet backbone, military networks, critical infrastructure.

---

### Question B: Alternative Choice (Internal Choice for KTU 2024)

**`[KTU University Exam – June 2024 / University Model Paper]`**

**a)** What is a Protocol? Explain the three key elements of a protocol (Syntax, Semantics, Timing) with examples. Differentiate between standards and protocols. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Define the following terms with examples: (i) Bandwidth, (ii) Latency, (iii) Jitter, (iv) Throughput, (v) Topology, (vi) Firewall, (vii) Router. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution

**Definition of Protocol:**

A **protocol** is a set of rules that govern data communication between two or more devices. It defines the format, order, timing, and error-handling procedures for exchanging messages.

**Three Key Elements of a Protocol:**

1. **Syntax** — Refers to the **structure or format** of the data being exchanged. It specifies the order in which bits, characters, or fields are arranged.
   - *Example:* In an Ethernet frame, the first 6 bytes are the destination MAC address, the next 6 bytes are the source MAC address, followed by the type field, payload, and CRC. The exact order of these fields is the syntax.

2. **Semantics** — Refers to the **meaning of each section of bits** in the transmitted data. It specifies what each field represents and what action should be taken upon receiving it.
   - *Example:* The "Type" field in an Ethernet frame tells the receiver which upper-layer protocol (e.g., IPv4, IPv6, ARP) the payload belongs to. The receiver uses this field to correctly hand off the data to the appropriate software module.

3. **Timing** — Refers to **when data should be sent and how fast**. It defines the synchronization between sender and receiver, the order of messages, and the speed at which they are exchanged.
   - *Example:* In a TCP three-way handshake, the sender must first send a SYN packet, then wait for a SYN-ACK from the receiver, and finally send an ACK. This strict ordering is the timing element.

**Difference between Standards and Protocols:**

| Aspect | Protocol | Standard |
|--------|----------|----------|
| **Definition** | A set of rules for communication | An agreed-upon protocol or specification that ensures interoperability |
| **Scope** | Defines how devices communicate | Defines what should be implemented to ensure compatibility |
| **Example** | TCP, HTTP, FTP | IEEE 802.3 (Ethernet), IEEE 802.11 (Wi-Fi), RS-232 |
| **Role** | Operative — actually used in networks | Normative — provides the blueprint for protocol implementations |

A protocol is the *recipe*, while a standard is the *published cookbook* that the entire industry follows to ensure their recipes are compatible with others.

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

| # | Term | Definition | Example |
|---|------|------------|---------|
| 1 | **Bandwidth** | The maximum rate at which data can be transmitted over a network link, measured in bits per second (bps). | A 100 Mbps Ethernet link can carry up to 100 megabits of data per second. |
| 2 | **Latency** | The time delay between the moment a data packet is sent and the moment it is received. Includes propagation delay, transmission delay, processing delay, and queuing delay. | Satellite internet has high latency (~600 ms) due to the long distance to the satellite. |
| 3 | **Jitter** | The variation in latency over time. If packets arrive at irregular intervals, jitter is high. | In a VoIP call, variable jitter causes choppy or robotic-sounding audio. |
| 4 | **Throughput** | The actual rate at which data is successfully delivered over a network, measured in bps. It is usually lower than bandwidth due to overhead, congestion, and errors. | A 100 Mbps link may only achieve 80 Mbps throughput under heavy load. |
| 5 | **Topology** | The geometric arrangement of devices and links in a network (e.g., star, bus, ring, mesh, hybrid). | A home Wi-Fi network typically uses a star topology with the router at the center. |
| 6 | **Firewall** | A network security device (hardware or software) that monitors and filters incoming and outgoing traffic based on predefined security rules. | A corporate firewall blocks access to social media websites during work hours. |
| 7 | **Router** | A Layer 3 device that forwards data packets between different computer networks, using IP addresses to determine the best path. | The home Wi-Fi router connects the local LAN to the ISP's WAN (the Internet). |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls to Avoid:**
> 1. **Confusing "Bandwidth" and "Throughput":** Many students use these terms interchangeably. *Bandwidth is the theoretical maximum capacity; throughput is the actual achieved rate.* Examiners will deduct 1–2 marks if you do not differentiate.
> 2. **Forgetting the formula in mesh topology:** A common error is writing $N(N-1)$ instead of $\frac{N(N-1)}{2}$. The division by 2 is crucial because links are bidirectional and should not be double-counted.
> 3. **Not providing diagrams for topology questions:** Even if the question says "with diagram", students often skip it. A neat labeled diagram is worth 2–3 marks by itself.
> 4. **Confusing LAN with WAN boundaries:** A 50 km network is MAN, not WAN. A 200 km network is WAN, not MAN. Remember the rough thresholds: LAN < 5 km, MAN 5–100 km, WAN > 100 km.
> 5. **Mixing up Simplex and Half-Duplex examples:** Walkie-talkie is half-duplex, NOT simplex. TV broadcast is simplex (one-way), NOT half-duplex.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Data Communication** = transfer of data between two devices; **Networking** = interconnection of multiple devices to share resources.
- A data communication system has exactly **5 components**: Message, Sender, Receiver, Transmission Medium, Protocol.
- **Three data flow modes**: Simplex (one-way), Half-Duplex (alternating two-way), Full-Duplex (simultaneous two-way).
- **Three network criteria**: Performance (transit time, response time), Reliability (failure rate, recovery time), Security (data protection, privacy, authentication).
- **Three primary network categories**: LAN (< 5 km), MAN (5–100 km), WAN (> 100 km). Plus PAN, CAN, and the Internet (internetwork).
- **Two connection types**: Point-to-Point (two devices) and Multipoint (three or more devices sharing a link).
- **Five major topologies**: Mesh, Star, Bus, Ring, Hybrid.
- **Mesh links formula** (HIGH YIELD): $N_{\text{links}} = \frac{N(N-1)}{2}$ — derived from $\binom{N}{2}$ combinations.
- **Three protocol elements**: Syntax (structure), Semantics (meaning), Timing (synchronization).
- **Five key standards bodies**: ISO, ITU-T, IEEE, IETF, EIA.
- **Two reference models**: OSI (7 layers — theoretical) and TCP/IP (4 layers — practical).
- **Key metric definitions**: Bandwidth (theoretical max), Throughput (actual rate), Latency (delay), Jitter (latency variation).
- **Common devices**: Router (Layer 3, between networks), Switch (Layer 2, within LAN), Firewall (security), Hub (Layer 1, basic repeater).
- **Memory anchors for the exam**: Mesh = *maximum reliability, maximum cost*; Star = *minimum cost, single point of failure*; Bus = *single backbone, cable break kills all*; Ring = *token passing, deterministic*.
- **Real-world anchor**: The Internet = the largest WAN; a college campus LAN = typical LAN; a citywide cable TV network = typical MAN; your Bluetooth earphones = typical PAN.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCERT602 - DATA COMMUNICATION AND NETWORKING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Analog and Digital Data Transmission

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Data and Signals — The Foundation of Communication

In the formal vocabulary of **Data Communication and Networking**, *data* refers to a *fact*, a *figure*, or an *entity* that can be processed by a computer, whereas *signals* are the *electromagnetic or optical representations* by which data is propagated from one point to another.

#### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> **Data**: A representation of facts, concepts, or instructions in a formalized manner suitable for communication, interpretation, or processing by humans or by automatic means.
> 
> **Signal**: The physical quantity (typically a time-varying voltage, current, light intensity, or radio wave) that carries the data through the transmission medium.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (KTU Board Favourite):**
> - **Data** is the *information* to be conveyed.
> - **Signal** is the *vehicle* that carries that information across the medium.

#### Conceptual Analogy — The "Postal System"

Imagine you are sending a *letter* (the **data**) from Trivandrum to Kochi. You cannot physically teleport the letter — you put it inside an *envelope* (the **signal**) and hand it to a postal carrier (the **transmission medium**). Different envelopes (analog envelopes vs. digital envelopes) work differently:
- An **analog envelope** is a continuous waveform — like a sealed wax scroll where the message's pressure and ink flow vary smoothly.
- A **digital envelope** is a discrete ON/OFF pattern — like a torch blinking in Morse code (light = 1, dark = 0).

The contents (data) remain the same, but the *envelope type* (signal) determines how reliably and how fast the message travels.

---

### 1.2 Data — Two Fundamental Types

| Data Type | Definition | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Analog Data** | Continuous in nature, takes continuous values over an interval. | Human voice, temperature readings from a mercury thermometer |
| **Digital Data** | Discrete in nature, takes a finite set of values (typically binary 0s and 1s). | Data stored in a computer's memory, alphanumeric characters |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The distinction between analog and digital is **state-based**, not technology-based. The same waveform can be digital if it represents discrete states (e.g., +5V = 1, 0V = 0), even though it is an electrical signal.

---

### 1.3 Signals — Two Fundamental Types

#### 1.3.1 Analog Signal
An **analog signal** is a continuous waveform that varies smoothly over time and can take an *infinite* number of values in any given interval.

$$s(t) = A \sin(2\pi f t + \phi)$$

where $A$ = amplitude, $f$ = frequency, $\phi$ = phase.

#### 1.3.2 Digital Signal
A **digital signal** is a discrete signal that maintains a *constant* value for a given period and then abruptly changes to another value. It carries information as a sequence of **discrete pulses**, each in one of a finite set of levels (most commonly, two — representing binary bits).

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Example:** The audio waveform entering a microphone is **analog**; the same audio stored as an MP3 file is a stream of **digital** samples.

#### 1.3.3 Periodic vs. Aperiodic Signals

| Property | Periodic Signal | Aperiodic Signal |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Definition | Repeats its pattern after a fixed time period $T$ | Does NOT repeat at regular intervals |
| Mathematical Form | $s(t + T) = s(t)$ for all $t$ | $s(t + T) \neq s(t)$ for any fixed $T$ |
| Example | Sine wave, square wave | Speech signal, stock prices, data burst |

The **period** of a periodic signal is the time required to complete one full cycle, and the **frequency** is the reciprocal of the period:

$$f = \frac{1}{T} \quad [\text{Hertz, Hz}]$$

---

### 1.4 Composite Signals and the Concept of Bandwidth

In real engineering systems, most signals encountered are **composite signals** — a combination of many simple sine waves (called *frequency components*) of different amplitudes, frequencies, and phases.

By **Fourier Analysis**, any periodic composite signal $s(t)$ can be decomposed as:

$$s(t) = \frac{a_0}{2} + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} a_n \cos(2\pi n f_0 t) + b_n \sin(2\pi n f_0 t)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bandwidth (B) of a Composite Signal** is defined as the difference between the highest and lowest frequency components present in the signal:
> 
> $$B = f_{\text{max}} - f_{\text{min}} \quad [\text{Hz}]$$

For a *baseband* signal starting near 0 Hz, $f_{\text{min}} \approx 0$, so $B = f_{\text{max}}$. This is the operational definition most often used in KTU problems.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Utility:** Bandwidth determines the *capacity* of a channel. A standard voice-grade telephone channel is engineered to pass frequencies from **300 Hz to 3400 Hz**, giving a bandwidth of **3100 Hz** = **3.1 kHz**. This is why human voice is intelligible but loses its very low and very high tones during a phone call.

---

### 1.5 Transmission Modes

| Mode | Direction of Flow | Real-World Analogy | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Simplex** | One direction ONLY | A one-way street; a radio broadcast | Television broadcast, keyboard → CPU |
| **Half-Duplex** | Both directions, but NOT simultaneously | A walkie-talkie; a single-lane bridge with traffic signals | Walkie-talkie, CB radio |
| **Full-Duplex** | Both directions SIMULTANEOUSLY | A two-lane road; a phone call | Mobile phone call, Ethernet |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse *half-duplex* with *full-duplex*. A *full-duplex* system can be implemented either by using **two physical channels** (separate wires/frequencies) or by **time-division multiplexing** of a single channel at twice the data rate.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two-Domain Representation: Time vs. Frequency

Any signal can be analysed in two complementary domains:

- **Time Domain** — plots amplitude $s(t)$ as a function of time. Shows the *shape* of the signal.
- **Frequency Domain** — plots amplitude (or phase) as a function of frequency. Shows the *spectral content* of the signal.

The transformation between these two domains is given by the **Fourier Series** (for periodic signals) and the **Fourier Transform** (for aperiodic signals):

$$S(f) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} s(t) \, e^{-j 2\pi f t} \, dt$$

$$s(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} S(f) \, e^{+j 2\pi f t} \, df$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Two Domains?** The time domain shows the *engineering* view (how the signal varies on an oscilloscope). The frequency domain shows the *transmission* view (which frequencies the channel must pass). KTU problems frequently ask to convert between these two.

---

### 2.2 Digital-to-Digital Conversion (Line Coding)

Line coding is the process of converting **binary data** (a sequence of 0s and 1s) into a **digital signal** suitable for transmission.

#### 2.2.1 Categories of Line Coding

```
Line Coding
├── Unipolar        → One voltage level (e.g., 0V for 0, +5V for 1)
├── Polar
│   ├── NRZ-L       → Non-Return-to-Zero Level
│   ├── NRZ-I       → Non-Return-to-Zero Inverted
│   ├── RZ          → Return-to-Zero
│   ├── Biphase
│   │   ├── Manchester     → 0 = low-to-high, 1 = high-to-low transition
│   │   └── Differential Manchester → always has transition in middle
└── Bipolar
    ├── AMI        → Alternate Mark Inversion
    ├── Pseudoternary
    └── B8ZS, HDB3 (scrambling techniques)
```

#### 2.2.2 Unipolar, Polar, and Bipolar — The Voltage-Level Logic

| Scheme | Bit 0 | Bit 1 | DC Component | Bandwidth |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Unipolar NRZ** | 0 V | +5 V | High | $B = N/2$ |
| **Polar NRZ-L** | -5 V | +5 V | Low | $B = N/2$ |
| **Polar NRZ-I** | No transition | Invert at start | Low | $B = N/2$ |
| **Bipolar AMI** | 0 V | Alternating $+5$V, $-5$V | None | $B = N$ |
| **Manchester** | High-to-Low | Low-to-High | None | $B = N$ |
| **Differential Manchester** | Transition at start | No transition at start | None | $B = N$ |

where $N$ = bit rate (bits/second).

> [!NOTE]
> **DC Component Rule:** Any signal with a sustained non-zero average value has a **DC component**, which cannot pass through transformers or AC-coupled channels. Schemes like *Manchester* and *AMI* eliminate the DC component, making them **transformer-isolable**.

---

### 2.3 Analog-to-Digital Conversion (Digitization)

The classic pipeline for converting analog data (e.g., voice) to digital is:

```
Analog Signal → [PAM Sampling] → [Quantization] → [Encoding] = PCM bitstream
```

#### 2.3.1 Pulse Code Modulation (PCM)

PCM is the *foundational* analog-to-digital conversion technique.

**Step 1 — Sampling (PAM):** Based on the **Nyquist Sampling Theorem**:

$$f_s \geq 2 f_{\text{max}} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad T_s \leq \frac{1}{2 f_{\text{max}}}$$

If $f_s < 2 f_{\text{max}}$, an effect called **aliasing** distorts the signal irretrievably.

**Step 2 — Quantization:** The continuous sample value is mapped to one of $2^n$ discrete levels, where $n$ is the number of bits per sample.

**Step 3 — Encoding:** Each quantized level is represented as an $n$-bit binary code.

The **minimum data rate** after PCM is:

$$R_b = f_s \times n = 2 f_{\text{max}} \times n \quad \text{[bits/sec]}$$

> [!TIP]
> **CD Audio Worked Example:** Audio is band-limited to 20 kHz, so $f_s = 44.1$ kHz. With $n = 16$ bits/sample and stereo (2 channels):
> 
> $$R_b = 44{,}100 \times 16 \times 2 = 1{,}411{,}200 \text{ bps} \approx 1.41 \text{ Mbps}$$

#### 2.3.2 Delta Modulation (DM)

A simplified PCM that uses **1 bit per sample** — the bit indicates whether the signal *increased* or *decreased* relative to the previous sample.

The output bit stream is generated as:

$$b(t) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } s(t) > s'(t - T_s) \\ 0 & \text{if } s(t) \leq s'(t - T_s) \end{cases}$$

where $s'(t)$ is the reconstructed staircase approximation.

The **maximum amplitude** the DM can handle without **slope-overload distortion** is:

$$A_{\text{max}} = \frac{\Delta}{T_s} = \Delta \cdot f_s$$

where $\Delta$ is the step size.

> [!WARNING]
> **Slope Overload:** When the analog signal changes faster than the staircase can track it (i.e., its slope exceeds $\Delta f_s$), the digital output cannot keep up and the signal is severely distorted.

---

### 2.4 Digital-to-Analog Conversion (Bandpass Modulation)

When digital data must travel through a **bandpass channel** (e.g., a radio link, a telephone line with a carrier), the digital bit stream modulates a high-frequency **carrier signal** $c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$.

| Technique | Property Modulated | Modulated Output | Bits per Symbol |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **ASK** (Amplitude Shift Keying) | Amplitude | $s(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) \cdot b(t)$ | 1 |
| **FSK** (Frequency Shift Keying) | Frequency | $s(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi (f_c \pm \Delta f) t)$ | 1 |
| **PSK** (Phase Shift Keying) | Phase | $s(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t + \phi)$ | 1 |
| **QAM** (Quadrature AM) | Amplitude *and* Phase | Combination of two carriers | 2, 4, 6, … |

> [!NOTE]
> **Bandwidth Efficiency:** A pure ASK signal needs a bandwidth equal to the bit rate ($B = N$). FSK needs approximately $B = (1+d) \cdot N$ where $d$ is a frequency-deviation factor. QAM is the most bandwidth-efficient and is used in Wi-Fi, 4G, 5G, and cable modems.

---

### 2.5 Analog-to-Analog Conversion (Carrier Modulation of Analog Data)

When an *analog* baseband signal (e.g., music) modulates a carrier:

| Modulation | Varying Parameter | Resulting Bandwidth | Equation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **AM** (Amplitude Modulation) | Amplitude | $B_{\text{AM}} = 2B$ | $s(t) = [A_c + m(t)] \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ |
| **FM** (Frequency Modulation) | Frequency | $B_{\text{FM}} = 2(\Delta f + B)$ | $s(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi (f_c + k_f m(t)) t)$ |
| **PM** (Phase Modulation) | Phase | $B_{\text{PM}} = 2(\beta + 1)B$ | $s(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t + k_p m(t))$ |

where $B$ is the bandwidth of the message signal, $\Delta f$ is the maximum frequency deviation, and $\beta$ is the modulation index.

> [!TIP]
> **AM vs FM in Engineering Practice:** AM radio uses $B \approx 10$ kHz (voice band), while FM broadcast uses $B \approx 200$ kHz — that's why FM is "high fidelity" but consumes 20× the spectrum.

---

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Statement | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $f = 1/T$ | Frequency is the inverse of period | Hertz (Hz) |
| 2 | $B = f_{\text{max}} - f_{\text{min}}$ | Bandwidth of a composite signal | Hz |
| 3 | $N_{\text{bit}} = 2 \cdot B \cdot \log_2 M$ | **Nyquist Bit Rate** (noiseless channel) | bps |
| 4 | $C = B \cdot \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ | **Shannon Capacity** (noisy channel) | bps |
| 5 | $C = B \cdot \log_2(1 + S/N)$ | Shannon Capacity in dB form (use $\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(S/N)$) | bps |
| 6 | $f_s \geq 2 f_{\text{max}}$ | Nyquist Sampling Theorem | Hz |
| 7 | $R_b = f_s \cdot n$ | PCM bit rate | bps |
| 8 | $A_{\text{max}} = \Delta \cdot f_s$ | Delta Modulation max trackable amplitude | Volts |
| 9 | $B_{\text{AM}} = 2B$ | AM bandwidth | Hz |
| 10 | $B_{\text{FM}} = 2(\Delta f + B)$ | FM bandwidth (Carson's Rule) | Hz |
| 11 | $B_{\text{NRZ}} = N/2$ | NRZ line coding bandwidth | Hz |
| 12 | $B_{\text{Manchester}} = N$ | Manchester line coding bandwidth | Hz |
| 13 | $V_{\text{max}} = M - 1$ | Voltage levels for $M$-ary signaling | unitless |
| 14 | $\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(\text{SNR})$ | SNR in decibels | dB |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Nyquist sets the *theoretical maximum* in the absence of noise; Shannon sets the *practical maximum* in the presence of noise. A real KTU design problem often combines both — first determine the channel's Shannon capacity $C$, then choose $M$ such that $C \leq 2B \log_2 M$.

---

### 2.7 Why This System Exists in Engineering

The choice of analog vs. digital transmission is one of the most consequential decisions a communication engineer makes:

- **Analog Transmission** is bandwidth-efficient for naturally analog signals (radio, TV broadcast) and uses simpler hardware.
- **Digital Transmission** offers vastly superior noise immunity, error detection/correction, encryption, compression, and multiplexing — at the cost of needing AD/DA converters and synchronization.

> Modern telecommunication backbones (4G/5G, fibre-optic links, undersea cables) use **digital transmission** exclusively. The conversion from analog to digital and back is the *bedrock* of all modern data communication.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — The Nyquist Bit-Rate Formula

**Statement (to be proved):** For a *noiseless* channel of bandwidth $B$ Hz, the maximum data rate that can be transmitted using $M$ discrete signal levels is:

$$C = 2B \log_2 M \quad \text{[bits per second]}$$

**Proof:**

Consider a noiseless channel with bandwidth $B$ Hz. A fundamental result from signal processing (the *Nyquist–Shannon sampling theorem*, in its band-limited form) states that a signal of bandwidth $B$ Hz can be reconstructed from samples taken at a rate of at least $2B$ samples per second.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Let } f_s &= 2B \quad \text{(samples per second, Nyquist rate)} \\
\text{Step 2: Let each sample carry one of } M &= \text{distinct signal levels} \\
\text{Step 3: Number of bits carried per sample} &= \log_2 M \\
\text{Step 4: Bits per second (data rate)} &= (\text{samples per second}) \times (\text{bits per sample}) \\
C &= f_s \times \log_2 M \\
C &= 2B \log_2 M
\end{aligned}
$$

**Q.E.D.**

> [!TIP]
> **Special Case — Binary Signaling ($M = 2$):**
> 
> $$C = 2B \log_2 2 = 2B \times 1 = 2B \text{ bps}$$
> 
> A noiseless 3 kHz voice channel can carry at most $2 \times 3000 = 6000$ bps using binary signaling.

---

### 3.2 Derivation — The Shannon Capacity Formula

**Statement (to be proved):** For a *noisy* channel of bandwidth $B$ Hz with signal-to-noise ratio $\text{SNR} = S/N$, the theoretical maximum error-free data rate is:

$$C = B \log_2(1 + S/N) \quad \text{[bits per second]}$$

**Conceptual Proof Outline (KTU Board Style):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Number of distinguishable signal levels in noise} &= \sqrt{1 + S/N} \quad \text{(approximate)} \\
\text{Step 2: Substitute } M &= \sqrt{1 + S/N} \text{ into Nyquist's formula} \\
C &= 2B \log_2 M = 2B \log_2 \sqrt{1 + S/N} \\
\text{Step 3: Use the identity } 2 \log_2 \sqrt{x} &= \log_2 x \\
C &= B \log_2(1 + S/N)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **In decibels:** $\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(S/N)$, so $S/N = 10^{\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}}/10}$.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Examples (Exhaustive Step-by-Step)

#### Example 3.3.1 — Nyquist Capacity (Binary, $M = 2$)

**Problem:** A noiseless channel has a bandwidth of **4 kHz**. Find the maximum data rate using:
1. Binary signaling ($M = 2$)
2. Octal signaling ($M = 8$)

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Given: } B &= 4000 \text{ Hz} \\
\text{(1) Binary case: } M &= 2 \\
C &= 2B \log_2 M \\
C &= 2 \times 4000 \times \log_2 2 \\
C &= 8000 \times 1 \\
C &= 8000 \text{ bps} = 8 \text{ kbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(2) Octal case: } M &= 8 \\
C &= 2B \log_2 M \\
C &= 2 \times 4000 \times \log_2 8 \\
C &= 8000 \times 3 \\
C &= 24{,}000 \text{ bps} = 24 \text{ kbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Insight:** Tripling the bits per symbol triples the data rate — but at the cost of needing 8 distinct signal levels (more sensitive to noise).

---

#### Example 3.3.2 — Shannon Capacity with SNR in dB

**Problem:** A channel has $B = 3$ kHz and $\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 36$ dB. Find:
1. The Shannon capacity $C$.
2. The maximum $M$ such that Nyquist capacity $\leq$ Shannon capacity.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Convert SNR from dB to linear scale} \\
\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} &= 36 \\
\text{SNR (linear)} &= 10^{36/10} = 10^{3.6} \\
10^{3.6} &= 10^3 \times 10^{0.6} = 1000 \times 3.981 \\
\text{SNR (linear)} &= 3981.07 \\
\\
\text{Step 2: Apply Shannon's formula} \\
C &= B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR}) \\
C &= 3000 \times \log_2(1 + 3981.07) \\
C &= 3000 \times \log_2(3982.07) \\
C &= 3000 \times \frac{\log_{10}(3982.07)}{\log_{10}(2)} \\
C &= 3000 \times \frac{3.6}{0.30103} \\
C &= 3000 \times 11.958 \\
C &= 35{,}874 \text{ bps} \approx 35.87 \text{ kbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 3: Find maximum } M \text{ such that } 2B \log_2 M &\leq C \\
\log_2 M &\leq \frac{C}{2B} = \frac{35{,}874}{2 \times 3000} = 5.979 \\
M &\leq 2^{5.979} = 62.96 \\
M_{\text{max}} &= 62 \quad (\text{since } M \text{ must be a power of } 2 \text{ in many schemes}) \\
\text{So the maximum usable } M &= 64 \text{ if we allow slight error-rate penalty}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Trick:** If $M$ is a power of 2 and the calculated upper bound is non-integer, take the **next-lower power of 2**. So $\log_2 M \leq 5.979 \Rightarrow M \leq 62.96 \Rightarrow M_{\text{max}} = 32$ (next power of 2 $\leq 62.96$).

---

#### Example 3.3.3 — PCM Bit Rate (Voice Telephony)

**Problem:** Voice is band-limited to 4 kHz. We sample at 1.25× the Nyquist rate, with 8 bits per sample. Find the bit rate.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{\text{max}} &= 4000 \text{ Hz} \\
f_s &= 1.25 \times 2 f_{\text{max}} = 1.25 \times 8000 = 10{,}000 \text{ Hz} \\
n &= 8 \text{ bits/sample} \\
R_b &= f_s \times n = 10{,}000 \times 8 = 80{,}000 \text{ bps} = 80 \text{ kbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### Example 3.3.4 — FM Bandwidth (Carson's Rule)

**Problem:** A music signal has $B = 15$ kHz. FM modulation uses a maximum frequency deviation $\Delta f = 75$ kHz. Find the FM bandwidth.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{\text{FM}} &= 2(\Delta f + B) \\
B_{\text{FM}} &= 2(75{,}000 + 15{,}000) \\
B_{\text{FM}} &= 2 \times 90{,}000 \\
B_{\text{FM}} &= 180{,}000 \text{ Hz} = 180 \text{ kHz}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is why commercial FM radio allocates **200 kHz** per station.

---

### 3.4 Comparison Matrix — All Transmission Schemes

| Scheme | Type | Bandwidth | Noise Immunity | Synchronization Need | Common Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| NRZ-L | Polar | $N/2$ | Moderate | Clock recovery needed | Digital logic links |
| NRZ-I | Polar | $N/2$ | Better (inversion-based) | Clock recovery needed | Magnetic recording |
| Manchester | Biphase | $N$ | High | Self-clocking | Ethernet 10Base-T |
| AMI (Bipolar) | Bipolar | $N$ | High | Clock recovery | T1/E1 telecom |
| PCM | A→D | Depends on $f_s$ and $n$ | Excellent | Needs framing | Digital voice |
| ASK | D→A | $\approx N$ | Poor | Coherent detection | Optical links, RFID |
| FSK | D→A | $> N$ | Good | Easy | Modems, paging |
| PSK | D→A | $\approx N$ | Very Good | Coherent detection | Satellite, Wi-Fi |
| QAM | D→A | Compact | Very Good | Coherent detection | Cable modem, 4G/5G |
| AM | A→A | $2B$ | Poor (envelope detector) | Carrier recovery | AM radio broadcast |
| FM | A→A | $2(\Delta f + B)$ | Excellent (limiter removes AM noise) | Carrier recovery | FM radio, two-way radio |

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End Data Communication System Block Diagram

The figure below shows the high-level architecture of a generic data communication system, identifying the roles of analog/digital data, signals, and the conversion blocks discussed in this module.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src[Source Data]:::data
    sd1{Analog or Digital?}:::decision
    enc[Encoder / Source Encoder]:::process
    a2d[Analog to Digital Converter]:::process
    d2d[Line Coder / Digital to Digital]:::process
    d2a[Digital to Analog Modulator]:::process
    a2a[Analog to Analog Modulator]:::process
    tx[Transmitter Tx]:::hw
    ch[Transmission Channel Medium]:::channel
    rx[Receiver Rx]:::hw
    demod[Demodulator / Decoder]:::process
    sink[Destination Sink]:::data

    src --> sd1
    sd1 -- Analog --> a2d
    sd1 -- Digital --> d2d
    a2d --> d2d
    d2d --> d2a
    d2d --> tx
    d2a --> tx
    a2a --> tx
    tx --> ch
    ch --> rx
    rx --> demod
    demod --> sink

    classDef data fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0,stroke-width:2px,color:#0d47a1
    classDef process fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px,color:#e65100
    classDef hw fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a,stroke-width:2px,color:#4a148c
    classDef channel fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:2px,color:#1b5e20
    classDef decision fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#ad1457,stroke-width:2px,color:#880e4f
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **decision diamond** classifies incoming data as analog or digital.
- **Digital data** goes directly to the *Line Coder* (D-to-D) → *Modulator* (D-to-A) if needed → *Tx*.
- **Analog data** first goes through an **A-to-D** converter, then follows the digital path.
- The **channel** introduces noise and attenuation; the receiver reverses the operations.

---

### 4.2 Frequency-Domain Spectral Visualization

A composite periodic signal decomposes into a fundamental sine wave and its harmonics. The diagram below maps the **time-domain waveform** to its **frequency-domain spectrum**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TD[Time Domain View]
        T1[Square Waveform s of t]:::signal
        T2[Period T equals 1 over f0]:::info
        T1 --- T2
    end

    subgraph FD[Frequency Domain View]
        F1[Fundamental at f0]:::freq
        F2[3rd Harmonic at 3 f0 amplitude one third]:::freq
        F3[5th Harmonic at 5 f0 amplitude one fifth]:::freq
        F4[7th Harmonic at 7 f0 amplitude one seventh]:::freq
        F5[Bandwidth B equals 7 f0 minus f0 equals 6 f0]:::info
        F1 --- F2 --- F3 --- F4 --- F5
    end

    TD <-->|Fourier Transform| FD

    classDef signal fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b,stroke-width:2px
    classDef info fill:#fff8e1,stroke:#ff6f00,stroke-width:2px
    classDef freq fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why It Matters:** A digital signal (square wave) theoretically needs **infinite bandwidth** to be reproduced perfectly, because its Fourier series contains *all* odd harmonics. In practice, engineers pass only the first few harmonics; the resulting rounded waveform is still recognized by the receiver as a binary 0 or 1.

---

### 4.3 PCM Pipeline Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    analog[Analog Input Signal]:::data
    filter[Anti Aliasing Low Pass Filter]:::process
    sample[PAM Sampling at fs]:::process
    quant[Quantizer with 2 to the n levels]:::process
    encode[Binary Encoder n bit codes]:::process
    tx[Digital Channel Tx]:::channel
    rx[Digital Channel Rx]:::channel
    decode[Binary Decoder]:::process
    hold[Sample and Hold]:::process
    lpf[Reconstruction Low Pass Filter]:::process
    out[Analog Output]:::data

    analog --> filter --> sample --> quant --> encode --> tx --> rx --> decode --> hold --> lpf --> out

    classDef data fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:2px
    classDef process fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px
    classDef channel fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0,stroke-width:2px
```

**Block Function Notes:**
- **Anti-Aliasing Filter:** Removes all frequency components above $f_s/2$ to prevent aliasing.
- **PAM Sampler:** Multiplies the analog signal by a pulse train of frequency $f_s$.
- **Quantizer:** Maps each continuous PAM sample to its nearest discrete level out of $2^n$ levels.
- **Encoder:** Replaces each quantized level with a unique $n$-bit binary word.
- **Reconstruction Filter (Rx):** Smooths the staircase back into an analog waveform.

---

### 4.4 Digital-to-Analog Modulation Family

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    carrier[High Frequency Carrier A cos 2 pi fc t]:::hw
    bitstream[Binary Bit Stream b of t]:::data
    choose{Modulation Type}:::decision

    ask[ASK Multiply Carrier by b of t]:::process
    fsk[FSK Shift Frequency by plus or minus delta f]:::process
    psk[PSK Shift Phase by 0 or pi]:::process
    qam[QAM Modulate Two Carriers In Phase and Quadrature]:::process

    out1[ASK Output]:::signal
    out2[FSK Output]:::signal
    out3[PSK Output]:::signal
    out4[QAM Output]:::signal

    carrier --> choose
    bitstream --> choose
    choose -- Amplitude --> ask --> out1
    choose -- Frequency --> fsk --> out2
    choose -- Phase --> psk --> out3
    choose -- Amplitude and Phase --> qam --> out4

    classDef data fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:2px
    classDef hw fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a,stroke-width:2px
    classDef process fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px
    classDef signal fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b,stroke-width:2px
    classDef decision fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#ad1457,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Note:** QAM is a *superset* of ASK and PSK. A 16-QAM constellation encodes 4 bits per symbol, a 64-QAM encodes 6 bits, and 256-QAM encodes 8 bits — that is why higher-order QAM is the workhorse of modern broadband (DOCSIS 3.1, 5G NR, Wi-Fi 6).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

> Differentiate between **analog signals** and **digital signals**. State one real-world example of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Analog Signal | Digital Signal |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Definition | A continuous waveform that varies smoothly with time and can take an *infinite* number of values within a range. | A discrete signal that maintains a constant value for a period and then changes abruptly to one of a *finite* set of values. |
| Values | Continuous (infinite set) | Discrete (e.g., binary 0 and 1) |
| Example | Human voice signal, audio output of a microphone | Binary data from a computer, Ethernet Manchester-encoded bits |

**Marking distribution:** 1 mark for definition of analog, 1 mark for definition of digital, 1 mark for examples.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

> Define **bandwidth** of a signal. A voice-grade telephone channel passes frequencies from 300 Hz to 3400 Hz. Calculate its bandwidth and comment on why speech is intelligible but loses fidelity.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Bandwidth of a composite signal is the difference between its highest and lowest frequency components:

$$
\begin{aligned}
B &= f_{\text{max}} - f_{\text{min}} \\
B &= 3400 - 300 \\
B &= 3100 \text{ Hz} = 3.1 \text{ kHz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment (for the 3rd mark):** Human voice contains frequencies from about 80 Hz to 8 kHz for full fidelity, but the channel restricts the band to 300–3400 Hz. This preserves the *intelligibility* of speech (most vowel and consonant energy lies in this range) but strips away very low-frequency *richness* and very high-frequency *sibilance*, making the voice sound slightly muffled and "telephone-like."

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 1]**

**(a)** Explain with neat diagrams the three digital-to-digital line coding schemes: **NRZ-L, NRZ-I, and Manchester**, using the bit stream `1 0 0 0 1 1 1 0`. Compare their bandwidth, DC component, and synchronization capability. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Nyquist bit-rate formula** and the **Shannon capacity formula**. A channel has $B = 4$ kHz and SNR = 30 dB. Find (i) the maximum error-free data rate, and (ii) the maximum number of signal levels $M$ allowed. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part A(a) [7 marks]:**

**(1) NRZ-L (Non-Return-to-Zero Level) — 1 mark for diagram + 1 mark for rule**

Rule: Bit 1 → +V volts (high), Bit 0 → −V volts (low). The voltage level depends *only* on the bit, not on the previous bit.

For input `1 0 0 0 1 1 1 0`:

```
Bit:    1   0   0   0   1   1   1   0
NRZ-L:  +V  -V  -V  -V  +V  +V  +V  -V
```

**Draw a step waveform showing high/low rectangles of equal width.** Bandwidth $B = N/2$.

**(2) NRZ-I (Non-Return-to-Zero Inverted) — 1 mark for diagram + 1 mark for rule**

Rule: Bit 1 → transition (invert) at the start of the bit interval. Bit 0 → no transition.

For input `1 0 0 0 1 1 1 0` (assume starting at +V):

```
Bit:    1   0   0   0   1   1   1   0
NRZ-I:  -V  -V  -V  -V  +V  -V  +V  +V
```

(First bit causes inversion from +V to −V; subsequent 0s cause no change; 1s cause inversion.)

**Bandwidth:** $B = N/2$. **Advantage over NRZ-L:** provides a transition for every 1, aiding synchronization.

**(3) Manchester — 1 mark for diagram + 1 mark for rule**

Rule: Bit 0 → high-to-low transition in the middle of the bit. Bit 1 → low-to-high transition. Always has a transition in the middle of every bit period (self-clocking).

For input `1 0 0 0 1 1 1 0`:

```
Bit:    1   0   0   0   1   1   1   0
Manch: ↗↘  ↘↗  ↘↗  ↘↗  ↗↘  ↗↘  ↗↘  ↘↗
```

(show with mid-bit transitions).

**Bandwidth:** $B = N$ (double NRZ). **DC component:** None (perfectly balanced). **Sync:** Excellent — every bit has a mid-bit transition.

**Comparison Table (1 mark):**

| Scheme | Bandwidth | DC Component | Sync |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| NRZ-L | $N/2$ | Present (when more 1s than 0s or vice-versa) | Poor |
| NRZ-I | $N/2$ | Reduced | Better than NRZ-L |
| Manchester | $N$ | Zero | Excellent (self-clocking) |

---

**Model Solution — Part A(b) [7 marks]:**

**(1) Nyquist Formula Derivation — 2 marks:** See derivation in Section 3.1 of these notes. Final form: $C = 2B \log_2 M$ bps.

**(2) Shannon Formula Derivation — 2 marks:** See derivation in Section 3.2. Final form: $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ bps.

**(3) Numerical Solution — 3 marks:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Given: } B &= 4 \text{ kHz} = 4000 \text{ Hz} \\
\text{SNR} &= 30 \text{ dB} \\
\text{SNR (linear)} &= 10^{30/10} = 10^3 = 1000 \\
\\
\text{(i) Shannon Capacity: } C &= B \log_2(1 + S/N) \\
C &= 4000 \times \log_2(1 + 1000) \\
C &= 4000 \times \log_2(1001) \\
C &= 4000 \times \frac{\log_{10}(1001)}{\log_{10}(2)} \\
C &= 4000 \times \frac{3.0004}{0.30103} \\
C &= 4000 \times 9.967 \\
C &\approx 39{,}870 \text{ bps} \approx 39.87 \text{ kbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(ii) Maximum } M \text{ from Nyquist: } C &= 2B \log_2 M \\
\log_2 M &= \frac{C}{2B} = \frac{39{,}870}{2 \times 4000} = 4.984 \\
M &= 2^{4.984} \approx 31.66 \\
M_{\text{max}} &= 32 \quad (\text{taking next-lower power of 2}) \\
\text{Final answer: } M &= 32 \text{ signal levels}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Lose up to 2 marks):**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert SNR from dB to linear scale** — students often plug 30 directly into Shannon's formula, getting $C = 4000 \log_2 31$, which is off by a factor of ~6.
> 2. **Taking the wrong power of 2** — students should take the *next-lower* power of 2 if the calculated bound is non-integer. Using $M = 64$ would violate Shannon's limit.

---

#### **Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1]**

**(a)** Explain **Pulse Code Modulation (PCM)** with a neat block diagram. A voice signal is band-limited to 3.4 kHz and is sampled at 8 kHz with 8 bits per sample. Calculate the bit rate. If we use Delta Modulation with step size $\Delta = 0.05$ V, what is the maximum amplitude that can be encoded without slope-overload distortion? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **AM, FM, and PM** analog-to-analog modulation techniques in terms of varying parameter, bandwidth equation, and noise immunity. An FM system transmits a 15 kHz audio signal with a maximum frequency deviation of 75 kHz. Calculate the transmission bandwidth using Carson's rule. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part B(a) [7 marks]:**

**PCM Block Diagram (3 marks):**

Draw the standard 4-stage PCM transmitter:
- **Anti-aliasing filter** → **Sampler (PAM)** → **Quantizer** → **Encoder**

**Working Principle (2 marks):**
- **Sampling** (Nyquist theorem): The analog signal must be sampled at $f_s \geq 2 f_{\text{max}}$ to avoid aliasing.
- **Quantization**: Each sample is approximated to the nearest of $2^n$ levels, where $n$ is the bits per sample. This introduces *quantization noise*.
- **Encoding**: Each quantized level is replaced by a unique $n$-bit binary codeword.

**Numerical Solution (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_s &= 8 \text{ kHz} = 8000 \text{ Hz} \\
n &= 8 \text{ bits/sample} \\
R_b &= f_s \times n = 8000 \times 8 = 64{,}000 \text{ bps} = 64 \text{ kbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Delta Modulation Maximum Amplitude: } A_{\text{max}} &= \Delta \times f_s \\
A_{\text{max}} &= 0.05 \times 8000 \\
A_{\text{max}} &= 400 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Wait — the step size is 0.05 V and $f_s = 8000$ Hz, so $A_{\text{max}} = 0.05 \times 8000 = 400$ V. This is unusually large, but mathematically valid: a larger $f_s$ allows the staircase to track steeper slopes.

---

**Model Solution — Part B(b) [7 marks]:**

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Parameter | AM | FM | PM |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Varying parameter | Amplitude | Frequency | Phase |
| Bandwidth | $2B$ | $2(\Delta f + B)$ | $2(\beta + 1)B$ |
| Noise immunity | Poor (envelope detection picks up AM noise) | Excellent (limiter removes AM noise) | Good |
| Equipment complexity | Low | High | High |
| Power efficiency | Low (carrier carries no info) | High | High |

**Carson's Rule Calculation (4 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Given: } B &= 15 \text{ kHz} = 15{,}000 \text{ Hz} \\
\Delta f &= 75 \text{ kHz} = 75{,}000 \text{ Hz} \\
B_{\text{FM}} &= 2(\Delta f + B) \\
B_{\text{FM}} &= 2(75{,}000 + 15{,}000) \\
B_{\text{FM}} &= 2 \times 90{,}000 \\
B_{\text{FM}} &= 180{,}000 \text{ Hz} = 180 \text{ kHz}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Lose up to 3 marks):**
> 1. **Confusing $B$ in Carson's rule with the *modulation index* $\beta$** — $B$ here is the *message bandwidth*, not the modulation index.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in AM bandwidth** — AM bandwidth is $2B$, not $B$.
> 3. **Skipping the unit conversions** — KTU expects kHz answers in kHz.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Analog and Digital Data Transmission**

- [x] **Data vs. Signal** — Data is information; signal is the physical carrier.
- [x] **Analog vs. Digital Data/Signals** — Continuous infinite values vs. discrete finite values.
- [x] **Periodic vs. Aperiodic** — Repeating pattern (sinusoid) vs. non-repeating (speech).
- [x] **Frequency** = $1/T$; **Bandwidth** = $f_{\text{max}} - f_{\text{min}}$; **Period** in seconds; **Frequency** in Hz.
- [x] **Time Domain** shows *shape*; **Frequency Domain** shows *spectral content*; linked by Fourier Transform.
- [x] **Transmission Modes:** Simplex (1-way), Half-Duplex (both ways, not simultaneously), Full-Duplex (both ways, simultaneously).
- [x] **Nyquist Bit Rate** (noiseless): $C = 2B \log_2 M$ bps.
- [x] **Shannon Capacity** (noisy): $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ bps. SNR in dB: $10 \log_{10}(S/N)$.
- [x] **Line Coding Categories:** Unipolar (1 level), Polar (2 levels: NRZ-L, NRZ-I, RZ, Manchester), Bipolar (3 levels: AMI).
- [x] **Manchester** = self-clocking, no DC, bandwidth $B = N$. Used in 10Base-T Ethernet.
- [x] **NRZ** = bandwidth $B = N/2$, has DC component, poor synchronization.
- [x] **AMI (Bipolar)** = alternating ±V for 1s, zero DC, used in T1/E1.
- [x] **PCM Pipeline:** Anti-alias filter → Sample (PAM) → Quantize → Encode.
- [x] **Nyquist Sampling Theorem:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{\text{max}}$ to avoid aliasing.
- [x] **PCM Bit Rate:** $R_b = f_s \times n$ bps.
- [x] **Delta Modulation:** 1 bit per sample, max trackable amplitude $A_{\text{max}} = \Delta \times f_s$.
- [x] **D-to-A Modulation:** ASK (amplitude), FSK (frequency), PSK (phase), QAM (both amplitude and phase — most bandwidth-efficient).
- [x] **A-to-A Modulation:** AM (bandwidth $2B$), FM (bandwidth $2(\Delta f + B)$ — Carson's Rule), PM (bandwidth $2(\beta+1)B$).
- [x] **DC Component Rule:** Self-clocking codes (Manchester, AMI) have zero DC; NRZ codes do not.
- [x] **Bandwidth Relationship:** A perfect square wave has infinite bandwidth; in practice, a channel passes only the first few harmonics.

> **Final Examination Advice (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> - When a question gives "SNR in dB," **always convert to linear** before substituting into Shannon's formula.
> - When asked for the maximum number of signal levels, take the **next-lower power of 2** if the calculated bound is not an integer power of 2.
> - In line-coding waveform problems, **show the mid-bit transition explicitly** for Manchester and Differential Manchester.
> - Always specify the **unit** (Hz, kHz, bps, kbps) in your final numerical answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transmission Impairments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCERT602 - DATA COMMUNICATION AND NETWORKING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Transmission Impairments # 📘 Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Transmission Impairments** are the unwanted phenomena and effects that degrade the quality, fidelity, and integrity of a signal as it propagates from the **source (transmitter)** to the **destination (receiver)** through a physical transmission medium (copper wire, optical fiber, or wireless channel). The three canonical impairments recognized in the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus are **Attenuation**, **Distortion**, and **Noise**. In a perfect world, a signal launched into a medium would arrive at the receiver identical to what was sent. In reality, the medium is an imperfect, energy-dissipating, and interference-prone channel. Transmission impairments quantify *how* and *why* the received signal differs from the transmitted one. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Long Hallway Whisper" Imagine **Person A** whispering a message to **Person B** at the far end of a long, noisy hallway: - The message gets **quieter** the farther it travels (this is **Attenuation**). - The crisp consonants and vowels become **muddled and warped** because high-frequency and low-frequency components travel at slightly different speeds (this is **Distortion**). - Background conversations, door slams, and AC hum get **added** to the message (this is **Noise**). The receiver (Person B) thus hears a *weaker, distorted, and noise-corrupted* version of the original message. The job of a communication engineer is to **measure, model, and mitigate** these three impairments. ## 1.3 The Three Pillars of Impairment | # | Impairment | One-Line Meaning | |---|------------|------------------| | 1 | **Attenuation** | Loss of signal **strength / power** with distance | | 2 | **Distortion** | Change in the **shape / form** of the signal | | 3 | **Noise** | Unwanted random signals **added** to the original | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Module 1 Highlight:** A signal can suffer from one, two, or all three impairments simultaneously. The design of repeaters, amplifiers, equalizers, and shielding techniques directly addresses these three phenomena. ## 1.4 Why Transmission Impairments Matter Without understanding impairments, we cannot answer critical design questions such as: - *How far can a signal travel before it becomes unreadable?* - *What is the maximum data rate achievable on a noisy channel?* - *Why do we need repeaters in long-distance optical links?* These questions form the backbone of the famous **Shannon Capacity Theorem** and the **Nyquist Bit-Rate Formula**, both of which are **high-yield KTU 2024 examination topics** linked to this discussion. --- # 📘 Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Attenuation — Loss of Signal Strength ### 2.1.1 What Is It? **Attenuation** is the gradual loss of signal power (or amplitude) as it propagates through a medium. It is caused by resistance in the conductor, scattering in optical fiber, and free-space path loss in wireless channels. Attenuation is **distance-dependent** and is the reason cables have a maximum reliable length. ### 2.1.2 Why It Happens - **Copper cables**: Resistive heating dissipates signal energy as heat. - **Optical fiber**: Scattering, absorption, and bending losses. - **Wireless**: Free-space path loss (Friis transmission equation). ### 2.1.3 Measured In — The Decibel (dB) Engineers do **not** measure attenuation in linear units (W or V) because signal levels span many orders of magnitude. Instead, attenuation is expressed in **decibels (dB)** — a logarithmic unit. > [!IMPORTANT] > A **negative dB value** means **loss (attenuation)**, while a **positive dB value** means **gain (amplification)**. ### 2.1.4 Operational Logic — Step by Step 1. The transmitter launches a signal of power $P_1$ (or voltage $V_1$). 2. The medium dissipates a portion of this energy. 3. The receiver receives a smaller power $P_2$ (or voltage $V_2$). 4. The ratio $P_2 / P_1$ (or $V_2 / V_1$) is converted to decibels. ## 2.2 Distortion — Change in Signal Shape ### 2.2.1 What Is It? **Distortion** occurs when a signal's components (different frequencies) undergo **different amounts of delay or attenuation** while passing through the medium. As a result, the received signal has a different *shape* than the transmitted one. ### 2.2.2 Two Major Sub-Types 1. **Attenuation Distortion (Amplitude Distortion)** — Different frequency components are attenuated by different amounts. 2. **Delay Distortion (Phase Distortion)** — Different frequency components travel at different velocities, arriving at different times. ### 2.2.3 Why It Happens The medium's **impedance** and **propagation velocity** are frequency-dependent. In a voice-grade channel (≈ 300 Hz to 3400 Hz), mid-frequencies travel faster than edge frequencies, causing intersymbol interference at high data rates. ## 2.3 Noise — Unwanted Random Additions > [!NOTE] > **Noise** is any unwanted energy that interferes with the signal, distorting or masking it. The four types of noise covered in the KTU 2024 syllabus are summarized below. | Type of Noise | Cause | Nature | |---------------|-------|--------| | **Thermal Noise** (Johnson-Nyquist) | Random motion of electrons in conductor | Uniform across all frequencies (White) | | **Induced Noise** | Motors, switches, fluorescent lights | Found near source of disturbance | | **Crosstalk** | Coupling between adjacent wires | From neighboring channels | | **Impulse Noise** | Sudden spikes (lightning, power surges) | Irregular, high-amplitude, short-duration | ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **The following formulas are examinable for both Part A (3 marks) derivations and Part B (14 marks) numerical problems in the KTU 2024 ESE.** $$ \boxed{\;\text{Attenuation (dB)} \;=\; 10 \,\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right) \;=\; 20 \,\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_2}{V_1}\right)\;} $$ $$ \boxed{\;\text{Noise Power (Thermal)} \;=\; N \;=\; k \cdot T \cdot B\;} $$ $$ \boxed{\;\text{SNR (dB)} \;=\; 10 \,\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{S}{N}\right)\;} $$ $$ \boxed{\;\text{Nyquist Bit Rate} \;=\; N \;=\; 2 B \,\log_2 M\;} $$ $$ \boxed{\;\text{Shannon Capacity} \;=\; C \;=\; B \,\log_2\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)\;} $$ ### 2.4.1 Standard Constants & Units (Bold for Emphasis) - **Boltzmann constant $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K** - **Room temperature $T \approx 300$ K** - Bandwidth $B$ — measured in **Hertz (Hz)** - Power $P$ — measured in **Watts (W)** or milliwatts (**mW**) - SNR — dimensionless, expressed in **dB** for engineering use - $M$ — number of discrete signal levels (e.g., $M = 2$ for binary, $M = 4$ for 4-PAM) ### 2.4.2 Comparative Table — Nyquist vs. Shannon | Parameter | Nyquist (Noiseless Channel) | Shannon (Noisy Channel) | |-----------|------------------------------|--------------------------| | **Considers Noise?** | No (ideal channel) | Yes (realistic channel) | | **Formula** | $N = 2B \log_2 M$ | $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ | | **Limiting Factor** | Bandwidth $B$ & levels $M$ | Bandwidth $B$ & SNR | | **Maximum Speed** | Limited by technology | Fundamental theoretical limit | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility**: Shannon's formula gives the **theoretical maximum**; Nyquist gives a **practical achievable rate** for a given signaling scheme. Real systems always operate **below** the Shannon limit. --- # 📘 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Decibel Formula (Power) Starting from the definition of a **bel** — the base-10 logarithm of a power ratio: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Bel} &= \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) \end{aligned} $$ Since the bel is too large a unit for practical engineering, the decibel is defined as one-tenth of a bel: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{dB} &= 10 \times \text{Bel} \\ \text{dB} &= 10 \,\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) \end{aligned} $$ For voltage (or current) ratios, we use the relationship $P = V^2 / R$. Assuming the same load resistance $R$ on both sides: $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{P_2}{P_1} &= \frac{V_2^2 / R}{V_1^2 / R} = \left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)^2 \\ \text{dB} &= 10 \,\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)^2 \\ \text{dB} &= 2 \times 10 \,\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right) \\ \text{dB} &= 20 \,\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right) \end{aligned} $$ ## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Attenuation Calculation > **Problem (KTU Pattern):** A signal with power $P_1 = 200$ mW is launched into a cable. At the receiving end, the power is $P_2 = 2$ mW. Find the attenuation in dB. If the same cable reduces a 10 V signal to 1 V, what is the corresponding voltage attenuation? ### Step 1 — Power Attenuation We substitute directly into the decibel formula: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Attenuation (dB)} &= 10 \,\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) \\ &= 10 \,\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{2 \text{ mW}}{200 \text{ mW}}\right) \\ &= 10 \,\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{100}\right) \\ &= 10 \,\log_{10}\!\left(10^{-2}\right) \\ &= 10 \times (-2) \\ &= -20 \text{ dB} \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation:** A signal loss of **20 dB** over the cable length (the minus sign indicates loss). ### Step 2 — Voltage Attenuation $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Attenuation (dB)} &= 20 \,\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right) \\ &= 20 \,\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1 \text{ V}}{10 \text{ V}}\right) \\ &= 20 \,\log_{10}\!\left(10^{-1}\right) \\ &= 20 \times (-1) \\ &= -20 \text{ dB} \end{aligned} $$ Both calculations confirm a **20 dB attenuation**, which is the KTU-standard expected answer. ## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Shannon & Nyquist Combined > **Problem:** A channel has a bandwidth $B = 3 \text{ kHz}$ and an SNR of 30 dB. Calculate: > (i) Maximum channel capacity using **Shannon's** formula. > (ii) Maximum data rate using **Nyquist's** formula for $M = 2$ signal levels. > (iii) Maximum data rate using **Nyquist's** formula for $M = 4$ signal levels. ### Step 1 — Convert SNR from dB to Linear The decibel form of SNR is: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} &= 10 \,\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{S}{N}\right) \\ 30 &= 10 \,\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{S}{N}\right) \\ \frac{S}{N} &= 10^{30/10} = 10^3 = 1000 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 2 — Shannon Capacity $$ \begin{aligned} C &= B \,\log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \\ &= 3000 \times \log_2(1 + 1000) \\ &= 3000 \times \log_2(1001) \end{aligned} $$ Computing the logarithm: $$ \begin{aligned} \log_2(1001) &= \frac{\log_{10}(1001)}{\log_{10}(2)} \approx \frac{3.0004}{0.3010} \approx 9.967 \\ C &\approx 3000 \times 9.967 \\ C &\approx 29{,}902 \text{ bps} \;\;(\approx 29.9 \text{ kbps}) \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3 — Nyquist Bit Rate for $M = 2$ $$ \begin{aligned} N_{M=2} &= 2B \,\log_2 M \\ &= 2 \times 3000 \times \log_2(2) \\ &= 2 \times 3000 \times 1 \\ &= 6000 \text{ bps} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4 — Nyquist Bit Rate for $M = 4$ $$ \begin{aligned} N_{M=4} &= 2B \,\log_2 M \\ &= 2 \times 3000 \times \log_2(4) \\ &= 2 \times 3000 \times 2 \\ &= 12{,}000 \text{ bps} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 5 — Comparative Insight | Scheme | Bit Rate | Comment | |--------|----------|---------| | **Shannon Limit** | 29,902 bps | Theoretic ceiling (noisy channel) | | **Nyquist, $M=2$** | 6,000 bps | Binary signaling | | **Nyquist, $M=4$** | 12,000 bps | 4-level signaling (2 bits/symbol) | > [!IMPORTANT] > The **Shannon limit (29.9 kbps)** is the absolute ceiling — no real system can exceed it, regardless of the signaling scheme. The Nyquist results are **achievable** only if the channel is noiseless and the medium is ideal. ## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Thermal Noise Power > **Problem:** Calculate the thermal noise power in a 10 kHz bandwidth at room temperature ($T = 300$ K). Using $N = k T B$: $$ \begin{aligned} N &= (1.38 \times 10^{-23}) \times 300 \times (10 \times 10^{3}) \\ N &= 1.38 \times 300 \times 10^{-20} \\ N &= 414 \times 10^{-20} \\ N &= 4.14 \times 10^{-18} \text{ W} \end{aligned} $$ Converting to **dBm** (decibels relative to 1 milliwatt): $$ \begin{aligned} N_{\text{dBm}} &= 10 \,\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4.14 \times 10^{-18}}{10^{-3}}\right) \\ &= 10 \,\log_{10}(4.14 \times 10^{-15}) \\ &\approx 10 \times (-14.383) \\ &\approx -143.83 \text{ dBm} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > A noise floor of **$-144$ dBm** is the irreducible thermal noise floor in a 10 kHz channel at 300 K — a figure frequently cited in KTU previous-year questions. --- # 📘 Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Transmission System with Impairments The following **Mermaid block diagram** maps the flow of a signal from source to destination, highlighting where each impairment is introduced. ```mermaid flowchart LR TX[Source
Transmitter] --> MED[Transmission
Medium] MED --> RX[Destination
Receiver] MED -.-> ATT[Attenuation] MED -.-> DIST[Distortion] MED -.-> NOISE[Noise] NOISE --> THERM[Thermal Noise] NOISE --> IND[Induced Noise] NOISE --> CROSS[Crosstalk] NOISE --> IMP[Impulse Noise] ATT --> SOL1[Mitigation:
Amplifiers / Repeaters] DIST --> SOL2[Mitigation:
Equalizers] NOISE --> SOL3[Mitigation:
Shielding / Coding] ``` ## 4.2 Functional Flow — Impairment Classification ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Transmission Impairments] --> B[Attenuation] A --> C[Distortion] A --> D[Noise] B --> B1[Measured in dB] B --> B2[Compensated by Amplifiers] C --> C1[Attenuation Distortion] C --> C2[Delay Distortion] C --> C3[Compensated by Equalizers] D --> D1[Thermal] D --> D2[Induced] D --> D3[Crosstalk] D --> D4[Impulse] ``` ## 4.3 Signal Degradation Profile (Conceptual) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Original Signal] --> B[Medium Entry] B --> C[Medium Travel] C --> D[Receiver Input] D --> E[Recovered Signal] C -. X1 .-> F1[Power Loss: Attenuation] C -. X2 .-> F2[Shape Warping: Distortion] C -. X3 .-> F3[Added Randomness: Noise] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Interpretation of the Diagram:** As the signal passes through the medium (the **X1 / X2 / X3** annotations), it cumulatively suffers from the three impairments. The *Recovered Signal* is therefore an **approximation** of the *Original Signal* — this gap is precisely what Shannon's theorem quantifies. --- # 📘 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question]** ### **Q1. Define attenuation. Express it in dB and explain why it is measured logarithmically.** *(3 Marks)* **Model Answer:** Attenuation is the gradual loss of signal power as it propagates through a transmission medium. It is expressed in decibels (dB) using the formula: $$ \text{Attenuation (dB)} = 10 \,\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = 20 \,\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right) $$ It is measured logarithmically because signal power levels span many orders of magnitude (microwatts to megawatts), and the logarithmic scale conveniently compresses this range, making computations (like cascaded losses) additive rather than multiplicative. --- ### **Q2. List and briefly explain the four types of noise that affect data transmission.** *(3 Marks)* **Model Answer:** 1. **Thermal Noise** — Random motion of electrons in a conductor; present in all electronic media; uniform across frequencies. 2. **Induced Noise** — Generated by external sources like motors, fluorescent lights, and switching devices. 3. **Crosstalk** — Coupling of signals from adjacent wires or channels into the desired signal. 4. **Impulse Noise** — Irregular, high-amplitude spikes caused by lightning, power-line surges, or faulty equipment. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — End Semester Model Pattern, Module 1]** ### **Question A (14 Marks)** #### **(a) Explain the three main types of transmission impairments in detail with suitable diagrams. How does each affect the received signal?** *(7 Marks)* **Model Answer:** The three main transmission impairments recognized by the KTU 2024 syllabus are: **1. Attenuation (Loss of Strength)** Attenuation refers to the reduction in signal amplitude/power as it travels through the medium. It is caused by resistance (in copper), scattering (in fiber), and free-space path loss (in wireless). Measured in dB, attenuation is compensated using **amplifiers** and **repeaters**. A heavily attenuated signal may fall below the receiver's sensitivity threshold, causing bit errors. **2. Distortion (Change in Shape)** Distortion occurs when different frequency components of a signal are attenuated and delayed by different amounts. There are two sub-types: - **Attenuation Distortion** — non-uniform gain across the passband. - **Delay Distortion** — different propagation velocities for different frequencies, causing intersymbol interference (ISI). Compensated using **equalizers**. **3. Noise (Unwanted Additions)** Noise is unwanted energy that corrupts the signal. The four types (thermal, induced, crosstalk, impulse) all *add* to the signal, raising the noise floor and reducing the SNR. A lower SNR means higher bit error rate (BER), limiting the practical data rate. > [!NOTE] > **Effect on Received Signal:** The received signal is a *weaker*, *shape-distorted*, and *noise-corrupted* version of the transmitted signal. The *Signal-to-Noise Ratio* (SNR) quantifies this combined degradation. --- #### **(b) A signal of power 50 mW is transmitted through a 5 km cable. The received power is 0.5 mW. Calculate: (i) the attenuation in dB, (ii) the attenuation per kilometer, and (iii) the SNR in dB if the noise power is measured as 5 µW.** *(7 Marks)* **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Total Attenuation (dB)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Attenuation} &= 10 \,\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) \\ &= 10 \,\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{0.5 \text{ mW}}{50 \text{ mW}}\right) \\ &= 10 \,\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{100}\right) \\ &= 10 \times (-2) \\ &= -20 \text{ dB} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Attenuation per km** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Attenuation/km} &= \frac{-20 \text{ dB}}{5 \text{ km}} = -4 \text{ dB/km} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Signal-to-Noise Ratio (dB)** Signal power $S = 0.5 \text{ mW} = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ W}$ Noise power $N = 5 \,\mu\text{W} = 5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ W}$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} &= 10 \,\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{5 \times 10^{-4}}{5 \times 10^{-6}}\right) \\ &= 10 \,\log_{10}(100) \\ &= 10 \times 2 \\ &= 20 \text{ dB} \end{aligned} $$ **Final Answers:** (i) **$-20$ dB**, (ii) **$-4$ dB/km**, (iii) **$20$ dB** --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** #### **(a) With neat derivations, explain the Shannon-Hartley theorem and the Nyquist bit-rate formula. How are they related?** *(7 Marks)* **Model Answer:** **1. Nyquist Bit-Rate Formula (Noiseless Channel):** For a noiseless channel of bandwidth $B$ Hertz and $M$ discrete signal levels, the maximum data rate is: $$ N = 2B \,\log_2 M \text{ bps} $$ **Derivation Outline:** A signal of bandwidth $B$ can be sampled at the Nyquist rate $2B$ samples per second. If each sample carries $\log_2 M$ bits, the bit rate is $2B \log_2 M$. This formula does **not** consider noise — it represents the **best-case scenario**. **2. Shannon-Hartley Theorem (Noisy Channel):** For a noisy channel of bandwidth $B$ and signal-to-noise ratio $S/N$, the maximum capacity is: $$ C = B \,\log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \text{ bps} $$ **Derivation Outline:** Shannon's 1948 result considers an arbitrary amount of noise and proves that error-free transmission is possible *only* up to this capacity, regardless of signaling scheme. Increasing $B$ or $S/N$ increases $C$, but only logarithmically. **3. Relationship:** - **Nyquist** is the *physical signaling limit* (limited by bandwidth and signaling levels). - **Shannon** is the *information-theoretic limit* (limited by noise). - The actual channel capacity is the **smaller** of the two values. - A real system that respects both must satisfy: $N \le C$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight:** Doubling bandwidth $B$ doubles the Nyquist bit rate but only increases the Shannon capacity by $\log_2(1 + S/N)$ factor. --- #### **(b) Consider a channel with bandwidth $B = 4$ kHz and SNR of 24 dB. Determine: (i) the Shannon capacity, (ii) the minimum number of signal levels $M$ required by Nyquist to achieve the Shannon capacity, and (iii) the thermal noise power at $T = 300$ K.** *(7 Marks)* **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Convert SNR from dB to Linear** $$ \begin{aligned} 24 &= 10 \,\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{S}{N}\right) \\ \frac{S}{N} &= 10^{24/10} = 10^{2.4} \approx 251.19 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Shannon Capacity** $$ \begin{aligned} C &= B \,\log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \\ &= 4000 \times \log_2(1 + 251.19) \\ &= 4000 \times \log_2(252.19) \\ &\approx 4000 \times 7.978 \\ &\approx 31{,}912 \text{ bps} \;\;(\approx 31.9 \text{ kbps}) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Minimum Signal Levels $M$ for Nyquist to Match Shannon** Setting $N_{Nyquist} = C$: $$ \begin{aligned} 2B \,\log_2 M &= C \\ \log_2 M &= \frac{C}{2B} = \frac{31{,}912}{2 \times 4000} = \frac{31{,}912}{8000} \approx 3.989 \\ M &= 2^{3.989} \approx 15.91 \end{aligned} $$ Since $M$ must be an integer (and a power of 2 in practice), we round up: $M = 16$ levels are required to *match* the Shannon limit. **Step 4 — Thermal Noise Power** Using $N = k T B$ with $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K, $T = 300$ K, $B = 4000$ Hz: $$ \begin{aligned} N &= 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 300 \times 4000 \\ N &= 1.38 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-17} \\ N &= 1.656 \times 10^{-17} \text{ W} \end{aligned} $$ **Final Answers:** (i) **$\approx 31.9$ kbps**, (ii) **$M = 16$**, (iii) **$1.656 \times 10^{-17}$ W** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks** > > 1. **Sign error on dB answers** — A $-20$ dB result *does* mean 20 dB of loss; many students incorrectly report $-20$ dB as a *gain*. > 2. **Confusing $\log_2$ with $\log_{10}$** in Shannon and Nyquist formulas — these are **natural logarithms of base 2**, not 10. Use the conversion $\log_2 x = \log_{10} x / \log_{10} 2$. > 3. **Forgetting to convert SNR from dB to linear** before substituting into Shannon's formula — a classic 1-mark deduction. > 4. **Mixing up voltage and power formulas** — voltage uses the factor of 20, power uses 10. > 5. **Skipping units** in the final answer — KTU strictly deducts 0.5 marks for missing units like **dB**, **Hz**, **bps**, or **W**. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **📋 Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 1 — Transmission Impairments)** - **Three impairments** — Attenuation, Distortion, Noise. - **Attenuation** = loss of signal **power** with distance; measured in **dB**. - **Distortion** = change in signal **shape**; two sub-types — *attenuation distortion* and *delay distortion*. - **Noise** = unwanted random energy; four types — *thermal*, *induced*, *crosstalk*, *impulse*. - **Thermal noise** formula: $N = k T B$, with $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K. - **Attenuation (dB)** = $10 \log_{10}(P_2/P_1)$ = $20 \log_{10}(V_2/V_1)$. - **SNR (dB)** = $10 \log_{10}(S/N)$. - **Nyquist bit rate** = $2B \log_2 M$ (noiseless, $M$ levels). - **Shannon capacity** = $B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ (noisy, no level limit). - **Negative dB** → loss; **Positive dB** → gain. - **Cascaded attenuation** is **additive in dB**, multiplicative in linear units. - **Shannon limit is the ceiling** — no real system can exceed it. - **Real bit rate ≤ min(Nyquist, Shannon)** — exam-favorite comparison point. - **Repeaters** restore amplitude; **equalizers** correct distortion; **shielding** reduces noise. - **Bandwidth $B$ in Hz**, **Power in W or mW**, **Frequencies in Hz**. - **Always convert dB-SNR to linear** before applying Shannon's formula. - **Room temperature** in such problems is conventionally $T = 300$ K. - **KTU Module 1** expects fluency in distinguishing the four noise types — *thermal is unavoidable*, the rest are design-mitigable. ---

topics.title: Channel Capacity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCERT602 - DATA COMMUNICATION AND NETWORKING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Channel Capacity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Channel Capacity** is the absolute maximum rate (in bits per second) at which information can be reliably transmitted over a communication channel of a given bandwidth, under specified noise conditions, with arbitrarily low probability of error. It represents the theoretical upper bound of the data rate a physical channel can support.

Mathematically, Channel Capacity (C) is bounded by two fundamental theorems:
- **Nyquist Bit Rate Formula (Noiseless Channel):** Governs the maximum data rate when the channel is ideal and noise-free.
- **Shannon-Hartley Theorem (Noisy Channel):** Governs the maximum data rate when the channel is subjected to additive white Gaussian noise (AWGN).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCERT602 - Module 1):**
> Channel capacity is classified under the core data communication limits and forms the basis for understanding link utilization, modulation schemes, and physical layer design. The two governing theorems — Nyquist and Shannon — are high-weightage topics in university examinations.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **water pipeline**:
- The **diameter of the pipe** is the **Bandwidth (B)** — a wider pipe allows more water (data) to flow.
- The **pressure of water** represents the **Signal Power (S)**.
- The **dirt and impurities** in the water represent the **Noise (N)**.
- The **maximum clean water flow per second** is the **Channel Capacity (C)**.

Even if you increase pressure (signal power), impurities (noise) restrict clean flow. Conversely, a wider pipe (more bandwidth) allows more flow but cannot overcome heavy contamination (high noise) beyond a certain point. This physical intuition is precisely what Nyquist and Shannon mathematically express.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Notation | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Channel Capacity | C | bits per second (bps) |
| Bandwidth | B | Hertz (Hz) |
| Signal Power | S | Watts (W) or dBm |
| Noise Power | N | Watts (W) or dBm |
| Signal-to-Noise Ratio | SNR or S/N | Dimensionless (often in dB) |
| Number of Signal Levels | L | Discrete integer count |
| Bit Rate / Data Rate | R | bits per second (bps) |
| Boltzmann's Constant | k = 1.38 × 10⁻²³ | Joules/Kelvin |

> [!NOTE]
> The conversion between linear SNR and decibel SNR is:
> $$\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{S}{N}\right)$$

---

## 1.4 Importance in KTU Curriculum

Channel capacity is foundational for:
