\underbrace{011011101110\,111110110\,011111010}_{\text{Stuffed Data}}\;
\underbrace{[\text{16-bit FCS}]}_{\text{CRC}}\;
\underbrace{01111110}_{\text{Close Flag}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks split:** Concept (2), Rule statement (1.5), Bit-by-bit application (2.5), Final frame (1).

**(b) HDLC Frame Types and P/F Bit:**

```
        bit 7  bit 6  bit 5  bit 4  bit 3  bit 2  bit 1  bit 0
I-frame  [ 0 ][   N(S)   ][ P/F ][   N(R)   ]
S-frame  [ 1 ][ 0 ][ S ][ P/F ][   N(R)   ]
U-frame  [ 1 ][ 1 ][   U   ][ P/F ][   U   ]
```

- **I-Frame (Information):** Carries user data. Contains N(S) = send sequence number, N(R) = ACK of next expected frame.
- **S-Frame (Supervisory):** Performs flow/error control. The 2 S-bits select RR, RNR, REJ, or SREJ. Contains N(R) only.
- **U-Frame (Unnumbered):** Link management — SETUP (`SNRM`, `SABM`), DISCONNECT (`DISC`), ACKNOWLEDGE (`UA`), etc.

**Role of the P/F (Poll/Final) Bit:**
- In **commands** (sent by primary) it is called **P (Poll) = 1** → "I am giving you permission to send your response."
- In **responses** (sent by secondary) it is called **F (Final) = 1** → "This is the last (final) frame in response to your poll."
- For **S-frames** with P=1, the receiver **must** respond with the appropriate S-frame indicating its status.
- For **I-frames** in NRM, P=1 authorizes the secondary to send **all** its I-frames until it sets F=1 on the last one.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Many students mix up *Poll* (used in commands) and *Final* (used in responses). Both share bit 4 of the control field, but **the meaning is direction-dependent** — losing 1 mark for this confusion is extremely common in KTU valuation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Print and Keep)**

### Core Concepts
- HDLC = **bit-oriented**, **synchronous**, **ISO Layer-2** protocol (ISO 3309 / 4335).
- Three station types: **Primary**, **Secondary**, **Combined**.
- Two logical configurations: **Unbalanced** (P + Ss) and **Balanced** (C + C).
- Three data transfer modes: **NRM**, **ARM**, **ABM** (most common).
- **NRM** = unbalanced, **ABM** = balanced (used in LAP-B, PPP).

### Frame Structure Mnemonic — "**FACIFS**"
- **F**lag (8 bits) → **A**ddress (8/8n) → **C**ontrol (8/16) → **I**nformation (variable) → **F**CS (16/32) → **S**ame Flag

### Control Field Decoder
- Bit 7 = `0` → **I-frame**
- Bits 7,6 = `10` → **S-frame**
- Bits 7,6 = `11` → **U-frame**
- I-frames carry N(S) and N(R); S-frames carry only N(R); U-frames carry neither.

### Bit Stuffing Rule (Most Repeated KTU Question)
- **Insert `0` after every 5 consecutive `1`s** in the payload (Address + Control + Information).
- Receiver removes any `0` that follows a run of 5 ones.
- Required because the flag `01111110` must never appear inside a frame.

### P/F Bit (Poll/Final)
- **P=1** in command → solicit response.
- **F=1** in response → indicate the last frame in the response.

### FCS / CRC
- Standard HDLC: **CRC-16-CCITT** with polynomial `x^16 + x^12 + x^5 + 1`.
- Detects all single-bit and double-bit errors, all odd numbers of errors, and all burst errors up to 16 bits.
- Computed over **Address + Control + Information** only — **not** the flags.

### Common Derivatives
- **LAP-B** → X.25 packet networks.
- **LAP-D** → ISDN D-channel.
- **LAPF** → Frame Relay.
- **PPP** → Modern point-to-point links (Internet dial-up, DSL, HDLC over serial).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Tip:** If a question asks to "explain HDLC frame structure with bit stuffing," always present them in this order: (1) Frame structure diagram, (2) Why bit stuffing is needed, (3) Worked stuffing example, (4) De-stuffing at receiver. This exact ordering fetches **full marks** consistently across KTU valuation scripts.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Synchronous TDM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCERT602 - DATA COMMUNICATION AND NETWORKING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Synchronous TDM ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Synchronous Time Division Multiplexing (Synchronous TDM)** is a digital multiplexing technique in which multiple low-speed digital data streams (from independent sources) are combined into a single high-speed transmission stream by allocating **fixed, pre-assigned, periodic time slots** to each input source, regardless of whether the source actually has data to send at that moment. The multiplexer rotates through the input channels in a rigid round-robin fashion, sampling one unit (typically one bit or one character) from each source per cycle, and the resulting composite signal is transmitted as a continuous sequence of **frames**. In the KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, Synchronous TDM is classified as a **deterministic multiplexing scheme** because the time-slot assignment is fixed at the time of connection setup and does not vary dynamically with traffic load. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In Synchronous TDM, the term "synchronous" refers to the fact that the multiplexer scans the input lines and accepts a unit of data (bit/byte) at **fixed, equal time intervals**, *not* that the data sources themselves are synchronized in clock frequency. The slot allocation is **synchronous to the multiplexer’s internal clock**, not to the source clocks. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **circular conveyor belt in a bakery** with **5 fixed stalls** (slots), one belonging to each of 5 bakers (data sources). Every **10 seconds**, the belt completes one revolution. Regardless of whether Baker #3 has actually prepared a fresh loaf, his stall is visited and an item (or an empty tray if nothing was ready) is placed on the belt. The belt rotates synchronously — every baker gets their slot at perfectly predictable intervals. **The Analogy Mapping:** | Bakery Concept | Synchronous TDM Equivalent | |---|---| | Conveyor belt | Composite high-speed digital link | | 5 stalls per revolution | 5 time slots per TDM frame | | One revolution every 10 s | One frame duration (T_f) | | Empty tray | Idle/empty slot (no data) | | Conveyor motor | Multiplexer internal clock | This makes the central truth of Synchronous TDM obvious: **even an idle source wastes its slot**, leading to bandwidth inefficiency when traffic is bursty. ### 1.3 Core Metrics and Boundary Conditions - **Number of input sources (n):** Typically 2 to 64 in textbook problems. - **Source bit rate (R):** Each input channel's data rate in **bits per second (bps)**. - **Composite link rate (C):** The output rate of the multiplexed link in **bps**. - **Frame duration (T_f):** The time taken to transmit one complete frame = **1/R seconds** (for bit-interleaved TDM). - **Bit duration on link (T_b):** Time for one bit on the composite link = **1/(nR) seconds** (assuming no framing overhead). - **Slot:** A position within a frame reserved for a specific source. > [!NOTE] > **Standard KTU Default:** Forouzan’s textbook (the KTU reference) and Stallings both assume bit-interleaving unless explicitly stated otherwise. The composite frame contains exactly **n bits** (one from each source) when framing bits are ignored. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step The Synchronous TDM multiplexer operates in a strict, repeating cycle: 1. **Channel Assignment Phase:** At system design, each of the *n* input lines is assigned a fixed slot position (1 through *n*) in every frame. Source *i* always occupies slot *i*. 2. **Sampling Phase:** The multiplexer examines each input line at the start of its allocated time slot and reads **one unit of data** (typically 1 bit for digital synchronous TDM, or 1 character for older systems). 3. **Interleaving Phase:** The sampled units are placed sequentially into a buffer, forming a single **TDM frame** of *n* data units. 4. **Framing Phase:** One or more **framing/synchronization bits** (often a fixed pattern like `01111110`) are prepended to the frame so the demultiplexer at the receiver can identify slot boundaries. 5. **Transmission Phase:** The composite frame (data + framing bits) is clocked out onto the high-speed link at rate **C bps**. 6. **Demultiplexing (Reverse Path):** The receiving demultiplexer uses the framing bits to lock onto the frame, then routes each slot to its corresponding output line. ### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step - **Why fixed slots?** It drastically simplifies the demultiplexer's job — no addressing is needed inside the frame, since slot *i* always means source *i*. This was crucial in the 1960s–80s when digital hardware was expensive. - **Why framing bits?** They allow the receiver to know "where frame N ends and frame N+1 begins." Without them, a long run of identical bits from one source would make slot boundaries unidentifiable. - **Why one bit per slot (bit interleaving)?** It minimizes **jitter** (variation in delay) and is the natural choice for synchronous digital hierarchies like **PDH (Plesiochronous Digital Hierarchy)** and **SDH (Synchronous Digital Hierarchy)**. ### 2.3 The Concept of an "Empty Slot" If a source has no data when its slot arrives, the multiplexer must still transmit something. There are three conventional approaches: - **Approach A (Naïve):** Transmit a `0` bit. *Problem:* Indistinguishable from real data. - **Approach B (Better):** Transmit a predefined **idle pattern** (e.g., `01111111` for an idle line in RS-232/Telco systems). - **Approach C (Statistical TDM):** Skip the slot entirely and use it for another active source. *This is NOT synchronous TDM anymore.* > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Tip:** The third approach defines **Statistical TDM (Asynchronous TDM / Intelligent TDM)**, which is a *different* topic. Do not confuse the two. Synchronous TDM *never* reassigns slots dynamically. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet The following table contains every formula you will need for Synchronous TDM problems in KTU University Examinations. All quantities use **SI units** unless otherwise noted. | # | Quantity | Formula | Unit | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Composite link rate (no overhead) | $C = n \times R$ | bps | $n$ = sources, $R$ = per-source rate | | 2 | Composite link rate (with framing) | $C = n \times R + F$ | bps | $F$ = framing bits per second | | 3 | Frame duration | $T_f = \dfrac{1}{R}$ | seconds | One slot per source per frame | | 4 | Bit duration on composite link | $T_b = \dfrac{1}{C} = \dfrac{1}{nR}$ | seconds | Without framing overhead | | 5 | Number of data bits per frame | $N_{data} = n$ | bits | Bit-interleaved case | | 6 | Total bits per frame | $N_{frame} = n + f$ | bits | $f$ = framing bits per frame | | 7 | Frames per second | $F_{ps} = \dfrac{C}{N_{frame}} = R$ | frames/s | Equals source rate (key insight!) | | 8 | Multiplexer efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{nR}{nR + F} \times 100\%$ | % | Reduces as $F$ grows | | 9 | Time to transmit one frame | $T_f = \dfrac{N_{frame}}{C}$ | seconds | Alternative to formula 3 | | 10 | Synchronization bit overhead | $\%\text{OH} = \dfrac{f}{n+f} \times 100\%$ | % | Measures framing burden | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Identity:** The frame rate (frames/second) of a Synchronous TDM link is **always equal to the source bit rate $R$** — because exactly one bit per source per frame keeps the data rate per channel unchanged. This is one of the most heavily tested facts in KTU exams. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility Synchronous TDM is the historical and operational foundation of: - **T1 Carrier System (1.544 Mbps):** 24 voice channels × 64 kbps + 1 framing bit = 1.536 + 0.008 Mbps. - **E1 Carrier System (2.048 Mbps):** 30 voice channels × 64 kbps + 2 framing channels = 2.048 Mbps. - **PDH (Plesiochronous Digital Hierarchy):** E1, E2, E3, E4 levels. - **SDH/SONET:** Modern fiber-optic backbone multiplexing (though these are technically *synchronous* in a different, clock-synchronization sense). > [!WARNING] > **Do not confuse** the "synchronous" in **SDH (Synchronous Digital Hierarchy)** with the "synchronous" in **Synchronous TDM**. SDH refers to **network-wide clock synchronization** between nodes; Synchronous TDM refers to **fixed time-slot allocation** in a multiplexer. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Composite Link Bit Rate **Given:** *n* input sources, each generating data at rate $R$ bps. The multiplexer samples **one bit per source per frame** and outputs them sequentially. **Derivation:** Let one frame contain exactly $n$ bits (one from each source). The time available to transmit one frame must equal the time for one bit to arrive from each source (so no source is "starved" or "flooded"). $$ \begin{aligned} T_{frame} &= \text{time to receive 1 bit from Source 1} \\ T_{frame} &= \frac{1}{R} \text{ seconds} \end{aligned} $$ During $T_{frame}$, the multiplexer must push $n$ bits onto the output link. Therefore the output bit rate $C$ is: $$ \begin{aligned} C &= \frac{\text{bits per frame}}{T_{frame}} \\ C &= \frac{n}{1/R} \\ C &= nR \text{ bps} \end{aligned} $$ **Conclusion:** The output rate is exactly $n$ times the input rate per source. $\blacksquare$ ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Bit Duration on the Composite Link **Given:** Composite link rate $C = nR$ bps, with no framing overhead. The bit duration is the inverse of the bit rate: $$ \begin{aligned} T_b &= \frac{1}{C} \\ T_b &= \frac{1}{nR} \text{ seconds} \end{aligned} $$ **Intuition check:** A single source bit (duration $1/R$) is now "sliced" into one of $n$ time slots, each of width $1/(nR)$. The sum of $n$ such slots recovers the original $1/R$ frame duration. ✓ ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Effect of Framing Bits on Output Rate **Given:** *f* framing bits are added per frame. Frame size becomes $(n + f)$ bits. Frame rate remains $R$ frames/second (because each source still contributes one bit per frame, so frames are generated at the source's bit rate). The framing bits per second added to the link rate are: $$ \begin{aligned} F_{bps} &= f \times F_{ps} = f \times R \end{aligned} $$ Hence the new composite rate becomes: $$ \begin{aligned} C_{new} &= nR + fR \\ C_{new} &= R(n + f) \text{ bps} \end{aligned} $$ The efficiency $\eta$ of the multiplexer is the ratio of useful data rate to total transmitted rate: $$ \begin{aligned} \eta &= \frac{nR}{C_{new}} \\ \eta &= \frac{nR}{R(n+f)} \\ \eta &= \frac{n}{n+f} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Important Observation:** $\eta$ is **independent of $R$**. The framing overhead is purely a structural function of the number of sources and the framing-bit count. ### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example **Problem:** Four sources each transmit at **200 kbps** using Synchronous TDM. The system uses **2 framing bits per frame**. Compute: (a) The composite link bit rate. (b) The bit duration on the link. (c) The frame duration. (d) The multiplexer efficiency. **Given:** $n = 4$, $R = 200{,}000$ bps, $f = 2$ bits/frame. **(a) Composite link rate:** $$ \begin{aligned} C &= nR + fR \\ C &= (4 \times 200{,}000) + (2 \times 200{,}000) \\ C &= 800{,}000 + 400{,}000 \\ C &= 1{,}200{,}000 \text{ bps} = 1.2 \text{ Mbps} \end{aligned} $$ **(b) Bit duration on link:** $$ \begin{aligned} T_b &= \frac{1}{C} = \frac{1}{1.2 \times 10^{6}} \\ T_b &\approx 0.833 \text{ } \mu\text{s} \end{aligned} $$ **(c) Frame duration:** $$ \begin{aligned} T_f &= \frac{1}{R} = \frac{1}{200{,}000} \\ T_f &= 5 \text{ } \mu\text{s} \end{aligned} $$ **Verification:** In $T_f = 5\ \mu\text{s}$, we transmit $C \times T_f = 1.2 \times 10^{6} \times 5 \times 10^{-6} = 6$ bits. This matches $(n + f) = (4 + 2) = 6$ bits per frame. ✓ **(d) Efficiency:** $$ \begin{aligned} \eta &= \frac{n}{n+f} = \frac{4}{4+2} = \frac{4}{6} \\ \eta &\approx 66.67\% \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Problem Variants | Problem Type | Knowns to Extract | Primary Formula(s) | |---|---|---| | Find output bit rate | $n$, $R$, $f$ | $C = R(n + f)$ | | Find bit/frame duration | $R$, $n$ | $T_f = 1/R$, $T_b = 1/(nR)$ | | Find number of sources | $C$, $R$, $f$ | $n = C/R - f$ | | Find efficiency | $n$, $f$ | $\eta = n/(n+f)$ | | Find framing bits required | Target $\eta$, $n$ | $f = n(1-\eta)/\eta$ | | Verify frame integrity | $C$, $T_f$ | $N_{frame} = C \times T_f$ | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Synchronous TDM The following Mermaid diagram depicts the complete end-to-end architecture of a Synchronous TDM system — from the parallel input sources, through the multiplexer, across the high-speed link, and into the demultiplexer with its framing-bit detector. ```mermaid flowchart LR S1[Source 1
R bps] --> MUX S2[Source 2
R bps] --> MUX S3[Source 3
R bps] --> MUX Sn[Source N
R bps] --> MUX MUX[Synchronous TDM
Multiplexer
Slot Allocator] --> FR FR[Framing Bit
Inserter] --> HS HS[High Speed
Composite Link
C bps] --> FD FD[Framing Bit
Detector] --> DMX DMX[Synchronous TDM
Demultiplexer] --> D1[Destination 1] DMX --> D2[Destination 2] DMX --> D3[Destination 3] DMX --> Dn[Destination N] CLK[Central Clock
Reference] -.-> MUX CLK -.-> DMX CLK -.-> FR CLK -.-> FD ``` ### 4.2 Frame Structure Schematic The TDM frame is the fundamental repeating unit. The diagram below shows how *n* data slots plus *f* framing bits are concatenated. ```mermaid graph TB F[One TDM Frame] F --> FB[Framing Bits
f bits
Example 01111110] F --> S1S[Slot 1
Source 1 data] F --> S2S[Slot 2
Source 2 data] F --> S3S[Slot 3
Source 3 data] F --> SnS[Slot N
Source N data] S1S --> S2S S2S --> S3S S3S --> SnS SnS --> FB ``` ### 4.3 Timing Diagram of the Multiplexer Rotation The round-robin slot allocation can be visualized as a rotating arm that visits each source in a fixed sequence, sampling one bit at a time. ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant CLK as MUX Clock participant S1 as Source 1 participant S2 as Source 2 participant S3 as Source 3 participant Sn as Source N participant LINK as Composite Link Note over CLK: T = 0 CLK->>S1: Read 1 bit S1-->>LINK: Bit to Slot 1 CLK->>S2: Read 1 bit S2-->>LINK: Bit to Slot 2 CLK->>S3: Read 1 bit S3-->>LINK: Bit to Slot 3 CLK->>Sn: Read 1 bit Sn-->>LINK: Bit to Slot N Note over LINK: Frame Complete T_f Note over CLK: Next Frame Begins ``` ### 4.4 Conceptual Distinction: Synchronous vs Statistical TDM ```mermaid flowchart TD TDM[Time Division Multiplexing] TDM --> STDM[Synchronous TDM
Fixed Slots
Idle sources waste bandwidth] TDM --> ATDM[Statistical TDM / Asynchronous TDM
Dynamic Slot Allocation
Requires addressing in each slot] STDM --> P1[Pros: Simple hardware, no addressing] STDM --> M1[Cons: Wastes slots on idle sources] ATDM --> P2[Pros: Higher efficiency with bursty traffic] ATDM --> M2[Cons: Complex demux, address overhead] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** *Define Synchronous Time Division Multiplexing. Why is it called "synchronous"?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** Synchronous Time Division Multiplexing is a digital multiplexing technique in which multiple low-speed digital signals are combined into one high-speed signal by allocating a **fixed time slot** to each input source in a repeating sequence called a **frame**. It is called *synchronous* because the multiplexer **synchronously** (i.e., at fixed, equal time intervals governed by an internal clock) reads one unit of data from each input line, regardless of whether that source is active or idle. The "synchronous" qualifier refers to the **multiplexer's scanning rhythm**, not to any clock relationship between the input sources themselves. **(3 marks)** --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** *What is the role of framing bits in a Synchronous TDM system? What happens if they are omitted?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** Framing bits are a **predefined bit pattern** (e.g., `01111110`) inserted at the beginning (or end) of every TDM frame. Their role is to enable the **demultiplexer at the receiver to identify the start of each frame**, so that it can correctly route each slot to its corresponding destination. If framing bits are omitted, the demultiplexer cannot reliably determine slot boundaries — especially during long runs of identical bits from one source — leading to **loss of synchronization** and corrupted data delivery. **(3 marks)** --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **KTU ESE Pattern Note:** Part B questions in Module 3 carry **14 marks** and typically offer an **internal choice** between two sub-questions (e.g., Q7(a) OR Q7(b)). Below we present two fully independent 14-mark alternatives, each with two 7-mark sub-parts. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]** **(a)** Explain the working principle of Synchronous TDM with a neat block diagram. Show how *n* input data streams of rate $R$ bps each are combined into a single composite link of rate $C = nR$ bps. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Four voice channels, each digitized at **64 kbps**, are multiplexed using Synchronous TDM with **1 framing bit per frame**. (i) Compute the composite link bit rate. (ii) Compute the frame duration and the bit duration on the composite link. (iii) What is the multiplexer efficiency? **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Working Principle (7 marks):** - The multiplexer has *n* input ports and 1 output port. **(1 mark)** - It contains a **rotating switch** (or counter-based slot allocator) synchronized to an internal clock of period $T_b = 1/(nR)$. **(1 mark)** - In every **frame of duration $T_f = 1/R$**, the switch visits each input line **exactly once** and reads **one bit** (or one byte) from it. **(1 mark)** - The bits are placed sequentially into a **shift register buffer** to form a frame of *n* data bits. **(1 mark)** - A **framing-bit generator** prepends *f* synchronization bits to the frame. **(1 mark)** - The composite frame is transmitted at rate $C = nR$ (without overhead) or $C = R(n+f)$ (with overhead). **(1 mark)** - At the receiver, the **framing-bit detector** locks onto the sync pattern, and the demultiplexer uses the slot position to route bits to the correct output line. **(1 mark)** *Block diagram:* (Refer to Section 4.1 above for the complete Mermaid block diagram.) **(included in the 7 marks)** **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **Given:** $n = 4$ channels, $R = 64{,}000$ bps, $f = 1$ framing bit/frame. **(i) Composite link bit rate: (2 marks)** $$ \begin{aligned} C &= R(n + f) \\ C &= 64{,}000 \times (4 + 1) \\ C &= 64{,}000 \times 5 \\ C &= 320{,}000 \text{ bps} = 320 \text{ kbps} \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) Frame duration and bit duration: (3 marks)** $$ \begin{aligned} T_f &= \frac{1}{R} = \frac{1}{64{,}000} \\ T_f &= 15.625 \text{ } \mu\text{s} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} T_b &= \frac{1}{C} = \frac{1}{320{,}000} \\ T_b &= 3.125 \text{ } \mu\text{s} \end{aligned} $$ **Verification:** $C \times T_f = 320{,}000 \times 15.625 \times 10^{-6} = 5$ bits = $(n + f) = (4 + 1) = 5$ bits per frame. ✓ **(iii) Multiplexer efficiency: (2 marks)** $$ \begin{aligned} \eta &= \frac{n}{n+f} = \frac{4}{4+1} = \frac{4}{5} \\ \eta &= 0.80 = 80\% \end{aligned} $$ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Alternative]** **(a)** Compare Synchronous TDM and Statistical TDM in terms of slot allocation, efficiency, hardware complexity, and typical use cases. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A Synchronous TDM system combines **6 sources**, each operating at **150 kbps**, using **2 framing bits per frame**. (i) Determine the output bit rate of the composite link. (ii) If the link is required to operate at a **minimum efficiency of 90%**, what is the maximum number of sources that can be multiplexed with 2 framing bits per frame? **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Comparison Table (7 marks):** | Parameter | Synchronous TDM | Statistical TDM | |---|---|---| | Slot allocation | Fixed, pre-assigned | Dynamic, on demand | | Idle source behavior | Wastes its slot | Slot goes to active source | | Addressing required | No (position implies source) | Yes (address in each slot) | | Efficiency with bursty traffic | Low (often 20–40%) | High (often 80–95%) | | Hardware complexity | Simple | Complex (buffer management) | | Frame structure | Fixed length | Variable length | | Typical use | T1/E1 leased lines, PDH | LAN switches, packet mux | | Synchronization | Easy (fixed slot count) | Harder (variable frames) | *Note:* Each row of the table carries roughly **0.5–1 mark** in valuation. Add a concluding sentence (1 mark) summarizing that Statistical TDM is preferred for **bursty** traffic, while Synchronous TDM is preferred for **constant-rate** traffic like digitized voice. **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **Given (i):** $n = 6$, $R = 150{,}000$ bps, $f = 2$ bits/frame. **(i) Output bit rate: (3 marks)** $$ \begin{aligned} C &= R(n + f) \\ C &= 150{,}000 \times (6 + 2) \\ C &= 150{,}000 \times 8 \\ C &= 1{,}200{,}000 \text{ bps} = 1.2 \text{ Mbps} \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) Maximum sources for $\eta \geq 90\%$ (4 marks):** For efficiency: $$ \begin{aligned} \eta &= \frac{n}{n+f} \geq 0.90 \\ \frac{n}{n+2} &\geq 0.90 \\ n &\geq 0.90(n + 2) \\ n &\geq 0.90n + 1.8 \\ 0.10n &\geq 1.8 \\ n &\geq 18 \end{aligned} $$ Therefore, the **minimum number of sources required** to achieve 90% efficiency is $n = 18$. Any value of $n \geq 18$ satisfies the constraint. **Note:** The question asks for the *maximum* number, but since the inequality is satisfied for *all* $n \geq 18$ and not bounded above by any other constraint, the answer is **$n \geq 18$ sources**, i.e., at least 18 sources are needed. If the problem meant a fixed composite link capacity, additional steps would be needed — but with no such ceiling given, the answer is $n \geq 18$. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in Synchronous TDM Problems:** > 1. **Forgetting framing bits:** Many students write $C = nR$ even when $f$ is given. Always read the problem for the word "framing" or "synchronization bits." **(−1 to −2 marks)** > 2. **Unit confusion:** Mixing kbps and Mbps in the final answer. Always convert to a **single unit** before computing. **(−1 mark)** > 3. **Confusing $T_f$ with $T_b$:** Frame duration is $1/R$ (one cycle of the source), bit duration on the composite link is $1/C$. They differ by a factor of *n*. **(−1 mark)** > 4. **Inefficiency claim:** Students often state that Synchronous TDM is "inefficient" without quantifying it. Use the formula $\eta = n/(n+f)$. **(−0.5 to −1 mark)** > 5. **Mixing up with Statistical TDM:** Do not write that synchronous TDM reassigns idle slots — that is **Statistical TDM**, a different topic. **(−2 marks)** > 6. **Skipping the verification step:** Always plug your computed $T_f$ and $C$ back to check that $C \times T_f$ equals the total bits per frame. This catches arithmetic errors that examiners reward you for catching. **(−1 mark if missed)** > 7. **Block diagram missing labels:** In 7-mark theory questions, an unlabeled block diagram fetches 0 marks. Label *every* block (Source, MUX, Link, DMUX, Destination) and the link rate. **(−2 marks)** ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Synchronous TDM = Fixed-slot, round-robin multiplexing.** Every source gets a slot in every frame, even if it has no data. - **The "synchronous" refers to the multiplexer clock**, not to the input sources' clocks. - **Composite link rate (no overhead):** $C = nR$. - **Composite link rate (with *f* framing bits/frame):** $C = R(n + f)$. - **Frame duration:** $T_f = 1/R$ — independent of *n*. - **Bit duration on composite link:** $T_b = 1/C = 1/(nR)$. - **Frame rate = Source bit rate:** This is the cornerstone identity. - **Efficiency:** $\eta = n/(n+f)$ — also independent of $R$. - **Empty slot** in Synchronous TDM is filled with an idle pattern (or `0`); it is **not skipped** (that would be Statistical TDM). - **Framing bits** are mandatory for receiver synchronization; commonly 1 bit (T1) or 2 bits per frame. - **Bit interleaving** is the default in modern digital hierarchies; character interleaving is historical. - **Real-world deployments:** T1 (24 channels), E1 (30 channels), PDH, SDH/SONET. - **Comparison anchor:** Synchronous TDM ↔ Statistical TDM; FDM is a parallel-domain analog. - **Always verify** $C \times T_f = (n + f)$ — this single check eliminates 90% of arithmetic errors. - **For 14-mark theory questions:** Always include a **labeled block diagram**, a **formula**, and a **short real-world example** (T1 or E1) to fetch full marks.

topics.title: Statistical TDM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCERT602 - DATA COMMUNICATION AND NETWORKING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Statistical TDM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Statistical Time Division Multiplexing (Statistical TDM)**, also widely known as **Asynchronous TDM** or **Intelligent TDM**, is a dynamic channel-access technique in which the available bandwidth of a high-speed digital transmission link is allocated to the attached low-speed input sources **on demand**, rather than by pre-assigning a fixed, dedicated time slot to each source as is done in Synchronous (Conventional) TDM.

In Statistical TDM, a central device called a **Statistical Multiplexer** (often called a **Stat-Mux** or **Concentrator**) scans the attached input lines in a round-robin fashion, accepts data only from those sources that are actively transmitting (i.e., sources that have data ready in their buffer), and dynamically packs this variable amount of data into a single outgoing frame. The frame therefore contains data from a varying number of sources, identified by an addressing or slot-mapping field within the frame.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Statistical TDM is the conceptual foundation of modern **packet switching**, **ATM (Asynchronous Transfer Mode)**, and statistical bandwidth allocation in routers and multiplexers. The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes the efficiency comparison between Synchronous TDM and Statistical TDM.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **shared taxi stand** outside a busy airport.

- **Synchronous TDM** is like a **scheduled public bus** that leaves every 10 minutes regardless of how many passengers are waiting. There are 20 fixed seats; if only 3 passengers arrive, 17 seats go empty — but the bus still leaves "on schedule," wasting 85% of its capacity.
- **Statistical TDM** is like a **shared Uber Pool**. The driver only leaves when enough ride requests are received, dynamically picking up passengers from wherever they are, packing the car efficiently based on *actual* demand. If no one is requesting a ride, the driver simply waits. The taxi uses its full capacity almost all the time.

This **on-demand allocation** is the soul of Statistical TDM. It assumes — correctly — that data sources are **bursty** (they transmit in short bursts with idle gaps in between), and therefore reserving a fixed slot for every source is wasteful.

## 1.3 Key Distinguishing Characteristics

| Feature | Synchronous TDM | Statistical TDM |
|---|---|---|
| Slot Assignment | Fixed & dedicated | Dynamic & on-demand |
| Frame Length | Fixed | Variable |
| Addressing | By position in frame | By explicit address field |
| Idle Sources | Wastes bandwidth | Uses their share for active sources |
| Hardware Complexity | Lower | Higher (buffering needed) |
| Typical Use | Legacy digital telephony (T1/E1) | Packet networks, ATM, modern routers |

> [!NOTE]
> **Terminology Clarification:** The word "Asynchronous" in **Asynchronous TDM** does **NOT** mean the bits are transmitted without a clock. It means the **time slots are not permanently bound to specific sources** — slots are assigned asynchronously to whatever source has data ready.

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Frame overhead** includes address bits per source, framing bits, and error control bits.
- **Efficiency** in Statistical TDM typically ranges from **1× to 4×** that of Synchronous TDM, often quoted as a gain factor of **2× to 4×** for typical voice/data traffic with a 4:1 concentration ratio.
- **Standard Multiplexer Capacities:** Typical Stat-Muxes support **4, 8, 12, 16, 24, or 32** input channels.
- **Buffer/Queue depth** is commonly measured in **bits, bytes, or packets**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Working Principle — Step-by-Step

The Statistical Multiplexer operates as a **buffered, intelligent switching node**. Its working can be broken down into a precise sequence:

1. **Line Scanning (Polling):** The multiplexer cyclically polls each of the **N** input data sources at a very high internal rate. Modern implementations use a high-speed memory bus; older implementations used parallel I/O ports.
2. **Buffer Inspection:** For each source polled, the multiplexer checks the associated **input buffer (FIFO memory)**. If the buffer is non-empty, the multiplexer reads a chunk of data (e.g., 1 byte or 1 bit) into a transmit register.
3. **Dynamic Frame Assembly:** The multiplexer constructs an **output frame** whose structure is *not* rigidly fixed. The frame contains:
   - A **data field** carrying the actual payload from the active source.
   - An **address/control field** identifying which of the N sources this payload belongs to.
   - Possibly a **length field** (especially in variable-byte statistical TDM).
4. **Idle Source Skipping:** If a source's buffer is empty at poll time, the multiplexer **does not** waste an output slot on it. It immediately moves to the next source. This is the core source of Statistical TDM's efficiency.
5. **High-Speed Transmission:** The assembled variable-content frame is transmitted on the high-speed link at the multiplexer's output line rate.
6. **Demultiplexing at Receiver:** The receiving end reads the address field of each incoming chunk and routes the payload to the correct destination port.

## 2.2 The Core "Why" — Why Statistical TDM is More Efficient

The fundamental premise of Statistical TDM rests on the **burstiness of real data sources**.

- A voice source, even when active, is silent roughly **50–60%** of the time (the speaker pauses, listens, etc.).
- A data terminal (keyboard, sensor) is idle **>90%** of the time.
- Therefore, in any given instant, only a **fraction** of the N sources are actually transmitting.

Synchronous TDM allocates a slot to **every** source every cycle — wasting bandwidth on idle sources. Statistical TDM reclaims that wasted bandwidth and **dynamically redistributes** it to active sources, achieving higher throughput on the same physical link.

## 2.3 The Core "How" — Frame Structure Variants

There are two principal frame assembly strategies:

- **Statistical TDM with Fixed-Size Slots (Bit- or Byte-Interleaved Stat-Mux):** Each output slot is exactly the same size (e.g., 1 byte), but the slot may carry data from *any* of the N sources, identified by an address prefix. Idle sources simply do not generate a slot.
- **Statistical TDM with Variable-Length Packets:** Each output entity is a *packet* of variable length, with explicit source/destination addresses. This is functionally identical to **packet switching** and forms the basis of IP networks.

## 2.4 ⚡ KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Description / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Multiplexer Output Line Rate | $R_{out} = N \cdot R_{in} \cdot \rho \cdot \left(1 + \frac{H}{D}\right)^{-1}$ | Where $\rho$ is activity factor, $H$ = header bits, $D$ = data bits per slot |
| 2 | Number of Output Slots per Frame | $k = N \cdot \rho$ (average) | $N$ = input lines, $\rho$ = fraction of active lines per scan |
| 3 | Address Bits Required | $a = \lceil \log_2 N \rceil$ | $N$ = number of input sources; unit: bits |
| 4 | Frame Overhead Fraction | $O = \dfrac{a}{a + b}$ | $b$ = data bits per slot, $a$ = address bits |
| 5 | Output Link Utilization (Efficiency) | $\eta = \dfrac{N \cdot \rho \cdot b}{N \cdot \rho \cdot b + N \cdot \rho \cdot a + F}$ | $F$ = framing bits per output frame |
| 6 | Maximum Theoretical Gain over Sync TDM | $G_{max} = \dfrac{1}{\rho}$ | Valid only when $\rho < 1$ (almost always) |
| 7 | Buffer Size (Worst Case) | $B_{min} = R_{in} \cdot (T_{poll} - T_{tx})$ | To prevent overflow during one polling cycle |
| 8 | Effective Throughput per Source | $R_{eff} = \dfrac{R_{out}}{N}$ | Average data rate available to each input source |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMULA NOTATION RULE:** In the KTU exam, always use $\lceil \log_2 N \rceil$ (ceiling of log base 2) for address bits. For example, with $N=8$ sources, $a = \log_2 8 = 3$ bits exactly. With $N=5$ sources, $a = \lceil \log_2 5 \rceil = 3$ bits.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Statistical TDM is the **conceptual ancestor of virtually all modern data networks**:

- **Packet Switching Networks (Internet):** IP routers operate on exactly this principle — they accept packets from any source, queue them, and forward them dynamically based on destination.
- **ATM (Asynchronous Transfer Mode):** ATM cells are 53-byte fixed-size packets; ATM is essentially a *fixed-cell-size* variant of Statistical TDM.
- **Statistical Multiplexers in Telecom:** Used to combine multiple voice circuits onto a single T1/E1 trunk, achieving 2×–4× the capacity of Synchronous TDM for the same physical line.
- **Statistical Time Division Multiple Access (STDMA)** in satellite and radio networks.
- **Modern Server I/O & Storage:** NVMe over Fabrics, Fibre Channel switches all use statistical multiplexing concepts.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Required Output Line Rate of a Statistical Multiplexer

### **Problem Setup**
Consider a Statistical Multiplexer with:
- $N$ = number of input sources (e.g., 10 terminals)
- $R_{in}$ = data rate of each input source (e.g., **9600 bps**)
- $\rho$ = **activity factor** (probability a given source is transmitting), e.g., 0.4
- Output slot size = $b$ data bits (e.g., 8 bits = 1 byte)
- Address field = $a$ bits per output slot

We want to determine the **minimum output line rate $R_{out}$** that the multiplexer must sustain so that the high-speed link does not become a bottleneck.

### **Step-by-Step Derivation**

**Step 1: Determine the average number of active input sources at any moment.**

Since each of the $N$ sources is independently active with probability $\rho$, the *expected* (mean) number of active sources is:

$$
\bar{k} = N \cdot \rho
$$

This represents the **average number of data slots** that must be transmitted on the output line per scanning cycle. For $N=10$ and $\rho=0.4$:

$$
\bar{k} = 10 \times 0.4 = 4 \text{ active sources (on average)}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the number of bits that must be transmitted per slot.**

Every output slot must carry $b$ data bits **plus** $a$ address bits to identify the source:

$$
\text{Bits per slot} = a + b
$$

For $N=10$, $a = \lceil \log_2 10 \rceil = 4$ bits, and $b = 8$ bits:

$$
\text{Bits per slot} = 4 + 8 = 12 \text{ bits/slot}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the total output bits per second.**

Each of the $\bar{k}$ active sources must have its data forwarded. Each source generates $R_{in}$ bits per second. So the total useful data arriving at the multiplexer per second is:

$$
D_{in} = N \cdot \rho \cdot R_{in} = \bar{k} \cdot R_{in}
$$

For our example: $D_{in} = 10 \times 0.4 \times 9600 = 38400$ bps of useful data.

**Step 4: Account for slot overhead.**

Because each $b$-bit chunk of payload is wrapped in an $a$-bit address, the **effective** number of bits on the wire is:

$$
R_{out} = D_{in} \times \frac{a + b}{b}
$$

Plugging in:

$$
R_{out} = N \cdot \rho \cdot R_{in} \cdot \left(1 + \frac{a}{b}\right)
$$

**Step 5: Numerical Evaluation.**

For $N=10$, $\rho=0.4$, $R_{in}=9600$ bps, $a=4$, $b=8$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{out} &= 10 \times 0.4 \times 9600 \times \left(1 + \frac{4}{8}\right) \\
&= 38400 \times 1.5 \\
&= 57600 \text{ bps}
\end{aligned}
$$

So the Statistical Multiplexer only needs an output line of **57,600 bps** to carry 10 input lines each running at 9,600 bps with 40% activity — a remarkable saving compared to the **96,000 bps** required by Synchronous TDM (10 × 9600).

**Step 6: Compute the equivalent Synchronous TDM rate for comparison.**

$$
R_{sync} = N \cdot R_{in} = 10 \times 9600 = 96000 \text{ bps}
$$

**Step 7: Compute the bandwidth savings ratio.**

$$
\text{Saving} = 1 - \frac{R_{out}}{R_{sync}} = 1 - \frac{57600}{96000} = 1 - 0.6 = 0.4 = 40\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The Statistical Multiplexer saves **40% bandwidth** for this configuration. The savings grow as $\rho$ decreases — the burstier the sources, the more efficient Statistical TDM becomes.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Address Field Width

The address field must uniquely identify each of the $N$ input sources. With $a$ bits, we can address $2^a$ distinct sources. The minimum $a$ that satisfies $2^a \geq N$ is:

$$
a = \lceil \log_2 N \rceil
$$

| $N$ | $\log_2 N$ | $a = \lceil \log_2 N \rceil$ | Spare codes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 2 | 1.000 | 1 | 2 |
| 4 | 2.000 | 2 | 0 |
| 5 | 2.322 | 3 | 3 |
| 8 | 3.000 | 3 | 0 |
| 16 | 4.000 | 4 | 0 |
| 24 | 4.585 | 5 | 8 |
| 32 | 5.000 | 5 | 0 |

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: TDM Comparison Summary Table

| Performance Metric | Synchronous TDM | Statistical TDM | Winner |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bandwidth Utilization | Low (idle slots waste BW) | High (idle slots reclaimed) | Stat-TDM |
| Hardware Complexity | Simple (no buffering) | Complex (needs RAM buffers) | Sync TDM |
| Latency (Jitter) | Fixed & predictable | Variable (jitter-prone) | Sync TDM |
| Robustness to Burstiness | Poor | Excellent | Stat-TDM |
| Cost of Multiplexer | Low | Higher | Sync TDM |
| Suitability for Real-Time Voice | Excellent | Risky (buffer overflow) | Sync TDM |
| Suitability for Data | Poor (wasteful) | Excellent | Stat-TDM |

---

## 3.4 Advanced Analysis: Buffer Overflow and the "Trap" of Statistical TDM

A frequently asked exam question: *"If Statistical TDM is more efficient, why isn't it used everywhere?"*

The answer lies in **buffer overflow under high aggregate load**. If the aggregate input traffic $N \cdot \rho \cdot R_{in}$ approaches the output line capacity $R_{out}$, buffers begin to fill. When buffers overflow, data is **lost** (dropped), which is unacceptable for many applications. Thus:

- The **maximum allowable activity factor** is:

$$
\rho_{max} = \frac{R_{out}}{N \cdot R_{in} \cdot (1 + a/b)}
$$

- If $\rho$ approaches this limit, queuing delay grows without bound (Little's Law applies).
- This is why **Statistical TDM requires careful traffic engineering** and is unsuitable for hard-real-time systems like circuit-switched voice (where Synchronous TDM remains preferred).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Statistical Multiplexer

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the internal architecture of an $N$-to-1 Statistical Multiplexer, showing the buffering, scanning, and frame-assembly logic.

```mermaid
graph TD
    srcA[Source 1] --> bufA[Buffer 1 FIFO]
    srcB[Source 2] --> bufB[Buffer 2 FIFO]
    srcC[Source 3] --> bufC[Buffer 3 FIFO]
    srcD[Source N] --> bufD[Buffer N FIFO]
    
    bufA --> scanner[Round Robin Scanner Controller]
    bufB --> scanner
    bufC --> scanner
    bufD --> scanner
    
    scanner --> |Address bits| asm[Frame Assembler]
    scanner --> |Data bits| asm
    
    asm --> |Add header and trailer| framebuf[Output Frame Buffer]
    framebuf --> |High speed serial| tx[Transmitter onto High Speed Link]
    
    ctrl[Clock and Control Unit] --> scanner
    ctrl --> asm
    ctrl --> framebuf
    
    tx --> link[High Speed Output Link to Demultiplexer]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The Scanner Controller performs round-robin polling. It only reads from a buffer if the buffer is non-empty. The Frame Assembler concatenates the address field and the data field into a single output slot. Multiple such slots are packed into a frame and pushed onto the high-speed link.

---

## 4.2 Frame Structure Comparison: Synchronous vs. Statistical TDM

The following Mermaid diagram visualizes the fundamental difference in how frames are laid out in the two TDM schemes for the same $N=4$ sources, with Source 2 idle.

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph SYNCHRONOUS_TDM
        s1[Slot 1 Src A] --> s2[Slot 2 Src B IDLE]
        s2 --> s3[Slot 3 Src C]
        s3 --> s4[Slot 4 Src D]
        s4 --> s1b[Slot 1 Src A]
    end
    
    subgraph STATISTICAL_TDM
        a1[Addr A Data 1] --> a2[Addr C Data 3]
        a2 --> a3[Addr D Data 4]
        a3 --> a4[Addr A Data 1 cont]
        a4 --> a1b[Addr C Data 3 cont]
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Visual Insight:** In the Synchronous TDM frame, the slot for **Source B is reserved and transmitted empty** — pure waste. In the Statistical TDM frame, **Source B is completely absent** — its share of the bandwidth has been reassigned to the active Sources A, C, and D.

---

## 4.3 Statistical TDM Process Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    start[Start of Polling Cycle] --> poll1{Source 1 Buffer Empty?}
    poll1 --> |Yes| poll2{Source 2 Buffer Empty?}
    poll1 --> |No| read1[Read 1 slot from Source 1]
    read1 --> assemble1[Add Source 1 address and append to output frame]
    assemble1 --> poll2
    poll2 --> |Yes| poll3{Source 3 Buffer Empty?}
    poll2 --> |No| read2[Read 1 slot from Source 2]
    read2 --> assemble2[Add Source 2 address and append to output frame]
    assemble2 --> poll3
    poll3 --> |No| read3[Read 1 slot from Source 3]
    read3 --> assemble3[Add Source 3 address and append to output frame]
    poll3 --> |Yes| txframe[Transmit assembled frame on high speed link]
    assemble3 --> txframe
    txframe --> reset[Reset frame buffer and restart cycle]
    reset --> start
```

This flowchart explicitly demonstrates the **on-demand** nature: idle sources are skipped entirely, and only active sources contribute to the output frame.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define Statistical TDM. How does it differ from Synchronous TDM in terms of slot allocation?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Statistical Time Division Multiplexing (Statistical TDM) is a multiplexing technique in which the available bandwidth of a high-speed transmission link is **allocated dynamically** to the attached input sources **on demand**, rather than pre-assigning a fixed time slot to each source.

The key difference in slot allocation is:

- In **Synchronous TDM**, each source is assigned a **fixed and dedicated** time slot in every frame, regardless of whether the source has data to transmit. Idle slots are transmitted empty, wasting bandwidth.
- In **Statistical TDM**, slots are **dynamically assigned** only to sources that are actively transmitting. A source that is idle is simply skipped, and its slot is implicitly reassigned to an active source. This requires an **address field** in each slot to identify the source.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake (–1 mark deduction):** Many students write "Statistical TDM does not use time slots" — this is **incorrect**. Statistical TDM still uses time slots; the slots are simply **not permanently bound** to specific sources. Do not confuse this with no slotting at all.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is meant by the 'activity factor' in the context of Statistical TDM? Why is a high activity factor a problem for Statistical TDM?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **activity factor** $\rho$ of a data source is defined as the **fraction of time** (or equivalently, the probability) that the source is actively transmitting data. Formally:

$$
\rho = \frac{\text{Time source is transmitting}}{\text{Total observation time}}, \quad 0 \leq \rho \leq 1
$$

A high activity factor is problematic because:

- The **efficiency gain** of Statistical TDM over Synchronous TDM is approximately $\dfrac{1}{\rho}$. As $\rho \to 1$, this gain approaches unity — meaning Statistical TDM offers **no advantage** and actually performs **worse** due to address-bit overhead.
- More critically, if $\rho$ is high, the aggregate input traffic approaches the output line capacity, causing **buffer overflow, data loss, and unbounded queuing delay** in the multiplexer.
- Stat-TDM is therefore most beneficial when sources are **bursty** (low $\rho$).

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of Statistical TDM with a neat block diagram. Compare its frame structure with that of Synchronous TDM. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Statistical Multiplexer concentrates **12 input sources**, each of data rate **9.6 kbps**, with an **activity factor of 0.5**. The multiplexer uses **1 byte (8 bits)** of data per output slot. Compute:
- (i) The number of address bits required per slot.
- (ii) The minimum output line rate of the multiplexer.
- (iii) The percentage bandwidth saving compared to Synchronous TDM. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question A**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Definition (1 mark):** Statistical TDM is a dynamic multiplexing technique in which the multiplexer scans the input lines and accepts data only from those sources that are actively transmitting, packing the data into a single output frame along with an address field identifying each source.

**Block Diagram (3 marks):** The block diagram should show:
- N input sources → N FIFO buffers
- A Round Robin Scanner
- A Frame Assembler
- A High-Speed Output Link

**Frame Structure Comparison (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Synchronous TDM | Statistical TDM |
|---|---|---|
| Slot order | Fixed: A, B, C, D, A, B, ... | Variable: only active sources |
| Idle slot | Transmitted empty | Skipped entirely |
| Addressing | Implicit (by position) | Explicit (address field) |
| Frame length | Fixed | Variable |
| Bandwidth efficiency | Low | High (when $\rho$ small) |

**Explanation:** In Synchronous TDM, frame contains slots S1, S2, S3, S4 in fixed positions; if Source 2 is idle, an empty slot is still transmitted. In Statistical TDM, frame contains only entries for active sources, each prefixed by an address tag (e.g., `01|data`, `11|data`). The total bits transmitted is lower, achieving higher line efficiency.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Given:**
- $N = 12$ input sources
- $R_{in} = 9.6$ kbps per source
- $\rho = 0.5$
- $b = 8$ bits per output slot

**(i) Address bits per slot — 2 marks**

$$
a = \lceil \log_2 N \rceil = \lceil \log_2 12 \rceil = \lceil 3.585 \rceil = 4 \text{ bits}
$$

**(ii) Minimum output line rate — 3 marks**

Using the formula:

$$
R_{out} = N \cdot \rho \cdot R_{in} \cdot \left(1 + \frac{a}{b}\right)
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{out} &= 12 \times 0.5 \times 9600 \times \left(1 + \frac{4}{8}\right) \\
&= 6 \times 9600 \times 1.5 \\
&= 57600 \times 1.5 \\
&= 86400 \text{ bps} = 86.4 \text{ kbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Bandwidth saving compared to Synchronous TDM — 2 marks**

Synchronous TDM line rate:

$$
R_{sync} = N \cdot R_{in} = 12 \times 9600 = 115200 \text{ bps} = 115.2 \text{ kbps}
$$

Percentage saving:

$$
\text{Saving} = \left(1 - \frac{R_{out}}{R_{sync}}\right) \times 100\% = \left(1 - \frac{86400}{115200}\right) \times 100\%
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
&= \left(1 - 0.75\right) \times 100\% \\
&= 0.25 \times 100\% \\
&= 25\%
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Alternative]**

**(a)** With the help of a functional diagram, explain the operation of a Statistical Multiplexer. Discuss the role of buffers and the scanner in achieving on-demand bandwidth allocation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Statistical Multiplexer has **8 input channels** of **19.2 kbps** each, with **activity factor $\rho = 0.25$**. Each output slot carries **1 byte** of data. Determine:
- (i) The address bits per slot.
- (ii) The required output line rate of the multiplexer.
- (iii) The maximum number of such input channels that could be supported on a **64 kbps** output link, assuming the same activity factor and slot size. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question B**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Functional Diagram (3 marks):** The diagram should show input sources connected via individual **FIFO buffers** to a central scanner. The scanner is driven by a control unit and feeds data to a frame assembler. The frame assembler outputs a high-speed serial bit stream.

**Operation (2 marks):** The Statistical Multiplexer performs **round-robin polling**. At each poll instant, it inspects the buffer of the next source in sequence. If the buffer has data, the multiplexer reads a fixed-size chunk (e.g., 1 byte) into the output frame, prefixed by an address field. If the buffer is empty, the multiplexer immediately moves to the next source — no slot is wasted.

**Role of Buffers (1 mark):** Buffers (FIFO memory) absorb the bursty input traffic. They allow sources to transmit at their own peak rate, while the multiplexer drains the aggregated buffers at a controlled, constant output rate. This **rate decoupling** is the key to matching bursty inputs to a smooth high-speed trunk.

**Role of Scanner (1 mark):** The scanner enforces **fairness** by giving every active source a chance to be served. It implements the multiplexing decision logic and ensures no source is starved indefinitely (subject to the multiplexer not being saturated).

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Given:**
- $N = 8$, $R_{in} = 19.2$ kbps, $\rho = 0.25$, $b = 8$ bits, output link = 64 kbps.

**(i) Address bits — 2 marks**

$$
a = \lceil \log_2 8 \rceil = 3 \text{ bits}
$$

**(ii) Output line rate — 3 marks**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{out} &= N \cdot \rho \cdot R_{in} \cdot \left(1 + \frac{a}{b}\right) \\
&= 8 \times 0.25 \times 19200 \times \left(1 + \frac{3}{8}\right) \\
&= 2 \times 19200 \times 1.375 \\
&= 38400 \times 1.375 \\
&= 52800 \text{ bps} = 52.8 \text{ kbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Maximum number of channels on 64 kbps link — 2 marks**

We need to find the maximum $N'$ such that:

$$
N' \cdot \rho \cdot R_{in} \cdot \left(1 + \frac{a'}{b}\right) \leq R_{out\_link}
$$

Since $a' = \lceil \log_2 N' \rceil$, we iterate:

- Try $N' = 12$: $a' = 4$, $R_{req} = 12 \times 0.25 \times 19200 \times (1 + 4/8) = 57600 \times 1.5 = 86400$ bps. **Exceeds** 64 kbps.
- Try $N' = 8$: $R_{req} = 52800$ bps ✓
- Try $N' = 10$: $a' = 4$, $R_{req} = 10 \times 0.25 \times 19200 \times 1.5 = 72000$ bps — **Exceeds** 64 kbps.
- Try $N' = 9$: $a' = 4$, $R_{req} = 9 \times 0.25 \times 19200 \times 1.5 = 64800$ bps — **Exceeds** 64 kbps (just barely).

So **maximum $N' = 8$** input channels can be supported on a 64 kbps link under these conditions.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting address bits in the output rate formula.** A very common error is computing $R_{out} = N \cdot \rho \cdot R_{in}$ without the $(1 + a/b)$ factor. This loses **2 marks** in the KTU exam.
> 2. **Using $\log_2 N$ instead of $\lceil \log_2 N \rceil$.** Always round UP. For $N=10$, the answer is **4 bits**, not 3.32 bits.
> 3. **Confusing activity factor with bandwidth ratio.** $\rho$ is dimensionless (a probability), not in bps.
> 4. **Not drawing the address field in the frame diagram.** The KTU examiner specifically allocates marks for showing the address field in the output slot — its absence costs at least **1 mark**.
> 5. **Comparing with Synchronous TDM incorrectly.** When asked for "percentage saving," always compute against $N \cdot R_{in}$, not against the raw input.

---

## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Statistical TDM = Dynamic, on-demand, address-based slot allocation.** It is the conceptual parent of packet switching and ATM.
- ✅ **Synchronous TDM wastes bandwidth on idle sources; Statistical TDM does not.** This is the central efficiency argument.
- ✅ **Each output slot in Stat-TDM contains a data field + an address field.** Address bits per slot = $\lceil \log_2 N \rceil$.
- ✅ **Key formula to memorize:** $R_{out} = N \cdot \rho \cdot R_{in} \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{a}{b}\right)$
- ✅ **Activity factor $\rho$** is the fraction of time a source is transmitting; lower $\rho$ → higher Stat-TDM efficiency.
- ✅ **Stat-TDM requires buffers (FIFO)** to absorb bursty input traffic and decouple input/output rates.
- ✅ **Stat-TDM has variable frame length** and introduces **jitter and queuing delay**, unlike the predictable Synchronous TDM.
- ✅ **Buffer overflow** is the primary failure mode of Stat-TDM under high aggregate load.
- ✅ **Stat-TDM is the foundation of modern packet networks** (IP, ATM, MPLS) — be ready to draw this parallel in answers.
- ✅ **Synchronous TDM is preferred for real-time, constant-bit-rate traffic** (legacy voice circuits). Statistical TDM is preferred for **bursty data traffic**.
- ✅ **Always show frame diagrams with address fields in the KTU exam** — it is a heavily marked component.
- ✅ **Address bits formula** is $\lceil \log_2 N \rceil$, **not** $\log_2 N$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Asymmetric DSL
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCERT602 - DATA COMMUNICATION AND NETWORKING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Asymmetric DSL

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Asymmetric DSL (ADSL) - Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Asymmetric Digital Subscriber Line (ADSL)** is a broadband access technology standardized under **ITU-T G.992.1** (and later G.992.3 for ADSL2, G.992.5 for ADSL2+) that enables high-speed digital data transmission over standard **twisted-pair copper telephone lines** while simultaneously supporting plain old telephone service (POTS) on the same physical medium.

The term *asymmetric* denotes that the **downstream (provider-to-customer) bit-rate is significantly higher than the upstream (customer-to-provider) bit-rate**, reflecting the typical consumer usage pattern of web browsing, video streaming, and file downloads.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** ADSL belongs to the family of **xDSL (Digital Subscriber Line)** technologies that use the existing copper local loop of the Public Switched Telephone Network (PSTN) to deliver broadband services without requiring new physical cabling infrastructure.

| ADSL Parameter | Standard Value |
|----------------|----------------|
| Standard | ITU-T G.992.1 (ADSL), G.992.3 (ADSL2), G.992.5 (ADSL2+) |
| Line Coding | Discrete Multi-Tone (DMT) |
| Downstream Rate | Up to **8 Mbps** (ADSL), **12 Mbps** (ADSL2), **24 Mbps** (ADSL2+) |
| Upstream Rate | Up to **1 Mbps** |
| Line Type | Single twisted-pair copper |
| Maximum Range | Approximately **5.5 km** (18,000 ft) from CO |
| Frequency Range | **0 – 1.104 MHz** |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **two-lane water pipeline** laid right next to your house — a tiny lane that is *just enough* to send out a small request (like ordering a pizza) and a giant lane that brings the entire pizza box (with sides, dessert, and drink) back to you. That is essentially what ADSL does with the frequencies on your telephone wire.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy — The "Reverse Commute" Pattern:**
> - A typical household **downloads** heavy files (movies, web pages, songs) but only **uploads** tiny requests (URLs, mouse clicks, search queries).
> - ADSL mirrors this behavior: the **downstream channel is wide (≈ 224 × 4.3125 kHz)** while the **upstream channel is narrow (≈ 25 × 4.3125 kHz)**.
> - The voice (POTS) signal travels in a separate frequency band (below **4 kHz**) using a device called a **splitter** (or low-pass filter) so that you can talk on the phone while browsing the internet simultaneously.

## 1.3 Why "Asymmetric"?

The asymmetry is **engineered, not accidental**. Studies of residential and small-business internet traffic (notably by the late 1990s NIST/ADSL-Forum analyses) showed that the **downstream-to-upstream traffic ratio was typically 10:1 or higher**. ADSL is deliberately optimized for this traffic profile to maximize spectral efficiency on a single copper pair.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Anchor for Exam:** *A = Asymmetric = **A**symmetric **D**igital **S**ubscriber **L**ine → Download speed **>>** Upload speed. The mnemonic rule is "**A**lways **D**ownload **M**ore."*

## 1.4 ADSL in the DSL Family Tree

ADSL is one of several DSL variants:

- **ADSL** — Asymmetric, broadband downstream, narrow upstream.
- **HDSL** — High-bit-rate DSL, symmetric, used for T1/E1 extension.
- **SDSL** — Single-line DSL, symmetric, half the bandwidth of HDSL.
- **VDSL** — Very-high-bit-rate DSL, asymmetric, very short range.
- **RADSL** — Rate-Adaptive DSL, dynamically adjusts bit-rate to line conditions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 ADSL System Architecture

ADSL involves three principal hardware blocks:

1. **ATU-C (ADSL Transceiver Unit – Central Office):** Located at the telephone company's central office (CO), it terminates the digital end of the DSL link on the network side.
2. **ATU-R (ADSL Transceiver Unit – Remote):** Located at the customer premises, it terminates the digital end on the subscriber side (often integrated inside the modem/router).
3. **Splitters / POTS Filters:** Low-pass filters at both ends separate the **0 – 4 kHz** voice band from the higher-frequency data band.

> [!NOTE]
> **Nits, Bits, and DMT:** ADSL uses **Discrete Multi-Tone (DMT)** modulation, which divides the available spectrum (above 4 kHz, up to ~1.104 MHz) into **256 sub-channels** (called *bins* or *tones*) of width **4.3125 kHz** each. Each sub-channel uses **Quadrature Amplitude Modulation (QAM)** with a bit-loading ranging from **0 to 15 bits per symbol** depending on the measured **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** of that specific sub-channel.

## 2.2 Frequency Band Allocation (DMT)

| Band | Frequency Range | Purpose |
|------|-----------------|---------|
| 0 | 0 – 4 kHz | POTS (voice telephony) |
| 1 – 5 | 4 – 25 kHz | **Guard band** (unused) |
| 6 – 30 | 25 – 138 kHz | **Upstream** (25 sub-channels) |
| 31 – 255 | 138 – 1104 kHz | **Downstream** (225 sub-channels) |

The total usable bandwidth is divided as:
- **Upstream:** 25 bins × 4.3125 kHz ≈ 108 kHz
- **Downstream:** 225 bins × 4.3125 kHz ≈ 970 kHz
- **Voice + guard:** ~25 kHz

## 2.3 Bit Loading and Shannon's Capacity Limit

Each DMT sub-channel independently carries $b_i$ bits per symbol where:

$$b_i = \left\lfloor \log_2 \left(1 + \frac{\text{SNR}_i}{\Gamma}\right) \right\rfloor$$

Here $\Gamma$ is the **SNR gap** (typically $\Gamma \approx 9.75 \text{ dB}$ for a target BER of $10^{-7}$), and the per-channel SNR is given by Shannon's classic relation.

The **total downstream bit-rate** is the sum across all downstream sub-channels:

$$R_{DS} = \sum_{i=31}^{255} b_i \times f_s$$

where $f_s$ is the symbol rate of DMT (≈ 4000 symbols/s, since the bin spacing is 4.3125 kHz and a cyclic prefix is added).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every equation, parameter, and boundary condition required for solving ADSL-related problems in KTU examinations.

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---------------------|-----------|---------------|
| 1 | Sub-channel bandwidth | $\Delta f = 4.3125$ | kHz |
| 2 | Total sub-channels | $N_{total} = 256$ | tones |
| 3 | Upstream sub-channels | $N_{US} = 25$ | tones (bins 6–30) |
| 4 | Downstream sub-channels | $N_{DS} = 225$ | tones (bins 31–255) |
| 5 | Maximum bits per bin | $b_{max} = 15$ | bits/symbol |
| 6 | Shannon capacity per bin | $C_i = \Delta f \times \log_2(1 + \text{SNR}_i)$ | bits/s |
| 7 | Total downstream rate | $R_{DS} = \sum_{i=31}^{255} \log_2(1+\text{SNR}_i/\Gamma) \times \Delta f$ | bits/s |
| 8 | Maximum theoretical DS rate | $R_{DS}^{max} = N_{DS} \times b_{max} \times f_s$ | bits/s |
| 9 | Loop attenuation (approx.) | $A(d, f) \approx \alpha \sqrt{f} \times d$ | dB |
| 10 | SNR per bin | $\text{SNR}_i = \frac{P_{tx}}{\sigma^2} \times 10^{-A(d,f_i)/10}$ | dimensionless |
| 11 | Line length limit (ADSL) | $d \le 5.5$ | km |
| 12 | Range–bandwidth tradeoff | $R \propto d^{-1}$ (approx.) | Mbps vs. km |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineer's utility:** ADSL is the foundational technology behind every Indian broadband connection delivered over BSNL/MTNL/Airtel copper lines. Understanding DMT bit-loading is the gateway to understanding OFDM, LTE, and 5G NR physical-layer design.

## 2.5 The "Dead Zone" / "Killer Zone" Phenomenon

A subtle but high-yield KTU concept: as frequency increases along a copper pair, **attenuation grows roughly as $\sqrt{f}$**. Consequently, sub-channels at the high end of the downstream band (bins 200–255) suffer severely reduced SNR and may have to be **loaded with 0 bits** (effectively disabled). This creates a phenomenon known as the **dead zone** where line quality fluctuates with temperature, weather, and interference from AM radio (crosstalk in the 530–1700 kHz band).

---

## 2.6 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Initialization / Handshake:** ATU-R and ATU-C exchange training sequences, probing each of the 256 bins to measure **per-channel SNR**.
2. **Bit Allocation Table (BAT):** Both ends negotiate how many bits each bin will carry. High-SNR bins get up to 15 bits; low-SNR bins get fewer or zero.
3. **Data Transmission:** Data is split into parallel bit-streams, each QAM-modulated onto its assigned sub-carrier.
4. **Inverse FFT (IFFT) at Transmitter:** All 256 sub-carriers are combined into a single time-domain DMT symbol.
5. **Cyclic Prefix Insertion:** A guard interval (cyclic prefix) is added to absorb inter-symbol interference (ISI) from channel dispersion.
6. **FFT at Receiver:** Time-domain signal is converted back to 256 parallel QAM symbols; each is demodulated and the bits are recombined.
7. **Echo Cancellation:** Used to separate upstream and downstream signals when they share spectrum (in echo-canceller mode).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Maximum Theoretical Downstream Bit-Rate of ADSL

We compute the **upper bound** on the downstream rate using the DMT structure.

**Step 1 — Identify the symbol rate.**

The bin spacing is $\Delta f = 4.3125$ kHz. Since the IFFT samples the spectrum at exactly the bin spacing, the nominal symbol rate (without cyclic prefix) is:

$$f_s = \frac{1}{T_s} = \Delta f = 4312.5 \; \text{symbols/s}$$

> **Reasoning:** In a standard DMT symbol, one full IFFT block of $N = 256$ samples corresponds to a symbol period $T_s = 1 / \Delta f$, because the spacing between adjacent sub-carriers in the IFFT equals the reciprocal of the symbol period.

**Step 2 — Determine the maximum bits per downstream bin.**

Theoretical maximum is $b_{max} = 15$ bits per QAM constellation point. (In practice, ADSL uses up to 12 bits; ADSL2+ uses up to 15.)

**Step 3 — Compute the maximum bits per DMT symbol downstream.**

$$B_{DS}^{max} = N_{DS} \times b_{max} = 225 \times 15 = 3375 \; \text{bits/symbol}$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by the symbol rate to obtain the bit-rate.**

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{DS}^{max} &= B_{DS}^{max} \times f_s \\
&= 3375 \times 4312.5 \\
&= 14{,}554{,}687.5 \; \text{bits/s} \\
&\approx 14.55 \; \text{Mbps}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Reasoning:** This is the absolute theoretical ceiling when every downstream sub-channel is maximally loaded. Real systems deliver 6–8 Mbps because the cyclic prefix and practical SNR limits reduce effective bits.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — SNR per Sub-Channel vs. Distance

The attenuation of a copper pair is empirically approximated by:

$$A(d, f) = k \cdot \sqrt{f} \cdot d \; (\text{dB})$$

where $k$ is a constant depending on cable gauge (for 26-AWG copper, $k \approx 0.01$ dB per kHz$^{0.5}$ per km, simplified).

**Step 1 — Express received signal power in a bin at frequency $f_i$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{rx}(f_i) &= P_{tx} - A(d, f_i) \;(\text{dB}) \\
&= P_{tx} - k \sqrt{f_i} \cdot d
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to linear scale and form SNR.**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{rx}^{lin}(f_i) &= P_{tx}^{lin} \times 10^{-k\sqrt{f_i} \cdot d / 10} \\
\text{SNR}(f_i) &= \frac{P_{rx}^{lin}(f_i)}{\sigma^2} = \frac{P_{tx}^{lin}}{\sigma^2} \times 10^{-k\sqrt{f_i} \cdot d / 10}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Shannon bound per bin.**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_i &= \Delta f \cdot \log_2\!\left(1 + \text{SNR}(f_i)\right) \\
&= \Delta f \cdot \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{P_{tx}^{lin}}{\sigma^2} \times 10^{-k\sqrt{f_i} \cdot d / 10}\right) \; \text{bits/s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Sum over all downstream bins to obtain total rate.**

$$R_{DS}(d) = \sum_{i=31}^{255} \Delta f \cdot \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{P_{tx}^{lin}}{\sigma^2} \times 10^{-k\sqrt{f_i} \cdot d / 10}\right)$$

> **Reasoning:** This shows the **fundamental range–rate trade-off** of ADSL: as distance $d$ grows, SNR at high frequencies collapses, forcing the modem to load fewer bits in those bins and reducing the aggregate rate.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Number of Usable Bins vs. Distance (Worked Example)

Suppose a customer is **$d = 4$ km** from the CO, and for acceptable service, each bin must maintain an SNR of at least **20 dB** (i.e., $\text{SNR} \ge 100$). If the maximum bin number that meets this threshold is bin **120**, compute the resulting downstream bit-rate (assume 8 bits per loaded bin and 4312.5 symbols/s).

**Step 1 — Count downstream bins from 31 to 120.**

$$N_{usable} = 120 - 31 + 1 = 90 \; \text{bins}$$

**Step 2 — Compute bits per symbol at the average loading of 8 bits.**

$$B_{DS} = 90 \times 8 = 720 \; \text{bits/symbol}$$

**Step 3 — Compute downstream rate.**

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{DS} &= 720 \times 4312.5 \\
&= 3{,}105{,}000 \; \text{bits/s} \\
&\approx 3.1 \; \text{Mbps}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Reasoning:** The KTU board commonly tests whether a student understands that **only the bins with adequate SNR contribute bits** — and that the bit-loading is non-uniform, with high-frequency bins often dropped first.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of ADSL Versions

| Parameter | ADSL (G.992.1) | ADSL2 (G.992.3) | ADSL2+ (G.992.5) | VDSL2 (G.993.2) |
|-----------|---------------|-----------------|------------------|------------------|
| Max Downstream | 8 Mbps | 12 Mbps | 24 Mbps | 100 Mbps |
| Max Upstream | 1 Mbps | 1.5 Mbps | 3.5 Mbps | 100 Mbps |
| Downstream Bins | 224 | 224 | 224 | 4096 |
| Max Bits/Bin | 12 | 12 | 15 | 15 |
| Bandwidth | 1.104 MHz | 1.104 MHz | 2.208 MHz | 30 MHz |
| Range | 5.5 km | 5.5 km | 5.5 km | < 1.5 km |
| Year | 1999 | 2002 | 2003 | 2006 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 ADSL System End-to-End Block Diagram

The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the complete end-to-end functional architecture of an ADSL link, mapping every interaction from the Central Office (CO) to the Customer Premises (CP).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CO["CENTRAL OFFICE"]
        direction TB
        ATUC["ATU-C Modem"]
        COswitch["PSTN Voice Switch"]
        DSlam["ISP Backbone / DSLAM"]
    end

    subgraph SPLITTER["CENTRAL OFFICE SPLITTER"]
        direction TB
        FilterCO["Low-Pass 0-4 kHz and High-Pass Splitter"]
    end

    subgraph LOOP["TWISTED COPPER PAIR (Local Loop)"]
        direction TB
        LoopLine["d = up to 5.5 km of 26-AWG copper"]
    end

    subgraph SPLITTER_R["CUSTOMER PREMISES SPLITTER"]
        direction TB
        FilterCP["POTS Splitter / Microfilter"]
    end

    subgraph CP["CUSTOMER PREMISES"]
        direction TB
        ATUR["ATU-R Modem / Router"]
        PC["User PC / LAN"]
        Phone["Telephone Handset"]
    end

    DSlam -- "Downstream data" --> ATUC
    COswitch -- "0-4 kHz voice" --> ATUC
    ATUC -- "Composite DMT signal" --> FilterCO
    FilterCO -- "Voice + Data over copper" --> LoopLine
    LoopLine -- "Composite signal" --> FilterCP
    FilterCP -- "Data > 25 kHz" --> ATUR
    FilterCP -- "Voice 0-4 kHz" --> Phone
    ATUR -- "Ethernet / Wi-Fi" --> PC
    ATUR -- "Upstream data" --> LoopLine
    LoopLine -- "Upstream data" --> ATUC
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Architectural insight:** The two **splitters** are critical — they perform **frequency-domain multiplexing** between the POTS band (0–4 kHz) and the DMT data band (above 25 kHz), enabling simultaneous voice and broadband on a single physical pair.

## 4.2 ADSL Frequency Band Allocation Chart

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ADSLF["ADSL DMT FREQUENCY ALLOCATION"]
        direction LR
        Bin0["BIN 0 \n 0 - 4 kHz \n POTS VOICE"]
        Bin1to5["BINS 1-5 \n 4 - 25 kHz \n GUARD BAND"]
        Bin6to30["BINS 6-30 \n 25 - 138 kHz \n UPSTREAM 25 bins"]
        Bin31to255["BINS 31-255 \n 138 - 1104 kHz \n DOWNSTREAM 225 bins"]
    end

    Bin0 ~~~ Bin1to5
    Bin1to5 ~~~ Bin6to30
    Bin6to30 ~~~ Bin31to255
```

## 4.3 DMT Transmitter Pipeline (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Serial Bit Stream In"] --> B["Serial-to-Parallel Converter"]
    B --> C["Bit Allocator: 0-15 bits per bin"]
    C --> D["QAM Constellation Mapper 256 bins"]
    D --> E["Inverse FFT Block N equals 256"]
    E --> F["Cyclic Prefix Insertion"]
    F --> G["DAC and Line Driver"]
    G --> H["Twisted Pair Output"]

    subgraph RECV["Receiver side mirror"]
        H2["Twisted Pair Input"] --> I["ADC"]
        I --> J["Cyclic Prefix Removal"]
        J --> K["FFT Block"]
        K --> L["QAM Demapper"]
        L --> M["Parallel-to-Serial Converter"]
        M --> N["Serial Bit Stream Out"]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The transmitter uses an **IFFT** to combine 256 sub-carriers into a single time-domain DMT symbol; the receiver uses an **FFT** to split them back. This is the core of why DMT = **OFDM-based** and is the conceptual parent of **Wi-Fi (802.11a/g/n)**, **4G LTE**, and **5G NR** waveforms.

## 4.4 Bit-Loading Profile (Typical ADSL Snapshot)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BL["Bit-Loading Profile SNR per Bin"]
        direction TB
        LowBins["Bins 31-80 \n SNR 40-50 dB \n 12-15 bits/ton"]
        MidBins["Bins 81-150 \n SNR 25-40 dB \n 6-10 bits/ton"]
        HighBins["Bins 151-200 \n SNR 15-25 dB \n 2-4 bits/ton"]
        DeadBins["Bins 201-255 \n SNR below 10 dB \n 0 bits/ton DEAD ZONE"]
    end

    LowBins --> MidBins --> HighBins --> DeadBins
```

> [!TIP]
> **Exam insight:** The "dead zone" terminology describes the high-frequency bins that contribute **zero bits** because their SNR is too poor to support even **BPSK (1 bit/Hz)**. This is exactly the reason why *long loops cannot achieve maximum advertised rates.*

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] 
**Define ADSL. Why is it called "asymmetric"?**

**Model Answer:**
ADSL (Asymmetric Digital Subscriber Line) is a broadband technology defined by ITU-T G.992.1 that delivers high-speed digital data over standard twisted-pair telephone lines while supporting simultaneous voice (POTS). It is termed *asymmetric* because the **downstream bit-rate (provider-to-customer) is significantly higher than the upstream bit-rate (customer-to-provider)**, typically 8 Mbps downstream vs. 1 Mbps upstream. This asymmetry matches the typical user traffic profile of high-volume downloads and small upstream requests.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] 
**List the functions of the POTS splitter in an ADSL link.**

**Model Answer:**
The POTS splitter performs **frequency-domain separation** of voice and data signals on the same copper pair:
1. Routes the **0 – 4 kHz** voice band to the telephone handset.
2. Routes the **> 25 kHz** DMT data band to the ADSL modem (ATU-R).
3. Prevents high-frequency DSL signals from interfering with or damaging telephone equipment.
4. Maintains lifeline voice service even during power failures (in case of passive splitters).
5. Provides **impedance matching** to minimize echo and reflection on the local loop.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question — Internal Choice (14 Marks)

### Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Adapted]

**Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of an ADSL system**, identifying the role of ATU-C, ATU-R, and the POTS splitter. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
ADSL transmits high-speed digital data over the existing **PSTN copper local loop** using DMT modulation, with simultaneous support for POTS voice.

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**
Refer to the system architecture diagram in **Section 4.1**. The three principal blocks are:

1. **ATU-C (ADSL Transceiver Unit – Central Office):**
   - Located at the **telephone exchange (CO)**.
   - Terminates the network side of the DSL link.
   - Receives downstream data from the **DSLAM (Digital Subscriber Line Access Multiplexer)**.
   - Performs **DMT modulation, bit-loading, IFFT, and line driving**.
   - Connected to the **PSTN voice switch** for plain telephone service.

2. **ATU-R (ADSL Transceiver Unit – Remote):**
   - Located at the **customer premises**.
   - Terminates the subscriber side of the DSL link.
   - Performs **FFT-based DMT demodulation** and forwards Ethernet/Wi-Fi to user devices.

3. **POTS Splitter (at both ends):**
   - A **passive low-pass + high-pass filter** combination.
   - Low-pass: 0 – 4 kHz → telephone.
   - High-pass: 25 kHz – 1.1 MHz → ATU-R/ATU-C.

**Working Principle (2 marks):**
- The ATU-C and ATU-R perform a **training handshake** to measure SNR per sub-channel.
- They negotiate a **Bit Allocation Table (BAT)** that determines how many bits each of the 256 bins will carry.
- Data is then modulated using DMT and sent over the copper pair.

**Functional Insight (2 marks):**
- The **DSLAM at the CO** aggregates thousands of ATU-C modems and uplinks them to the ISP backbone.
- The **splitter enables simultaneous voice and data** without mutual interference.
- ADSL is a **physical-layer + data-link-layer** technology that provides a point-to-point dedicated link between the CO and the customer.

---

**Q. (b)** Explain **Discrete Multi-Tone (DMT) modulation** used in ADSL. Discuss how bits are loaded into sub-channels based on channel SNR. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**DMT Concept (2 marks):**
DMT is a multicarrier modulation technique that divides the **25 kHz – 1.104 MHz** spectrum into **256 orthogonal sub-channels** of **4.3125 kHz** each. Each sub-channel uses **QAM** modulation independently. DMT is mathematically implemented using **IFFT at the transmitter and FFT at the receiver**.

**Channel Partitioning (2 marks):**
- Sub-channels **1–5** (4 – 25 kHz): Guard band.
- Sub-channels **6–30** (25 – 138 kHz): Upstream (25 bins).
