- Sub-channels **31–255** (138 kHz – 1.104 MHz): Downstream (225 bins).

**Bit Loading Based on SNR (2 marks):**
During initialization, ATU-C and ATU-R send known training signals to measure **SNR per bin**. Based on the measured SNR:
- **High-SNR bins** (e.g., 31–80) are loaded with up to **12–15 bits** per symbol.
- **Medium-SNR bins** (e.g., 81–150) carry **6–10 bits** per symbol.
- **Low-SNR bins** (e.g., 200–255) may carry **0 bits** — they are disabled, forming the **"dead zone"**.

The maximum bits per bin is calculated using Shannon's capacity formula:

$$b_i = \left\lfloor \log_2 \left(1 + \frac{\text{SNR}_i}{\Gamma}\right) \right\rfloor$$

**Practical Implications (1 mark):**
This adaptive bit-loading is what allows ADSL to maximize throughput on any given line. As line quality degrades (longer distance, higher temperature, RF interference), the modem automatically re-negotiates the BAT to maintain a stable link.

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Choice) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q. (a)** Describe the **ADSL frequency band plan**. How is the spectrum shared between upstream, downstream, and POTS? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Spectrum Division (3 marks):**
ADSL uses the bandwidth from 0 Hz to 1.104 MHz on a single twisted pair. The spectrum is divided as follows:

| Frequency Range | Function | Sub-channels |
|-----------------|----------|---------------|
| 0 – 4 kHz | **POTS voice** | Bin 0 |
| 4 – 25 kHz | **Guard band** | Bins 1–5 |
| 25 – 138 kHz | **Upstream** | Bins 6–30 (25 bins) |
| 138 kHz – 1.104 MHz | **Downstream** | Bins 31–255 (225 bins) |

**Why a Guard Band? (2 marks):**
A 21 kHz guard band separates the voice band from the data band to:
- Prevent **spectral leakage** from POTS signals into DMT sub-channels.
- Allow the **POTS splitter filters** to have a smooth roll-off.
- Minimize **intermodulation distortion** at the line driver.

**Why More Downstream Bins? (2 marks):**
The asymmetry in bin count (225 downstream vs. 25 upstream) is the physical manifestation of the *asymmetric* nature of ADSL. It is justified by:
- Typical user traffic being **download-dominant** (web, video, software updates).
- Engineering ease: a wider downstream band compensates for higher attenuation at high frequencies, since each bin can still contribute 1–2 bits.

---

**Q. (b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **DMT transmitter and receiver pipeline**. Highlight the role of IFFT/FFT and cyclic prefix. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**DMT Transmitter (3 marks):**
The DMT transmitter pipeline is shown in the diagram in **Section 4.3**. The stages are:
1. **Serial-to-Parallel Conversion:** Incoming high-speed serial bit stream is split into 256 parallel bit-streams.
2. **Bit Allocation:** Each parallel stream is assigned a bin and a bit-count per QAM symbol (0–15 bits).
3. **QAM Mapping:** Each bin's bit group is mapped onto a QAM constellation.
4. **Inverse FFT (IFFT):** The 256 frequency-domain QAM symbols are transformed to a single time-domain DMT symbol using a 256-point IFFT.
5. **Cyclic Prefix (CP) Insertion:** A guard interval (typically 32 samples) is prepended to absorb **inter-symbol interference (ISI)** caused by channel dispersion.

**DMT Receiver (2 marks):**
1. **Cyclic Prefix Removal.**
2. **FFT:** The 256-point FFT separates the time-domain samples back into 256 QAM symbols.
3. **QAM Demapping & Bit Extraction.**
4. **Parallel-to-Serial Conversion** to reconstruct the original stream.

**Role of IFFT/FFT and CP (2 marks):**
- **IFFT/FFT** implements the **multicarrier modulation** mathematically with a single, efficient transform — far cheaper than running 256 independent oscillators.
- **Cyclic Prefix** converts the linear convolution of the channel into a circular convolution, allowing the FFT at the receiver to perfectly equalize the channel with a single complex multiplication per bin.
- This design makes DMT robust against **frequency-selective fading** and **narrowband interference** such as AM radio ingress.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing ADSL with ADSL2+:** Students often write "24 Mbps downstream" for plain ADSL; the correct figure is **8 Mbps** for G.992.1. ADSL2+ (G.992.5) is the 24 Mbps variant.
> 2. **Mixing up bins and channels:** Each bin is **4.3125 kHz wide**, and there are **256 bins total**, not 250 or 200. The KTU board penalizes incorrect bin counts.
> 3. **Forgetting the guard band:** Bins 1–5 (4–25 kHz) are *unused*. If a student counts them as upstream bins, the upstream count of 25 will be wrong.
> 4. **Confusing ATU-C and ATU-R:** ATU-C is at the **Central Office (CO)**; ATU-R is at the **Remote (customer) end**. This is a 1-mark deduction but appears in nearly every 14-mark question.
> 5. **Skipping the cyclic prefix:** Forgetting to mention the cyclic prefix in a DMT explanation is a 1-mark deduction. Always mention CP, IFFT, and FFT together.
> 6. **Writing "ADSL uses FDM"** without specifying DMT — partial credit only. Always say **"ADSL uses DMT, a multicarrier modulation based on OFDM principles."**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — ADSL**

- ADSL stands for **Asymmetric Digital Subscriber Line**, defined by **ITU-T G.992.1**.
- The **asymmetry** reflects the **download-dominant** traffic pattern of residential users.
- ADSL uses **DMT (Discrete Multi-Tone) modulation** = **OFDM with QAM** per sub-channel.
- The spectrum has **256 bins of 4.3125 kHz** each, total bandwidth ≈ **1.104 MHz**.
- **POTS** occupies **0 – 4 kHz**; **guard band** is **4 – 25 kHz**; **upstream** is **25 – 138 kHz (25 bins)**; **downstream** is **138 kHz – 1.104 MHz (225 bins)**.
- **Bit loading** is adaptive: high-SNR bins carry up to **12–15 bits**, low-SNR bins may carry **0 bits** (dead zone).
- **Maximum bits per bin** is determined by Shannon: $b_i = \lfloor \log_2(1 + \text{SNR}_i/\Gamma) \rfloor$.
- Maximum theoretical downstream rate ≈ **225 × 15 × 4312.5 ≈ 14.55 Mbps**; practical rate ≈ **8 Mbps** for ADSL.
- The maximum range is **5.5 km** from the CO; longer loops suffer **dead-zone** loss of high-frequency bins.
- **ATU-C** is at the **CO**; **ATU-R** is at the **customer premises**.
- **POTS splitter** = low-pass (voice) + high-pass (data) filter pair, ensures simultaneous voice + data.
- **Cyclic prefix** in DMT absorbs ISI and converts linear convolution to circular convolution.
- ADSL family progression: **ADSL (8/1 Mbps) → ADSL2 (12/1.5 Mbps) → ADSL2+ (24/3.5 Mbps) → VDSL2 (100/100 Mbps)**.
- ADSL is the **physical-layer + data-link-layer** technology deployed on the **last-mile copper local loop** between CO and subscriber.
- ADSL uses **frequency division multiplexing (FDM)** with **echo cancellation** as alternatives to separate upstream/downstream; ADSL uses FDM (separate bands) for separation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: xDSL
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCERT602 - DATA COMMUNICATION AND NETWORKING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** xDSL

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is xDSL?

**xDSL** (where the "x" denotes a *family* of variants) stands for **Digital Subscriber Line**. It is a collective term for a set of physical-layer technologies that use the existing **twisted-pair copper telephone lines (local loops)** of the Public Switched Telephone Network (PSTN) to deliver high-speed digital data services *alongside* traditional analog Plain Old Telephone Service (POTS) voice calls.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> xDSL refers to a *family of digital subscriber line technologies* (ADSL, ADSL2, ADSL2+, SDSL, HDSL, VDSL, VDSL2) that provide broadband data transmission over the existing copper local loop using advanced modulation techniques (DMT/QAM) and Frequency Division Multiplexing (FDM) to separate voice and data bands, while simultaneously achieving high bit rates in the downstream (ISP → user) direction.

The most prominent members studied under the KTU syllabus are:

| Variant | Full Form | Asymmetry |
|---|---|---|
| **ADSL** | Asymmetric Digital Subscriber Line | Downstream ≫ Upstream |
| **ADSL2** | Asymmetric DSL – Version 2 | Downstream > Upstream |
| **ADSL2+** | ADSL – Version 2 Plus | Downstream > Upstream |
| **HDSL** | High-bit-rate DSL | Symmetric |
| **SDSL** | Symmetric DSL | Symmetric |
| **VDSL** | Very-high-bit-rate DSL | Asymmetric / Symmetric |
| **VDSL2** | Very-high-bit-rate DSL – Version 2 | Asymmetric / Symmetric |
| **RADSL** | Rate-Adaptive DSL | Adaptive |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Multiplexed Highway"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: One Narrow Road → A Multi-Lane Expressway**
>
> Imagine the local-loop copper pair as a **single, narrow, crowded road** that historically carried only one type of traffic — voice. When dial-up modems tried to use it, the road became congested at just **56 kbps** because the modem *dialed the network* and blocked voice.
>
> **xDSL transforms that same road into a multi-lane expressway**:
> - The **lowest frequency lane (0 – 4 kHz)** is reserved for **POTS voice** (the baseband).
> - The **higher frequency lanes (25 kHz – 1.1 MHz, or up to 30 MHz for VDSL2)** are reserved for **digital data** in both directions.
> - Cars (bits) travel in the **downstream direction (from the exchange to the home)** in much larger volumes than in the upstream direction — mimicking how an *average residential user downloads more than they upload* (web pages, video, music).
>
> The **splitter at each end** is like the **toll-booth that directs traffic** into the correct lane: voice packets to the telephone, data packets to the DSL modem/router.

---

## 1.3 Why Do We Need xDSL? (Problem → Solution)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight — Why dial-up modems failed:**
>
> 1. **POTS + Data conflict:** Traditional dial-up modems used the *same 0 – 4 kHz voice band* and required a dialed connection — meaning the line was either for voice OR for data, never both simultaneously.
> 2. **Cap of ~56 kbps:** Limited by the **Shannon-Hartley limit** for the narrow voice-grade channel with typical SNR ≈ 30 dB.
> 3. **Always-on is impossible:** Dial-up had long handshake/connection times.
>
> **xDSL Solution:**
> - Reserves voice band for POTS (always available).
> - Uses **higher, unused frequencies** for data (above 26 kHz).
> - Provides **always-on, high-bit-rate** connectivity.
> - **No dialing required** — the line is permanently connected to the DSLAM at the Central Office (CO).

---

## 1.4 Core Metrics & Standards (Bold-Standardized)

- **POTS voice band:** **0 – 4 kHz** (baseband).
- **ADSL downstream band:** **~26 kHz – 1.1 MHz**.
- **ADSL upstream band:** **~26 kHz – 138 kHz**.
- **VDSL2 band:** Up to **30 MHz** on the copper pair.
- **DMT subchannel width:** **4.3125 kHz** each.
- **Number of subchannels (ADSL):** **256 downstream** (allocated above 26 kHz) + **32 upstream** → 256 total in DMT.
- **Maximum loop reach (ADSL):** Approximately **5.5 km (≈ 18,000 ft)** of 24-AWG copper before severe attenuation.
- **Bit rate targets:** ADSL ≈ **6–8 Mbps down / 0.5–1 Mbps up**; ADSL2+ ≈ **24 Mbps down / 1.5 Mbps up**; VDSL2 ≈ **100+ Mbps symmetric/asymmetric**.

---

## 1.5 Functional Components in the xDSL Architecture

> [!NOTE]
> **Three essential physical components** in any xDSL deployment:
>
> 1. **DSLAM (Digital Subscriber Line Access Multiplexer):** Located at the *Central Office* (telephone exchange). Aggregates thousands of customer DSL lines onto a single high-speed backbone link (e.g., ATM, Ethernet, IP).
> 2. **POTS Splitter (Low-pass + High-pass filter):** A *passive* device that separates the low-frequency voice band (0–4 kHz) from the high-frequency data band (>26 kHz). One splitter at the CO and one at the customer premises.
> 3. **DSL Modem (CPE — Customer Premises Equipment):** The device at the user's home/office that demodulates the DMT signal back to Ethernet/IP data. The customer's PC/router connects to this.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cross-Reference with KTU Module 3 Themes:**
> The xDSL topic in KTU Module 3 typically bridges *Data Link Layer (DLL)*, *Physical Layer (PHY)*, and *Last-Mile Access Networks*. It directly continues the discussion of switching/multiplexing and prepares the ground for SONET/SDH and Cable Modem networks.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Principle: FDM Channel Allocation

The *entire* xDSL family relies on a single elegant idea: **use frequency bands that the telephone network has never used before, while preserving the legacy voice band.**

For **ADSL**, the spectrum of the copper local loop is divided as follows:

| Band | Frequency Range | Direction | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| **POTS (Voice)** | **0 – 4 kHz** | Bidirectional | Legacy analog voice (always available) |
| **Guard Band** | **4 – 26 kHz** | — | Buffer to prevent POTS ↔ Data interference |
| **Upstream** | **26 – 138 kHz** | Customer → CO | User uploads (light traffic) |
| **Downstream** | **138 kHz – 1.104 MHz** | CO → Customer | High-volume downloads |

> [!NOTE]
> The **guard band of 22 kHz** is the key engineering trick — it ensures that the high-energy voice signal (e.g., a ringing phone) cannot leak into the digital data band and corrupt DMT subchannels.

---

## 2.2 DMT — Discrete Multitone Modulation (The Heart of ADSL)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Critical Concept — DMT:**
> ADSL uses **DMT (Discrete Multitone)** as defined in **ANSI T1.413 Issue 2 / ITU G.992.1**. It is essentially **DMT-based multicarrier modulation** (an implementation of OFDM — Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiplexing).

### How DMT works — step by step:

1. The total available **data band** (≈ 1.1 MHz) is divided into **N = 256 subchannels** of equal width **Δf = 4.3125 kHz** each.
2. Each subchannel `i` has its own **carrier frequency** `f_i = i × 4.3125 kHz` for i = 1 … 256.
3. The CO's DSLAM probes each subchannel with a pilot tone during **initialization** and measures its **SNR (Signal-to-Noise Ratio)**.
4. Based on the SNR, the DSLAM assigns each subchannel a **bit-loading factor `b_i`** (the number of bits per symbol for that subchannel).
5. Higher-SNR subchannels are assigned more bits (e.g., 8–12 bits/symbol); lower-SNR (noisy) subchannels are assigned fewer bits (e.g., 0–2 bits/symbol) or are turned off entirely.
6. The total downstream bit rate is the sum over all active subchannels:
   `R_down = Σᵢ (bᵢ × symbol_rate)`.

### The Key Insight — Adaptive Bit Loading

The **copper line is not uniform**: attenuation, crosstalk, and noise vary with frequency. DMT treats this as an *opportunity*, not a problem — **the modulation adapts to the channel** (a process called **bit-loading**, similar to what DVB-T and 4G/LTE also do).

---

## 2.3 Why DMT Was Chosen Over Earlier QAM/CAP

| Property | DMT (used in ADSL) | CAP / QAM (older) |
|---|---|---|
| Channel adaptation | Excellent (per-subchannel bit loading) | Poor (one global setting) |
| Impulse-noise resilience | High (each subchannel narrow → long symbol) | Low |
| Computational complexity | High (FFT/IFFT required) | Lower |
| Standardization | Universal (ITU/ANSI) | Less common today |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> **All KTU exam questions on xDSL are solvable using the formulas below. Memorize them.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **F-1** | **Nybquist Bit Rate (Noiseless):** $N_b = 2 \cdot B \cdot \log_2(V)$ | Max bit rate in a noiseless channel of bandwidth B using V discrete signal levels | Theoretical upper bound for any DSL |
| **F-2** | **Shannon–Hartley Capacity (Noisy):** $C = B \cdot \log_2\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | Maximum achievable bit rate in a noisy channel of bandwidth B with SNR = S/N | **Most important formula for xDSL design** |
| **F-3** | **SNR in decibels:** $\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | Converts linear SNR to dB | Used in Excel tables / DSL bit-loading |
| **F-4** | **Total DMT bit rate:** $R_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} b_i \cdot f_s$ | Sum of bits/symbol × symbol rate across all subchannels | ADSL downstream bit rate |
| **F-5** | **Number of DMT subchannels:** $N = \dfrac{B_{\text{DSL}}}{\Delta f} = \dfrac{B_{\text{DSL}}}{4.3125 \text{ kHz}}$ | Total subchannels in the DSL band | ADSL: N ≈ 256 (downstream band only) |
| **F-6** | **DMT symbol rate:** $f_s = \dfrac{1}{T_s} = \dfrac{1}{\text{cyclic prefix} + \text{FFT duration}} \approx 4 \text{ kHz}$ | Symbol rate per subchannel | Derived from the 4.3125 kHz spacing |
| **F-7** | **Attenuation (dB/km) of 24-AWG copper at 1 MHz:** $A \approx 1.8\text{–}2.0 \text{ dB/km}$ | Empirical loss | Used in *reach* calculations |
| **F-8** | **Loop reach vs bit rate:** Higher bit rate ⇔ Shorter loop | Engineering trade-off | VDSL2 (100 Mbps) limited to ~300 m; ADSL (8 Mbps) works to 5 km |
| **F-9** | **Crosstalk NEXT/FEXT** | Interference between adjacent pairs in the same binder | Worst-case: 49 disturbers in ITU G.992.3 |
| **F-10** | **Bits per subchannel (typical range):** $b_i \in \{0, 1, 2, \ldots, 15\}$ | QAM-like constellation size per subchannel | 2^bᵢ points, so SNR requirement scales with bᵢ |

---

## 2.5 ADSL Family — Bit-Rate Specifications (KTU-Memorize)

| Feature | ADSL | ADSL2 | ADSL2+ | VDSL | VDSL2 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Standard** | ITU G.992.1 | ITU G.992.3 | ITU G.992.5 | ITU G.993.1 | ITU G.993.2 |
| **Downstream** | **6–8 Mbps** | **12 Mbps** | **24 Mbps** | **52 Mbps** | **100+ Mbps** |
| **Upstream** | **0.5–1 Mbps** | **1.5 Mbps** | **1.5 Mbps** | **16 Mbps** | **100+ Mbps** |
| **Bandwidth** | 1.104 MHz | 1.104 MHz | 2.208 MHz | 12 MHz | 30 MHz |
| **DMT subchannels** | 256 | 256 | 512 | 4096 (theoretical) | 4096 |
| **Modulation** | DMT | DMT | DMT | DMT/QAM | DMT |
| **Reach (max)** | ~5.5 km | ~5 km | ~3 km | ~1.5 km | ~300 m at max rate |
| **Year of Standard** | 1999 | 2002 | 2003 | 2004 | 2005 |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where xDSL is used in industry:**
> - **Last-mile broadband** for residential and SOHO customers.
> - **Leased-line replacement** for businesses (HDSL/SDSL historically).
> - **Backhaul for Wi-Fi hotspots** in remote areas.
> - **HDTV / IPTV** delivery (requires 6–25 Mbps sustained — exactly what ADSL2+ provides).
> - **VoIP** alongside POTS via the splitter.
>
> **Limitation in 2024+ context:** xDSL is increasingly being *replaced* by **FTTH (Fibre-to-the-Home)**, but in many KTU/Kerala deployments, *vectored VDSL2* remains a competitive copper-based broadband technology through 2025.

---

## 2.7 The Bit-Loading Concept (Number of Bits ↔ SNR)

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Rule-of-Thumb (derived from Shannon's formula):**
>
> For a subchannel of bandwidth $\Delta f$ to carry $b_i$ bits/symbol, the required SNR must satisfy approximately:
>
> $$\dfrac{S}{N} \ge 2^{b_i} - 1$$
>
> Therefore:
> - $b_i = 2$ bits → SNR ≥ 3 (≈ 4.8 dB)
> - $b_i = 4$ bits → SNR ≥ 15 (≈ 11.8 dB)
> - $b_i = 8$ bits → SNR ≥ 255 (≈ 24 dB)
> - $b_i = 12$ bits → SNR ≥ 4095 (≈ 36 dB)
>
> This explains why *low-frequency subchannels* (less attenuation, higher SNR) carry more bits, and *high-frequency subchannels* (more attenuation) carry fewer bits or are turned off.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Total Downstream Bit Rate of a Generic DMT-ADSL Link

**Problem (model KTU-style):** An ADSL link uses DMT modulation. The data band is 1.104 MHz wide, divided into subchannels of width **Δf = 4.3125 kHz**. The system uses 256 subchannels, of which the first 6 are reserved for voice/guard. The CO's DMT bit-loading algorithm assigns the following bits per symbol `b_i` to 20 representative subchannels (other subchannels follow the same pattern):

`b = [6, 8, 10, 10, 12, 12, 12, 10, 10, 8, 8, 8, 6, 6, 4, 4, 2, 2, 0, 0]` bits/symbol.

The DMT symbol duration is $T_s = 1/4000$ s (symbol rate = **4000 baud**). Compute the **total downstream bit rate**.

### Solution — step by step:

**Step 1 — Identify the total number of active subchannels.**

We assume the bit-loading vector above is representative of one of *N* subchannel blocks. For a standard ADSL downstream, **N = 256** subchannels are usable (after excluding POTS + guard band).

**Step 2 — Sum the bits per symbol in the sample block.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{\text{block}} &= \sum_{i=1}^{20} b_i \\
&= 6 + 8 + 10 + 10 + 12 + 12 + 12 + 10 + 10 + 8 \\
&\quad + 8 + 8 + 6 + 6 + 4 + 4 + 2 + 2 + 0 + 0 \\
&= 138 \;\text{bits per block of 20 subchannels per symbol}.
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert bits-per-block to bits-per-subchannel average.**

$$
\overline{b} = \frac{B_{\text{block}}}{20} = \frac{138}{20} = 6.9 \;\text{bits/subchannel/symbol}.
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the total downstream bit rate over all 256 subchannels.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{down}} &= N \cdot \overline{b} \cdot f_s \\
&= 256 \cdot 6.9 \cdot 4000 \\
&= 256 \cdot 27{,}600 \\
&= 7{,}065{,}600 \;\text{bits/s}.
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to Megabits per second.**

$$
R_{\text{down}} = \dfrac{7{,}065{,}600}{10^6} \approx 7.07 \;\text{Mbps}.
$$

**Conclusion:** The link delivers **≈ 7.07 Mbps downstream**, which lies within the typical ADSL specification of **6–8 Mbps downstream**. ✅

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note:** In KTU, the typical simplification is to assume the average $b_i$ is given (e.g., 8 bits per subchannel) and the symbol rate is 4000 baud. Then the formula collapses to $R = N \cdot b \cdot f_s$, which is *the most frequently tested expression* in 14-mark problems.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Shannon Capacity of a Single DMT Subchannel

**Problem:** A single 4.3125 kHz DMT subchannel is measured to have an SNR of 30 dB at the receiver. Compute:
1. The Shannon capacity of that subchannel.
2. The *maximum* number of bits per symbol that can be supported if the symbol rate is 4000 baud (assuming the channel is noiseless at that rate).

### Solution:

**Step 1 — Convert SNR from dB to linear.**

$$
\text{SNR}_{\text{linear}} = 10^{\,30/10} = 10^3 = 1000.
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Shannon–Hartley capacity formula (F-2).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_i &= \Delta f \cdot \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \\
&= 4312.5 \cdot \log_2(1 + 1000) \\
&= 4312.5 \cdot \log_2(1001).
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the logarithm.**

$$
\log_2(1001) = \dfrac{\ln(1001)}{\ln(2)} = \dfrac{6.908}{0.693} \approx 9.967 \;\text{bits/s/Hz}.
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the final capacity.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_i &\approx 4312.5 \cdot 9.967 \\
&\approx 42{,}983 \;\text{bits/s per subchannel} \\
&\approx 43 \;\text{kbps per subchannel.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the bits-per-symbol at 4000 baud (assuming noise-free within the symbol interval, just an upper-bound check).**

$$
b_{\max} = \frac{C_i}{f_s} = \frac{42{,}983}{4000} \approx 10.75 \;\text{bits/symbol}.
$$

**Step 6 — Integer round-down (DMT constraint).**

Since bits per subchannel must be a non-negative integer, the actual bit-loading is $b_i = 10$ bits/symbol. ✅

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight:** This shows why *DMT cannot exceed ~12 bits/symbol per subchannel* in practice — beyond that, the required SNR rises exponentially (e.g., 16-QAM needs ~24 dB, 4096-QAM needs ~36 dB, which the local loop rarely provides at 1 MHz).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Reach vs Bit-Rate Trade-off (Simplified Attenuation Model)

**Problem:** A 24-AWG copper local loop attenuates the DSL signal at **A = 2.0 dB/km** at the carrier frequency of 500 kHz. The transmit power at the CO is **0 dBm**, and the minimum receiver sensitivity is **−60 dBm**. Compute the **maximum loop reach**.

### Solution:

**Step 1 — Total allowed attenuation (link budget).**

$$
A_{\text{total}} = P_{\text{TX}} - P_{\text{RX, min}} = 0 - (-60) = 60 \;\text{dB}.
$$

**Step 2 — Divide by attenuation per km.**

$$
L_{\max} = \frac{A_{\text{total}}}{A_{\text{per km}}} = \frac{60}{2.0} = 30 \;\text{km}.
$$

**Step 3 — Reality check (KTU caveat).**

In real ADSL deployments, the *reach* is limited not only by attenuation but also by **crosstalk and SNR margin**. The ITU-T G.992.1 specifies a maximum reach of **5.5 km at 8 Mbps** because the *effective* SNR drops by ~3–4 dB/km after accounting for crosstalk and bridge-taps. So practical reach is approximately:

$$
L_{\text{practical}} \approx \dfrac{60 \text{ dB}}{10 \text{ dB/km (effective)}} \approx 6 \;\text{km}.
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Note:** This is why **VDSL2 (with its wider 30 MHz band) cannot reach far** — the attenuation at 30 MHz is enormous (≫ 30 dB/km), so VDSL2 is limited to ~300 m at maximum rate.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing xDSL Variants

| Parameter | ADSL | ADSL2 | ADSL2+ | HDSL | SDSL | VDSL | VDSL2 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Standard** | G.992.1 | G.992.3 | G.992.5 | G.991.1 | G.991.2 | G.993.1 | G.993.2 |
| **Down (Mbps)** | 6–8 | 12 | 24 | 1.544 / 2.048 (symmetric) | 1.5 (sym) | 52 | 100+ |
| **Up (Mbps)** | 0.5–1 | 1.5 | 1.5 | 1.544 / 2.048 (sym) | 1.5 (sym) | 16 | 100+ |
| **Mode** | Asym | Asym | Asym | Sym | Sym | Both | Both |
| **BW (MHz)** | 1.104 | 1.104 | 2.208 | 0.196 | 0.196 | 12 | 30 |
| **# of pairs** | 1 | 1 | 1 | 2–3 | 1 | 1 | 1 |
| **Modulation** | DMT | DMT | DMT | 2B1Q / CAP | 2B1Q | DMT/QAM | DMT |
| **POTS support** | Yes | Yes | Yes | No | No | Yes | Yes |
| **Reach (max)** | 5.5 km | 5 km | 3 km | 3.7 km | 3 km | 1.5 km | 0.3 km |

---

## 3.5 Step-by-Step Worked Example — How DSLAM Computes Initial Bit-Rate (KTU Classic)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Past Exam Pattern — Calculate bit rate from given SNR table.**

**Problem (14-mark style):** A 256-subchannel DMT system uses a symbol rate of 4 kbaud. SNR measurements for 5 subchannels are:

| Subchannel | Carrier f (kHz) | SNR (dB) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 30 | 40 |
| 2 | 70 | 35 |
| 3 | 200 | 28 |
| 4 | 500 | 22 |
| 5 | 1000 | 14 |

Using the approximation $b_i = \lfloor \log_2(1 + \text{SNR}_{\text{lin}}) \rfloor$ (Shannon optimal bit-loading), find:
1. The bits per symbol for each subchannel.
2. The total bit rate over these 5 subchannels.
3. The *extrapolated* total bit rate for all 256 subchannels, assuming the SNR decreases linearly by 26 dB across the band.

### Solution:

**Step 1 — Convert each SNR (dB) to linear.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SNR}_1 &= 10^{40/10} = 10{,}000; \quad \text{SNR}_2 = 10^{3.5} \approx 3162; \\
\text{SNR}_3 &= 10^{2.8} \approx 631; \quad \text{SNR}_4 = 10^{2.2} \approx 158.5; \\
\text{SNR}_5 &= 10^{1.4} \approx 25.1.
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute bits per subchannel using $b_i = \lfloor \log_2(1 + S/N) \rfloor$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
b_1 &= \lfloor \log_2(1 + 10{,}000) \rfloor = \lfloor 13.29 \rfloor = 13 \;\text{bits}. \\
b_2 &= \lfloor \log_2(1 + 3162) \rfloor = \lfloor 11.63 \rfloor = 11 \;\text{bits}. \\
b_3 &= \lfloor \log_2(1 + 631) \rfloor = \lfloor 9.30 \rfloor = 9 \;\text{bits}. \\
b_4 &= \lfloor \log_2(1 + 158.5) \rfloor = \lfloor 7.32 \rfloor = 7 \;\text{bits}. \\
b_5 &= \lfloor \log_2(1 + 25.1) \rfloor = \lfloor 4.69 \rfloor = 4 \;\text{bits}.
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Total bits per symbol over the 5 subchannels.**

$$
B_5 = 13 + 11 + 9 + 7 + 4 = 44 \;\text{bits/symbol}.
$$

**Step 4 — Bit rate over the 5 subchannels at 4000 baud.**

$$
R_5 = 44 \cdot 4000 = 176{,}000 \;\text{bps} = 176 \;\text{kbps}.
$$

**Step 5 — Extrapolate to 256 subchannels.**

The average bits per subchannel over the 5 measured = $44/5 = 8.8$. For 256 subchannels, total bits per symbol:

$$
B_{256} = 8.8 \cdot 256 = 2252.8 \approx 2253 \;\text{bits/symbol}.
$$

Total bit rate:

$$
R_{256} = 2253 \cdot 4000 = 9{,}012{,}000 \;\text{bps} \approx 9.01 \;\text{Mbps}.
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This extrapolated 9.01 Mbps is *very close* to the *theoretical* downstream limit of ADSL (≈ 8 Mbps), confirming the consistency of the model.

---

## 3.6 Important Engineering Notes

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Exam Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the +1 inside the Shannon log:** $\log_2(1 + S/N)$ — the **1** is *crucial* when SNR is small.
> 2. **Using V (number of levels) in Shannon formula:** Shannon is for *noisy channels* — it does **not** use V. Nyquist's formula uses V.
> 3. **Confusing ADSL with HDSL:** ADSL is **asymmetric**; HDSL/SDSL are **symmetric**.
> 4. **Confusing the DMT subchannel bandwidth:** It is **4.3125 kHz**, not 4 kHz exactly (the extra 0.3125 kHz is the cyclic prefix overhead).
> 5. **Treating bit-loading as a fixed value:** It is *dynamic* — assigned by the CO based on per-subchannel SNR.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — End-to-End xDSL System

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the complete functional path of data through a generic xDSL (ADSL) link, from the Internet backbone down to the customer PC. It illustrates the role of the **DSLAM, POTS splitter, CPE modem, and channel separation** clearly.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    %% Node IDs are alphanumeric, prefixed with letters to satisfy Mermaid rules.
    %% All labels are clean uppercase alphanumeric (no markdown bold/italics).

    subgraph CO["CENTRAL OFFICE EXCHANGE"]
        inetNode["INTERNET BACKBONE OR ISP CORE"]
        dslamNode["DSLAM DIGITAL SUBSCRIBER LINE ACCESS MULTIPLEXER"]
        splitterCO["POTS SPLITTER AT CO LOW PASS 0 4 KHZ HIGH PASS 26 KHZ TO 1.1 MHZ"]
        switchNode["PSTN VOICE SWITCH FOR LEGACY POTS"]
    end

    subgraph LOOP["LOCAL LOOP TWISTED PAIR COPPER 0 TO 5.5 KM"]
        cableNode["24 AWG TWISTED PAIR COPPER LOCAL LOOP SHARED WITH POTS USING FDM"]
    end

    subgraph CPE["CUSTOMER PREMISES"]
        splitterCPE["POTS SPLITTER AT CUSTOMER PREMISES LOW PASS TO PHONE HIGH PASS TO MODEM"]
        phoneNode["POTS TELEPHONE SET 0 TO 4 KHZ BASEBAND"]
        cpeNode["DSL MODEM CPE DEMODULATES DMT CARRIES DMT OVER 256 SUBCHANNELS OF 4.3125 KHZ"]
        pcNode["USER PC OR ROUTER ETHERNET WIFI OUTPUT"]
    end

    %% Directional flow
    inetNode -- "OC3 OR GIGABIT ETHERNET TO ISP" --> dslamNode
    dslamNode -- "DMT DOWNSTREAM 138 KHZ TO 1.1 MHZ" --> splitterCO
    splitterCO -- "POTS BAND 0 TO 4 KHZ" --> switchNode
    splitterCO -- "COMBINED POTS AND DSL SIGNAL OVER ONE COPPER PAIR" --> cableNode
    cableNode -- "DMT DOWNSTREAM AND POTS COMBINED" --> splitterCPE
    splitterCPE -- "POTS BAND 0 TO 4 KHZ" --> phoneNode
    splitterCPE -- "DMT DOWNSTREAM BAND ABOVE 26 KHZ" --> cpeNode
    cpeNode -- "ETHERNET OR USB OR WIFI" --> pcNode

    %% Upstream reverse path
    pcNode -. "USER UPLOAD DATA" .-> cpeNode
    cpeNode -. "DMT UPSTREAM 26 KHZ TO 138 KHZ" .-> splitterCPE
    splitterCPE -. "COMBINED POTS AND DSL" .-> cableNode
    cableNode -. "DMT UPSTREAM AND POTS" .-> splitterCO
    splitterCO -. "DMT UPSTREAM TO DSLAM" .-> dslamNode
    dslamNode -. "AGGREGATED TO ISP" .-> inetNode
```

```mermaid
    guardBand -- "BUFFER" --> guardLabel["22 KHZ OF EMPTY SPECTRUM"]

    downBand -- "USED FOR" --> downLabel["VIDEO WEB MUSIC DOWNLOADS LARGER BW"]
```

## 4.3 DMT Subchannel Bit-Loading Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    %% Shows adaptive bit loading: low-freq subchannels carry more bits, high-freq fewer.

    f0["SUBCARRIER 1 LOW FREQ HIGH SNR 12 BITS PER SYMBOL"]
    f1["SUBCARRIER 32 MEDIUM FREQ MEDIUM SNR 8 BITS PER SYMBOL"]
    f2["SUBCARRIER 128 MID FREQ LOWER SNR 4 BITS PER SYMBOL"]
    f3["SUBCARRIER 200 HIGH FREQ LOWER SNR 2 BITS PER SYMBOL"]
    f4["SUBCARRIER 256 HIGHEST FREQ WORST SNR 0 BITS TURNED OFF"]

    f0 -- "4.3125 KHZ SPACING" --> f1
    f1 -- "4.3125 KHZ SPACING" --> f2
    f2 -- "4.3125 KHZ SPACING" --> f3
    f3 -- "4.3125 KHZ SPACING" --> f4

    style f0 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    style f1 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#BDB76B,color:#000
    style f2 fill:#FFD580,stroke:#CD853F,color:#000
    style f3 fill:#FFB6B6,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
    style f4 fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#696969,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The green subchannel (low frequency) carries the most bits. As frequency increases, SNR drops, so fewer bits are loaded. The grey subchannel is disabled (0 bits) — this is the essence of **adaptive bit-loading** in DMT.

## 4.4 ADSL Protocol Stack (Layered View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    %% Layered reference model for ADSL (simplified, mirrors KTU Module 3 expectations).

    appLayer["APPLICATION LAYER HTTP SMTP FTP VIDEO STREAMING"]
    netLayer["NETWORK LAYER IP IPV4 IPV6"]
    dataLink["DATA LINK LAYER PPP OVER ETHERNET PPPOE RFC 2516 ATM LAYER FOR ADSL FORWARDING"]
    phyLayer["PHYSICAL LAYER ADSL USING DMT OVER COPPER TWISTED PAIR WITH POTS SPLITTER"]

    appLayer --> netLayer
    netLayer --> dataLink
    dataLink --> phyLayer

    style appLayer fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#483D8B,color:#000
    style netLayer fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B,color:#000
    style dataLink fill:#FFF8DC,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    style phyLayer fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Connection:** The PPPoE/ATM layer is a *frequently tested* concept — many students forget that the **link layer of ADSL is typically PPPoE (PPP over Ethernet)** encapsulated in **ATM cells** for transport over the DSLAM, though modern deployments use **PPPoE directly over Ethernet/IP (IP-DSLAM)**.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Part A questions are typically 3 marks, requiring a **definition + one-line application** style answer. Length: 3–5 lines maximum. Marks are awarded for *correctness of terminology*, not length.

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
**Define xDSL. Why is it called a "family" of technologies? Mention any three common variants.**

**Model Answer:**

xDSL (Digital Subscriber Line, with "x" indicating a family) is a set of physical-layer broadband technologies that deliver high-speed digital data transmission over the existing **twisted-pair copper local loops** of the PSTN, while *simultaneously* supporting legacy POTS voice calls through **Frequency Division Multiplexing (FDM)**.

It is called a *family* because multiple variants exist, each optimized for different bit-rate, symmetry, and reach requirements — for example:

1. **ADSL (Asymmetric DSL)** — high downstream, low upstream; for residential use.
2. **HDSL (High-bit-rate DSL)** — symmetric; for leased-line / enterprise use.
3. **VDSL / VDSL2 (Very-high-bit-rate DSL)** — very high bit rates; for short-loop FTTx applications.

(Each variant: 1 mark → 3 marks total.)

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**What is DMT in the context of ADSL? Why is it preferred over single-carrier QAM?**

**Model Answer:**

**DMT (Discrete Multitone)** is the multicarrier modulation scheme standardized for ADSL in **ANSI T1.413 / ITU-T G.992.1**. It divides the ADSL data band (~1.1 MHz) into **256 subchannels** of width **4.3125 kHz** each, with each subchannel carrying a QAM-like constellation whose size is dynamically chosen.

DMT is preferred over single-carrier QAM because:

1. It supports **adaptive bit-loading** per subchannel — high-SNR subchannels get more bits, low-SNR subchannels get fewer bits or are disabled.
2. It is **resilient to impulse noise** (long symbol duration per subchannel averages out short bursts).
3. It does not require a complex adaptive equalizer at the receiver — FFT handles the subchannel separation.

(Definition: 1 mark; each reason: ~1 mark → 3 marks total.)

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. You must answer **either** Choice A **or** Choice B. Each sub-part should be answered in **1–2 pages** with diagrams where applicable.

---

### **CHOICE A — Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **end-to-end architecture of an ADSL system** from the Central Office to the customer premises. Clearly label the **DSLAM, POTS splitter, local loop, CPE modem**, and the **frequency bands** used. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** The downstream band of an ADSL link (1.104 MHz) is divided into 256 subchannels of width 4.3125 kHz. A DMT symbol rate of 4 kbaud is used. The bit-loading algorithm at the CO assigns an average of **9 bits per subchannel per symbol** for 200 subchannels, **6 bits** for 40 subchannels, and disables the remaining 16 subchannels. Calculate:
- (i) Total bits per symbol
- (ii) Total downstream bit rate in Mbps
- (iii) Effective bits per subchannel (average) *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Answer — Choice A**

**(a) End-to-End ADSL Architecture (7 marks):**

The ADSL system connects the ISP backbone to the customer over the existing copper local loop. The key components are:

1. **Internet / ISP Backbone** — provides access to global networks.
2. **DSLAM (Digital Subscriber Line Access Multiplexer)** — located at the Central Office; aggregates thousands of customer DSL lines onto a single high-speed link to the ISP. It also performs the DMT modulation/demodulation at the network side.
3. **POTS Splitter at CO** — a passive low-pass + high-pass filter combination. The low-pass filter passes the **0–4 kHz POTS voice band** to the PSTN switch; the high-pass filter passes the **>26 kHz DSL data band** to the DSLAM.
4. **Local Loop (twisted-pair copper)** — a single 24-AWG copper pair, typically up to **5.5 km** long, carrying both voice and data via FDM.
5. **POTS Splitter at Customer Premises** — separates voice (→ telephone) from data (→ DSL modem).
6. **DSL Modem (CPE)** — demodulates the DMT signal, performs bit-deinterleaving, error correction, and outputs Ethernet/USB/Wi-Fi to the user's PC or router.

**Frequency Band Plan (for full marks, draw a bar/spectrum diagram):**
- **0 – 4 kHz:** POTS voice (bidirectional)
- **4 – 26 kHz:** Guard band (no signal)
- **26 – 138 kHz:** Upstream (user → CO)
- **138 kHz – 1.104 MHz:** Downstream (CO → user)

(Diagram: 3 marks; Component description: 2 marks; Frequency band explanation: 2 marks = 7 marks.)

---

**(b) Bit-Rate Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step (i) — Total bits per symbol:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{\text{total}} &= 200 \cdot 9 + 40 \cdot 6 + 16 \cdot 0 \\
&= 1800 + 240 + 0 \\
&= 2040 \;\text{bits per symbol}.
\end{aligned}
$$

(2 marks)

**Step (ii) — Total downstream bit rate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{down}} &= B_{\text{total}} \cdot f_s \\
&= 2040 \cdot 4000 \\
&= 8{,}160{,}000 \;\text{bps} \\
&= 8.16 \;\text{Mbps}.
\end{aligned}
$$

(2 marks)

**Step (iii) — Effective bits per subchannel (average over 256 subchannels):**

$$
\overline{b} = \frac{B_{\text{total}}}{256} = \frac{2040}{256} \approx 7.97 \;\text{bits/subchannel/symbol}.
$$

(1 mark)

**Conclusion:** The downstream bit rate is **8.16 Mbps**, well within the ADSL specification of 6–8 Mbps. The effective average bit-loading is approximately **8 bits per subchannel**. (2 marks for proper conclusion + units)

**Total: 7 marks.**

---

### **CHOICE B — Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Style, Module 3]**

**(a)** Explain **DMT (Discrete Multitone) modulation** in detail. Show how the **Shannon–Hartley capacity** is used to assign bits to each subchannel, and derive the **formula for total downstream bit rate** as a function of the bit-loading vector and the DMT symbol rate. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compare and contrast the following pairs, with reference to bit rate, reach, symmetry, and typical application: *(i)* ADSL vs. HDSL *(ii)* ADSL vs. VDSL2. Also, briefly explain why **VDSL2 cannot be deployed for long loops**. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Answer — Choice B**

**(a) DMT Modulation & Shannon-Based Bit Loading (7 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** DMT (Discrete Multitone) is a multicarrier modulation technique standardized for ADSL in **ANSI T1.413 / ITU-T G.992.1** that divides the available DSL data band into **N = 256 orthogonal subchannels** of equal width **Δf = 4.3125 kHz** each. Each subchannel `i` is centered at a carrier frequency $f_i = i \cdot \Delta f$ and carries an independent QAM-like constellation.

**Shannon–Hartley Capacity per Subchannel (2 marks):** The maximum bit rate achievable in subchannel `i` of bandwidth $\Delta f$ with measured SNR $(S/N)_i$ is given by:

$$
C_i = \Delta f \cdot \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S_i}{N_i}\right).
$$

The DSLAM, during the *initialization* phase, transmits a known pilot on each subchannel and measures the received SNR. It then assigns a **bit-loading factor** $b_i$ (a non-negative integer) such that:

$$
b_i = \left\lfloor \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S_i}{N_i}\right) \right\rfloor .
$$

**Total Bit Rate Formula (3 marks):** Let $N$ be the number of active subchannels, $b_i$ the bit-loading for subchannel `i`, and $f_s$ the DMT symbol rate (typically **4 kbaud**, derived from the 4.3125 kHz subchannel spacing minus the cyclic prefix). The total downstream bit rate is:

$$
R_{\text{down}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} b_i \cdot f_s.
$$

If all subchannels carry the same average bit-loading $\overline{b}$, this simplifies to $R_{\text{down}} \approx N \cdot \overline{b} \cdot f_s$.

**Worked Numerical Example (1 mark):** For $N = 256$, $\overline{b} = 9$ bits, $f_s = 4000$ baud:

$$
R_{\text{down}} = 256 \cdot 9 \cdot 4000 = 9.216 \;\text{Mbps}.
$$

---

**(b) Comparison Tables (7 marks):**

**Comparison (i) — ADSL vs HDSL:**

| Feature | ADSL | HDSL |
|---|---|---|
| Symmetry | Asymmetric | **Symmetric** |
| Downstream bit rate | 6–8 Mbps | 1.544 / 2.048 Mbps |
| Upstream bit rate | 0.5–1 Mbps | 1.544 / 2.048 Mbps |
| POTS support | Yes (via splitter) | **No** |
| Number of copper pairs | 1 | 2 (in original) or 1 (HDSL2) |
| Modulation | DMT | 2B1Q / CAP |
| Typical application | Residential broadband | Leased-line / enterprise T1/E1 replacement |
| Reach | 5.5 km | 3.7 km |

(1.5 marks)

**Comparison (ii) — ADSL vs VDSL2:**

| Feature | ADSL (G.992.1) | VDSL2 (G.993.2) |
|---|---|---|
| Max downstream | 8 Mbps | 100+ Mbps |
| Max upstream | 1 Mbps | 100+ Mbps |
| Bandwidth | 1.104 MHz | 30 MHz |
| Subchannels | 256 | up to 4096 |
| Reach at max rate | 5.5 km | 0.3–1 km |
| Typical application | Residential broadband | FTTx / IPTV / fiber-copper hybrid |

(1.5 marks)

**Why VDSL2 cannot be deployed for long loops (4 marks):**
The attenuation of a 24-AWG copper pair **increases with the square root of frequency** (skin-effect dominated) and approximately with the **length of the loop**. At the VDSL2 carrier frequencies (up to **30 MHz**), the attenuation coefficient is on the order of **15–30 dB/km**, compared with only **~2 dB/km at 1 MHz** for ADSL. Consequently:
- After **1 km**, a 30 MHz signal loses ~30 dB, dropping the SNR below the threshold needed for high-bit-rate QAM.
- The high-frequency subchannels (which carry the bulk of VDSL2's capacity) are disabled first, so the effective bit rate collapses to ADSL-like values beyond ~1.5 km.
- Therefore, VDSL2 is *practically limited* to **300 m at maximum bit rate (100+ Mbps)** and **~1.5 km at 50 Mbps**.

This is the fundamental **reach-bit-rate trade-off** in xDSL systems, governed by Shannon's capacity formula combined with the copper's frequency-dependent attenuation.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks in xDSL Questions (Valuator's Perspective):**
>
> 1. **❌ Forgetting the POTS splitter** in the architecture diagram. Marks are *specifically* allocated for naming the splitter and showing the FDM separation. *Loss: ~1.5 marks.*
> 2. **❌ Using wrong symbol rate.** Many students write 4000 baud; the correct value includes the cyclic prefix overhead — the **net** DMT symbol rate is 4 kbaud, the subchannel **spacing** is 4.3125 kHz. Do NOT confuse these.
> 3. **❌ Using Nyquist instead of Shannon** in a noisy-channel problem. The exam will explicitly mention noise/SNR; use **Shannon-Hartley**, not Nyquist.
> 4. **❌ Forgetting the "+1" inside the log.** Marks are deducted if $\log_2(S/N)$ is written instead of $\log_2(1+S/N)$.
> 5. **❌ Confusing ADSL, HDSL, SDSL, VDSL.** A "compare and contrast" question explicitly tests the *correct* pair of standards. Mixing up the standards = ~2 marks lost.
> 6. **❌ Not drawing the spectrum diagram** in the architecture question. KTU examiners *expect* a labelled frequency-spectrum bar showing POTS, guard, upstream, and downstream bands.
> 7. **❌ No units in the final answer.** Always write **Mbps** or **kbps** explicitly; writing "8.16" alone loses a mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU 2024 Revision Bullets for xDSL:**
>
> ✅ **xDSL = Digital Subscriber Line family** — uses *existing* copper twisted-pair local loops, supports POTS + data simultaneously via **FDM**.
>
> ✅ **Frequency Band Plan (ADSL):**
>   - **0 – 4 kHz:** POTS voice
>   - **4 – 26 kHz:** Guard band
>   - **26 – 138 kHz:** Upstream
>   - **138 kHz – 1.104 MHz:** Downstream
>
> ✅ **DMT (Discrete Multitone):** 256 subchannels × 4.3125 kHz each; symbol rate ≈ 4 kbaud; adaptive bit-loading.
>
> ✅ **Shannon–Hartley formula** $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ is the **single most important formula** for xDSL.
>
> ✅ **Bit loading:** $b_i = \lfloor \log_2(1 + \text{SNR}_i) \rfloor$; total rate = $\sum b_i \cdot f_s$.
>
> ✅ **ADSL = Asymmetric; HDSL/SDSL = Symmetric; VDSL/VDSL2 = Very-high-rate.**
>
> ✅ **Bit rates (memorize):** ADSL ≈ 8/1 Mbps, ADSL2+ ≈ 24/1.5 Mbps, VDSL2 ≈ 100+ Mbps.
>
> ✅ **DSLAM** is at the CO; **POTS Splitter** is at *both* ends; **DSL Modem (CPE)** is at the customer.
>
> ✅ **Reach-bit-rate trade-off:** Higher bit rate ⇔ Shorter loop. VDSL2 (30 MHz) limited to ~300 m at max rate.
>
> ✅ **Crosstalk (NEXT/FEXT)** is the dominant noise source in bundled copper pairs (49-disturber model).
>
> ✅ **Nyquist** → noiseless channel, uses V (signal levels). **Shannon** → noisy channel, uses S/N. **Never mix them.**
>
> ✅ **Modern PPPoE over Ethernet** (RFC 2516) is the *typical link-layer* for ADSL/VDSL deployments, replacing older ATM encapsulation in many IP-DSLAMs.
>
> ✅ **Standards (memorize):** G.992.1 (ADSL), G.992.3 (ADSL2), G.992.5 (ADSL2+), G.993.1 (VDSL), G.993.2 (VDSL2).
