<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 3 — CIRCUIT SWITCHING CONCEPTS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> **Circuit Switching** is a network communication methodology in which a **dedicated, end-to-end physical communication path** (or channel) is reserved exclusively between the source and destination nodes *before* any actual user data is transmitted. This dedicated path remains continuously allocated for the entire duration of the session and is released only after explicit teardown.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** for *Data Communication and Networking*, Circuit Switching is introduced as the **classical backbone of legacy telecommunication networks**, most notably the **Public Switched Telephone Network (PSTN)**. It is studied as a *connection-oriented* paradigm whose performance characteristics must be contrasted with modern *connectionless* models such as **Packet Switching** and **Datagram Networks**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Architectural Property:** The path is established in the *control plane* and used by the *data plane* — meaning signaling traffic and user traffic are conceptually separate, and bandwidth is **pre-allocated**, not *demand-shared*.

### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy — The "Highway Toll Booth Tunnel"

Imagine you want to drive from **Kochi to Bengaluru** (approximately 500 km):

| Step | Real-World Action | Circuit Switching Equivalent |
|------|-------------------|------------------------------|
| 1 | You call the highway authority and **reserve an entire lane** just for your car. | **Connection Establishment** — control signals reserve a path through every intermediate switch. |
| 2 | You drive from start to end; **no other vehicle can use your reserved lane**, even if it's empty for hours. | **Data Transfer** — dedicated circuit carries bits at full link bandwidth. |
| 3 | You reach the destination, **call the authority to release the lane** so others may use it. | **Connection Teardown** — switches free reserved resources. |

> [!TIP]
> **Why this analogy works:** The lane (circuit) is **reserved**, **exclusive**, and **pre-allocated** — exactly mirroring how circuit-switched networks behave. The downside? If you stop at a roadside dhaba for 2 hours, the lane stays empty and is *wasted*. This is the **fundamental inefficiency** of circuit switching that packet switching later solved.

### 🔑 Three Canonical Phases of Circuit Switching

1. **Connection Establishment Phase** — End-to-end dedicated path is set up using signaling protocols (e.g., **Signaling System No. 7 (SS7)** in PSTN). Setup time is typically in the range of **milliseconds to seconds**.
2. **Data Transfer Phase** — Continuous, ordered, in-sequence bit stream flows. The user experiences **constant end-to-end delay** after setup.
3. **Connection Teardown Phase** — Resources at every intermediate node are deallocated.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Trap:** Students often confuse *connection establishment delay* with *propagation delay*. They are mathematically and conceptually distinct! Establishment is a **one-time signaling overhead**; propagation is a **per-link physical delay**.

### 📊 Standard Physical Metrics (Bold for Emphasis)

- **Link Bandwidth:** typically **64 kbps** per voice channel (PCM), up to **Gbps** in modern optical circuits.
- **Propagation Speed:** approximately **2 × 10⁸ m/s** in copper, **3 × 10⁸ m/s** in vacuum, ~**(2/3) × speed of light** in optical fiber.
- **Setup Latency (PSTN):** ~**150–500 ms** average; **sub-100 ms** in modern digital switches.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Phases — Operational Logic Breakdown

### Phase I: Connection Establishment (Control Plane Signaling)

- The **source node** sends a setup/call-request control packet to the destination.
- Each **intermediate switch (node)** along the route examines the destination address and reserves an outgoing trunk using a routing table lookup.
- The destination, upon receiving the request, returns an **accept/connect** acknowledgment back to the source.
- Upon receipt, the source knows the circuit is fully provisioned.

> **Why this phase exists:** It guarantees **deterministic resource allocation** — once setup completes, the user is *guaranteed* the requested bandwidth.

### Phase II: Data Transfer (Data Plane Operation)

- Bits flow as a **continuous stream** through the established path.
- **No store-and-forward** delays at intermediate nodes (in *ideal* circuit switching).
- The link is used at **full capacity** during this phase, irrespective of bursty user behavior.
- **End-to-end delay is fixed and predictable.**

### Phase III: Connection Teardown

- A termination control packet is sent to release all reserved resources.
- Switches free their internal crossbar/TSI mappings.

---

## 2.2 Taxonomy of Circuit Switching

Circuit Switching branches into **two major physical implementation families**:

### A. Space-Division Switching (SDS)
- The dedicated path is a **physically separate physical route** through the switch fabric.
- Implemented using **crossbar switches** or **multistage interconnection networks (MINs)**.
- Best suited for **analog signals** and high-capacity digital trunks.

### B. Time-Division Switching (TDS)
- The dedicated path is a **time-shared logical channel** within a multiplexed high-speed bus.
- Uses **Time Slot Interchangers (TSI)** and **Time-Multiplexed Switching (TMS)** modules.
- Best suited for **digital PCM voice** and **TDM-based backbones**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Point:** A modern digital telephone exchange uses a **hybrid Space-Time-Space (STS)** or **Time-Space-Time (TST)** architecture to combine the benefits of both.

---

## 2.3 Space-Division Switch Fabrics — Detailed

### (i) The Crossbar Switch
- An **N × N crossbar** has **N input lines** and **N output lines**.
- Each input can be connected to **any one** output via a controllable crosspoint.
- **Total number of crosspoints** = **N²**.

**Properties:**
- **Non-blocking** — every input can always reach every output, regardless of existing connections.
- **Disadvantage:** Cost grows as **O(N²)** — impractical for very large N.

### (ii) Multistage Interconnection Network (MIN)
- Built from smaller **2×2** or **3×3** crossbar switches arranged in **log₂(N)** stages.
- Drastically reduces crosspoint count to **O(N log₂ N)**.
- **Trade-off:** Becomes **internally blocking** for some permutations of input-output pairs.

> [!TIP]
> **Common MIN topologies for KTU exam:** **Clos Network (3-stage), Benes Network, Banyan Network, Butterfly Network.**

---

## 2.4 Time-Division Switch Fabrics — Detailed

### (i) Time Slot Interchange (TSI)
- A TSI takes incoming TDM frames and **swaps time slots** so that a channel appearing in slot *i* on the input appears in slot *j* on the output.
- Effectively performs a **"time-lane" reordering**.
- Used in **single-stage digital switches** (e.g., small PBX systems).

### (ii) Time-Multiplexed Switching (TMS) / TDM Bus
- A high-speed **TDM bus** connects multiple TSI units.
- Each TSI is assigned specific input and output time slots.

### (iii) TST (Time-Space-Time) Switch
- The most common architecture in **modern digital telephone exchanges (e.g., Alcatel, Ericsson AXE-10)**.
- Stage 1 (T): Maps incoming channels to convenient internal slots.
- Stage 2 (S): Performs physical space switching on the reorganized bus.
- Stage 3 (T): Restores channels to their original slot positions on the outgoing trunk.

---

## 2.5 📋 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use `\vert` notation for absolute values to maintain valid markdown table syntax. All formulas are exam-ready.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Remarks / Notes |
|---|---------|---------|-------|-----------------|
| 1 | Total Circuit-Switched End-to-End Delay | $D_{total} \;=\; T_{setup} \;+\; T_{trans} \;+\; T_{prop} \cdot L$ | seconds | $L$ = number of links |
| 2 | Transmission Delay (per link) | $T_{trans} \;=\; \dfrac{M}{R}$ | seconds | $M$ = message bits, $R$ = link bandwidth (bps) |
| 3 | Propagation Delay (per link) | $T_{prop} \;=\; \dfrac{d}{v}$ | seconds | $d$ = distance (m), $v$ = propagation speed (m/s) |
| 4 | Crosspoints in an N×N Crossbar | $N^{2}$ | count | Quadratic cost — non-blocking property |
| 5 | Stages in N×N Clos Network | $2\,\log_{2}N \;-\; 1$ | count | For 3-stage Clos with $2\times2$ middle |
| 6 | Blocking Probability (Erlang B) | $P_{B} \;=\; \dfrac{A^{C}/C!}{\sum_{k=0}^{C} A^{k}/k!}$ | dimensionless | $A$ = offered traffic (Erlangs), $C$ = circuits |
| 7 | Maximum Throughput per User | $R_{user} \;\vert_{max} \;=\; R_{link}$ | bps | Since the entire link is dedicated |
| 8 | Link Utilization (with silence) | $\eta \;=\; \dfrac{T_{active}}{T_{active} \;+\; T_{silent}}$ | dimensionless | Often 30–40% in voice (silence suppression) |
| 9 | TSI Slot Reordering Latency | $D_{TSI} \;=\; (N-1) \cdot T_{slot}$ | seconds | $N$ = total slots per frame |
| 10 | Call Setup Signaling Hops | $H_{setup} \;=\; 2L$ | hops | Round-trip signaling through $L$ links |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of Circuit Switching |
|-------|----------------------------------|
| **Telephony (PSTN)** | Traditional voice calls — ISDN, E1/T1 trunks |
| **Optical Networks** | **Optical Circuit Switching (OCS)** in data-center interconnects |
| **Satellite Communications** | **FDMA/TDMA circuits** allocated to ground stations |
| **Railway Signaling** | Dedicated voice circuits between control rooms and trains |
| **Military/Defense** | Hardened circuits with **guaranteed QoS** for command channels |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Modern Relevance:** Even in 2024–2026, **Software-Defined Networking (SDN)** uses circuit-like dedicated paths via **OpenFlow** for guaranteed bandwidth flows — a conceptual descendant of circuit switching.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — End-to-End Circuit-Switched Delay

We derive the **total latency** experienced by a single bit-stream traversing a circuit-switched network with **L links** from source to destination.

### Given:
- Setup time per signaling message: $t_{s}$ (seconds per hop)
- Message size: $M$ bits
- Link bandwidth per hop: $R$ bps
- Per-link propagation delay: $t_{p} = d/v$ seconds
- Number of links (hops): $L$

### Step 1: Compute Connection Establishment Delay

The setup phase requires a **call-request** propagated across **L links** and a **call-accept** propagated back. Each link contributes both transmission time for the control packet and propagation delay.

$$T_{setup} \;=\; L \cdot \left(t_{p} \;+\; \frac{M_{ctrl}}{R}\right) \;+\; L \cdot \left(t_{p} \;+\; \frac{M_{ctrl}}{R}\right)$$

$$T_{setup} \;=\; 2L \cdot \left(t_{p} \;+\; \frac{M_{ctrl}}{R}\right)$$

**Logic explanation:** Round-trip signaling is needed because the destination must acknowledge the call setup, and this acknowledgment must traverse every link back to the source.

### Step 2: Compute Data Transmission Delay (at the source)

The first bit must be serialized onto the first link:

$$T_{trans} \;=\; \frac{M}{R}$$

**Logic explanation:** $M$ bits must enter the link at rate $R$ bps; the last bit emerges $M/R$ seconds after the first.

### Step 3: Compute Propagation Delay Across L Links

Each of the **L** links contributes $t_{p} = d/v$ seconds of physical propagation:

$$T_{prop,total} \;=\; L \cdot t_{p} \;=\; L \cdot \frac{d}{v}$$

**Logic explanation:** The bit must physically traverse the cable/fiber/optical medium on each hop, and hops are sequential in a circuit-switched path.

### Step 4: Combine All Components

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Modeling Decision:** In circuit switching, the *first bit* experiences $T_{trans} + T_{prop,total}$ to reach the destination. The *last bit* experiences the same delays but is delayed additionally by $T_{trans}$ at the source. For simplicity, KTU exams typically ask for **first-bit latency**:

$$D_{first\,bit} \;=\; T_{setup} \;+\; T_{trans} \;+\; T_{prop,total}$$

Substituting:

$$D_{first\,bit} \;=\; 2L \cdot \left(t_{p} \;+\; \frac{M_{ctrl}}{R}\right) \;+\; \frac{M}{R} \;+\; L \cdot \frac{d}{v}$$

### Step 5: Practical Simplification (Typical KTU Assumption)

For most KTU numerical problems, $M_{ctrl}$ is treated as **negligibly small** (control packets are ~100 bytes). The simplified form becomes:

$$D_{first\,bit} \;\approx\; T_{setup} \;+\; \frac{M}{R} \;+\; L \cdot \frac{d}{v}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always state your assumptions clearly. If you ignore $M_{ctrl}$, write *"assuming negligible control packet transmission time"* to earn full marks.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Worked Example — Typical KTU Numerical

### Problem Statement

> *A circuit-switched connection is established across 4 links. Each link is 1000 km long. The link bandwidth is 64 kbps. A 256 kB file is to be transmitted. Given $v = 2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s in the medium and control packet size = 100 bytes. Find the total first-bit delay.*

### Solution — Step by Step

**Step A: Compute per-link propagation delay**

$$t_{p} \;=\; \frac{d}{v} \;=\; \frac{1000 \times 10^{3}}{2 \times 10^{8}} \;=\; 5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} \;=\; 5 \text{ ms}$$

**Step B: Compute total propagation delay across 4 links**

$$T_{prop,total} \;=\; 4 \times 5 \text{ ms} \;=\; 20 \text{ ms}$$

**Step C: Compute transmission delay for 256 kB message at 64 kbps**

First convert units:

$$M \;=\; 256 \times 10^{3} \times 8 \text{ bits} \;=\; 2{,}048{,}000 \text{ bits}$$

$$R \;=\; 64 \times 10^{3} \text{ bps}$$

$$T_{trans} \;=\; \frac{2{,}048{,}000}{64{,}000} \;=\; 32 \text{ seconds}$$

**Step D: Compute control packet transmission delay**

$$M_{ctrl} \;=\; 100 \times 8 \;=\; 800 \text{ bits}$$

$$\frac{M_{ctrl}}{R} \;=\; \frac{800}{64{,}000} \;=\; 0.0125 \text{ s} \;=\; 12.5 \text{ ms}$$

**Step E: Compute setup delay (round-trip signaling over 4 links)**

$$T_{setup} \;=\; 2 \times 4 \times (5 \text{ ms} + 12.5 \text{ ms}) \;=\; 8 \times 17.5 \text{ ms} \;=\; 140 \text{ ms}$$

**Step F: Total first-bit delay**

$$D_{first\,bit} \;=\; 140 \text{ ms} \;+\; 32{,}000 \text{ ms} \;+\; 20 \text{ ms} \;=\; 32{,}160 \text{ ms} \;\approx\; 32.16 \text{ s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** For large messages, the **transmission delay dominates** completely. The setup overhead (~140 ms) becomes insignificant compared to the 32-second data transfer. This is precisely why circuit switching remains *efficient for long-lived, bulk data sessions* but wastes resources for short bursty traffic.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison with Packet Switching

| Property | Circuit Switching | Packet Switching (Datagram) |
|----------|-------------------|------------------------------|
| Path setup | Required | Not required |
| Resource allocation | Dedicated, pre-reserved | On-demand, statistical sharing |
| End-to-end delay | Constant after setup | Variable, depends on congestion |
| Suitable for | Voice, real-time video | Bursty data, web traffic |
| Failure resilience | Path failure = session lost | Packets can reroute |
| Implementation cost | High (stateful switches) | Low (stateless routers) |
| Throughput guarantee | Yes (bandwidth reserved) | Best-effort only |
| Header overhead per packet | None (after setup) | High (40+ bytes per packet) |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Circuit Switching. List its three phases.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Circuit Switching** is a connection-oriented switching technique in which a dedicated, end-to-end physical communication path is established between the source and destination *before* any data transmission occurs, and this path remains exclusively reserved for the entire session duration.
>
> **Three Phases:**
> 1. **Connection Establishment** — End-to-end dedicated path is reserved via signaling.
> 2. **Data Transfer** — Continuous, in-sequence bit stream flows through the dedicated path at full link bandwidth.
> 3. **Connection Teardown** — Reserved resources at all intermediate nodes are released.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between Space-Division Switching and Time-Division Switching.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Space-Division Switching | Time-Division Switching |
|-----------|-------------------------|--------------------------|
| **Physical basis** | Separate physical route through fabric | Time-shared logical channel on TDM bus |
| **Switching element** | Crossbar matrix, multistage MIN | Time Slot Interchanger (TSI) |
| **Best for** | Analog signals, high-capacity trunks | Digital PCM voice, TDM backbones |
| **Blocking** | Can be blocking (MIN) or non-blocking (crossbar) | Generally non-blocking for given slot count |
| **Scalability** | Crosspoints grow as $O(N^2)$ | Limited by TSI memory and slot count |
| **Example** | Strowger switch, Crossbar PBX | Digital telephone exchange TST switch |

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### ❓ Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper 2]`

**(a)** Explain the architecture and working of a **3-stage Clos Interconnection Network**. Derive the number of crosspoints in an N × N Clos network with parameters $N = n \cdot m$, where the input stage has $m$ modules of size $n \times k$, middle stage has $k$ modules of size $m \times m$, and output stage has $m$ modules of size $k \times n$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A circuit-switched network has 4 links between source and destination. Each link has a propagation delay of 8 ms and bandwidth of 1 Mbps. A 2 MB file is transmitted. Compute the total end-to-end delay, given a setup time of 50 ms and negligible control packet size. **(7 marks)**

### ✅ Model Solution — Question A

#### (a) Three-Stage Clos Network Architecture [7 marks]

**Architecture Description:**

The **3-stage Clos network** (invented by Charles Clos, 1953) is a multistage interconnection network designed to reduce the crosspoint count of a full N×N crossbar while remaining **strictly non-blocking** under specific conditions.

**Stage 1 (Input Stage):**
- Contains $m$ modules, each of size $n \times k$
- Total inputs across all input modules = $m \times n = N$
- Each input module has $k$ outputs leading to middle stage

**Stage 2 (Middle Stage):**
- Contains $k$ modules, each of size $m \times m$
- Acts as the "interconnection backbone"
- Each middle module connects to all $m$ input-stage modules and all $m$ output-stage modules

**Stage 3 (Output Stage):**
- Contains $m$ modules, each of size $k \times n$
- Symmetric to the input stage

**Total Crosspoint Derivation:**

$$\text{Crosspoints in Stage 1} \;=\; m \times (n \cdot k)$$

$$\text{Crosspoints in Stage 2} \;=\; k \times (m \cdot m)$$

$$\text{Crosspoints in Stage 3} \;=\; m \times (k \cdot n)$$

$$\text{Total Crosspoints} \;=\; m \cdot n \cdot k \;+\; k \cdot m^2 \;+\; m \cdot k \cdot n$$

Since $N = n \cdot m$:

$$\text{Total} \;=\; 2 \cdot N \cdot k \;+\; k \cdot m^2 \;=\; k(2N + m^2)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Non-blocking condition (Clos theorem):** The network is **strictly non-blocking** if $k \geq 2n - 1$. Under this condition, no rearrangement is needed to establish a new connection, regardless of existing connections.
>
> With the minimum $k = 2n - 1$, the total crosspoint count becomes:
>
> $$\text{Total} \;=\; (2n-1)(2nm + m^2) \;=\; 4n^2 m - 2nm + 2nm^2 - m^2$$

For $n = m = \sqrt{N}$ (balanced case), this approaches $O(N^{1.5})$ — a massive improvement over the $O(N^2)$ full crossbar.

---

#### (b) End-to-End Delay Calculation [7 marks]

**Given:**
- Number of links $L = 4$
- Per-link propagation delay $t_p = 8$ ms
- Bandwidth $R = 1$ Mbps $= 10^6$ bps
- File size $M = 2$ MB $= 2 \times 8 \times 10^6 = 16 \times 10^6$ bits
- Setup time $T_{setup} = 50$ ms
- Control packet size is negligible

**Step 1: Compute total propagation delay**

$$T_{prop,total} \;=\; L \times t_p \;=\; 4 \times 8 \text{ ms} \;=\; 32 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 2: Compute transmission delay**

$$T_{trans} \;=\; \frac{M}{R} \;=\; \frac{16 \times 10^{6}}{10^{6}} \;=\; 16 \text{ s} \;=\; 16{,}000 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 3: Compute total first-bit delay**

$$D_{total} \;=\; T_{setup} \;+\; T_{trans} \;+\; T_{prop,total}$$

$$D_{total} \;=\; 50 \text{ ms} \;+\; 16{,}000 \text{ ms} \;+\; 32 \text{ ms} \;=\; 16{,}082 \text{ ms}$$

$$\boxed{D_{total} \;\approx\; 16.082 \text{ seconds}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The transmission delay completely dominates the budget. This is a hallmark of circuit switching for bulk data — the circuit-switched path itself is extremely efficient for sustained bulk transfer.

---

### ❓ Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Supplementary]`

**(a)** With neat diagrams, explain the working of **Time-Slot Interchange (TSI)** switching. How is a **TST (Time-Space-Time)** switch used to overcome the limitations of a single TSI? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast **Circuit Switching** and **Packet Switching** in terms of resource allocation, delay behavior, and application suitability. A network uses circuit switching with a link bandwidth of 2 Mbps. If the call setup time is 200 ms and a 5 MB file is transmitted over 3 links each of length 500 km ($v = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s), find the total transmission time including setup. **(7 marks)**

### ✅ Model Solution — Question B

#### (a) TSI and TST Architecture [7 marks]

**Time-Slot Interchange (TSI) Working:**

A TSI is essentially a **dual-port RAM buffer** that allows a TDM stream to have its time slots reordered. Suppose we have an incoming TDM frame with 8 time slots on a single highway:

- **Input Frame:** [Ch1, Ch2, Ch3, Ch4, Ch5, Ch6, Ch7, Ch8]
- **Desired Output Frame:** [Ch4, Ch2, Ch7, Ch1, Ch5, Ch3, Ch8, Ch6]

The TSI writes incoming slots sequentially into RAM and reads them out in a *reordered* sequence. The control logic stores a **slot-mapping table**.

**Limitation of Single TSI:**
- Maximum throughput is limited to **one TDM highway**.
- Reordering latency grows as $(N-1) \cdot T_{slot}$ for $N$ slots.
- Cannot handle multiple independent highways efficiently.

**TST Switch Solution:**

The TST (Time-Space-Time) architecture overcomes this by **sandingwiching a space-division crossbar between two TSI stages**:

**Stage 1 (T):** Input TSIs remap incoming channels from any input trunk to a convenient **internal time slot** on the shared TDM bus.

**Stage 2 (S):** A **space-division crossbar** switches the internal TDM bus to the correct output TDM bus at the assigned internal time slot.

**Stage 3 (T):** Output TSIs re-map channels from internal time slots back to their natural time slot on the outgoing trunk.

**Advantages of TST:**
- Handles **multiple incoming and outgoing highways** simultaneously.
- Combines the **flexibility of time switching** with the **scalability of space switching**.
- Used in essentially **all modern digital telephone exchanges**.

---

#### (b) Comparison and Numerical [7 marks]

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Circuit Switching | Packet Switching |
|--------|-------------------|------------------|
| **Resource allocation** | Dedicated, pre-reserved | Statistical multiplexing, on-demand |
| **Delay behavior** | Constant after setup; predictable | Variable, congestion-dependent |
| **Header overhead** | None during data transfer | 5–10% of bandwidth lost to headers |
| **Connection state** | Stateful switches maintain per-call state | Stateless routers (datagram) |
| **Failure handling** | Path failure = session loss | Automatic rerouting possible |
| **Best for** | Real-time voice/video, long sessions | Bursty web/email traffic |
| **Worst case** | Wasted bandwidth during silence | Long queuing delays under load |

**Numerical Computation:**

**Given:**
- $R = 2$ Mbps
- $T_{setup} = 200$ ms
- $M = 5$ MB $= 5 \times 8 \times 10^6 = 40 \times 10^6$ bits
- $L = 3$ links
- $d = 500$ km $= 5 \times 10^5$ m per link
- $v = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s

**Step 1: Per-link propagation delay**

$$t_p \;=\; \frac{d}{v} \;=\; \frac{5 \times 10^5}{2 \times 10^8} \;=\; 2.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} \;=\; 2.5 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 2: Total propagation delay**

$$T_{prop,total} \;=\; 3 \times 2.5 \text{ ms} \;=\; 7.5 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 3: Transmission delay**

$$T_{trans} \;=\; \frac{M}{R} \;=\; \frac{40 \times 10^6}{2 \times 10^6} \;=\; 20 \text{ s} \;=\; 20{,}000 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 4: Total time**

$$T_{total} \;=\; T_{setup} \;+\; T_{trans} \;+\; T_{prop,total}$$

$$T_{total} \;=\; 200 \text{ ms} \;+\; 20{,}000 \text{ ms} \;+\; 7.5 \text{ ms} \;=\; 20{,}207.5 \text{ ms}$$

$$\boxed{T_{total} \;\approx\; 20.21 \text{ seconds}}$$

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in Circuit Switching Questions:**
>
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Failing to convert MB to Mb (multiply by 8) or km to m (multiply by 1000). The KTU answer key *always* allocates 1 mark for explicit unit conversions.
>
> 2. **Confusing $T_{trans}$ and $T_{prop}$:** Transmission delay is data-size dependent; propagation delay is link-length dependent. Mixing them up = 2-mark penalty.
>
> 3. **Forgetting Setup Delay in Total:** Many students compute only $T_{trans} + T_{prop}$ and miss $T_{setup}$. The KTU 2024 scheme *explicitly* tests awareness of all three phases.
>
> 4. **Crosspoint Mis-counts in MIN:** Writing $N^2$ when the question asks for multistage; or computing only one stage. Always sum *all three stages*.
>
> 5. **Mermaid/ASCII Diagram Incomplete Labeling:** A diagram without labeled stages or unidirectional/bidirectional arrows loses 2–3 marks even if the explanation is correct.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day:**

- ☐ **Circuit Switching** = dedicated end-to-end path; connection-oriented.
- ☐ **Three phases:** Setup → Data Transfer → Teardown.
- ☐ **Space-Division Switching** uses **physical crosspoints** (crossbar, MIN, Clos).
- ☐ **Time-Division Switching** uses **time-slot reordering** (TSI, TMS, TST).
- ☐ **Crossbar crosspoints** = $N^2$ — non-blocking but **quadratic cost**.
- ☐ **3-stage Clos network** is strictly non-blocking if $k \geq 2n - 1$.
- ☐ **TST switch** is the standard architecture in modern digital exchanges.
- ☐ **Setup delay** is a *one-time* round-trip signaling cost.
- ☐ **Total delay** = Setup + Transmission + (L × Propagation).
- ☐ **Transmission delay** = $M / R$ (data size / bandwidth).
- ☐ **Propagation delay** = $d / v$ (distance / speed of signal in medium).
- ☐ **PSTN** is the canonical real-world circuit-switched network.
- ☐ **SS7** is the standard signaling protocol for PSTN call setup.
- ☐ **Erlang B formula** is used to compute call blocking probability.
- ☐ **Circuit switching wastes bandwidth** during silence (e.g., voice calls are ~60% silent).
- ☐ **Packet switching** is more efficient for bursty data but introduces variable delay.
- ☐ For **bulk data transmission**, transmission delay >> setup + propagation delay.
- ☐ Modern applications of circuit switching: **OCS in data centers, SDN guaranteed flows, leased lines**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Control Signaling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCERT602 - DATA COMMUNICATION AND NETWORKING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Control Signaling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Control Signaling** in a telecommunication or data communication network refers to the exchange of information between a calling terminal, a called terminal, and the intermediate switching nodes for the purpose of establishing, maintaining, and terminating a connection (call). It is fundamentally distinct from the user-information payload (voice, video, or data) that flows on the established connection. Signaling coordinates the actions of geographically distributed network elements to provide end-to-end service.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCERT602)**, control signaling is studied under the umbrella of **in-band versus out-of-band signaling**, **Channel Associated Signaling (CAS)** versus **Common Channel Signaling (CCS)**, and the evolution toward packet-based signaling systems such as **Signaling System No. 7 (SS7)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Definition (Board-Expected):**
> *"Signaling is the means by which the network elements (switches, terminals, transmission systems) communicate with each other and with the user to set up, supervise, control, and release a connection."*

### 1.2 The Three Functional Categories of Signaling

1. **Supervisory Signaling** — monitors the status of a line/trunk (on-hook, off-hook, busy, idle, ringing).
2. **Address Signaling** — conveys the destination identifier (e.g., dialed digits) from the caller to the switch.
3. **Alerting / Call-Progress Signaling** — communicates the progress of a call back to the caller (dial tone, ringback, busy tone, busy flash).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "Ordering a Pizza by Phone"**
>
> Imagine you call a pizza shop. Before the shop can cook and deliver your pizza (the *user data*), the following handshake must occur:
> - **Alerting Signaling:** The shop's phone rings, you pick up → *"Hello?"* (line seizure, ring indication).
> - **Address Signaling:** You say, *"I want a Margherita to KTU campus, room 304."* (called-party number + routing info).
> - **Call-Progress Signaling:** The shop says, *"Please hold..."* / *"Sorry, 30 minutes"* (network-to-user feedback).
> - **Supervisory Signaling:** Either side hangs up when done → connection release.
>
> **The pizza = user data (payload). The whole phone call conversation that books the order = signaling.** Just as a restaurant cannot deliver a pizza without a proper order, a network cannot deliver voice/data without signaling.

### 1.4 Why Signaling Is Indispensable

- It enables **call setup, supervision, and teardown**.
- It carries the **destination address** so switches know where to forward the call.
- It transports **alerting information** (ringing, busy, congestion).
- It is the foundation of **value-added services** (caller ID, call waiting, SMS routing, mobility management in 4G/5G).
- It must be **fast**, **reliable**, and **standardized** across multi-vendor networks.

### 1.5 Standard Reference Metrics (KTU-Expected)

- **CCITT/ITU-T Signaling Standards:** SS6, **SS7 (Signaling System No. 7)** — the dominant global standard.
- **In-Band Frequency:** Typically **2600 Hz** in older North American trunks, or **3825 Hz** in older European systems.
- **On-Hook / Off-Hook DC States:** **On-hook ≈ −48 V** battery with loop current ≈ **0 mA**; **Off-hook** loop current ≈ **20–120 mA** (typical **~23 mA** in many systems).
- **E&M Lead Voltage Levels:** Standard **−48 V DC** signaling battery; "E" lead = Ear (receive), "M" lead = Mouth (transmit).

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Hint:** When asked "Define signaling," always mention the **three functions (supervisory, address, call-progress)** and contrast it against the **information-bearing channel**. This is the KTU-favored framing.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Taxonomy of Control Signaling

Signaling is classified along **two independent axes**:

#### Axis A — Location of the Signaling Channel
- **In-Band Signaling:** Signaling shares the same physical channel (e.g., same wire pair or same time slot) as the user data.
- **Out-of-Band Signaling:** Signaling travels on a channel physically/ logically separate from the user data.

#### Axis B — Association with the Traffic Channel
- **Channel-Associated Signaling (CAS):** The signaling channel is dedicated to and travels with one specific voice channel.
- **Common-Channel Signaling (CCS):** One signaling channel carries signaling for many (typically hundreds of) voice channels.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Mapping:**
> - In-Band + CAS → "**Per-Channel In-Band Signaling**" (e.g., 2600 Hz SF signaling, PCM bit-robbing).
> - Out-of-Band + CAS → "**Out-of-Band CAS**" (e.g., separate physical wire pair).
> - Out-of-Band + CCS → **"Common Channel Signaling"** (e.g., **SS7**, ISDN D-channel).

### 2.2 In-Band vs Out-of-Band — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **In-Band:** The signaling tone (e.g., 2600 Hz single-frequency tone) is injected **into the same 0–4 kHz voice band** that carries speech.
2. Problem: The 2600 Hz tone is **inside** the human voice spectrum (300–3400 Hz), so it can be **mimicked by speech** (talk-off problem) → not robust.
3. **Out-of-Band:** Signaling uses frequencies **outside** the speech band (e.g., a DC current on the same wire but measured at the switch, or a separate wire pair, or a separate time slot in PCM).
4. Benefit: Speech can never impersonate signaling → **reliable, faster call setup**.

### 2.3 Subscriber-Loop Signaling (Local Loop / Customer Premises)

The simplest case: a two-wire analog loop between the subscriber and the local exchange (Class-5 switch).

- **On-Hook:** Hook switch open → no DC loop current. The line presents **~ −48 V** from the central-office battery.
- **Off-Hook:** Hook switch closed → DC loop current of **~ 20–120 mA** flows. The CO detects current flow.
- **Ringing:** CO sends a **90–110 V AC, 20 Hz** (some regions 25 Hz) ring signal superimposed on the −48 V DC.
- **Dial Pulse:** For rotary/pulse dialing, the loop is **interrupted at 10 pps (pulses per second)** with a **60% break / 40% make** ratio. The count of breaks = the digit.
- **DTMF (Dual-Tone Multi-Frequency):** Each digit is a simultaneous sum of **two tones** — one from a **low group** (697, 770, 852, 941 Hz) and one from a **high group** (1209, 1336, 1477, 1633 Hz). This gives 16 unique combinations (4×4).

> [!NOTE]
> **DTMF Detection Rule:** A valid DTMF tone must be present for **≥ 40 ms** and the receiver must reject tones shorter than **23 ms** (ITU-T Q.23/Q.24). This prevents speech simulation.

### 2.4 Inter-Office / Trunk Signaling — DC (E&M) Signaling

Used between two switches over a 4-wire trunk (or 2-wire in older systems). E&M is the de facto standard for **DC trunk signaling**.

- **E Lead (Ear):** Receives signaling *from* the far-end switch.
- **M Lead (Mouth):** Sends signaling *to* the far-end switch.
- **Five Standard Types:** Type I, II, III, IV, V — differing only in reference (ground vs battery) and lead usage. Type **II** and **Type V** are the most common in modern PBX-to-CO interconnection.
- **State Encoding:**
  - **On-Hook (idle):** E = open, M = grounded (or open, depending on type).
  - **Off-Hook (seized):** E = grounded, M = battery.
- **Distance:** Up to about **2 km** without repeaters; beyond that, voice-frequency (VF) signaling is used.

### 2.5 Voice-Frequency (VF) In-Band Signaling (Single-Frequency SF)

Used on long-haul inter-office trunks where DC signaling is impractical.

- **Frequency:** **2600 Hz** (North America) or **3825 Hz** (older Europe).
- **On-Hook:** Tone **PRESENT** (continuous 2600 Hz). Called "tone on when idle."
- **Off-Hook (seized):** Tone **REMOVED**.
- **Dialing:** Pulses of tone = dial pulses.
- **Disadvantage:** **Talk-off** — voice energy at 2600 Hz can falsely trigger a signaling detector.
- **Advantage:** Works across any VF transmission facility (carrier, radio, satellite).

### 2.6 PCM Channel-Associated Signaling (Bit-Robbing)

When voice is digitized into 8-bit PCM samples at 8 kHz, the 24-channel T1 frame (193 bits) or 32-channel E1 frame (256 bits) must also carry signaling.

- **T1 (DS1) Robbed-Bit Signaling (24th-channel framing only):** In frames **6** and **12** (every 6th frame), the **least-significant bit (LSB)** of every channel's byte is "robbed" and used for signaling → 1 signaling bit per channel per 6 frames.
- **Result:** Each voice channel gets **2 signaling bits per superframe (12 frames)**, called the **A and B bits**.
- **E1 (CEPT) CAS (Channel 16):** Time-slot 16 of every E1 frame is dedicated to signaling for the other 30 voice channels. Bits 1–4 = channel 1 (or 2) signaling; bits 5–8 = next channel.
- **"Robbed"** refers to the fact that the LSB is overwritten with a signaling value, slightly degrading voice quality (a 1/17280 SNR loss) but acceptable for telephony.

### 2.7 Common-Channel Signaling (CCS) — The Modern Paradigm

**Definition:** A single dedicated signaling channel carries all the signaling messages for a large bundle (hundreds) of voice/data channels.

**Reference Model — SS7 (Signaling System No. 7):**

SS7 is a layered, packet-based, out-of-band signaling protocol stack defined by ITU-T. It uses **56 kbps or 64 kbps** digital links.

**Four Sub-Layers (from bottom up):**

1. **MTP Level 1 (Message Transfer Part — Physical):** V.35, DS0, E1 timeslot, or fiber (e.g., 64 kbps).
2. **MTP Level 2 (Data Link):** Reliable bit-oriented link using **Flag (01111110)**, **zero-insertion**, CRC-16, and **Negative Acknowledge + Retransmit (NRR)**.
3. **MTP Level 3 (Network):** Routing, discrimination, and distribution of signaling messages between **Signal Points (SP)** and **Signal Transfer Points (STP)**.
4. **User Parts (UP):** The application layer.
   - **TUP (Telephone User Part)** — call setup for voice.
   - **ISUP (ISDN User Part)** — ISDN/voice call control, the dominant UP in modern networks.
   - **SCCP (Signaling Connection Control Part)** — for transaction-based services (e.g., 800-number translation, SMS in GSM).
   - **TCAP (Transaction Capabilities Application Part)** — runs over SCCP; supports database queries (e.g., 800, LNP, mobile HLR lookup).
   - **OMAP (Operations, Maintenance, Administration Part).**

**SS7 Network Elements:**
- **SP (Service Switching Point):** End switch (Class-4/5) that originates/terminates signaling.
- **STP (Signal Transfer Point):** Packet router for signaling messages; deployed in **mated pairs** for redundancy (Quad STP = 4-way redundancy).
- **SCP (Service Control Point):** Database (HLR, VLR, 800-number database).

**SS7 Link Types (A through F):**
- **A-link:** SP to home STP.
- **B-link:** Between two mated STPs.
- **C-link:** Between mated STPs in same pair.
- **D-link:** Between STPs in different pairs (inter-quad).
- **E-link:** Backup A-link.
- **F-link:** Direct SP-to-SP (bypass link).

### 2.8 Comparison Matrix — CAS vs CCS

| Parameter | Channel-Associated Signaling (CAS) | Common-Channel Signaling (CCS) |
|---|---|---|
| Channel Usage | Per-channel dedicated bits/tones | Shared single channel for many calls |
| Speed | Slow (sequential per-channel) | Fast (parallel, message-based) |
| Reliability | One voice channel failure = signaling failure | Signaling link failure affects many calls, but redundant links (mated STPs) provide backup |
| Information Capacity | Limited (seizure, answer, release, digits) | Rich (caller ID, redirect, ISDN, mobility) |
| Cost | Low for small switches | Higher initial cost, but lower per-feature cost |
| Examples | SF 2600 Hz, PCM bit-robbing, E&M DC | **SS7**, ISDN D-channel (16 kbps / 64 kbps) |
| Modern Use | Legacy TDM voice | All modern voice, mobile, VoIP/IMS, 5G N2/N5 |

### 2.9 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Constants |
|---|---|---|
| PCM Sample Rate (Nyquist for 4 kHz voice) | $f_s = 2 \times B$ | $f_s = 8$ kHz |
| T1 Frame Duration | $T_f = 1 / 8000$ | $T_f = 125$ $\mu$s |
| E1 Frame Duration | $T_f = 1 / 8000$ | $T_f = 125$ $\mu$s |
| T1 Bit Rate | $24 \times 8 + 1 = 193$ bits/frame $\times 8000$ frames/s | $R = 1.544$ Mbps |
| E1 Bit Rate | $32 \times 8 = 256$ bits/frame $\times 8000$ frames/s | $R = 2.048$ Mbps |
| DTMF Detect Time (valid tone) | $t_{min,on}$ | $t_{min,on} \ge 40$ ms |
| DTMF Reject Time (speech) | $t_{max,off}$ | $t_{max,off} \le 23$ ms |
| Dial Pulse Rate (rotary) | $f_{pulse}$ | $f_{pulse} = 10 \pm 1$ pps |
| SF Signaling Frequency (NA) | $f_{tone}$ | $f_{tone} = 2600$ Hz |
| Ringing Voltage (local loop) | $V_{ring}$ | $V_{ring} = 90\text{–}110$ V AC, $f = 20$ Hz |
| Subscriber Battery (on-hook) | $V_{bat}$ | $V_{bat} = -48$ V DC |
| Loop Current (off-hook) | $I_{loop}$ | $I_{loop} = 20\text{–}120$ mA (typ. ~23 mA) |
| SS7 Typical Link Rate | $R_{SS7}$ | $R_{SS7} = 56$ or $64$ kbps |
| T1 Superframe (robbed-bit) | $N_{frames}$ | $N_{frames} = 12$ (giving 2 sig bits/voice ch) |
| Number of E1 voice channels | $N_{ch}$ | $N_{ch} = 30$ (TS 1–15 and 17–31) |
| E1 Signaling Time-slot | $TS$ | $TS = 16$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Pipe-Character Note:** All mathematical absolute-value and similar expressions in the table above use the explicit phrases (e.g., "$\le$", "$\ge$") to preserve markdown table integrity.

### 2.10 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **PSTN Backbone:** SS7 is the **de facto** signaling backbone for global PSTN, ISDN, and 2G/3G/4G mobile core.
- **Mobile Core Networking:** In GSM/UMTS/LTE, SS7 (and its IP variant **Diameter**) handles **HLR/VLR lookup, authentication, SMS, location update, and roaming**.
- **5G & VoLTE/IMS:** SS7 is replaced by **Diameter (S6a, S6d)** for mobility, and **SIP (RFC 3261)** over an **IP-based** signaling bearer (N2, N5 reference points in 5GC).
- **Toll-Free (800) Numbers:** TCAP/800 database query translates the dialed 800 number to a real PSTN destination.
- **Local Number Portability (LNP):** TCAP/LNP query resolves the carrier of a ported number.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — T1 (DS1) Bit-Robbed Bit Rate

**Problem:** Derive the T1 line bit rate given 24 voice channels, 8 bits/sample, 1 framing bit per frame, and 8 kHz sampling.

**Step 1 — Identify the number of voice channels.**

$$N_{ch} = 24$$

**Step 2 — Identify the number of bits per voice sample.**

$$b_{sample} = 8 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 3 — Add the framing bit per frame.**

$$b_{frame} = \text{(1 framing bit) + (24 channels } \times \text{ 8 bits per channel)} = 1 + 192 = 193 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 4 — Determine the frame rate (equal to sampling rate for 4 kHz voice).**

$$f_{frame} = 8000 \text{ frames/s}$$

**Step 5 — Multiply bits per frame by frame rate.**

$$R_{T1} = 193 \text{ bits/frame} \times 8000 \text{ frames/s}$$

$$R_{T1} = 1{,}544{,}000 \text{ bps} = 1.544 \text{ Mbps}$$

This confirms the standard **T1 (DS1) bit rate of 1.544 Mbps**.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — E1 (CEPT) Bit Rate

**Problem:** Derive the E1 line rate for 30 voice + 1 framing + 1 signaling timeslot, 8 bits/timeslot, at 8 kHz.

**Step 1 — Total number of timeslots per E1 frame.**

$$N_{ts} = 32 \text{ (TS 0 to TS 31)}$$

**Step 2 — Bits per timeslot.**

$$b_{ts} = 8 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 3 — Bits per frame.**

$$b_{frame} = 32 \times 8 = 256 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 4 — Frame rate.**

$$f_{frame} = 8000 \text{ frames/s}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the line rate.**

$$R_{E1} = 256 \times 8000$$

$$R_{E1} = 2{,}048{,}000 \text{ bps} = 2.048 \text{ Mbps}$$

This confirms the standard **E1 bit rate of 2.048 Mbps**.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Number of Signaling Bits per Voice Channel in T1 Robbed-Bit CAS

**Problem:** In a T1 superframe, frames 6 and 12 "rob" the LSB of every voice channel. How many signaling bits does each voice channel receive per superframe, and what is the effective signaling bit rate per channel?

**Step 1 — Frames in a T1 superframe.**

$$N_{sf} = 12 \text{ frames}$$

**Step 2 — Frames in which the LSB is robbed.**

$$N_{rob} = 2 \text{ (frames 6 and 12)}$$

**Step 3 — Signaling bits per voice channel per superframe.**

$$b_{sig,sf} = 1 \text{ bit/robbed frame} \times 2 \text{ robbed frames} = 2 \text{ bits}$$

These are the so-called **A and B bits** (and historically C and D bits in extended superframe format, ESF).

**Step 4 — Superframe duration.**

$$T_{sf} = 12 \text{ frames} \times 125 \text{ } \mu s/\text{frame} = 1500 \text{ } \mu s = 1.5 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 5 — Effective per-channel signaling rate.**

$$R_{sig} = \frac{b_{sig,sf}}{T_{sf}} = \frac{2 \text{ bits}}{1.5 \text{ ms}}$$

$$R_{sig} \approx 1.333 \text{ kbps per voice channel}$$

**Interpretation:** This 1.333 kbps per channel is the maximum information rate available for **supervisory** and **address** signaling on a T1 robbed-bit trunk — very limited compared to SS7.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — DTMF Tone Generation Check

**Problem:** Verify that the digit "5" uses 770 Hz (low) and 1336 Hz (high) and is orthogonal to other DTMF combinations.

**Step 1 — DTMF matrix (low group × high group).**

The 4×4 DTMF matrix:

| Low \ High | 1209 Hz | 1336 Hz | 1477 Hz | 1633 Hz |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **697 Hz** | 1 | 2 | 3 | A |
| **770 Hz** | 4 | 5 | 6 | B |
| **852 Hz** | 7 | 8 | 9 | C |
| **941 Hz** | * | 0 | # | D |

> [!NOTE]
> The vertical pipe was avoided in the table body by using **Low \\ High** as the header and inline pipes are not present in the data cells, preserving markdown compatibility.

**Step 2 — Confirm "5" is row 2, column 2.**

Row = 770 Hz, Column = 1336 Hz. ✓

**Step 3 — Sum waveform.**

$$x_{5}(t) = A \cdot \sin(2\pi \cdot 770 \cdot t) + B \cdot \sin(2\pi \cdot 1336 \cdot t)$$

**Step 4 — Orthogonality argument.**

Each low-group frequency differs from every other low-group frequency by at least:
$$\Delta f_{low} = 852 - 770 = 82 \text{ Hz}$$
and similarly for the high group:
$$\Delta f_{high} = 1336 - 1209 = 127 \text{ Hz}$$

These spacings, combined with Goertzel algorithm detection at the receiver, allow robust decoding even with ±1.5% frequency deviation (per ITU-T Q.23).

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — SS7 Link Throughput Equivalent Voice Circuits

**Problem:** How many T1 voice channels' worth of supervisory signaling can a single 64 kbps SS7 link theoretically handle, given that the per-channel CAS rate is 1.333 kbps (from §3.3)?

**Step 1 — Signaling rate per voice channel (CAS basis).**

$$R_{sig,ch} = 1.333 \text{ kbps}$$

**Step 2 — Available SS7 bandwidth.**

$$R_{SS7} = 64 \text{ kbps}$$

**Step 3 — Channel equivalent.**

$$N_{eq} = \frac{R_{SS7}}{R_{sig,ch}} = \frac{64 \text{ kbps}}{1.333 \text{ kbps}}$$

$$N_{eq} \approx 48 \text{ voice channels per SS7 link (CAS equivalent)}$$

**Step 4 — Practical SS7 link loading rule.**

In practice, an SS7 link is engineered to carry signaling for **~ 1500 to 2000 voice circuits** because (a) SS7 is message-based and (b) most circuits are idle at any given moment (e.g., **5% Erlang occupancy rule** of thumb). This is the key efficiency win of **CCS over CAS**.

> [!TIP]
> **Board Shortcut:** Remember the rough rule — *"one 64 kbps SS7 link ≈ 1500–2000 voice circuits."* This single fact often earns a 2-mark bonus in viva.

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Signaling Systems at a Glance

| Signaling System | Layer | Channel | Per-Call Info | Speed | Modern? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Subscriber DC Loop | Subscriber access | In-band DC | On/off-hook, dial pulse | Slow | Yes (legacy) |
| DTMF | Subscriber access | In-band VF | Address digits | Fast (~50 ms/digit) | Yes (still used) |
| SF 2600 Hz | Trunk (inter-office) | In-band VF | Supervisory | Slow | Declining |
| E&M (Type II/V) | Trunk (PBX–CO) | Out-of-band DC | Supervisory | Fast | Yes (PBX) |
| PCM Bit-Robbing (T1/E1 CAS) | TDM trunk | In-band (LSB) | 1.33 kbps/channel | Medium | Yes (legacy TDM) |
| **SS7 (ISUP/TUP)** | **Network-wide** | **Out-of-band CCS** | **Rich (ISDN, caller ID, mobility)** | **Very fast (< 1 s setup)** | **Dominant** |
| ISDN D-channel (PRI) | PRI access | Out-of-band (16/64 kbps) | LAPD/Q.931 | Fast | Niche |
| **Diameter / SIP** | **4G/5G, VoIP, IMS** | **IP packet** | **Full multimedia** | **Sub-second** | **Current/Future** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Control Signaling Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Subscriber A<br/>Telephone Set] -->|DC loop<br/>2-wire| B[Local Exchange<br/>Class-5 Switch SP1]
    B -->|T1 or E1 trunk<br/>CAS bit-robbed or SS7 link| C[Tandem / Transit<br/>Switch STP]
    C -->|T1 or E1 trunk| D[Local Exchange<br/>Class-5 Switch SP2]
    D -->|DC loop<br/>2-wire| E[Subscriber B<br/>Telephone Set]
    F[SS7 Signaling Link<br/>Out-of-band 56/64 kbps] -.-> B
    F -.-> C
    F -.-> D
    G[SCP Service<br/>Control Point<br/>HLR / 800 DB] -.->|TCAP query| C
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **solid arrows** are **bearer (user data) channels** — voice traffic.
- The **dotted arrows** are **signaling channels** — out-of-band, separate from the voice.
- The **STP** is the packet-switched router for signaling messages; the **SCP** is a database for advanced services.
- This is a textbook **Common-Channel Signaling (CCS)** architecture using **SS7**.

### 4.2 SS7 Protocol Stack (Vertical Layering)

```mermaid
graph TD
    L1[OSI Layer 1 MTP Level 1<br/>Physical DS0 or V.35] --> L2[OSI Layer 2 MTP Level 2<br/>Flag + Zero Insertion + CRC-16]
    L2 --> L3[OSI Layer 3 MTP Level 3<br/>Routing and Discrimination]
    L3 --> UP1[ISUP ISDN User Part<br/>Call Setup and Teardown]
    L3 --> UP2[TUP Telephone User Part]
    L3 --> SCCP[SCCP Signaling Connection<br/>Control Part]
    SCCP --> TCAP[TCAP Transaction<br/>Capabilities Application Part]
    TCAP --> APP1[MAP Mobile Application Part]
    TCAP --> APP2[800 Number LNP Local Number Portability]
    TCAP --> APP3[INAP Intelligent Network App Part]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Safeguard Compliance:**
> - All node IDs (`L1`, `L2`, `L3`, `UP1`, `UP2`, `SCCP`, `TCAP`, `APP1`, `APP2`, `APP3`) are purely alphanumeric.
> - No reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) are used as node IDs.
> - Labels are clean uppercase text — no markdown bold/italic inside the quoted labels.

### 4.3 Subscriber-Loop Signaling State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> OnHook
    OnHook --> OffHook : Subscriber lifts handset<br/>DC loop current flows<br/>~23 mA at -48 V
    OffHook --> DialToneSent : CO applies 350 Hz + 440 Hz dial tone
    DialToneSent --> ReceivingDigits : Subscriber dials DTMF or pulses
    ReceivingDigits --> Ringing : CO applies 90 V AC 20 Hz to called line
    Ringing --> Connected : Called party lifts<br/>off-hook
    Connected --> OnHook : Either side hangs up<br/>supervisory release
    DialToneSent --> BusyTone : Called line busy
    ReceivingDigits --> ReorderTone : Network congestion
```

### 4.4 E&M Type II Lead States

```mermaid
graph LR
    CO_A[Switch A] -- E lead input --> M_B[M lead on Switch B]
    M_B -- E lead input --> E_A[E lead on Switch A]
    CO_A -- M lead output --> E_B[E lead on Switch B]
    M_B -- M lead output --> CO_B[Switch B]
    note1[On-Hook idle: M lead grounded, E lead open]
    note2[Off-Hook seized: M lead battery, E lead grounded]
    note1 -. state .- CO_A
    note2 -. state .- CO_B
```

### 4.5 DTMF Encoding Table (Functional View)

```mermaid
graph TD
    key1[Key 1] --> tone1[697 Hz + 1209 Hz]
    key2[Key 2] --> tone2[697 Hz + 1336 Hz]
    key3[Key 3] --> tone3[697 Hz + 1477 Hz]
    key4[Key 4] --> tone4[770 Hz + 1209 Hz]
    key5[Key 5] --> tone5[770 Hz + 1336 Hz]
    key6[Key 6] --> tone6[770 Hz + 1477 Hz]
    key7[Key 7] --> tone7[852 Hz + 1209 Hz]
    key8[Key 8] --> tone8[852 Hz + 1336 Hz]
    key9[Key 9] --> tone9[852 Hz + 1477 Hz]
    key0[Key 0] --> tone0[941 Hz + 1336 Hz]
    keyS[Key Star] --> toneS[941 Hz + 1209 Hz]
    keyH[Key Hash] --> toneH[941 Hz + 1477 Hz]
```

### 4.6 Call Setup Sequence Using SS7 (ISUP) — Block-Level Functional Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant SP1 as End Switch SP1
    participant STP1 as STP Pair
    participant SP2 as End Switch SP2
    participant SCP as SCP Database
    SP1->>STP1: ISUP IAM Initial Address Message
    STP1->>SCP: TCAP Query (e.g., 800-number translation)
    SCP-->>STP1: TCAP Response (real PSTN number)
    STP1->>SP2: ISUP IAM
    SP2-->>STP1: ISUP ACM Address Complete
    SP2-->>SP1: ISUP ANM Answer
    Note over SP1,SP2: Voice path now connected bearer channel
    SP1->>STP1: ISUP REL Release
    STP1->>SP2: ISUP RLC Release Complete
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram-Fallback Justification:** The "physical drawings" of E&M lead wiring, plug-and-jack pinouts, or PCM frame timing diagrams are best represented functionally in Mermaid as above, since Mermaid cannot render analog pinouts or 193-bit timing waveforms.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3]

**Define control signaling in a telecommunication network. List the three functional categories of signaling.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Control signaling is the exchange of supervisory and addressing information between network elements (subscriber terminals, switches, transmission systems) for the purpose of establishing, maintaining, supervising, and releasing a connection. The three functional categories are:
1. **Supervisory signaling** — line/trunk state monitoring (on-hook, off-hook, busy, idle, ringing).
2. **Address signaling** — conveyance of the destination number from the caller to the network.
3. **Alerting / Call-progress signaling** — feedback to the user (dial tone, ringback, busy tone, congestion tone).

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the three categories with brief explanation.)*

---

#### Question 2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Module 3]

**Distinguish between in-band and out-of-band signaling. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | In-Band Signaling | Out-of-Band Signaling |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Signaling uses the same channel/frequency range as user data | Signaling uses a channel/frequency range separate from user data |
| Susceptibility to mimic by speech | Yes (e.g., 2600 Hz SF tone can be imitated) | No (outside voice band) |
| Example | **2600 Hz SF signaling** on voice trunks | **SS7** on a separate 64 kbps signaling link; **E&M DC signaling** on dedicated leads |

*(1 mark definition of each + 1 mark example = 3 marks.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

#### Question A (Choice 1). [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Module 3]

**(a)** Explain **Channel Associated Signaling (CAS)** and **Common Channel Signaling (CCS)** with neat diagrams. Compare their features. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, describe the **four sub-systems / layers of SS7** and the function of each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — CAS vs CCS (7 marks):**

**Channel Associated Signaling (CAS):** The signaling for a voice channel travels *with* that channel — either as a tone in-band (e.g., 2600 Hz SF) or as a robbed bit in PCM (e.g., T1 frame-6/frame-12 LSB), or on a separate physical lead pair (E&M DC). Each voice channel "owns" its own dedicated signaling resource. Limitations: limited information (supervisory + address only), slow, vulnerable to talk-off (in-band), and a voice-channel failure disables its signaling.

**Common Channel Signaling (CCS):** A single, dedicated, packet-based signaling channel (e.g., 64 kbps SS7 link) carries signaling messages for hundreds of voice channels. The signaling is out-of-band, message-oriented, and supports rich services (caller ID, ISDN, mobility, 800-number translation). Redundancy via mated-STP pairs ensures high reliability.

**Comparison Table (to be drawn in the answer script):**

| Aspect | CAS | CCS |
|---|---|---|
| Channel Use | Per voice channel | Shared |
| Speed | Slow | Fast |
| Information | Limited | Rich |
| Cost | Low for small | Higher initially, lower per feature |
| Reliability | Per-channel | Mated-pair redundant |
| Example | SF 2600 Hz, bit-robbing | **SS7**, ISDN D-channel |

**Part (b) — SS7 Four-Layer Model (7 marks):**

A neat block diagram (as in §4.2) is expected showing:

1. **MTP Level 1 (Physical):** Defines the electrical/optical interface (V.35, DS0 56/64 kbps). Provides bit-serial transmission.
2. **MTP Level 2 (Data Link):** Provides error-free framing using **HDLC-like flags (01111110)**, **bit-stuffing** for transparency, and **CRC-16** error detection with retransmission (NRR — Negative Acknowledge and Retransmit). This is the SL-1 link.
3. **MTP Level 3 (Network):** Performs **message discrimination** (is this for me?), **distribution** (route to local user part or another SP), and **routing** (toward destination SP via STP hops). Maintains link-set load sharing.
4. **User Parts (UPs):** The application layer. The dominant one is **ISUP (ISDN User Part)**, which defines call-setup messages — **IAM (Initial Address Message)**, **ACM (Address Complete Message)**, **ANM (Answer Message)**, **REL (Release)**, **RLC (Release Complete)**. **SCCP + TCAP** sit atop MTP-3 to support transaction-based queries (e.g., 800-number, HLR lookup, LNP).

*(Marks split: 3 for CAS/CCS description, 2 for comparison, 2 for the four-layer diagram + description, 2 for the message names. Adjust as the examiner wishes.)*

---

#### Question B (Choice 2). [KTU University Exam - June 2023, Module 3]

**(a)** Explain **E&M signaling**. With a diagram, describe the **on-hook and off-hook states** in **Type II E&M**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of **DTMF** address signaling. With a table, list the **low and high group frequencies** and the digits/characters they encode. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — E&M Signaling (7 marks):**

E&M is a **DC trunk signaling** scheme used between two switches (typically a PBX and a CO, or two COs) over a 4-wire voice path plus separate signaling leads. The **E lead (Ear)** carries signaling *received* from the far end; the **M lead (Mouth)** carries signaling *sent* to the far end.

**Five standard types (I–V):** Type I uses ground on M and battery on E. Type II uses **battery on M and ground on E** (the most common in modern PBX-CO interfaces, with isolation transformers). Type III has M lead referenced to ground; Type IV is similar to Type I; Type V is similar to Type II but with different reference directions. Type II and Type V are the de facto standards.

**Type II State Diagram (table to be drawn):**

| State | E Lead (incoming from far end) | M Lead (outgoing to far end) | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| On-Hook (Idle) | Open (no current) | Grounded | Trunk idle |
| Off-Hook (Seized) | Grounded (current flows) | Connected to −48 V battery | Trunk in use; voice path active |

**Diagrams (as in §4.4) must show two switches A and B with cross-connected E and M leads, and a 4-wire voice path (Tip/Ring, Tip1/Ring1).**

**Part (b) — DTMF (7 marks):**

DTMF (**Dual-Tone Multi-Frequency**) is the modern address-signaling scheme on the subscriber loop. Each digit is encoded as the **simultaneous sum of two audio tones** — one from a **low group** (697, 770, 852, 941 Hz) and one from a **high group** (1209, 1336, 1477, 1633 Hz). The 4×4 matrix yields 16 unique symbols: digits 0–9, the **\*** and **#** keys, and four reserved letters A–D.

**DTMF Encoding Table (to be drawn in the answer script):**

| Low \ High | 1209 Hz | 1336 Hz | 1477 Hz | 1633 Hz |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **697 Hz** | 1 | 2 | 3 | A |
| **770 Hz** | 4 | 5 | 6 | B |
| **852 Hz** | 7 | 8 | 9 | C |
| **941 Hz** | * | 0 | # | D |

**Key Properties (state at least three):**
- Each tone must be present for **≥ 40 ms** (per ITU-T Q.24) to be accepted as a valid digit.
- Tones shorter than **23 ms** must be rejected to prevent speech mimicry.
- Frequency tolerance: **±1.5%** of nominal.
- Receiver uses the **Goertzel algorithm** for efficient single-frequency energy detection.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps — Control Signaling (KTU Pattern):**
> 1. **Confusing "in-band CAS" with "out-of-band CCS."** Many students mix up the **channel-association** axis (CAS vs CCS) with the **band** axis (in-band vs out-of-band). They are *orthogonal*. Always answer in two axes.
> 2. **Forgetting to name the SS7 link types (A through F).** A 7-mark question on SS7 typically expects at least the **A, B, C, D** link types with their roles.
> 3. **Writing "SS7 = 4 layers" without naming the User Parts.** ISUP/TUP/SCCP/TCAP/MAP are *not* layers — they are **User Parts** sitting *on top of* the three MTP levels. Many students treat them as a fourth layer.
> 4. **Wrong T1/E1 bit rate.** 1.544 Mbps and 2.048 Mbps must be quoted with units and the framing-bit contribution. Students often write 1.544 without showing the 193-bit/frame derivation.
> 5. **DTMF without timing parameters.** Always quote **40 ms (accept) and 23 ms (reject)** along with the matrix.
> 6. **E&M types.** Naming only "Type II" without distinguishing it from Type I, III, IV, V costs 1–2 marks. Always state "Type II uses battery on M, ground on E, with isolation transformer" for full credit.
> 7. **Robbed-bit description.** Students say "8th bit is robbed" — wrong. It is the **LSB (bit 1) of every 8th sample (frames 6 and 12)** in the T1 superframe, not every frame.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Control Signaling (Module 3)**

- **Signaling definition:** exchange of control info to set up, supervise, and release a connection.
- **Three functions:** Supervisory, Address, Alerting/Call-progress.
- **Two classification axes:** (a) in-band vs out-of-band, (b) CAS vs CCS — orthogonal, must be combined.
- **Subscriber loop:** on-hook = −48 V, 0 mA; off-hook = ~23 mA loop current; ringing = 90–110 V AC at 20 Hz; dial pulse = 10 pps, 60% break; DTMF = dual tone, 4×4 matrix, 40 ms / 23 ms timing.
- **E&M DC signaling:** five types (I–V); Type II most common; E = receive, M = send.
- **SF in-band:** 2600 Hz (NA) / 3825 Hz (EU); tone-on = idle, tone-off = seized; talk-off problem.
- **PCM bit-robbing (T1):** T1 = 1.544 Mbps = 24×8 + 1 framing bit × 8 kHz; frames 6 and 12 LSB robbed → A and B signaling bits per voice channel per superframe; per-channel signaling rate ≈ 1.333 kbps.
- **E1:** 2.048 Mbps, 32 timeslots × 8 bits × 8 kHz; 30 voice + TS0 framing + TS16 signaling.
- **SS7 = Common Channel Signaling:** out-of-band, 56/64 kbps digital links.
- **SS7 layers:** MTP-1 (physical), MTP-2 (link, HDLC-like, CRC-16), MTP-3 (network routing), User Parts (ISUP, TUP), SCCP, TCAP, MAP, INAP.
- **SS7 elements:** SP (switch), STP (router, deployed in mated pairs), SCP (database).
- **SS7 link types:** A (SP↔STP), B (STP↔STP same network), C (paired STP), D (inter-quad STP), E (backup A), F (direct SP↔SP).
- **SS7 vs CAS efficiency:** one 64 kbps SS7 link serves ~1500–2000 voice circuits (vs 1.333 kbps per voice channel in CAS).
- **Modern evolution:** SS7 → Diameter (4G/LTE) → SIP/IMS (5G, VoLTE).
- **Must-memorize constants:** T1 = **1.544 Mbps**; E1 = **2.048 Mbps**; SS7 link = **56 or 64 kbps**; SF tone = **2600 Hz**; ring = **90 V AC, 20 Hz**; loop battery = **−48 V**; loop current ≈ **23 mA**; DTMF valid = **40 ms**, reject ≤ **23 ms**.
- **Diagrams to practice:** SS7 protocol stack, CAS vs CCS architecture, E&M Type-II lead wiring, DTMF matrix, call-setup sequence with ISUP message names (IAM, ACM, ANM, REL, RLC).
- **Examiner's favorite question styles:** (i) "Explain CAS vs CCS" — 7 marks; (ii) "SS7 architecture with four sub-systems" — 7 marks; (iii) "DTMF encoding with matrix" — 7 marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Soft Switch Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCERT602 - DATA COMMUNATION AND NETWORKING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Soft Switch Architecture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition
A **Softswitch (Software Switch)** is a programmable network element that manages and routes voice, data, video, and multimedia communications over IP-based networks (packet-switched networks) by separating the **call control logic** (signaling) from the **media transport** (bearer/voice path). It acts as the central intelligence element in a **Next Generation Network (NGN)** architecture, performing functions traditionally handled by circuit-switched telephone exchanges, but implemented entirely in software running on commercial off-the-shelf (COTS) hardware.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCERT602 – Module 3):** Softswitch architecture is a core topic in the *Internet Telephony & Next Generation Networks* segment. Students must focus on the layered architecture, the separation of control and bearer planes, and the protocols used between softswitch components.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of a **Softswitch as the "Air Traffic Controller of Voice Calls"**:
- Just as an air traffic controller sits in a tower and gives instructions to pilots (when to take off, which runway to use, when to land) **without ever flying the plane itself**, a softswitch controls telephone calls by instructing media gateways on how to connect them, but it never actually carries the voice traffic.
- The **Controller (Softswitch)** is the brain that decides *who* talks to *whom*, *when*, and *for how long*.
- The **Media Gateway** is the "airplane" that physically transports the actual voice payload from one caller to another.
- The **Signaling Gateway** acts like a translator at the international airport, converting messages between old-style pilots (PSTN/SS7 signals) and new-style pilots (IP/SIP signals).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** A traditional **Class 4/Class 5 PSTN switch** is a monolithic hardware box where signaling and bearer are tightly coupled inside the same chassis. A **softswitch** decouples them, enabling "**bearer-control separation**" — a revolutionary idea in telecom that allowed carriers to scale independently and cut costs.

### 1.3 Core Terminology Snapshot
- **Bearer Plane (Media Plane):** The actual voice/data traffic path (RTP/RTCP streams).
- **Control Plane (Signaling Plane):** The "intelligence" path that sets up, manages, and tears down calls.
- **Media Gateway (MG):** Hardware device that converts between circuit-switched (TDM) voice and packet-switched (IP) voice.
- **Media Gateway Controller (MGC) / Call Agent (CA):** The "softswitch" itself — software that controls the MGs.
- **Signaling Gateway (SG):** Translates between legacy SS7 signaling and IP-based signaling (SIGTRAN).
- **Application Server (AS):** Hosts advanced services like voicemail, IVR, conferencing, and billing.
- **Feature Server:** Provides supplementary services (call waiting, call forwarding, 3-way calling).
- **PSTN (Public Switched Telephone Network):** The legacy global circuit-switched voice network.
- **NGN (Next Generation Network):** All-IP converged network architecture (ITU-T Y.2001).
- **IMS (IP Multimedia Subsystem):** A standardized 3GPP/ETSI NGN architecture (often called the "all-SIP" evolution of softswitch).

### 1.4 Historical Context (Why Softswitch Existed)
Before softswitches, every Class 5 central office cost **$1–$5 million** in proprietary hardware. A softswitch could run on a Linux server costing a fraction of that. The technology peaked around 2000–2010, and has since evolved into full **IMS** architectures used in 4G/5G VoLTE.

### 1.5 Standard Metrics to Remember
- **Call Setup Latency:** Typically **100–500 ms** for SIP-based softswitch.
- **Bearer Bandwidth per VoIP Call:** **64 kbps** (PCM) compressed to **8 kbps** (G.729) or **6.4 kbps** (G.723.1).
- **MOS (Mean Opinion Score):** Target **≥ 3.5** for acceptable VoIP quality.
- **H.248/Megaco** transaction latency: **< 50 ms** under normal load.
- **SS7 MTP2** link speed: **56 kbps** / **64 kbps** in legacy PSTN.

> [!TIP]
> For KTU 2-mark questions, remember the keyword: **"Separation of Bearer and Control Planes."** This is the *defining* feature of a softswitch.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Fundamental Problem Softswitch Solves
Legacy PSTN switches had **three tightly-coupled planes** inside a single proprietary box:
1. **Switching Fabric** (moves bits between TDM time slots)
2. **Call Processing Logic** (routing, billing, signaling)
3. **Operations & Maintenance** (OAM)

This coupling meant:
- **High CAPEX** (expensive proprietary hardware).
- **Slow feature rollout** (new services required new hardware).
- **Vendor lock-in** (no interoperability).
- **Inefficient scaling** (adding capacity required a whole new switch).

The softswitch model **decouples** the bearer (switching fabric → Media Gateway) from the control logic (Call Agent/Softswitch) and runs everything on **open, standards-based, COTS hardware/software**.

### 2.2 The Three Logical Planes (Revisit & Deepen)

| Plane | Function | Real-World Example | KTU Keyword |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Bearer / Media Plane** | Transports voice/video payload | Media Gateway, RTP streams | "Packet forwarding" |
| **Control / Signaling Plane** | Sets up & tears down calls | MGC, Call Agent, H.248/MGCP, SIP | "Call routing logic" |
| **Application / Service Plane** | Provides value-added services | App Server, Feature Server, IVR | "Service execution" |

### 2.3 Detailed Component Architecture

#### 2.3.1 Media Gateway (MG)
- **Role:** Converts between **TDM (Time Division Multiplexing) circuits** and **IP packets**.
- **Interfaces:**
  - TDM side: **E1/T1 (2.048 / 1.544 Mbps)**, DS3, STM-1.
  - IP side: **Ethernet (10/100/1000 Mbps)**, Optical (GE/10GE).
- **Functions:**
  - Echo cancellation (G.168, ≤ 128 ms tail length).
  - DTMF detection & generation (RFC 2833 / RFC 4733).
  - Codec transcoding (G.711 ↔ G.729 ↔ G.723.1 ↔ iLBC ↔ Opus).
  - RTP packetization & dejitter buffering.
  - VAD (Voice Activity Detection) + CNG (Comfort Noise Generation) for bandwidth saving.
- **Controlled by:** Media Gateway Controller (MGC) via **H.248/Megaco** or **MGCP** protocol.
- **Location:** At the **edge** of the IP network, between PSTN and IP backbone.

#### 2.3.2 Media Gateway Controller (MGC) / Call Agent (CA) / Softswitch
- **Role:** The "brain" of the network. Performs all call control logic.
- **Functions:**
  - **Call routing** (E.164 number → IP address translation, LCR — Least Cost Routing).
  - **Call admission control** (CAC) — ensures QoS.
  - **Billing & CDR (Call Detail Record) generation**.
  - **Interworking** between signaling protocols.
  - **Number translation, porting, ENUM lookups**.
  - **Load balancing** across multiple MGs.
- **Protocols it speaks:**
  - **Southbound** (to MG): **H.248/Megaco**, **MGCP**.
  - **Northbound** (to App Server): **SIP**, **Parlay/OSA**, **JAIN**.
  - **Peer** (to other softswitches): **SIP**, **SIP-T**, **SIP-I**, **BICC**.
- **Redundancy:** Typically deployed as **Active-Standby** or **Active-Active** cluster using protocols like **H.248 Heartbeat** or vendor-specific mechanisms.

#### 2.3.3 Signaling Gateway (SG)
- **Role:** Bridges **SS7 (Signaling System No. 7)** over TDM to **SIGTRAN (SS7 over IP)**.
- **Why needed:** Legacy PSTN switches speak SS7 natively over **MTP1/2/3** (Message Transfer Part) over 64 kbps timeslots. Modern IP networks use **SIGTRAN**: SCTP (Stream Control Transmission Protocol) + M2UA / M2PA / M3UA / SUA.
- **SIGTRAN Protocol Stack (from bottom to top):**
  - **SCTP** (RFC 4960) — reliable transport over IP, multi-homing.
  - **M2UA** (MTP2-User Adaptation Layer) — backhauls MTP3 over IP.
  - **M2PA** (MTP2-User Peer-to-Peer Adaptation Layer).
  - **M3UA** (MTP3-User Adaptation Layer) — most common; replaces MTP3.
  - **SUA** (SCCP-User Adaptation Layer) — for TCAP/SCCP traffic (IN, MAP, CAP).
- **Logical Function:** Translates lower-layer SS7 messaging into IP-deliverable form, but does **not** interpret call control — that is still the MGC's job.

#### 2.3.4 Application Server (AS) / Feature Server
- **Role:** Hosts supplementary and value-added services.
- **Examples of Services:**
  - **Voicemail / Unified Messaging** (UM).
  - **IVR (Interactive Voice Response)** — "Press 1 for Sales, 2 for Support".
  - **Conferencing** (audio/video bridge, RFC 4353 / MSRP).
  - **Call Center** (ACD — Automatic Call Distribution).
  - **Prepaid calling, callback, ringback tone**.
- **Interface:** SIP (RFC 3261) to the softswitch.
- **Standardized by:** 3GPP IMS, ETSI TISPAN, PacketCable.

#### 2.3.5 Back-to-Back User Agent (B2BUA)
- A logical function of the softswitch that terminates SIP signaling on both sides and re-originates it. Unlike a SIP proxy (which only forwards), a B2BUA **holds state for both call legs** and can independently manipulate each leg. This is why softswitches can do **billing, NAT traversal, and transcoding** better than pure SIP proxies.

### 2.4 Three Standard Softswitch Architectures (KTU-Favorite)

#### Architecture 1: End-to-End Softswitch (Trunking / Tandem Softswitch)
- **Use case:** Long-distance IP trunking between PSTN gateways.
- **Setup:** Two MGs at each end, one MGC controlling both, back-to-back connection.
- **No PSTN middle:** Calls stay on IP between cities, only converted to TDM at the endpoints.
- **KTU Keyword:** "**Toll bypass**" — bypass the long-distance PSTN carrier.

#### Architecture 2: Access Softswitch (Class-5 Replacement)
- **Use case:** Residential / SME VoIP service (the "Vonage model").
- **Setup:** Subscriber → ATA (Analog Telephone Adapter) or IP Phone → Access MG → Softswitch.
- **Provides:** Local number, dialing plan, voicemail, CLASS services.
- **KTU Keyword:** "**Line-side replacement**" for a Class 5 central office.

#### Architecture 3: Network Access / Trunking Softswitch (Class-4 Replacement)
- **Use case:** Wholesale carrier interconnect, inter-city VoIP.
- **Setup:** Class-5 switch ↔ Trunking MG ↔ Trunking Softswitch ↔ IP backbone ↔ ... ↔ other Class-5.
- **Functions:** High-volume call routing, LCR, SS7 interworking, large-scale billing.
- **KTU Keyword:** "**Tandem replacement**" for a Class 4 toll switch.

### 2.5 Protocol Stack Summary (KTU Quick Reference)

| Plane | Protocol | RFC / Standard | Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bearer | **RTP / RTCP** | RFC 3550 | Real-time voice transport |
| Bearer | **TDM** (E1/T1) | ITU-T G.703, G.704 | PSTN physical transport |
| Control | **H.248 / Megaco** | RFC 3525, ITU-T H.248 | MGC ↔ MG |
| Control | **MGCP** | RFC 3435 | MGC ↔ MG (older) |
| Control | **SIP** | RFC 3261 | Softswitch ↔ endpoints, softswitch ↔ softswitch |
| Control | **H.323** | ITU-T H.323 | Legacy VoIP signaling |
| Signaling | **SS7 (MTP1/2/3, SCCP, TCAP)** | ITU-T Q.700 | PSTN signaling |
| Signaling | **SIGTRAN (M3UA, M2PA, SUA)** | RFC 4666, 4166, 3868 | SS7 over IP |
| Service | **Parlay/OSA, JAIN, SIP Servlet** | ETSI, JCP | App server interface |

### 2.6 KTU Formula / Parameter Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Softswitch is mostly a **conceptual / architectural** topic, so formulas are limited. The key quantitative aspects are bandwidth, latency, and signaling performance.

| # | Parameter | Formula / Standard Value | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | G.711 PCM bit rate | $R_{PCM} = f_s \times n$ | kbps | 8 kHz × 8 bits = **64 kbps** |
| 2 | G.729 bit rate | $R_{G.729}$ = **8 kbps** | kbps | 8:1 compression over G.711 |
| 3 | G.723.1 bit rate | $R_{G.723.1}$ = **5.3 / 6.4 kbps** | kbps | ACELP / MP-MLQ |
| 4 | RTP packet size | $P_{RTP} = 160 + 12 + 8 + 20$ | bytes | 20 ms @ G.711 = 160 B payload + 12 B RTP + 8 B UDP + 20 B IP |
| 5 | Bandwidth per VoIP call (G.711, no cRTP) | $BW = \frac{(160+40) \times 8}{20 \text{ ms}}$ | kbps | $= 80$ kbps total |
| 6 | Bandwidth per call (G.711 + cRTP) | $BW_{cRTP} = \frac{(160+12+2) \times 8}{20}$ | kbps | ≈ **69.6 kbps** |
| 7 | Bandwidth per call (G.729) | $BW_{G.729} = \frac{(20+40) \times 8}{20}$ | kbps | = **24 kbps** |
| 8 | One-way delay budget (ITU-T G.114) | $D_{one\text{-}way} \leq 150$ | ms | "Acceptable" voice quality |
| 9 | Total mouth-to-ear delay target | $D_{total} \leq 300$ | ms | Beyond this = conversation difficulty |
| 10 | E1 (Europe) total bandwidth | $C_{E1} = 32 \times 64$ | kbps | 2.048 Mbps; 30 B-channels + 2 framing |
| 11 | T1 (US/Japan) total bandwidth | $C_{T1} = 24 \times 64 + 8$ | kbps | 1.544 Mbps |
| 12 | MOS target (acceptable) | $MOS \geq 3.5$ | — | Scale 1 (bad) – 5 (excellent) |
| 13 | R-Factor (E-model) | $R = 94.2 - I_d - I_{e\text{-}eff} + A$ | — | $R \geq 80$ = "Good"; $< 60$ = "Poor" |
| 14 | E-model impairment from delay | $I_d = 0.024 \cdot d + 0.11 \cdot (d - 177.3) \cdot H(d - 177.3)$ | — | $H(\cdot)$ is Heaviside step function |
| 15 | Signaling load (ISUP per call) | $\approx 7$ messages | — | IAM, ACM, ANM, REL, RLC, etc. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on table symbols:** The single-pipe notation has been deliberately replaced with `\vert` or removed entirely (e.g., `MOS ≥ 3.5`, not `MOS \vert\geq 3.5\vert`) to comply with the markdown table rendering rules.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Telecom Carriers (Bharti Airtel, Reliance Jio, AT&T, BT):** Use softswitch/IMS to carry VoIP and VoLTE traffic.
- **Call Centers:** Use softswitch + media server for predictive dialing, ACD, and CRM integration.
- **Unified Communications (Cisco CUCM, Avaya Aura, Microsoft Teams Phone):** All descended from the softswitch model.
- **Defense / Tactical Networks:** VoIP softswitches for secure battlefield comms.
- **NGN / IMS Migration Path:** Every PSTN switch will eventually be replaced by a softswitch or IMS core.
