## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Bandwidth per VoIP Call (G.711 with no cRTP)

This is the **most frequently asked numerical question** in KTU on this topic. The derivation is shown in full, step-by-step.

> **Given:**
> - Codec: **G.711 PCM** (64 kbps)
> - Sample size: 8 bits, sampled at 8 kHz
> - Packetization period: $T_{pkt} = 20$ ms
> - IP/UDP/RTP headers: 20 (IP) + 8 (UDP) + 12 (RTP) = **40 bytes** (uncompressed)

**Step 1 — Compute bytes of payload per packet.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Payload bits} &= R_{codec} \times T_{pkt} \\
&= 64\,000\ \text{bits/s} \times 20 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{s} \\
&= 1280\ \text{bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert payload to bytes.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Payload bytes} &= \frac{1280\ \text{bits}}{8\ \text{bits/byte}} \\
&= 160\ \text{bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Add header overheads (IP + UDP + RTP).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total bytes per packet} &= 160\ \text{(payload)} + 12\ \text{(RTP)} + 8\ \text{(UDP)} + 20\ \text{(IPv4)} \\
&= 200\ \text{bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute bits per packet.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bits per packet} &= 200\ \text{bytes} \times 8\ \text{bits/byte} \\
&= 1600\ \text{bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute packets per second.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Packets/s} &= \frac{1}{T_{pkt}} = \frac{1}{0.020\ \text{s}} \\
&= 50\ \text{packets/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute total bandwidth per call.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
BW_{call} &= 1600\ \text{bits/pkt} \times 50\ \text{pkts/s} \\
&= 80\,000\ \text{bits/s} \\
&= \mathbf{80\ kbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> The rule of thumb: **G.711 ≈ 80 kbps (no cRTP), ≈ 64 kbps (with cRTP), and G.729 ≈ 24–32 kbps (no cRTP), ≈ 11.2 kbps (with cRTP).**

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Total Bandwidth on an E1 Trunk Carrying G.729 Calls

> **Given:**
> - One **E1 trunk** = 30 usable B-channels (after 2 framing channels).
> - Codec: **G.729** (8 kbps).
> - Packetization: 20 ms.
> - No compressed RTP (no cRTP).

**Step 1 — Bandwidth per call (G.729, no cRTP).**

G.729 sends 20 bytes (160 bits) of payload per 20 ms packet. Same 40-byte header overhead.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total bytes} &= 20 + 12 + 8 + 20 = 60\ \text{bytes} \\
BW_{G.729} &= 60 \times 8 \times 50 \\
&= 24\,000\ \text{bits/s} \\
&= \mathbf{24\ kbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Total bandwidth for 30 concurrent calls on one E1.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
BW_{total} &= 30 \times 24\ \text{kbps} \\
&= \mathbf{720\ kbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Capacity check (silently used to confirm we have headroom for bearer, since 2.048 Mbps >> 0.72 Mbps).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Utilization} &= \frac{720\ \text{kbps}}{2048\ \text{kbps}} \times 100\% \\
&\approx 35.2\ \%
\end{aligned}
$$

The remaining 64.8% of the E1 is used for **signaling** (SS7/ISDN PRI), **VLAN overhead**, and **growth headroom**.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Functional Comparison of Softswitch vs. Class 4/5 Switch

| Function | Class 4 PSTN Switch | Class 5 PSTN Switch | Softswitch (MGC + MG) | IMS (Post-Evolution) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Architecture** | Monolithic hardware | Monolithic hardware | Distributed (MGC + MG + SG) | Fully distributed, all-SIP |
| **Bearer plane** | TDM switch fabric | TDM switch fabric | Media Gateway | Media Resource Function (MRF) |
| **Control plane** | Built-in CPU | Built-in CPU | Media Gateway Controller | Call Session Control Function (CSCF) |
| **Service plane** | SCP adjunct | Built-in features | Application Server | Application Server (AS) |
| **Signaling** | SS7 (ISUP) | SS7, ISDN PRI | SS7 + SIGTRAN + SIP | SIP + Diameter |
| **CAPEX** | **High (millions)** | **High (millions)** | **Low (COTS hardware)** | Moderate |
| **Scalability** | Switch-by-switch | Switch-by-switch | **Add MGs horizontally** | **Add CSCFs / MRFs** |
| **Feature rollout** | Months (new firmware) | Months | **Days (software push)** | Hours |
| **Vendor lock-in** | **Total** | **Total** | **Low (standards-based)** | Low |
| **Capacity** | 100k+ BHCA | 50k BHCA | 1M+ BHCA on cluster | 10M+ BHCA |
| **KTU Memory Aid** | "Old big iron" | "Old big iron, local" | "Brain + Hands" | "All-SIP future" |

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: H.248 vs. MGCP — Protocol Comparison

| Feature | **H.248 / Megaco** | **MGCP** |
|---|---|---|
| **Standard body** | ITU-T + IETF (joint) | IETF (RFC 3435) |
| **Architecture** | Master-Slave | Master-Slave (more rigid) |
| **Termination model** | **Rich, hierarchical (Root, Gateway, Context, Termination)** | Endpoints, Connections, Calls |
| **Scalability** | **Excellent** (huge MGs, e.g., 100k+ terminations) | Limited (designed for small residential gateways) |
| **Transport** | TCP or UDP; SCTP for SIGTRAN | UDP only |
| **Encoding** | ASN.1 (BER) **OR** Text | Text only |
| **Use case** | Carrier-grade trunking gateways | Residential / ATA gateways |
| **KTU Memory Aid** | "**H.248 = Heavy / Hierarchical**" | "**MGCP = Modest / Gateway-side**" |

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: SIGTRAN Adaptation Layers (Detailed)

| Layer | RFC | Replaces | Carries | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **M2UA** | RFC 3331 | MTP2 | MTP3 messages | SG backhaul to MGC |
| **M2PA** | RFC 4165 | MTP2 + MTP3 link | MTP3 + SCCP | IP-native SS7 nodes |
| **M3UA** | RFC 4666 | MTP3 | SCCP, ISUP, TCAP | **Most common** in softswitch SG-MGC link |
| **SUA** | RFC 3868 | SCCP | TCAP, MAP, CAP, INAP | IN / 3GPP signaling |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** M3UA does **not** carry MTP3 routing — it is the MTP3 layer. SCTP provides the transport. So the stack is **SCTP | M3UA | SCCP/ISUP | TCAP**, not "SCTP | M2PA | M3UA".

---

### 3.6 Softswitch Call Flow (Step-by-Step SIP + H.248 Example)

The most common KTU long-answer scenario: **"Explain a VoIP call from a SIP phone to a PSTN subscriber via a softswitch."**

**Topology:** `SIP Phone A` → `IP Network` → `Softswitch (MGC)` ↔ `Media Gateway (MG)` → `PSTN → Phone B`

**Phase 1 — Registration (one-time)**
1. SIP Phone A sends `REGISTER` to the softswitch's SIP registrar.
2. Softswitch replies with `200 OK` and stores A's contact IP.

**Phase 2 — Call Setup (Signaling)**
3. Phone A sends `INVITE sip:B@telco.com SIP/2.0` to the softswitch.
4. Softswitch checks **ENUM / routing table** → decides B is a PSTN number.
5. Softswitch (acting as B2BUA) instructs MG via **H.248**:
   - `Add` a termination on a free TDM timeslot.
   - `Modify` to seize the B-channel.
   - `Notify` with an ISUP `IAM` (Initial Address Message) on the SS7 link.
6. SS7 `IAM` traverses the PSTN to the destination Class-5 switch.

**Phase 3 — Call Acceptance**
7. PSTN sends back `ACM` (Address Complete Message) and rings Phone B.
8. Softswitch sends `180 Ringing` back to Phone A.

**Phase 4 — Answer**
9. Phone B answers → PSTN sends `ANM` (Answer Message).
10. Softswitch sends `200 OK` to Phone A.
11. Phone A sends `ACK`.
12. Softswitch (via H.248) tells MG to **connect the RTP path** between SIP Phone A and the TDM B-channel.

**Phase 5 — Media (Bearer)**
13. RTP media flows **directly** between Phone A and MG (softswitch is **not** in the media path).
14. MG transcodes between G.711 (IP side) and PCM (TDM side).

**Phase 6 — Teardown**
15. Phone B hangs up → PSTN sends `REL` → Softswitch sends `BYE` to Phone A.
16. Phone A sends `200 OK` to BYE.
17. Softswitch sends `RLC` (Release Complete) to PSTN.
18. Softswitch (via H.248) tells MG to **release** the TDM timeslot.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The question "Is the softswitch in the media path?" is a favorite 1-mark question. Answer: **No**. The softswitch only handles signaling. The media flows **directly** between endpoints (or between the endpoints and the MG).

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: H.248 Connection Model (Root → Gateway → Context → Termination)

```mermaid
graph TB
    R[Root<br/>MGC Logical Entity] --> GW[Gateway<br/>Represents the MG]
    GW --> CTX1[Context 1<br/>Call Leg A]
    GW --> CTX2[Context 2<br/>Call Leg B]
    GW --> CTX0[Context NULL<br/>Idle TDM Channels]
    CTX1 --> T1[TDM Termination<br/>E1 B-channel 5]
    CTX1 --> T2[RTP Termination<br/>UDP 5004 IP 10.0.0.5]
    CTX2 --> T3[TDM Termination<br/>E1 B-channel 6]
    CTX2 --> T4[RTP Termination<br/>UDP 5010 IP 10.0.0.6]
    CTX0 --> T5[Idle TDM Termination<br/>E1 B-channel 7]

    classDef ctx  fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000
    classDef term fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000
    classDef root fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#fd7e14,color:#000

    class R,GW root
    class CTX1,CTX2,CTX0 ctx
    class T1,T2,T3,T4,T5 term
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the H.248 model:** A **Termination** is one end of a media flow (e.g., one TDM timeslot OR one RTP stream). A **Context** is a logical grouping of terminations that are mixed together — usually a 2-way call has **2 terminations in 1 context**. **Null context** holds idle terminations.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 / 3 Marks Each)

**Q1. Define a Softswitch. What is its primary function?**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A **Softswitch (Software Switch)** is a software-based network element that performs call control, signaling, and routing functions in an IP-based telecommunications network. Its **primary function** is to **separate the control plane (call processing intelligence) from the bearer plane (media transport)**, enabling voice calls to be carried over packet-switched IP networks while still interoperating with the legacy PSTN. It acts as the "brain" that instructs Media Gateways on how and when to set up, route, and tear down calls, but it does not itself carry the voice media.

**Q2. List any four key functions of a Media Gateway (MG).**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> 1. **Transcoding** between TDM codecs (G.711 PCM) and VoIP codecs (G.729, G.723.1).
> 2. **Echo cancellation** (G.168, ≤ 128 ms tail).
> 3. **DTMF detection and generation** (RFC 2833 / 4733).
> 4. **RTP packetization, dejitter buffering, and VAD/CNG** for bandwidth optimization.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — "End-to-End Softswitch"** `[14 Marks]`
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Module Internal Choice]`

> **(a) With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a Softswitch. List its major functional components and explain the role of each.** `(7 marks)`
>
> **(b) Explain the three standard softswitch deployment models with suitable diagrams. Compare Class 4 and Class 5 softswitch models.** `(7 marks)`

##### **Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

A **Softswitch architecture** is built on the principle of **bearer-control separation**. It consists of four major components:

**1. Media Gateway (MG)**
- Sits at the **edge** between TDM PSTN and IP network.
- Performs **transcoding** (G.711 ↔ G.729), **echo cancellation**, **DTMF relay**, and **RTP packetization**.
- Is a "slave" — takes commands from the MGC.

**2. Media Gateway Controller (MGC) / Call Agent**
- The **core** of the softswitch; runs call-processing software (e.g., on Linux).
- Performs **call routing, CAC, billing (CDR), ENUM lookups, LCR**.
- Communicates with MGs via **H.248/Megaco**.
- Communicates with endpoints / other softswitches via **SIP**.

**3. Signaling Gateway (SG)**
- **Translates** legacy SS7 (over TDM) into **SIGTRAN** (SCTP + M3UA / M2PA / SUA over IP).
- Allows the softswitch to interwork with PSTN SS7 networks.
- Protocol stack: **SCTP | M3UA | SCCP/ISUP | TCAP**.

**4. Application Server (AS)**
- Hosts **value-added services** — voicemail, IVR, conferencing, prepaid, unified messaging.
- Speaks **SIP** to the softswitch.

**Why this design?**
- **Scalability:** Add more MGs without touching the MGC.
- **Open standards:** COTS hardware, multi-vendor interoperability.
- **Cost:** Cheaper than legacy Class 4/5 switches.
- **Rapid service rollout:** Add new services in days via software updates to AS.

**Key protocols in a softswitch system:**
- **H.248/Megaco** — MGC ↔ MG (control)
- **MGCP** — older alternative to H.248 for residential MGs
- **SIP** — endpoint ↔ softswitch and softswitch ↔ softswitch
- **SIGTRAN (M3UA/SUA over SCTP)** — SG ↔ MGC
- **RTP/RTCP** — bearer path

*(Refer to Section 4.1 block diagram.)*

##### **Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Three Softswitch Deployment Models:**

**1. End-to-End Softswitch (Tandem / Trunking)**
- **Use case:** Inter-city / inter-country VoIP trunking (international calls).
- **Setup:** Two Class-5 switches connected via two MGs, controlled by a single MGC.
- **Benefit:** **Toll bypass** — bypass long-distance PSTN carrier.
- **KTU Keyword:** "**Trunking softswitch**".

**2. Access Softswitch (Class-5 Replacement)**
- **Use case:** Residential and SMB VoIP service (like Vonage, JioFiber voice).
- **Setup:** Subscriber ATA / IP Phone → IP Network → Softswitch → MG → PSTN.
- **Provides:** Local DID number, voicemail, CLASS services (call waiting, forwarding).
- **KTU Keyword:** "**Line-side replacement**".

**3. Network Access / Trunking Softswitch (Class-4 Replacement)**
- **Use case:** Wholesale carrier interconnect, LCR, large-scale interoffice VoIP.
- **Setup:** Class-5 ↔ Trunking MG ↔ Softswitch ↔ IP backbone ↔ Peer Softswitch ↔ Trunking MG ↔ Class-5.
- **Provides:** High-capacity LCR, inter-carrier billing, SS7 interworking.
- **KTU Keyword:** "**Tandem replacement**".

| Parameter | Class 4 Softswitch | Class 5 Softswitch |
|---|---|---|
| Function | Inter-office tandem | Local central office |
| Subscriber awareness | **No** | **Yes** (DID, dial plan) |
| Volume | Very high BHCA | Moderate BHCA |
| Typical user | Wholesale carriers | End customers |
| Services | LCR, inter-carrier billing | Voicemail, CLASS, IVR |
| Example | Cisco PGW 2200, Sonus GSX | Cisco BTS, Genband C20 |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often **confuse Class 4 and Class 5**. Mnemonic: **"Class 5 = Customer-facing, Class 4 = Carrier-facing."** A Class 4 switch knows about trunks; a Class 5 switch knows about subscribers and dial plans. The softswitch equivalents (trunking vs. access) follow the same split.

---

#### **Question B — "Signaling, H.248, and Bandwidth"** `[14 Marks]`
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Module Internal Choice]`

> **(a) Explain the H.248/Megaco protocol architecture. Describe its connection model (Root, Gateway, Context, Termination) with a suitable diagram.** `(7 marks)`
>
> **(b) Compute the bandwidth required per VoIP call and the total bandwidth on an E1 trunk for the following scenario: Codec = G.711, Packetization period = 20 ms, No cRTP. Also compute the number of simultaneous calls an E1 can carry in pure VoIP (G.711) mode. Show all steps.** `(7 marks)`

##### **Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**H.248 / Megaco — Protocol Overview**
- Joint ITU-T (H.248) and IETF (RFC 3525) standard.
- Used between **MGC (master)** and **MG (slave)**.
- **Master-Slave** model: MGC sends commands (Add, Modify, Subtract, Move, AuditValue, AuditCapabilities, Notify, ServiceChange); MG replies.
- **Transport:** TCP, UDP, or **SCTP** (preferred for telecom-grade reliability).

**H.248 Connection Model (4 levels):**

1. **Root** — the logical identity of the MGC; can control multiple Gateways.
2. **Gateway** — represents a single physical or virtual MG.
3. **Context** — a logical mixing domain for terminations.
   - **NULL context** (ContextID = 0): holds idle terminations.
   - **Normal context**: holds ≥ 2 terminations in a call (e.g., TDM side + RTP side = 1 call).
4. **Termination** — one end of a media flow.
   - **Physical termination** (e.g., a specific E1 timeslot).
   - **Ephemeral termination** (e.g., an RTP stream).
   - Each termination has **properties** (media stream descriptor, local/remote addresses, codec, mode).

**Example call (PSTN ↔ IP):**
- One **Context** is created.
- Two **Terminations** are added: one **TDM** (E1 B-channel) + one **RTP** (UDP port).
- MGC sends **Add** with descriptors → MG connects them.
- On hangup, MGC sends **Subtract** → MG releases the timeslot.

**Key H.248 commands (KTU must-know):**
- `Add` — create a termination in a context.
- `Modify` — change properties (e.g., set codec).
- `Subtract` — remove termination, free resources.
- `Move` — move termination between contexts (e.g., during call transfer).
- `Notify` — MG → MGC (e.g., on-hook detected).
- `AuditValue` / `AuditCapabilities` — MGC queries MG state.
- `ServiceChange` — registration / failover.

*(Refer to H.248 Mermaid diagram in Section 4.3.)*

##### **Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Given:**
- Codec: **G.711** (64 kbps)
- Packetization: $T_{pkt} = 20$ ms
- No cRTP → Full IP/UDP/RTP headers

**Step 1 — Compute payload bits per packet.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Payload bits} &= R_{codec} \times T_{pkt} \\
&= 64\,000\ \text{bits/s} \times 20 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{s} \\
&= 1280\ \text{bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to bytes.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Payload bytes} &= \frac{1280}{8} = 160\ \text{bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Add header overhead.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RTP} &= 12\ \text{bytes} \\
\text{UDP} &= 8\ \text{bytes} \\
\text{IPv4} &= 20\ \text{bytes} \\
\text{Total header} &= 40\ \text{bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Total bytes per packet.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total} &= 160 + 40 = 200\ \text{bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Packets per second.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Pkts/s} &= \frac{1}{0.020} = 50\ \text{packets/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Bandwidth per call.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
BW_{call} &= 200\ \text{bytes} \times 8\ \text{bits/byte} \times 50\ \text{pkts/s} \\
&= 1600 \times 50 \\
&= 80\,000\ \text{bits/s} \\
&= \boxed{80\ \text{kbps}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Number of simultaneous VoIP calls on one E1 (pure bearer, G.711, no signaling overhead).**

One E1 has 2.048 Mbps raw; 30 B-channels × 64 kbps = 1.92 Mbps. But **packet-switched** bandwidth is consumed by **packets**, not time slots.

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{calls} &= \left\lfloor \frac{C_{E1}}{BW_{call}} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{2048\ \text{kbps}}{80\ \text{kbps}} \right\rfloor = \lfloor 25.6 \rfloor = \mathbf{25\ \text{calls}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8 — Total bandwidth for 25 calls.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
BW_{total} &= 25 \times 80\ \text{kbps} = \mathbf{2000\ \text{kbps}} = 2\ \text{Mbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #1:** Students often **add Ethernet overhead** (14-byte header + 4-byte CRC + 8-byte preamble = 26 bytes for 10/100 Mbps Ethernet, 38 bytes for GigE with VLAN tag). If the question specifies "**at the Ethernet layer**," you must add this. If not, assume IP layer only.
>
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #2:** Using **30 calls** (the number of E1 B-channels) is a common mistake. In a pure VoIP E1 carrying G.711, the answer is **25 calls**, not 30, because packet headers consume extra bandwidth beyond the 64 kbps codec rate.
>
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #3:** Forgetting that **30 B-channels** are available in an E1 (some students use 32 — that's the total timeslots including 2 framing slots).

### 5.3 Quick-Reference: KTU Board-Examiner Mark Distribution Pattern

| Sub-Question | Component | Marks Break-up | |
|---|---|---|---|
| (a) Architecture diagram | 2 marks | Components listed | 3 marks | Examples / protocols |
| 2 marks | | | |
| (b) Comparison / Calculation | 2 marks | Main calculation | 3 marks | Final answer with units |
| 2 marks | | | |

> **Pro Tip:** KTU examiners reward **neat labeled diagrams** generously. Always include one in any 7-mark question.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Softswitch Architecture**

- **Definition:** A **softswitch** is a software-based call controller that separates **bearer (media)** from **control (signaling)** in a packet-switched NGN.
- **Core components (4):** **MG** (bearer), **MGC/Call Agent** (control), **SG** (SS7↔SIGTRAN), **AS** (services).
- **Three planes:** **Bearer / Control / Application** — the *defining* architectural principle.
- **Three deployment models:** **End-to-End (Trunking/Tandem)**, **Access (Class-5 replacement)**, **Trunking (Class-4 replacement)**.
- **Key protocols:** **H.248/Megaco** (MGC↔MG), **MGCP** (residential), **SIP** (endpoints, peer softswitches), **SIGTRAN = SCTP + M3UA** (SS7 over IP), **RTP/RTCP** (media).
- **H.248 model:** **Root → Gateway → Context → Termination**; NULL context = idle.
- **H.248 commands:** **Add, Modify, Subtract, Move, Notify, AuditValue, AuditCapabilities, ServiceChange**.
- **SIGTRAN adaptation layers:** **M2UA** (replaces MTP2), **M2PA** (MTP2+MTP3), **M3UA** (replaces MTP3, most common), **SUA** (replaces SCCP).
- **Softswitch is NOT in the media path** — it only sets up/tears down calls; RTP flows directly between endpoints/MG.
- **Bandwidth formulas:**
  - G.711 ≈ **80 kbps** (no cRTP), **64 kbps** (with cRTP).
  - G.729 ≈ **24 kbps** (no cRTP), **11.2 kbps** (with cRTP).
- **E1 trunk facts:** 2.048 Mbps total, **30 B-channels** × 64 kbps + 2 framing.
- **T1 trunk facts:** 1.544 Mbps total, **24 B-channels** × 64 kbps + 1 framing.
- **Delay budget (ITU-T G.114):** one-way ≤ **150 ms** acceptable; mouth-to-ear ≤ **300 ms**.
- **MOS target:** ≥ **3.5**; **R-factor** ≥ **80** for "Good" quality.
- **B2BUA vs Proxy:** A softswitch is a **B2BUA** (terminates and re-originates SIP), not a pure proxy — this is why it can bill and transcode.
- **IMS vs Softswitch:** IMS is the **3GPP/ETSI standardized evolution** of softswitch with **all-SIP** control (CSCF, HSS, MRF, AS).
- **Class 4 vs Class 5 mnemonic:** **"Class 5 = Customer-facing, Class 4 = Carrier-facing."**
- **Historical:** Softswitch peaked 2000–2010, evolved into IMS for 4G/5G VoLTE.
- **KTU exam keyword search:** "Bearer-control separation", "B2BUA", "H.248", "M3UA", "SIGTRAN" — these are the **most-likely 2-mark keywords**.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Mantra:** *"Softswitch = Brain (MGC) + Hands (MG) + Translator (SG) + Services (AS). Standards: H.248, SIP, SIGTRAN. Bearer separate from control."* — Memorize this and you can answer 80% of the questions on this topic.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Packet Switching Principles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCERT602 - DATA COMMUNATION AND NETWORKING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Packet Switching Principles ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Packet Switching** is a digital networking communication method in which all data to be transmitted — regardless of content, type, or structure — is broken into suitably sized blocks called **packets**, which are routed through the network based on their destination address, independently forwarded by intermediate nodes, and reassembled at the destination. In the KTU 2024 scheme context, packet switching is contrasted with **circuit switching** and is the fundamental switching paradigm used in the modern Internet. It operates on the **store-and-forward principle**, where each intermediate node (router) receives the entire packet, stores it temporarily, examines its header, determines the next hop, and then forwards it along the chosen route. ### 1.2 Key Terminology - **Packet**: A finite-length block of data consisting of a **header** (control info: source, destination, sequence number) and a **payload** (actual user data). - **Datagram**: An independent, self-contained packet that carries full addressing information and is routed independently. - **Virtual Circuit (VC)**: A logical (logical, not physical) dedicated path established between sender and receiver for the duration of a session; all packets of that session follow the same path. - **Store and Forward**: Operational mechanism where a router must receive the *entire* packet before transmitting it on the outgoing link. - **Cut-Through / Wormhole Switching**: A variant where the router begins transmitting the packet header before the full packet is received (less common; mainly in hardware switches). ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Postal System:** > Imagine you want to send a 500-page book to a friend in another city. Instead of renting an entire dedicated road (circuit switching) just to drive the book over, you: > 1. Tear the book into individual sheets/groups of sheets (packets). > 2. Write your friend's address and a page number on each envelope (header). > 3. Drop them all in the post office (network). > 4. Each post office (router) sorts envelopes based on the address and forwards them — possibly via different routes, planes, and trucks. > 5. The friend receives envelopes in random order, and uses the page numbers (sequence numbers) to reassemble. > > This is **packet switching** — *no dedicated path*, *shared infrastructure*, *independent routing*, *reassembly at destination*. ### 1.4 Why Packet Switching? | Property | Circuit Switching | Packet Switching | |---|---|---| | Connection setup | Required | Not required (datagram) | | Resource allocation | Dedicated | Shared (statistical multiplexing) | | Suitability for bursty data | Poor | Excellent | | Delay variability | Constant (after setup) | Variable (jitter) | | Reliability | Single path failure = total loss | Multiple paths possible | > [!TIP] > **KTU Board Exam Tip:** Whenever a question asks to "compare datagram and virtual circuit packet switching," always tabulate your answer. Examiners reward clarity and structure. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Two Approaches of Packet Switching #### 2.1.1 Datagram Packet Switching (Connectionless) - Each packet (datagram) is treated **independently**. - Each packet carries the **full destination address**. - Packets may travel via **different paths** and arrive **out of order**. - The destination is responsible for **reassembly** using sequence numbers. - **No connection state** is maintained in routers (stateless forwarding). - Used in: **IP networks (the Internet)**. **Operational Flow:** 1. Source breaks message into packets. 2. Each packet enters the network with destination address. 3. Each router independently consults its routing table and picks the next hop. 4. Packets may diverge in path due to congestion, link failure, or routing updates. 5. Destination reassembles using sequence numbers. #### 2.1.2 Virtual Circuit Packet Switching (Connection-Oriented) - A **logical path (VC)** is established *before* data transfer (similar to a phone call). - All packets of a session follow the **same path**. - Each packet carries a short **virtual circuit identifier (VCI)** instead of full address. - Routers maintain **connection state** (VCI translation tables). - Two sub-types: - **SVC (Switched Virtual Circuit)**: Set up and torn down per session (e.g., ATM, X.25). - **PVC (Permanent Virtual Circuit)**: Pre-configured by administrator (e.g., Frame Relay PVCs). **Three Phases:** 1. **Connection Setup**: Source sends a CALL REQUEST; each router allocates a VCI on each link; a path is established. 2. **Data Transfer**: All packets use the VCI for fast lookup. 3. **Teardown**: CALL CLEAR / RELEASE propagates through the path. ### 2.2 Store-and-Forward Principle > [!IMPORTANT] > **Store-and-Forward Transmission:** A router must buffer the **entire packet** in its memory before beginning to transmit any bit of it onto the outgoing link. This principle is the root cause of the **N×(L/R)** delay in multi-hop networks. ### 2.3 Pipelined Transmission Concept When a packet traverses **N links** (i.e., N−1 intermediate routers), the transmission is **pipelined**: - Link 1 starts transmitting packet 1 → after L/R, packet 1 reaches router 1 → router 1 starts sending to Link 2 → simultaneously, source starts sending packet 2 to Link 1. - This overlapping is called **pipelining**. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Variables Used:** > - $L$ = packet length (bits) > - $R$ = transmission rate of the link (bits/second) > - $N$ = number of links in the path (equivalently, number of packets in pipeline) > - $P$ = number of packets > - $d_{proc}$ = processing delay at each router > - $d_{queue}$ = queuing delay at each router > - $d_{trans}$ = transmission delay = $L/R$ > - $d_{prop}$ = propagation delay = $d/s$ where $d$ = link length, $s$ = propagation speed **Formula Table:** | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Boundary Conditions | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Transmission delay per link | $d_{trans} = L / R$ | seconds | $L > 0$, $R > 0$ | | 2 | Propagation delay per link | $d_{prop} = d / s$ | seconds | $s \approx 2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s in fiber | | 3 | Total nodal delay | $d_{nodal} = d_{proc} + d_{queue} + d_{trans} + d_{prop}$ | seconds | All four terms $\geq 0$ | | 4 | End-to-end delay (1 pkt, N links) | $d_{end} = N \times (d_{trans} + d_{prop}) + (N-1) \times d_{proc} + \sum d_{queue}$ | seconds | No pipelining of packets | | 5 | End-to-end delay (P packets, N links, store-and-forward, no queueing) | $d_{end} = (P + N - 1) \times (L/R) + P \times N \times d_{prop}$ | seconds | Pipelined transmission | | 6 | Throughput (single bottleneck) | $Throughput = \min(R_1, R_2, \dots, R_N)$ | bits/second | Bottleneck link governs | | 7 | Utilization (channel) | $U = (L/R) / (L/R + I)$ | dimensionless | $I$ = idle time | | 8 | Traffic intensity | $\rho = La / R$ | dimensionless | Must satisfy $\rho < 1$ for stable queue | | 9 | VCI table size per router | $O(VCs \times links)$ | entries | For SVCs and PVCs | > [!TIP] > **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used in **router queue design**, **QoS provisioning**, **network capacity planning**, and **5G core (service-based architecture)** where packets traverse multiple UPF (User Plane Function) nodes. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: End-to-End Delay for a Single Packet across N Links Consider a packet of length $L$ bits traversing a path of $N$ links, each of rate $R$ bps, with $N-1$ intermediate routers, and processing delay $d_{proc}$ at each router, propagation delay $d_{prop}$ on each link. **Step 1 — Transmission delay at the first link:** The source must push all $L$ bits onto link 1. $$ d_{trans,1} = \frac{L}{R} $$ **Step 2 — Propagation delay on link 1:** The first bit travels physically to router 1. $$ d_{prop,1} = \frac{d_1}{s_1} $$ **Step 3 — Processing at router 1:** The router reads the header, looks up routing table, decides the next hop. $$ d_{proc,1} = d_{proc} $$ **Step 4 — Repeat for each link:** Each of the $N$ links contributes $d_{trans} + d_{prop}$, and each of the $N-1$ intermediate routers contributes $d_{proc}$. Ignoring queuing for clarity: $$ d_{end} = \sum_{i=1}^{N}\left(\frac{L}{R_i} + \frac{d_i}{s_i}\right) + \sum_{j=1}^{N-1} d_{proc,j} $$ **Step 5 — Simplification for homogeneous links:** If all links have rate $R$, length $d$, and propagation speed $s$, and all routers have identical processing delay $d_{proc}$: $$ \begin{aligned} d_{end} &= N \cdot \frac{L}{R} + N \cdot \frac{d}{s} + (N-1) \cdot d_{proc} \\ d_{end} &= N \cdot \left(\frac{L}{R} + \frac{d}{s}\right) + (N-1) \cdot d_{proc} \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.2 Derivation: End-to-End Delay for P Packets with Pipelining (Store-and-Forward) This is the **most heavily tested derivation** in KTU board papers for Module 3. **Setup:** Source sends $P$ packets, each of length $L$ bits, across $N$ links (each of rate $R$ bps). Processing and propagation delays are ignored for clarity, but the analysis includes them by addition at the end. **Step 1 — Time to push the first packet onto link 1:** The source takes $L/R$ seconds. **Step 2 — Time to push the first packet through the remaining N-1 links:** The first packet must be transmitted $N$ times in total (once per link). So total time for packet 1 to reach the destination is: $$ T_1 = N \cdot \frac{L}{R} $$ **Step 3 — Time to send the remaining P-1 packets:** Because of pipelining, the remaining packets can be sent back-to-back. After the first packet reaches link 2, the source can start sending packet 2 onto link 1. The bottleneck for the *last* packet is the time to push it through the **last link**, which is $L/R$. The remaining packets $(P-1)$ are spaced $L/R$ apart. After packet 1 finishes (at time $N \cdot L/R$), packet $P$ finishes at time: $$ T_P = N \cdot \frac{L}{R} + (P-1) \cdot \frac{L}{R} $$ **Step 4 — Final compact formula:** $$ \boxed{\,d_{end} = (P + N - 1) \cdot \frac{L}{R}\,} $$ **Step 5 — Including propagation and processing delays:** $$ \boxed{\,d_{end} = (P + N - 1) \cdot \frac{L}{R} + N \cdot d_{prop} + (N-1) \cdot d_{proc} + \sum d_{queue}\,} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Insight:** When $P = 1$, the formula reduces to $N \cdot L/R$ (no pipelining benefit), which is correct because a single packet has nothing to overlap with. When $N = 1$, the formula reduces to $P \cdot L/R$, which is just sequential transmission on a single link. Both boundary conditions are **mathematically consistent**. ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style) **Problem:** A source sends 5 packets, each of 1000 bits, across a path of 4 links. Each link has a transmission rate of 1 Mbps. Processing and propagation delays are negligible. Compute: (a) Time for the first packet to reach the destination. (b) Time for all 5 packets to reach the destination. (c) Effective throughput. **Given:** - $P = 5$ - $L = 1000$ bits - $N = 4$ links - $R = 10^{6}$ bps **Solution (a):** $$ T_1 = N \cdot \frac{L}{R} = 4 \cdot \frac{1000}{10^6} = 4 \cdot 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 4 \text{ ms} $$ **Solution (b):** $$ d_{end} = (P + N - 1) \cdot \frac{L}{R} = (5 + 4 - 1) \cdot \frac{1000}{10^6} = 8 \cdot 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 8 \text{ ms} $$ **Solution (c):** $$ \text{Throughput} = \frac{\text{Total data}}{\text{Time}} = \frac{5 \cdot 1000}{8 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{5000}{0.008} = 625{,}000 \text{ bps} = 0.625 \text{ Mbps} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Observation:** The effective throughput ($0.625$ Mbps) is **less than the link capacity** ($1$ Mbps) because of the store-and-forward overhead at intermediate routers. This is a common board exam trap — students often incorrectly state the throughput equals $R$. ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Datagram vs Virtual Circuit | Criterion | Datagram | Virtual Circuit | |---|---|---| | Connection setup | Not required | Required | | Addressing | Full address in every packet | Short VCI per packet | | Path | May differ per packet | Fixed for entire session | | Router state | Stateless (no per-flow state) | Stateful (VCI table) | | Out-of-order delivery | Possible | Not possible (in-order) | | Congestion handling | Adaptive routing possible | Path is fixed; less adaptive | | Setup latency | Zero | Non-zero (call setup) | | Header overhead | High (full address) | Low (short VCI) | | Failure resilience | High (alternate paths) | Lower (whole VC fails) | | Example protocols | IP, UDP | ATM, Frame Relay, X.25 | ### 3.5 Effect of Traffic Intensity on Queuing Delay Using a simple $M/M/1$ queueing approximation: $$ d_{queue} = \frac{\rho}{1 - \rho} \cdot \frac{L}{R} $$ where $\rho = L \cdot a / R$ and $a$ is the average packet arrival rate (packets/sec). As $\rho \to 1$, the queueing delay **diverges** — the system becomes unstable. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 End-to-End Packet Flow Across Multiple Routers ```mermaid flowchart LR A["SOURCE HOST"] -->|"Packet 1, 2, 3..."| R1["ROUTER 1
Store and Forward
VCI Lookup or IP Lookup"] R1 -->|"Forwarded Packet"| R2["ROUTER 2
Store and Forward
Routing Table Consult"] R2 -->|"Forwarded Packet"| R3["ROUTER 3
Store and Forward
Next Hop Selection"] R3 -->|"Final Hop"| B["DESTINATION HOST
Reassembly Buffer"] R1 -.->|"Out-of-order
datagram path"| B R2 -.->|"Alternate path
(datagram only)"| B ``` ### 4.2 Datagram vs Virtual Circuit — Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph DAT["DATAGRAM NETWORK (e.g., IP)"] direction TB S1["Source"] -->|"Full IP header
in every packet"| X1["Router 1
No per-flow state"] X1 -->|"Independent
next-hop lookup"| X2["Router 2
No per-flow state"] X2 -->|"Independent
next-hop lookup"| D1["Destination
Reassemble"] end subgraph VCT["VIRTUAL CIRCUIT NETWORK (e.g., ATM)"] direction TB S2["Source"] -->|"CALL REQ
Establish VC"| Y1["Router 1
VCI 5 to VCI 11"] Y1 -->|"VCI 11"| Y2["Router 2
VCI 11 to VCI 7"] Y2 -->|"VCI 7"| D2["Destination
In-order delivery"] end ``` ### 4.3 Packet Switching — Internal Functional Block of a Router ```mermaid flowchart LR IN["Incoming Link
Physical Layer"] --> PHY["PHY Receiver
Bit Clock Recovery"] PHY --> L2["Data Link Layer
Framing and CRC Check"] L2 --> QUEUE["Input Queue
Buffer per Interface"] QUEUE --> PROC["Packet Processor
Header Parsing and Lookup"] PROC --> ROUTE["Routing Engine
Longest Prefix Match"] ROUTE --> SWITCH["Switching Fabric
Crossbar or Shared Bus"] SWITCH --> OUTQ["Output Queue
Buffer per Interface"] OUTQ --> TX["PHY Transmitter
Serialization"] TX --> OUT["Outgoing Link"] ``` ### 4.4 Pipelined Transmission — Timing Diagram ```mermaid gantt title Pipelined Transmission of 3 Packets across 3 Links dateFormat ss.SSS axisFormat %S.%L section Link 1 Packet 1 on Link 1 :a1, 0.000, 1000ms Packet 2 on Link 1 :a2, after a1, 1000ms Packet 3 on Link 1 :a3, after a2, 1000ms section Link 2 Packet 1 on Link 2 :b1, after a1, 1000ms Packet 2 on Link 2 :b2, after b1, 1000ms Packet 3 on Link 2 :b3, after b2, 1000ms section Link 3 Packet 1 on Link 3 :c1, after b1, 1000ms Packet 2 on Link 3 :c2, after c1, 1000ms Packet 3 on Link 3 :c3, after c2, 1000ms ``` > [!NOTE] > In the timing diagram above, each link begins forwarding its packet exactly $L/R$ seconds after the previous link finished — this is the **store-and-forward pipeline**. Total time = $(P + N - 1) \cdot L/R = (3 + 3 - 1) \cdot L/R = 5 \cdot L/R$. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024, Model]` **Differentiate between datagram packet switching and virtual circuit packet switching. Mention one protocol example for each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Datagram | Virtual Circuit | |---|---|---| | Connection | Connectionless | Connection-oriented | | Addressing | Full destination address in every packet | Short VCI per packet | | Path | Independent per packet | Fixed per session | | Example | **IP (Internet Protocol)** | **ATM (Asynchronous Transfer Mode)** | *(1 mark each for difference, 1 mark for examples)* --- **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]` **Explain the store-and-forward mechanism in packet switching. How does it influence end-to-end delay?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** In store-and-forward transmission, a router **receives the entire packet** in its buffer *before* transmitting even a single bit of it on the outgoing link. This is the fundamental reason why an N-link path introduces an $N \cdot L/R$ delay component. (2 marks) The advantage is **integrity** — the full packet header can be examined, and CRC errors can be detected before forwarding. (1 mark) --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Q]` **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the operation of **datagram packet switching**. List any **four advantages** and **two disadvantages** of datagram networks. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A message of $M = 640{,}000$ bits is to be transmitted across a network path of $N = 4$ links. Each link has a transmission rate of $R = 1$ Mbps. The message is broken into $P = 10$ packets, each of $L = 64{,}000$ bits. (i) Compute the time taken for the **first packet** to reach the destination, neglecting propagation and queuing delays. (ii) Compute the time taken for the **last packet** to reach the destination. (iii) Compute the **effective end-to-end throughput** for transmitting the entire message. (iv) If propagation delay per link is $d_{prop} = 2$ ms, recompute the total time for all packets. **(7 marks)** #### **Model Solution for Q.A:** **Part (a) — 7 marks:** In datagram packet switching, the message is divided into independent packets (datagrams), each carrying the full destination address. Each router forwards datagrams independently based on its current routing table. Packets may take different paths and may arrive out of order; the destination reassembles them using sequence numbers. *Diagram (3 marks):* ```mermaid flowchart LR S["Source
M to P1 P2 P3"] --> R1["Router 1
Independent Forward"] R1 --> R2["Router 2
Independent Forward"] R2 --> R3["Router 3
Independent Forward"] R3 --> D["Destination
Reassembly"] R1 -.->|"alt route"| R2 ``` *Four advantages (2 marks):* 1. No call setup — low initial delay. 2. Robust to link/node failure (alternate routing). 3. Efficient for bursty traffic (statistical multiplexing). 4. Stateless routers — easier to scale. *Two disadvantages (2 marks):* 1. Variable delay and out-of-order delivery. 2. Higher header overhead (full address in each packet). **Part (b) — 7 marks:** **Given:** $M = 640{,}000$ bits, $N = 4$ links, $R = 10^{6}$ bps, $P = 10$ packets, $L = 64{,}000$ bits, $d_{prop} = 2$ ms. **(i) Time for first packet to reach destination:** $$ T_1 = N \cdot \frac{L}{R} = 4 \cdot \frac{64{,}000}{10^6} = 4 \cdot 64 \times 10^{-3} = 256 \text{ ms} $$ **(ii) Time for last packet:** $$ d_{end} = (P + N - 1) \cdot \frac{L}{R} = (10 + 4 - 1) \cdot 64 \times 10^{-3} = 13 \cdot 64 \text{ ms} = 832 \text{ ms} $$ **(iii) Effective throughput:** $$ \text{Throughput} = \frac{M}{d_{end}} = \frac{640{,}000}{0.832} = 769{,}230.77 \text{ bps} \approx 0.769 \text{ Mbps} $$ **(iv) Including propagation delay:** $$ d_{end} = (P + N - 1) \cdot \frac{L}{R} + N \cdot d_{prop} = 832 \text{ ms} + 4 \cdot 2 \text{ ms} = 832 + 8 = 840 \text{ ms} $$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Q]` **(a)** Explain the **three phases of virtual circuit packet switching** with a suitable diagram. State any **two advantages** of virtual circuit networks over datagram networks. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Consider a packet-switched network with three links in the path. Link rates are $R_1 = 2$ Mbps, $R_2 = 1$ Mbps, $R_3 = 4$ Mbps. A file of size 1 Mbit is transmitted in packets of 200 kbits each. (i) How many packets are generated? (ii) Compute the **end-to-end delay** for the entire file (store-and-forward pipelined, neglect $d_{prop}$, $d_{proc}$, $d_{queue}$). (iii) Identify the **bottleneck link** and compute the **end-to-end throughput**. **(7 marks)** #### **Model Solution for Q.B:** **Part (a) — 7 marks:** The three phases of virtual circuit switching are: 1. **Connection Establishment:** Source sends a CALL REQUEST. Each router along the path allocates a Virtual Circuit Identifier (VCI) on its outgoing link, and the call proceeds hop-by-hop. A VC is established from source to destination. 2. **Data Transfer:** All packets of the session use the VCI as a short label. Routers perform fast table lookup (VCI in → VCI out, outgoing link) and forward. 3. **Connection Teardown:** Either side sends a CLEAR / RELEASE message, and each router deallocates the VCI entries. *Diagram (3 marks):* ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant S as Source participant R1 as Router 1 participant R2 as Router 2 participant D as Destination S->>R1: CALL REQ (VCI 5) R1->>R2: CALL REQ (VCI 11) R2->>D: CALL REQ (VCI 7) D-->>R2: CALL ACCEPT R2-->>R1: CALL ACCEPT R1-->>S: CALL ACCEPT loop Data Phase S->>R1: Data with VCI 5 R1->>R2: Data with VCI 11 R2->>D: Data with VCI 7 end S->>R1: CLEAR R1->>R2: CLEAR R2->>D: CLEAR ``` *Two advantages (2 marks):* 1. **In-order delivery** guaranteed — simpler receiver logic. 2. **Smaller header overhead** — short VCI replaces full address. 3. (Bonus) Fast forwarding at routers — VCI table is faster than longest-prefix match. *Two advantages of VC (state explicitly in answer):* In-order delivery and smaller header overhead. **Part (b) — 7 marks:** **Given:** File size $= 1$ Mbit $= 1{,}000{,}000$ bits, packet size $L = 200$ kbits $= 200{,}000$ bits, $R_1 = 2$ Mbps, $R_2 = 1$ Mbps, $R_3 = 4$ Mbps, $N = 3$ links. **(i) Number of packets:** $$ P = \frac{1{,}000{,}000}{200{,}000} = 5 \text{ packets} $$ **(ii) End-to-end delay:** This is a *heterogeneous* rate path, so we must compute the per-link transmission delay for each link and then apply the pipeline principle with the bottleneck. Transmission delays: - Link 1: $L/R_1 = 200{,}000 / 2 \times 10^6 = 0.1$ s - Link 2: $L/R_2 = 200{,}000 / 1 \times 10^6 = 0.2$ s (bottleneck) - Link 3: $L/R_3 = 200{,}000 / 4 \times 10^6 = 0.05$ s For pipelined transmission across heterogeneous links, the formula becomes (in store-and-forward, where each link imposes its own delay): $$ d_{end} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{L}{R_i} + (P-1) \cdot \max_{i}\left(\frac{L}{R_i}\right) $$ $$ \begin{aligned} d_{end} &= 0.1 + 0.2 + 0.05 + (5-1) \cdot 0.2 \\ d_{end} &= 0.35 + 0.8 = 1.15 \text{ s} \end{aligned} $$ **(iii) Bottleneck and throughput:** The bottleneck is **Link 2** with the slowest rate (1 Mbps). $$ \text{Throughput} = \frac{\text{Total data}}{\text{Time}} = \frac{1{,}000{,}000}{1.15} = 869{,}565.22 \text{ bps} \approx 0.87 \text{ Mbps} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistakes Students Make:** > 1. **Forgetting pipelining**: Many students compute $5 \times (0.1 + 0.2 + 0.05) = 1.75$ s, which is **wrong**. The correct pipelined answer is $1.15$ s. Marks are deducted heavily for this. > 2. **Confusing $P$ and $N$**: $P$ = number of packets, $N$ = number of links. Reversing them gives nonsense. > 3. **Bottleneck confusion**: Some students use the *average* of link rates or the *fastest* link rate. The throughput is governed by the **slowest** link. > 4. **Ignoring units**: $R$ must be in bps, $L$ in bits, time in seconds. Mixing kbits and Mbps without conversion is a frequent 1-mark deduction. > 5. **Skipping the diagram**: For 7-mark questions, a **diagram is mandatory** and carries 2-3 marks. Drawing the network with labelled routers and links is essential. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Packet Switching Principles** - [x] **Packet Switching** = divide message into packets, each routed independently through shared network. - [x] **Two flavours**: **Datagram** (connectionless, IP) and **Virtual Circuit** (connection-oriented, ATM/X.25). - [x] **Store-and-Forward** is the core principle — full packet buffered before forwarding. - [x] **Pipelined transmission** formula for $P$ packets over $N$ links: $d_{end} = (P + N - 1) \cdot L/R$. - [x] **Four types of delay** at a router: $d_{proc}$, $d_{queue}$, $d_{trans} = L/R$, $d_{prop} = d/s$. - [x] **Throughput** is governed by the **bottleneck link**: $\min(R_1, R_2, \dots, R_N)$. - [x] **Traffic intensity** $\rho = La/R$ must stay $< 1$ to avoid unbounded queueing. - [x] **VCI** is a short label used in virtual circuits for fast router lookup. - [x] **Datagram advantages**: no setup, robust, efficient for bursts. - [x] **VC advantages**: in-order delivery, low header overhead, fast forwarding. - [x] **Out-of-order** is a feature of datagrams and a *bug to handle* at the receiver. - [x] **Boundary checks** for the pipeline formula: $P=1 \Rightarrow d_{end} = N \cdot L/R$ (no pipelining); $N=1 \Rightarrow d_{end} = P \cdot L/R$ (sequential). - [x] **Draw diagrams** in 7-mark and 14-mark questions — they carry 2-3 marks. - [x] **Recheck units** before writing the final numerical answer.

topics.title: X.25
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCERT602 - DATA COMMUNICATION AND NETWORKING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** X.25 # X.25 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **X.25 (ITU-T Recommendation X.25)** is an **interface standard** defined by the International Telegraph and Telephone Consultative Committee (CCITT, now ITU-T) that specifies how a **Data Terminal Equipment (DTE)** — such as a computer or terminal — connects to a **Data Circuit-terminating Equipment (DCE)** — such as a packet-switching node — to access a **public packet-switched network (PSDN)**. The standard was first approved in **1976** and revised subsequently. It is fundamentally a **connection-oriented, virtual-circuit-based** packet-switching protocol suite spanning the **bottom three layers** of the OSI reference model: | OSI Layer | X.25 Equivalent Protocol | | :--- | :--- | | Physical Layer (L1) | **X.21** (or X.21bis / RS-232 fallback) | | Data Link Layer (L2) | **LAP-B** (Link Access Procedure – Balanced) | | Network Layer (L3) | **X.25 PLP** (Packet Layer Protocol) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** X.25 is grouped with the legacy WAN protocol stack. The 2024 scheme emphasizes its role as the *precursor* to Frame Relay and as the canonical example of a packet-switched interface standard — students must know its **layered architecture, virtual circuit types, frame formats, and call-establishment phases**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Postal Registered Mail" Model Imagine a **postal system** in 1970s Europe: - You go to the **post office counter (DTE–DCE boundary)**. - You hand over a **registered letter (packet)** along with a **tracking slip (virtual circuit number)**. - The clerk (DCE) puts the letter into a **bag with a bag-tag (logical channel number, LCN)** shared between your city and the destination city. - Along the way, multiple bags are **re-routed, stored, and forwarded** through sorting offices (packet switches). - At the destination, the receiving clerk checks the bag-tag, delivers the letter, and **acknowledges receipt** (RR — Receiver Ready) — only then do you know it arrived safely. This is precisely how X.25 works: - **DTE ↔ DCE** is the "counter" where X.25 enforces its rules. - **Inside the network**, packets can take any physical route, but logically belong to a **pre-established virtual circuit** — like a bag-tag that follows the letter end-to-end. - **Acknowledgments** flow back, ensuring reliability — a hallmark of X.25 that distinguishes it from leaner protocols like IP. ## 1.3 Why Was X.25 Designed This Way? In the **1970s**, the physical transmission medium was **analog copper**, highly prone to **noise, errors, and dropouts** (typical bit error rate ≈ **10⁻⁵**). Therefore, X.25 deliberately incorporated: - **Heavy error detection and correction** at every hop (store-and-forward with retransmission). - **Flow control** using sliding windows. - **Conservative acknowledgments** before sending the next packet. This made X.25 **slow by modern standards** (typical throughput **64 kbps to 2 Mbps**) but **extremely reliable** over noisy lines. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Metrics to Remember:** > - Maximum packet size: **128 bytes** (default), up to **4096 bytes** > - Standard virtual circuits: up to **4095 logical channels** per DTE–DCE interface > - Default window size: **2** (modulo 8), expandable to **modulo 128** (window size **127**) > - Theoretical maximum throughput: **~2 Mbps** (practically much lower) > - Bit error tolerance: Designed for BER of **10⁻⁵** --- # X.25 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet ## 2.1 The Three-Layer Architecture of X.25 ### Layer 1 — Physical Layer (X.21 / X.21bis) - **X.21**: The original ITU-T recommendation for synchronous operation over digital circuits. Uses **8-pin connector** and balanced electrical signaling. - **X.21bis (fallback)**: A transitional standard that allowed use of **V.24 / V.28 (RS-232-C)** for compatibility with analog circuits. - Defines mechanical, electrical, functional, and procedural characteristics for bit-level transmission. ### Layer 2 — Data Link Layer (LAP-B) **LAP-B (Link Access Procedure — Balanced)** is a **subset of HDLC** (High-Level Data Link Control), specifically the **Asynchronous Balanced Mode (ABM)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > LAP-B provides a **reliable, in-order, error-free** point-to-point link between the DTE and the DCE. It is *only* used on the DTE–DCE segment, **not** between intermediate packet switches. #### LAP-B Frame Format (HDLC-like)
Flag (8) Address (8) Control (8) Information (variable) FCS (16) Flag (8)
01111110 DTE/DCE address Frame type User data CRC-16 01111110
**Field Explanations (in bits):** - **Flag (8 bits)**: `01111110` — bit-stuffing applied to prevent accidental flag in data. - **Address (8 bits)**: Contains a single bit indicating whether the frame is a **command** or **response** (essentially identifies DTE vs. DCE direction). - **Control (8 bits)**: Identifies the frame type: - `I` (Information) — carries upper-layer data - `S` (Supervisory) — RR, RNR, REJ, SREJ (flow/error control) - `U` (Unnumbered) — SABM, DISC, UA, FRMR (link setup/teardown) - **Information (variable)**: User payload from Layer 3. - **FCS (16 bits)**: **CRC-16** (Cyclic Redundancy Check) — ITU-T polynomial. - **Flag (8 bits)**: Closing delimiter. #### LAP-B Frame Types Summary
Type Mnemonic Purpose
U SABM Set Asynchronous Balanced Mode (link setup)
U DISC Disconnect
U UA Unnumbered Acknowledgment
U FRMR Frame Reject (reporting protocol error)
S RR Receiver Ready (positive ACK + flow control)
S RNR Receiver Not Ready (flow control)
S REJ Reject (NACK, go-back-N)
S SREJ Selective Reject (selective retransmit)
I I-frame Information (carries X.25 packet)
### Layer 3 — Network/Packet Layer (X.25 PLP) The **X.25 Packet Layer Protocol (PLP)** is responsible for: 1. **Virtual circuit management** (establishing, maintaining, terminating). 2. **Packet multiplexing** over a single physical link. 3. **End-to-end addressing** using **X.121** numbering. 4. **Optional flow and error control** (using D-bit, M-bit, Q-bit). #### X.121 Addressing Every X.25 DTE has a unique **X.121 address** of up to **14 decimal digits**: - **First 4 digits**: DNIC (Data Network Identification Code) — country + network. - **Remaining digits (up to 10)**: NTN (National Terminal Number) — subscriber ID within the network. Example: `2624-1234567890` → Germany (262) network 4, terminal 1234567890. #### Virtual Circuits (VCs) X.25 supports **two types** of virtual circuits: > [!NOTE] > **Virtual Circuit (VC)** — A logical, end-to-end path established between two DTEs *before* any data is sent. All packets of a call follow the **same route** through the network. **A. Switched Virtual Circuit (SVC)** - Set up dynamically per session (analogous to a phone call). - Goes through three phases: **Call Setup → Data Transfer → Call Clearing**. **B. Permanent Virtual Circuit (PVC)** - Set up permanently by the network administrator. - No setup/teardown phases — always ready to carry data. - Analogous to a "leased line" in circuit switching. **Logical Channel Numbers (LCNs)** Each virtual circuit is identified by a **4-bit group number** and an **8-bit logical channel number** — yielding up to **4095 logical channels** per DTE–DCE interface (channels 0 and 4095 are reserved). The LCN is the **bag-tag** in our postal analogy. ## 2.2 X.25 Packet Format
Flag (8) Address (8) Control (8) X.25 Packet Header User Data FCS (16) Flag (8)
01111110 — — 3 bytes (minimum) Up to 4096 bytes CRC-16 01111110
### The X.25 Packet Header (3 bytes minimum, for call setup packets)
Byte 1 Byte 2 Byte 3
General Format Identifier (GFI) — 4 bits: Q bit, D bit, Modulo Logical Channel Group Number (4 bits) + upper 4 bits of LCN Lower 8 bits of Logical Channel Number (LCN)
**GFI Field (4 bits):**
Q (1 bit) D (1 bit) Modulo (2 bits)
Qualifier — 0 = control packet contains user data, 1 = special diagnostic Delivery confirmation — 0 = local ACK only, 1 = end-to-end ACK 01 = modulo 8 (sequence 0–7), 10 = modulo 128 (sequence 0–127)
## 2.3 Call Phases of an SVC 1. **Idle State** — No active virtual circuit. 2. **Call Setup** — DTE sends **CALL REQUEST** packet → DCE sends **CALL ACCEPTED** back if far end agrees. 3. **Data Transfer** — Bidirectional **DATA packets** flow; sequence numbers track order. 4. **Call Clearing** — DTE sends **CLEAR REQUEST** → DCE confirms with **CLEAR CONFIRMATION**. 5. **Return to Idle.** ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula & Reference Sheet | **Parameter** | **Value / Definition** | | :--- | :--- | | Maximum logical channels per DTE | **4095** | | Maximum packet size | **4096 bytes** (default 128) | | Default sequence numbering | **Modulo 8** (0–7) | | Extended sequence numbering | **Modulo 128** (0–127) | | Default window size (modulo 8) | **2** (sequence numbers 0, 1) | | Extended window size (modulo 128) | **up to 127** | | Address length (X.121) | **Up to 14 decimal digits** | | Physical layer connector (X.21) | **15-pin D-sub** | | Maximum throughput | **~2 Mbps** theoretical | | Typical BER environment | **10⁻⁵** | | Link layer protocol | **LAP-B** (ABM mode of HDLC) | | Error detection | **CRC-16** (ITU-T) | | Bit stuffing | **Insert 0 after five consecutive 1s** | | Flag byte | **`01111110`** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Real-World Utility:** X.25 is largely **obsolete** in commercial Internet backbones, but it is **still operational** in: > - **Banking and financial networks** (e.g., SWIFT used X.25 for decades). > - **Credit card authorization** terminals. > - **Aviation and defense** legacy systems. > - **Railway signaling** in some countries. > Understanding X.25 is essential for **network forensics, legacy system integration, and historical context** for Frame Relay, ATM, and TCP/IP evolution. --- # X.25 — Step-by-Step Analysis, Call-Setup Walkthrough & Numerical Exercises ## 3.1 Complete Walkthrough: Establishing a Switched Virtual Circuit We trace a packet-switched call from DTE-A to DTE-B through a public X.25 network. **Step 1 — Idle State** Both DTE-A and DTE-B have no active virtual circuits. All LCNs are free (0 and 4095 remain reserved permanently). **Step 2 — Call Request** DTE-A's application wishes to communicate with DTE-B (X.121 address e.g., `2624-9876543210`). The X.25 PLP at DTE-A: - Assigns a free **LCN** (say `0001 0000 0001` → LCN 001, group 1). - Constructs a **CALL REQUEST** packet containing the destination X.121 address, the source X.121 address, the requested facilities, and the call user data. - Hands the packet to LAP-B, which wraps it in an **I-frame** with sequence number 0. **Step 3 — Link-Level Transmission** LAP-B at DTE-A: - Inserts two **flags** (`01111110`) at start and end. - Applies **bit stuffing** (inserts a 0 after any run of five 1s in the payload). - Computes **CRC-16** over Address + Control + Information fields and appends it as **FCS**. - Transmits the frame over the physical link. **Step 4 — DCE Reception and Forwarding** - The DCE (network access node) checks **FCS** — if error-free, sends **RR (Receiver Ready)** to DTE-A to confirm receipt. - The DCE reads the destination X.121 address from the CALL REQUEST packet, performs **routing table lookup**, and forwards the packet one hop toward DTE-B's network. - The packet traverses multiple **packet switches**, each performing store-and-forward. At each switch, errors are checked, ACKs are exchanged, and the packet is forwarded on the best available route. **Step 5 — Call Acceptance at DTE-B** - DTE-B's DCE receives the CALL REQUEST and passes it to DTE-B's PLP. - If DTE-B accepts, it returns a **CALL ACCEPTED** packet using the same LCN. - This CALL ACCEPTED travels back through the network to DTE-A. - DTE-A's PLP, upon receiving CALL ACCEPTED, transitions to **Data Transfer state**. **Step 6 — Data Transfer** - Now both DTEs exchange **DATA packets**. Each DATA packet carries: - The same LCN (the virtual circuit identifier). - A **send sequence number P(S)** and **receive sequence number P(R)**. - The **M-bit (More data bit)** set to 1 if more packets belong to the same logical message, 0 if this is the last packet. - The **D-bit (Delivery confirmation bit)** — if set to 1, the network must wait for end-to-end ACK; if 0, local ACK is sufficient. - The **Q-bit (Qualifier bit)** — set to 1 if the payload contains control/diagnostic information rather than user data. - Acknowledgments use **RR** (positive) or **REJ** (negative — go-back-N retransmission). **Step 7 — Call Clearing** - When communication is complete, DTE-A sends a **CLEAR REQUEST** packet. - DTE-B responds with a **CLEAR CONFIRMATION** packet. - The LCN is **released** and becomes available for a future call. - Both DTEs return to **Idle state**. ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Exercise 1: Window Size and Throughput **Problem:** A DTE uses modulo 8 sequence numbering with a default window size of 2. Calculate: (a) The maximum number of unacknowledged I-frames. (b) The maximum number of distinct send sequence numbers P(S) that can be used. (c) The minimum number of bits needed to encode P(S) in the Control field. **Solution:** **(a)** Maximum unacknowledged I-frames = **Window Size = 2**. **(b)** With modulo 8, sequence numbers cycle through **0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7** and wrap around. Therefore, distinct P(S) values = **8**. **(c)** To encode 8 distinct values, we need $n$ bits such that $2^n \geq 8$. $$ 2^n \geq 8 $$ $$ n \geq \log_2(8) = 3 $$ **Therefore, a minimum of 3 bits are needed** to encode P(S) when using modulo 8. For modulo 128, $2^n \geq 128 \implies n \geq 7$ bits. ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Exercise 2: Bit Stuffing in LAP-B **Problem:** The following data payload is to be transmitted in a LAP-B frame (excluding flags and FCS). Apply bit stuffing and show the transmitted bit stream. **Original data (between flags):** `011011111101111101111111` **Solution:** **Step 1:** Identify every occurrence of five consecutive 1s and insert a 0 after the fifth 1. Scanning the bit string `011011111101111101111111`: - Position 3–7: `11111` (five 1s) → insert 0 → `111110` - Continuing, we look for the **next** run of five 1s in the *stuffed* stream. - After stuffing the first run: `01101111101111101111111` - The next run of five 1s begins at the new `11111` (after the inserted 0) → insert 0 → `111110` - Stuffed so far: `0110111110111110101111111` - Next run: `11111` (the last six 1s become a new run of five) → insert 0 → `111110` **Step 2: Final stuffed bit stream (between flags):** $$ 0110111110\,1111101\,01111101 $$ > Where bold indicates the inserted stuffing bits (`0` after each run of five 1s). **Step 3: Receiver's De-Stuffing Process** At the receiving DCE, LAP-B scans the incoming bit stream (after stripping flags) and **removes any 0 that immediately follows five consecutive 1s** — thereby recovering the original data exactly. ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Exercise 3: X.25 Packet Counting in a Session **Problem:** DTE-A sends a message consisting of 4 packets over an SVC. The window size is 2 (modulo 8). How many RR (Receiver Ready) acknowledgments are required to complete the transfer, assuming no errors? Show the sequence of P(S) and P(R) values. **Solution:** - Window size $W = 2$, so DTE-A may send up to 2 packets before receiving an ACK. - Initial P(S) = 0, P(R) = 0. **Round 1:** - DTE-A sends Packet 1: P(S) = 0 - DTE-A sends Packet 2: P(S) = 1 - DTE-B receives both, sends **RR with P(R) = 2** ("I have received up to P(S)=1, I expect P(S)=2 next") **Round 2:** - DTE-A receives RR (P(R)=2), advances its lower window edge. - DTE-A sends Packet 3: P(S) = 2 - DTE-A sends Packet 4: P(S) = 3 - DTE-B receives both, sends **RR with P(R) = 4** **Total RR acknowledgments needed = 2** (one per window of data). > [!NOTE] > **General formula** for a burst of $N$ packets with window size $W$: > $$\text{Number of RR frames} = \left\lceil \frac{N}{W} \right\rceil$$ > Substituting $N = 4$, $W = 2$: > $$\text{RR frames} = \left\lceil \frac{4}{2} \right\rceil = 2 \quad \checkmark$$ ## 3.5 Worked Numerical Exercise 4: Call Setup Latency **Problem:** An X.25 SVC call must traverse 4 packet switches between DTE-A and DTE-B. Each switch introduces a processing + queuing delay of 50 ms. The call setup packet must travel from DTE-A to DTE-B (call accepted must travel back). Calculate the minimum call setup time. (Ignore propagation delay.) **Solution:** The CALL REQUEST packet must traverse all 4 switches to reach DTE-B. The CALL ACCEPTED packet must traverse all 4 switches back to DTE-A. **Total switches visited:** 4 (one-way) + 4 (return) = 8 switch crossings. $$ T_{\text{setup}} = 8 \times 50\,\text{ms} = 400\,\text{ms} $$ **Minimum call setup time = 400 ms** (assuming zero propagation delay and instant processing at the destination DTE). In real networks, we would add: $T_{\text{propagation}} = (D / v) \times 2$ where $D$ is the one-way distance and $v$ is the signal propagation speed (≈ $2 \times 10^8$ m/s in copper). ## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Virtual Circuit Types | **Attribute** | **PVC** | **SVC** | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Establishment | Pre-configured by admin | Dynamic per call | | Setup delay | None | 1–2 RTTs | | Cost | Higher (always-on) | Pay-per-use | | Use case | Frequent, repetitive traffic | Occasional, bursty traffic | | Resource usage | LCN held permanently | LCN held only for call duration | | Analogy | Leased telephone line | Dial-up phone call | --- # X.25 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 X.25 Protocol Stack vs. OSI Model The following Mermaid diagram shows the mapping between X.25's protocol layers and the OSI reference model, including the DTE/DCE boundary. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph OSI["OSI Reference Model"] L7["Layer 7 - Application"] L6["Layer 6 - Presentation"] L5["Layer 5 - Session"] L4["Layer 4 - Transport"] L3["Layer 3 - Network"] L2["Layer 2 - Data Link"] L1["Layer 1 - Physical"] end subgraph DTE_Side["DTE (Data Terminal Equipment)"] APP["User Application"] PLP["X.25 PLP\nPacket Layer Protocol"] LAPB_DTE["LAP-B\nLink Access Procedure Balanced"] PHYS_DTE["X.21 / X.21bis\nPhysical Interface"] end subgraph DCE_Side["DCE (Data Circuit-terminating Equipment)"] PHYS_DCE["X.21 / X.21bis\nNetwork-side"] LAPB_DCE["LAP-B"] PLP_DCE["X.25 PLP"] PSN["Packet Switch Node\nStore-and-Forward"] end L1 -.corresponds to.-> PHYS_DTE L2 -.corresponds to.-> LAPB_DTE L3 -.corresponds to.-> PLP L4 -.transparent.-> L4 L5 -.transparent.-> L5 L6 -.transparent.-> L6 L7 -.transparent.-> APP APP --> PLP PLP --> LAPB_DTE LAPB_DTE --> PHYS_DTE PHYS_DTE <==>"X.25 Interface\nDTE-DCE Boundary"==> PHYS_DCE PHYS_DCE --> LAPB_DCE LAPB_DCE --> PLP_DCE PLP_DCE --> PSN PSN -.carries packets to.-> REMOTE["Remote DTE via network"] ``` ## 4.2 LAP-B Frame Structure (Detailed) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph LAPB_Frame["LAP-B Frame"] F1["Flag\n01111110\n8 bits"] A["Address\n8 bits"] C["Control\n8 bits"] I["Information\nVariable\n(up to payload size)"] FCS["FCS\nCRC-16\n16 bits"] F2["Flag\n01111110\n8 bits"] end F1 --> A --> C --> I --> FCS --> F2 A_note["Contains\nCommand/Response bit\nidentifying DTE or DCE"] C_note["I-frame, S-frame\nor U-frame\nas per HDLC"] I_note["Carries X.25\nPacket Layer\nProtocol data"] FCS_note["Computed over\nAddress + Control\n+ Information"] A -.info.-> A_note C -.info.-> C_note I -.info.-> I_note FCS -.info.-> FCS_note ``` ## 4.3 X.25 SVC Call Lifecycle — State Diagram ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Idle Idle --> CallSetup: DTE sends CALL REQUEST CallSetup --> DataTransfer: DCE receives CALL ACCEPTED CallSetup --> Idle: CLEAR REQUEST / CALL REJECTED DataTransfer --> DataTransfer: DATA / RR / RNR / REJ DataTransfer --> CallClearing: DTE sends CLEAR REQUEST CallClearing --> Idle: CLEAR CONFIRMATION received Idle --> [*] note right of CallSetup Establishes virtual circuit LCN is allocated end note note right of DataTransfer Bidirectional data flow Sequence numbers used M-bit, D-bit, Q-bit set as needed end note ``` ## 4.4 Virtual Circuit Multiplexing over a Single Physical Link ```mermaid flowchart TB APP1["Application A"] --> VC1["VC 1\nLCN 0001"] APP2["Application B"] --> VC2["VC 2\nLCN 0002"] APP3["Application C"] --> VC3["VC 3\nLCN 0003"] APP4["Application D"] --> VC4["VC 4\nLCN 0004"] VC1 --> MUX["X.25 PLP Multiplexer\nat DTE"] VC2 --> MUX VC3 --> MUX VC4 --> MUX MUX --> LAPB["LAP-B\nSingle Physical Link\nto DCE"] LAPB --> NET["Packet Switched Network"] NET --> DMUX["Demultiplexer\nat remote DCE"] DMUX --> RVC1["VC 1\nto App A'"] DMUX --> RVC2["VC 2\nto App B'"] DMUX --> RVC3["VC 3\nto App C'"] DMUX --> RVC4["VC 4\nto App D'"] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Diagram Note:** A single physical DTE–DCE link can carry **up to 4095 simultaneous virtual circuits**, each identified by a unique 12-bit LCN. This multiplexing capability was X.25's key economic advantage — many users shared one physical line, paying only for the time their virtual circuit was active. --- # X.25 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions ### Question 1 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** **Define X.25 and list its three protocol layers. State one reason why X.25 became less popular in modern networks.** **Model Answer:** > X.25 is an ITU-T standard interface specification that defines how a DTE (Data Terminal Equipment) connects to a DCE (Data Circuit-terminating Equipment) to access a public packet-switched network. It is a connection-oriented protocol suite that operates across the bottom three layers of the OSI model. > > **The three protocol layers of X.25 are:** > 1. **Physical Layer** — X.21 (or X.21bis) — defines mechanical and electrical interface to the network. > 2. **Data Link Layer** — LAP-B (Link Access Procedure – Balanced) — provides reliable, error-free link between DTE and DCE using HDLC's Asynchronous Balanced Mode. > 3. **Network/Packet Layer** — X.25 PLP (Packet Layer Protocol) — handles virtual circuit establishment, packet multiplexing, and end-to-end addressing using X.121. > > **One reason X.25 became less popular:** The advent of high-quality digital fiber-optic links (with very low bit error rates, typically 10⁻⁹) made X.25's heavy error-correction overhead unnecessary, allowing leaner protocols like Frame Relay and IP to deliver much higher throughput. --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — June 2023]** **Distinguish between a Switched Virtual Circuit (SVC) and a Permanent Virtual Circuit (PVC) in X.25.** **Model Answer:**
Parameter SVC PVC
Establishment Dynamically established per call Permanently configured by network admin
Phases Call setup, data transfer, call clearing Only data transfer
LCN holding time Only during active call Always held (always-on)
Cost model Pay per call duration Fixed monthly subscription
Setup delay Yes (one or more RTTs) No setup delay
Analogy Dial-up phone call Leased telephone line
--- ## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern]** **(a) (7 Marks)** Explain the architecture of the X.25 protocol suite. With a neat diagram, describe the relationship between DTE, DCE, and the packet-switched network, and explain the role of LAP-B in the DTE–DCE link. **(b) (7 Marks)** Describe the X.25 packet format with a diagram. Explain the function of the **General Format Identifier (GFI)** — specifically the **Q-bit, D-bit, and modulo bits** — and compute the maximum number of logical channels available on a single DTE–DCE interface. **Model Solution:** **(a) Architecture and LAP-B Role** X.25 follows a three-layer protocol architecture corresponding to the lower three layers of the OSI model: 1. **Physical Layer (X.21 / X.21bis):** Provides the mechanical, electrical, functional, and procedural means to transmit raw bits between DTE and DCE. X.21 is the primary ITU-T standard for digital circuits; X.21bis is a fallback to RS-232-C for analog compatibility. 2. **Data Link Layer (LAP-B):** LAP-B (Link Access Procedure — Balanced) is a bit-oriented protocol derived from HDLC's Asynchronous Balanced Mode. It provides: - Frame delimiting using the `01111110` flag and bit-stuffing. - **Error detection** using CRC-16 (FCS field). - **Error recovery** via Go-Back-N ARQ (REJ) or Selective Reject (SREJ). - **Flow control** via sliding window (RR, RNR). - **Link establishment and release** via SABM, UA, DISC commands. LAP-B is used **only** between the DTE and the DCE — not between intermediate packet switches. Each switch runs its own internal link protocol to the next switch (often a different vendor's proprietary protocol). 3. **Packet Layer (X.25 PLP):** Manages virtual circuits, packet multiplexing, and end-to-end addressing (X.121). **Relationship:** The DTE (e.g., a host computer) sits on the customer premises. The DCE (e.g., a network access node / modem pool) belongs to the public network provider. The X.25 interface standard defines the **boundary protocol** between them. Once inside the network, packets are routed through one or more packet switches (PSEs — Packet Switching Exchanges) using internal routing protocols and store-and-forward techniques. (Refer to **Diagram 4.1** above for the layered relationship.) **(b) Packet Format and GFI Analysis** The X.25 packet, when carried over LAP-B, has the following structure: ``` | Flag (8) | Address (8) | Control (8) | Packet Header (3+ bytes) | User Data (variable) | FCS (16) | Flag (8) | ``` The **Packet Header (minimum 3 bytes)** contains: - **Byte 1:** General Format Identifier (GFI) — 4 bits, plus 4 bits of Logical Channel Group Number. - **Byte 2:** Lower 4 bits of LCN. - **Byte 3 (or 4 for extended format):** Packet Type Identifier (e.g., CALL REQUEST, DATA, CLEAR REQUEST, RR, RNR, REJ). **GFI Field Breakdown:** | Bit Position | Name | Function | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Bit 8 (MSB) | **Q-bit (Qualifier)** | 0 = payload is user data; 1 = payload contains control/diagnostic information (not delivered to user) | | Bit 7 | **D-bit (Delivery Confirmation)** | 0 = local ACK by DCE; 1 = end-to-end ACK from remote DTE required | | Bits 6–5 | **Modulo bits** | 01 = sequence numbers modulo 8 (P(S) and P(R) range 0–7); 10 = modulo 128 (range 0–127) | | Bit 4 | (reserved) | Set to 0 | **Maximum Logical Channels Calculation:** The LCN is encoded across two parts of the header: - **Logical Channel Group Number:** 4 bits → $2^4 = 16$ groups (group 0 reserved). - **Logical Channel Number within group:** 8 bits → $2^8 = 256$ channels per group (channel 0 within each group reserved). $$ \text{Maximum logical channels} = (2^4 - 1) \times (2^8 - 1) = 15 \times 255 = 3825 $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Correction Note:** The *commonly cited* figure is **4095 logical channels**. This corresponds to the **12-bit LCN field** as defined in some extended X.25 implementations (4-bit group + 8-bit channel = 12 bits, with channels 0 and 4095 reserved). The KTU syllabus uses the **4095** figure as the standard answer. > > $$\text{Total LCNs} = 2^{12} - 1 = 4095 \quad \text{(channels 0 and 4095 reserved)}$$ **Therefore, up to 4095 simultaneous virtual circuits can be multiplexed on a single DTE–DCE interface.** --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice* **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern]** **(a) (7 Marks)** With a clear diagram, explain the **LAP-B frame format**. Describe the functions of the **Flag, Address, Control, Information, and FCS** fields. Explain the concept of **bit stuffing** with a suitable example. **(b) (7 Marks)** Explain the **three phases of an X.25 Switched Virtual Circuit (SVC)** call — Call Setup, Data Transfer, and Call Clearing — listing the packets exchanged in each phase. State two differences between a **PVC and SVC**. **Model Solution:** **(a) LAP-B Frame Format** LAP-B uses a frame structure identical to HDLC. The bit-level layout is: ``` | Flag (8 bits) | Address (8 bits) | Control (8 bits) | Information (n bits) | FCS (16 bits) | Flag (8 bits) | ``` **Field Functions:** - **Flag (8 bits):** The byte `01111110` marks the start and end of every frame. It serves as a synchronization pattern and frame delimiter. - **Address (8 bits):** Contains a single meaningful bit (the C/R bit — Command/Response). It identifies whether the frame originated from the DTE or DCE, and whether it is a command or response. In point-to-point LAP-B, this field is used to differentiate the two peer entities. - **Control (8 bits):** Identifies the frame type. The three categories are: - **I-frames (Information):** Carry user data (X.25 packets). Contain send sequence number P(S) and receive sequence number P(R). - **S-frames (Supervisory):** Perform flow and error control. Variants: RR, RNR, REJ, SREJ. - **U-frames (Unnumbered):** Manage the link itself. Variants: SABM, DISC, UA, FRMR, DM. - **Information (variable):** Carries the upper-layer (X.25 PLP) packet payload. Only present in I-frames and some U-frames. - **FCS (16 bits):** Frame Check Sequence — a CRC-16 computed over the Address, Control, and Information fields. The receiver recomputes the CRC and compares; a mismatch triggers a REJ for retransmission. **Bit Stuffing — Concept and Example:** Because the flag byte `01111110` contains six consecutive 1s, a long run of 1s in user data could be mistaken for a flag. To prevent this, LAP-B applies **bit stuffing** at the sender: after every sequence of **five consecutive 1s** in the data stream (between flags), a single **0 bit is inserted**. **Example:** - Original data: `011011111101111101111111` - After stuffing: `011011111**0**11111**0**10111111**0**1` (bold zeros are inserted stuffing bits) The receiver **de-stuffs** by scanning the received bit stream (after flag detection) and **removing any 0 that follows five consecutive 1s** — exactly reversing the operation. **(b) Three Phases of an X.25 SVC** **Phase 1 — Call Setup:** The calling DTE initiates a connection by sending a **CALL REQUEST** packet to its DCE. This packet contains: - Destination X.121 address. - Source X.121 address. - Requested facilities (e.g., reverse charging, throughput class). - Call user data (optional). The DCE forwards the CALL REQUEST through the network toward the destination. The called DTE, if willing to accept, returns a **CALL ACCEPTED** packet. The original DTE receives CALL ACCEPTED, and the virtual circuit is now established. **Packets in this phase:** `CALL REQUEST` (caller → network), `INCOMING CALL` (network → callee), `CALL ACCEPTED` (callee → network), `CALL CONNECTED` (network → caller). **Phase 2 — Data Transfer:** Both DTEs exchange **DATA packets** bidirectionally. Each DATA packet carries: - The assigned LCN (identifying the virtual circuit). - A send sequence number P(S) and a receive sequence number P(R). - A **More-data bit (M-bit)** set to 1 if more packets belong to the same message. - A **Delivery-confirmation bit (D-bit)** controlling ACK scope. - A **Qualifier bit (Q-bit)** for control vs. user data. Acknowledgments are sent using **RR (Receiver Ready)** — implicitly piggybacked in I-frames or sent as standalone S-frames. Errors are corrected using **REJ (Reject)** for Go-Back-N retransmission. **Packets in this phase:** `DATA`, `RR`, `RNR` (flow control), `REJ` (error recovery), `INTERRUPT` (out-of-band signal). **Phase 3 — Call Clearing:** Either DTE can terminate the call. The initiating DTE sends a **CLEAR REQUEST** packet. The far-end DTE returns a **CLEAR CONFIRMATION** packet.
