The DCEs release the LCN, all internal switch state for that call is purged, and both DTEs return to the **Idle** state. **Packets in this phase:** `CLEAR REQUEST` (initiator → network), `CLEAR INDICATION` (network → other DTE), `CLEAR CONFIRMATION` (bidirectional acknowledgment). **Two Differences — PVC vs. SVC:** 1. **Establishment:** A PVC is **pre-configured** by the network administrator and is always available; an SVC must be **set up dynamically** for each call, incurring call-setup delay. 2. **Resource Usage:** A PVC holds a **dedicated LCN permanently**, even when idle; an SVC holds a LCN **only for the duration of the active call**, freeing it for other users when the call ends. --- ## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Confusing LAP-B with HDLC entirely** — LAP-B is a *subset* of HDLC, specifically the Asynchronous Balanced Mode. Mentioning the full HDLC structure (point-to-multipoint) loses credit. > 2. **Wrong LCN count** — The standard KTU answer is **4095 logical channels** (with 0 and 4095 reserved). Stating 4096 or forgetting the reservation loses 1 mark. > 3. **Ignoring the DTE/DCE boundary** — X.25 only governs the DTE–DCE interface, *not* the internal switches. Many students incorrectly say "X.25 provides end-to-end error control" — the error control is hop-by-hop at LAP-B *plus* optional end-to-end at PLP (D-bit). > 4. **Forgetting bit stuffing in LAP-B** — When drawing the LAP-B frame or explaining reliability, bit stuffing is essential. Omitting it loses 1–2 marks. > 5. **Mixing up M-bit, D-bit, and Q-bit** — Each has a distinct role: M = segmentation flag, D = ACK scope, Q = payload type. A wrong assignment loses 1 mark. > 6. **No diagram in Part B** — KTU evaluators deduct up to 2 marks if a labeled diagram is missing for a 7-mark sub-part. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for X.25 (Module 3):** > > ✅ **X.25** = ITU-T standard for DTE–DCE interface to public packet-switched networks (1976, revised). > > ✅ **Three layers:** X.21 (Physical) | LAP-B (Data Link) | X.25 PLP (Network). > > ✅ **LAP-B** = HDLC's Asynchronous Balanced Mode, point-to-point, uses `01111110` flags, CRC-16 FCS, bit stuffing (insert 0 after five 1s), and Go-Back-N ARQ. > > ✅ **LAP-B Frame:** Flag(8) | Address(8) | Control(8) | Info(variable) | FCS(16) | Flag(8). > > ✅ **LAP-B Frame Types:** I-frames, S-frames (RR, RNR, REJ, SREJ), U-frames (SABM, DISC, UA, FRMR). > > ✅ **X.25 PLP** handles virtual circuit management, packet multiplexing, X.121 addressing. > > ✅ **X.121 Address:** Up to 14 decimal digits — first 4 = DNIC (country + network), remaining = NTN. > > ✅ **Virtual Circuits:** SVC (dynamic, per call) and PVC (permanent, pre-configured). > > ✅ **SVC Phases:** Idle → Call Setup (CALL REQUEST / CALL ACCEPTED) → Data Transfer (DATA, RR, REJ) → Call Clearing (CLEAR REQUEST / CLEAR CONFIRMATION) → Idle. > > ✅ **Logical Channels:** Up to **4095** simultaneous VCs per DTE–DCE interface (12-bit LCN, with 0 and 4095 reserved). > > ✅ **Packet Header (min 3 bytes):** GFI (Q-bit, D-bit, Modulo bits), Logical Channel Group Number, Logical Channel Number. > > ✅ **M-bit** = "more data" flag for segmentation; **D-bit** = end-to-end vs. local ACK; **Q-bit** = control vs. user payload. > > ✅ **Default packet size:** 128 bytes; **Maximum:** 4096 bytes. > > ✅ **Sequence numbering:** Default modulo 8 (3 bits), extended modulo 128 (7 bits). > > ✅ **Default window size:** 2 (modulo 8), up to 127 (modulo 128). > > ✅ **Maximum throughput:** ~2 Mbps theoretical (practically much lower). > > ✅ **Error detection:** CRC-16 at LAP-B level; ARQ for retransmission. > > ✅ **X.25 vs. modern protocols:** X.25 is *obsolete* in commercial Internet use but still operational in **banking, aviation, defense, and railway** legacy systems; its design principles led to **Frame Relay, ATM, and ultimately TCP/IP** evolution. > > ✅ **Common numerical:** Number of RR frames for $N$ packets with window $W$ is $\lceil N/W \rceil$. > > ✅ **Key diagrams to memorize:** (1) X.25 vs. OSI layered diagram, (2) LAP-B frame layout, (3) SVC state-transition diagram, (4) VC multiplexing over a single physical link.

topics.title: Frame Relay
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCERT602 - DATA COMMUNICATION AND NETWORKING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Frame Relay

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Frame Relay** is a standardized **wide area network (WAN) data-link layer protocol** (OSI Layer 2) that defines a connection-oriented, packet-switched digital telecommunications interface. It uses **variable-length frames** to encapsulate data and operates over statistically multiplexed virtual circuits identified by a local label called the **DLCI (Data Link Connection Identifier)**.

Frame Relay was standardized by the **ITU-T (originally CCITT)** under recommendations **I.122** and **I.233**, and by **ANSI** under **T1.606**, **T1.618**, and **T1.617**. It was designed as a streamlined, high-performance descendant of **X.25**, optimized for the much more reliable digital fiber-optic and copper lines of the 1980s and 1990s (where the heavy error-correction of X.25 was no longer justified).

> [!NOTE]
> **Layer Position:** Frame Relay operates at the **bottom two sub-layers of the OSI Data Link Layer** (LLC + MAC) and requires a reliable physical layer (e.g., DDS, DS-1/E-1, DS-3). It does **not** implement Layer 3 (Network) functionality itself.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Multi-Lane Digital Expressway"

Imagine a **multi-lane expressway** with on-ramps and off-ramps:

- Each **car** is a Frame Relay **frame** (data packet).
- The **expressway** is the **physical line** (e.g., a leased T1 line).
- The **license plate** of each car is the **DLCI** — a small, local "tag" that tells every tollbooth (switch) which exit the car should take. The plate is only locally meaningful (different in every state), just as DLCIs are locally significant between two adjacent Frame Relay switches.
- **Committed lanes** are guaranteed to be available; the **extra breakdown lane** allows brief bursts beyond commitment.
- **Police cameras** at the tollbooths represent the **congestion notification bits (FECN, BECN)** that warn drivers of slowdowns ahead or behind.

This analogy highlights the three pillars of Frame Relay: **simplicity, statistical sharing, and minimal in-network processing**.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (Bolded Highlights)

- **Maximum Frame Payload (Information Field):** **1600 bytes** (default) — can be negotiated up to **4096 bytes** or higher.
- **Frame Address Field Size:** **2 bytes** (default, 10-bit DLCI) or **3–4 bytes** (extended, 16/17-bit DLCI).
- **Standard Access Rates:** **56 kbps, 64 kbps, DS-1 (1.544 Mbps), E-1 (2.048 Mbps), DS-3 (44.736 Mbps)**.
- **DLCI Range (2-byte):** **0 to 1023** (10 bits); values **0–15** and **1008–1023** are reserved for management.
- **Flags:** **01111110** (binary) = **0x7E** (hex).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked "what is Frame Relay?", always mention **three keywords**: *connection-oriented*, *variable-length frames*, and *statistical multiplexing over virtual circuits*. These three phrases cover 90% of theory questions.

---

## 1.4 Frame Relay in the Protocol Stack

| OSI Layer | Frame Relay Equivalent | Function |
|-----------|------------------------|----------|
| 7–3 (Upper layers & Network) | Not defined by Frame Relay | Provided by IP, IPX, etc. |
| 2 — Data Link | **Frame Relay core** | Framing, addressing, error detection, congestion notification |
| 1 — Physical | RS-232, V.35, G.703, DS-1, E-1, etc. | Bit transmission |

The mapping above shows that **Frame Relay is "Layer 2 only"** — it intentionally leaves the network layer to upper protocols (typically **IP**).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Building Blocks of a Frame Relay Network

A Frame Relay network is composed of three logical building blocks:

1. **DTE (Data Terminal Equipment):** The customer-side equipment (router, FRAD — Frame Relay Access Device, or host).
2. **DCE (Data Circuit-terminating Equipment):** The carrier-side equipment — the **Frame Relay switch** (also called a *node* or *switch*).
3. **PVC (Permanent Virtual Circuit):** A pre-provisioned logical path between two DTEs, identified at each end by a unique DLCI.

> [!NOTE]
> **SVC (Switched Virtual Circuit)** is also defined in the standards (Q.933), but in practice **PVCs are vastly more common**, and most KTU questions implicitly refer to PVCs.

## 2.2 How a Frame Travels End-to-End — Step-by-Step Logic

1. The **DTE** creates a frame containing payload data.
2. The frame's **DLCI** is set to the locally assigned identifier of the destination PVC.
3. The frame is transmitted onto the access line at the **access rate** (e.g., 1.544 Mbps for a T1).
4. Each **intermediate Frame Relay switch** examines the DLCI, looks up its internal routing table, and forwards the frame out the corresponding port, **swapping the DLCI** to the value locally significant for the next hop.
5. The receiving **DTE** strips the frame, extracts the payload, and passes it to the upper-layer protocol (e.g., IP).
6. **Error checking** is performed end-to-end (DTE to DTE) using the **FCS**; intermediate switches **never retransmit** — they simply discard corrupted frames.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why no hop-by-hop retransmission?** The entire design philosophy of Frame Relay rests on the assumption that modern digital fiber lines are extremely reliable. By pushing error recovery to the endpoints (Layer 4 — TCP), the network achieves **lower latency** and **higher throughput** than X.25.

## 2.3 The Frame Relay Frame Format (ITU-T Q.922 Annex A)

The standard LMI-compatible frame is structured as follows:

| Field | Size (bytes) | Purpose |
|-------|--------------|---------|
| Flag | 1 | Start delimiter (**01111110**) |
| Address | 2 (default) | Contains DLCI + control bits |
| Information | Variable (1 – 1600 typical) | Upper-layer payload |
| FCS | 2 | Frame Check Sequence (CRC-16) |
| Flag | 1 | End delimiter (**01111110**) |

### 2.3.1 Default 2-Byte Address Field Bit Layout

| Bit Position | Field | Meaning |
|--------------|-------|---------|
| 1 | DLCI (most significant bit) | Top bit of 10-bit DLCI |
| 2 | DLCI | DLCI bit 8 |
| 3 | DLCI | DLCI bit 7 |
| 4 | DLCI | DLCI bit 6 |
| 5 | DLCI | DLCI bit 5 |
| 6 | DLCI | DLCI bit 4 |
| 7 | DLCI | DLCI bit 3 |
| 8 | DLCI | DLCI bit 2 |
| 9 | E/A | Extension Address bit (1 = final octet) |
| 10 | C/R | Command/Response (user-defined) |
| 11 | DLCI | DLCI bit 1 |
| 12 | DLCI | DLCI bit 0 (least significant) |
| 13 | FECN | Forward Explicit Congestion Notification |
| 14 | BECN | Backward Explicit Congestion Notification |
| 15 | DE | Discard Eligibility |
| 16 | EA | Extended Address bit (0 = more octets follow) |

> [!NOTE]
> **DLCI is a 10-bit field** formed by bits 1–8 (upper octet) and bits 11–12 (lower octet). With 10 bits you get **1024 logical channels** (0–1023). The DLCI is **locally significant** — its value only matters between two adjacent nodes.

## 2.4 Traffic Management Parameters — The KTU "Money Table"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These four parameters govern **how much data** a user may send, **how fast**, and **what happens during congestion**. They are the most heavily tested formulas on Frame Relay.

| Symbol | Parameter | Unit | Meaning |
|--------|-----------|------|---------|
| **CIR** | Committed Information Rate | **bits per second** | The rate the network **guarantees** to deliver under normal load. |
| **Bc** | Committed Burst Size | **bits** | The maximum amount of data the network **commits** to deliver in a measurement interval **Tc**. |
| **Be** | Excess Burst Size | **bits** | The amount above Bc that the network **may attempt** to deliver on a best-effort basis. |
| **Tc** | Committed Rate Measurement Interval | **seconds** | The time window over which Bc and Be are measured. |
| **AR** | Access Rate | **bits per second** | The raw clock rate of the physical port (e.g., 1.544 Mbps). |

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize the three relationships below. They are guaranteed to appear in any KTU 14-mark question involving traffic shaping.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_c &= \frac{B_c}{CIR}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Maximum Achievable Throughput} &= CIR + \frac{B_e}{T_c} = CIR \cdot \left(1 + \frac{B_e}{B_c}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Transmittable per } T_c &= B_c + B_e
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Frame Transmission Time} &= \frac{\text{Frame Size (bits)}}{\text{Access Rate (bps)}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Number of frames in } T_c &= \frac{B_c}{\text{Average Frame Size (bits)}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 2.5.1 Critical Boundary Conditions

- Always: **$0 \le B_c \le B_c + B_e \le AR \cdot T_c$**
- If the user's instantaneous rate is **≤ CIR**, all frames are marked **non-DE** and are guaranteed delivery.
- If the instantaneous rate is between **CIR and the access rate**, frames are marked **DE = 1** (Discard Eligible) and may be dropped during congestion.
- A frame arriving at the network above **$B_c + B_e$** in a single $T_c$ is **always discarded** at the ingress switch.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Students confuse **CIR** (bits per second, a *rate*) with **Bc** (bits, a *quantity*). They are different. **CIR × Tc = Bc.**

## 2.6 Congestion Control — The Three Traffic Police

Frame Relay uses **three lightweight in-band signaling mechanisms** to alert endpoints about congestion. **No retransmission is performed by the network.**

### 2.6.1 FECN — Forward Explicit Congestion Notification

- Set by a **downstream switch** experiencing congestion.
- Tells the **receiving DTE** that traffic on this PVC is congested.
- The receiver should signal its upper-layer protocol (e.g., throttle TCP).

### 2.6.2 BECN — Backward Explicit Congestion Notification

- Set by an **upstream switch** (or reflected by the congested switch back along the reverse path).
- Tells the **sending DTE** that the path it is using is congested.
- The sender should slow its transmission rate.

### 2.6.3 DE — Discard Eligibility

- A **policy bit** set either by the **DTE** (to mark low-priority frames) or by the **network** (when a DTE exceeds its CIR).
- When congestion occurs, **DE-marked frames are dropped first**.

### 2.6.4 CLLM — Consolidated Link Layer Management

- An **optional out-of-band** enhancement (ITU-T I.370) that carries a single congestion message for an entire link rather than per-PVC.
- Less commonly tested, but mentioned for completeness.

> [!NOTE]
> **One-line exam answer:** *"Frame Relay congestion control is end-to-end and notification-based; the network uses FECN/BECN bits and the DE bit to give early warning and to discard low-priority traffic first."*

## 2.7 LMI — Local Management Interface

LMI (ANSI T1.617 Annex D / ITU-T Q.933 Annex A) is a **keep-alive protocol** that runs over a reserved DLCI (typically **DLCI = 1023**) to:

- Verify that PVCs are **active**.
- Report the **DLCI** of each PVC.
- Notify the DTE when a PVC is **added, deleted, or modified**.

Three LMI variants exist: **Cisco, ANSI, and Q.933a** — these are **not interoperable**; both ends must agree.

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Branch-office WAN connectivity** (1990s–2000s): replaced leased private lines for "any-to-any" branch traffic.
- **Predecessor to MPLS**: Many of Frame Relay's ideas (virtual circuits, label switching, traffic engineering) are direct ancestors of **MPLS**.
- **Embedded telecom backhaul**: still used in some SCADA and ATM networks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Computing Tc and Maximum Throughput

> **Problem:** A Frame Relay PVC is provisioned with **CIR = 128 kbps**, **Bc = 64 kbits**, and **Be = 32 kbits**. Compute **(i)** the measurement interval **Tc**, and **(ii)** the maximum achievable throughput on this PVC.

### Step (i): Computing Tc

We use the fundamental relation $T_c = B_c / CIR$. Note that **Bc is given in kbits** and **CIR in kbps**, but the units are dimensionally consistent (kbits ÷ kbits/s = s).

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_c &= \frac{B_c}{CIR} \\
    &= \frac{64 \text{ kbits}}{128 \text{ kbps}} \\
    &= 0.5 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logical interpretation:** Every **0.5 seconds**, the network refreshes its measurement window. In each such window it is willing to commit to delivering up to 64 kbits.

### Step (ii): Maximum Achievable Throughput

The maximum burst rate the user can attempt (in the ideal, uncongested case) is **CIR + Be/Tc**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\max} &= CIR + \frac{B_e}{T_c} \\
         &= 128 \text{ kbps} + \frac{32 \text{ kbits}}{0.5 \text{ s}} \\
         &= 128 + 64 \\
         &= 192 \text{ kbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logical interpretation:** During the 0.5-second window the user may send $B_c + B_e = 64 + 32 = 96$ kbits. Spread over 0.5 s, that is $96 / 0.5 = 192$ kbps.

### Verification (Consistency Check)

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_c + B_e &\le AR \cdot T_c \\
96 \text{ kbits} &\le AR \cdot 0.5 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

This requires $AR \ge 192 \text{ kbps}$, which is trivially satisfied on a T1/E1 access line.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — How Many Frames in Tc?

> **Problem:** Using the same PVC as above, if the average frame size (including Frame Relay header and trailer) is **512 bytes**, how many frames can the user transmit as **non-DE** (committed) within a single Tc?

### Step 1: Convert average frame size to bits

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= 512 \text{ bytes} \times 8 \text{ bits/byte} = 4096 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Compute number of committed frames

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{committed} &= \left\lfloor \frac{B_c}{L} \right\rfloor \\
              &= \left\lfloor \frac{64\,000 \text{ bits}}{4096 \text{ bits/frame}} \right\rfloor \\
              &= \left\lfloor 15.625 \right\rfloor \\
              &= 15 \text{ frames}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logical interpretation:** The user can send **15 full frames** at committed priority, with a residual of $64\,000 - 15 \times 4096 = 2560$ bits remaining, which is **insufficient** for a 16th full frame but is still counted against the budget.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Frame Transmission Time on the Access Line

> **Problem:** A DTE sends a 4096-bit frame over a Frame Relay link whose access rate is **256 kbps**. How long does the frame take to be clocked out onto the wire?

### Step 1: Apply the transmission-time formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{tx} &= \frac{L}{AR} \\
       &= \frac{4096 \text{ bits}}{256\,000 \text{ bps}} \\
       &= 0.016 \text{ s} \\
       &= 16 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logical interpretation:** Every 16 ms, a new frame finishes being serialized. This determines the **inter-frame gap** at the DTE side and is a critical parameter when computing end-to-end latency for voice-over-Frame-Relay applications.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Identifying a Violation

> **Problem:** A user with **CIR = 64 kbps, Bc = 32 kbits, Be = 0 kbits** sends **50 kbits in a single Tc interval**. Is this traffic within contract? How many bits will be marked DE, and how many will be discarded by the network?

### Step 1: Compute Tc (for context)

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_c &= \frac{32 \text{ kbits}}{64 \text{ kbps}} = 0.5 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Compare to contract

The user may send $B_c + B_e = 32 + 0 = 32$ kbits as committed. The user sent **50 kbits**.

- **Committed (non-DE):** 32 kbits — accepted unconditionally.
- **Excess:** $50 - 32 = 18$ kbits.
- Since **Be = 0**, the network has **no allowance for excess burst**; the 18 kbits exceed the contract.

### Step 3: Apply policy

Because **Be = 0**, the excess 18 kbits are **not merely marked DE — they are discarded at the ingress switch**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bits accepted} &= 32 \text{ kbits} \\
\text{Bits discarded} &= 18 \text{ kbits} \\
\text{Throughput loss} &= \frac{18}{50} = 36\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logical interpretation:** With **Be = 0**, the contract is *strict*; the user must pace itself at CIR or below. This is a common configuration for **delay-sensitive voice circuits**.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Formula ↔ Use-Case

| Engineering Question | Formula to Apply | Typical Use |
|----------------------|------------------|-------------|
| How long is the measurement window? | $T_c = B_c / CIR$ | Provisioning PVCs |
| What is peak burst throughput? | $R_{\max} = CIR + B_e / T_c$ | Capacity planning |
| How many frames per Tc? | $N = B_c / L_{frame}$ | QoS design |
| Will this burst violate the contract? | Compare burst to $B_c + B_e$ | SLA enforcement |
| How long does one frame take on the wire? | $t_{tx} = L / AR$ | Latency budgeting |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Frame Relay Network Architecture (End-to-End Topology)

This diagram illustrates a typical **hub-and-spoke Frame Relay WAN** with three branch offices connecting to a central headquarters, and the role of the carrier's Frame Relay cloud (switches inside the cloud).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HQ["HQ DTE Router"] -- "PVC DLCI 100" --> SW1["Carrier Switch A"]
    BR1["Branch 1 DTE Router"] -- "PVC DLCI 200" --> SW1
    SW1 -- "Trunk" --> SW2["Carrier Switch B"]
    SW2 -- "Trunk" --> SW3["Carrier Switch C"]
    BR2["Branch 2 DTE Router"] -- "PVC DLCI 300" --> SW2
    BR3["Branch 3 DTE Router"] -- "PVC DLCI 400" --> SW3
    SW1 -- "Trunk" --> SW3
    HQ -- "PVC DLCI 100":::pvc --> BR1
    HQ -- "PVC DLCI 110":::pvc --> BR2
    HQ -- "PVC DLCI 120":::pvc --> BR3
    classDef pvc stroke:#ff6600,stroke-width:2px,fill:#fff5e6
```

**Interpretation:**
- Each **solid black line** is a **physical access line** (leased line from DTE to the nearest carrier switch).
- Each **orange dashed line** is a **logical PVC** identified by a DLCI at each end. Note that **DLCIs are locally significant**, so HQ↔Branch1 uses DLCI 100 on HQ's side and DLCI 200 on Branch1's side, even though they describe the same PVC.
- The **carrier cloud** (switches A, B, C and the trunks between them) is opaque to the user — the customer only sees DTEs and DLCIs.

---

## 4.2 Frame Relay Frame Format — Field-by-Field Layout

This block-level schematic shows the bit-level structure of a standard **2-byte address** Frame Relay frame, which is the most-tested variant in KTU examinations.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FRAME["FRAME RELAY FRAME"]
        direction LR
        F1["FLAG\n1 Byte\n01111110"]:::flag
        subgraph ADDR["ADDRESS FIELD 2 Bytes"]
            direction LR
            D1["DLCI Bits 9-2\n8 bits"]:::dlci
            subgraph LOW["Lower Octet"]
                direction LR
                CR["C/R\n1 bit"]:::ctrl
                D2["DLCI Bits 1-0\n2 bits"]:::dlci
                FECN["FECN\n1 bit"]:::ctrl
                BECN["BECN\n1 bit"]:::ctrl
                DE["DE\n1 bit"]:::ctrl
                EA["EA\n1 bit"]:::ctrl
            end
        end
        INFO["INFORMATION\nVariable\n1 to 1600 Bytes"]:::info
        FCS["FCS\n2 Bytes\nCRC-16"]:::fcs
        F2["FLAG\n1 Byte\n01111110"]:::flag
    end
    F1 --> D1 --> CR --> D2 --> FECN --> BECN --> DE --> EA --> INFO --> FCS --> F2
    classDef flag fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#000
    classDef dlci fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    classDef ctrl fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#663300
    classDef info fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#006633
    classDef fcs fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#660000
```

**Reading the diagram left-to-right:**
- **Flag (1 B)** marks the start.
- **Address (2 B)** carries the 10-bit DLCI plus the **3 control bits (FECN, BECN, DE)** and the **EA** and **C/R** housekeeping bits.
- **Information (variable)** is the upper-layer payload.
- **FCS (2 B)** is the CRC-16 used for end-to-end error detection.
- **Flag (1 B)** marks the end.

---

## 4.3 Congestion Notification Flow — FECN, BECN, DE

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DTEA["DTE A\nSender"] -- "Frame with DE=0" --> SW1["Switch 1\nIngress"]
    SW1 -- "Forward" --> SW2["Switch 2\nCONGESTED\nSets FECN=1"]:::cong
    SW2 -- "Forward\nwith FECN=1" --> SW3["Switch 3\nEgress"]
    SW3 -- "Forward\nwith FECN=1" --> DTEB["DTE B\nReceiver\nThrottles TCP"]:::alert
    DTEA -- "Frame with DE=0" --> SW1
    SW1 -- "Forward" --> SW2
    SW2 -. "Reverse direction\nBECN frame" .-> SW1
    SW1 -. "BECN=1\nto DTE A" .-> DTEA
    DTEA:::alert
    classDef cong fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#990000
    classDef alert fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#996600
```

**Interpretation of the flow:**
1. **DTE A** sends data at a rate above CIR; Switch 1 begins tagging frames with **DE = 1**.
2. **Switch 2** becomes congested. It sets **FECN = 1** in all forward frames destined for **DTE B**, and injects a **BECN = 1** frame back toward DTE A.
3. **DTE B** receives FECN = 1 and signals upper layers (e.g., shrinks the TCP window).
4. **DTE A** receives BECN = 1 and reduces its send rate to CIR.
5. If congestion persists, the network discards DE-marked frames first.

---

## 4.4 Layered Mapping — Where Frame Relay Sits

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph APP["APPLICATION LAYER"]
        A1["HTTP, FTP, SMTP"]
    end
    subgraph TRAN["TRANSPORT LAYER"]
        T1["TCP / UDP"]
    end
    subgraph NET["NETWORK LAYER"]
        N1["IP / IPX"]
    end
    subgraph DL["DATA LINK LAYER"]
        FR["FRAME RELAY\nFraming, DLCI, FECN/BECN/DE"]
    end
    subgraph PHY["PHYSICAL LAYER"]
        P1["RS-232, V.35, DS-1, E-1"]
    end
    A1 --> T1 --> N1 --> FR --> P1
```

**Interpretation:** Frame Relay is a **pure Layer 2 protocol**. It transparently carries any Layer 3 protocol (typically IP). This is why Frame Relay is often called a **"Layer 2 VPN"** in modern terminology.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1 [KTU University Exam — June 2023]

> **Explain the concept of DLCI in Frame Relay. Why is it said to be locally significant?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **DLCI (Data Link Connection Identifier)** is a 10-bit field in the Frame Relay address that identifies a **virtual circuit** between a DTE and its directly connected Frame Relay switch. **(1 mark)**
- The DLCI is **locally significant**, meaning its value is unique **only between two adjacent endpoints** (DTE–switch or switch–switch). The same PVC will have **different DLCI values** at its two ends. **(1 mark)**
- This local significance allows the carrier to **reuse DLCI values** across many customers and simplifies switch routing tables. **(1 mark)**

---

### Question A.2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

> **Differentiate between FECN and BECN in Frame Relay congestion control.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **FECN (Forward Explicit Congestion Notification):** Set by a **congested switch** in frames flowing in the **forward direction** (toward the destination). It alerts the **receiving DTE** that congestion exists on the path. **(1.5 marks)**
- **BECN (Backward Explicit Congestion Notification):** Set in frames flowing in the **reverse direction** (toward the source) to alert the **sending DTE** to slow down. **(1.5 marks)**
- Together, they enable **end-to-end flow control** with the cooperation of upper-layer protocols like TCP.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> **Note:** Per KTU 2024 Scheme, ESE Part B questions are typically 14 marks each and offer an **internal choice**. The two alternatives below are fully independent 14-mark question papers.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

> **(a)** Draw and explain the **Frame Relay frame format** in detail. What is the role of the **FCS** field? **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the **traffic management parameters** in Frame Relay — CIR, Bc, Be, and Tc — and derive the relationship between them. **(7 marks)**

#### Part (a) — Frame Format (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

A Frame Relay frame consists of the following fields (in order):

1. **Flag (1 byte):** A fixed bit pattern `01111110` (0x7E) used to mark the beginning and end of the frame. Bit stuffing is used to prevent this pattern from appearing inside the data. **(1 mark)**
2. **Address Field (2, 3, or 4 bytes):** The default is 2 bytes containing:
   - **DLCI (10 bits):** Identifies the virtual circuit.
   - **C/R (1 bit):** Command/Response indicator, user-defined.
   - **FECN (1 bit):** Forward congestion notification.
   - **BECN (1 bit):** Backward congestion notification.
   - **DE (1 bit):** Discard Eligibility.
   - **EA (1 bit):** Extended Address bit; 0 = more address octets follow, 1 = current octet is final. **(2 marks)**
3. **Information Field (variable length, 1 – 1600 bytes default):** Carries the upper-layer payload (e.g., IP packet). **(1 mark)**
4. **FCS (2 bytes):** Frame Check Sequence — a **CRC-16** computed over the entire frame from the opening flag up to the FCS. The receiver recomputes the CRC; a mismatch means the frame is **discarded** (no retransmission by the network). **(2 marks)**
5. **Closing Flag (1 byte):** Same pattern as the opening flag. **(1 mark)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** Many students forget that the **FCS covers the data PLUS the address field**, not just the payload. Also, **bit stuffing** applies to all data between the two flags — mention it explicitly to gain the full mark.

#### Part (b) — Traffic Management Parameters (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

The four primary traffic parameters defined by Frame Relay (ITU-T I.370 / ANSI T1.606) are:

1. **CIR (Committed Information Rate):** The average rate, in **bits per second**, that the network commits to deliver under normal conditions. **(1 mark)**
2. **Bc (Committed Burst Size):** The maximum number of **bits** the network commits to deliver in one measurement interval. **(1 mark)**
3. **Be (Excess Burst Size):** The number of bits above Bc that the network may attempt to deliver on a best-effort basis. Frames above Bc + Be are **dropped at the ingress**. **(1 mark)**
4. **Tc (Committed Rate Measurement Interval):** The time period over which Bc and Be are accumulated. **(1 mark)**

**Derivation of the relationship:**

The **fundamental relation** is that the committed burst size equals the committed rate times the measurement interval. This is because CIR is defined as the rate that the network *commits* to delivering **on average** over the interval Tc.

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_c &= CIR \times T_c \\
\Rightarrow \quad T_c &= \frac{B_c}{CIR}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(1 mark)** for the equation, **(1 mark)** for the units-and-meaning explanation.

**Engineering interpretation:** If a customer subscribes to **CIR = 256 kbps** and **Bc = 128 kbits**, the network refreshes its accounting window every $T_c = 128\,000 / 256\,000 = 0.5$ s. In each 0.5-second window, the network guarantees to deliver 128 kbits as committed, and any excess up to **Be** is best-effort.

**(1 mark)** for the worked example.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

> **(a)** Compare **Frame Relay with X.25** under at least six parameters. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A Frame Relay PVC is provisioned with **CIR = 64 kbps, Bc = 32 kbits, Be = 16 kbits**. A user transmits **40 kbits** in a single Tc interval. Determine:
>   (i) the measurement interval **Tc**,
>   (ii) the maximum achievable throughput,
>   (iii) how many bits are accepted as committed, excess, and discarded. **(7 marks)**

#### Part (a) — Frame Relay vs X.25 (7 marks)

**Model Solution Table (1 mark per major row, plus 1 mark for summary statement):**

| Parameter | X.25 | Frame Relay |
|-----------|------|-------------|
| **OSI Layers Covered** | Layers 1, 2, and 3 (Network) | Layers 1 and 2 only |
| **Error Recovery** | Hop-by-hop ACK and retransmission | End-to-end only; no in-network retransmission |
| **Flow Control** | Per-hop window-based | None in network; relies on upper layers |
| **Typical Speeds** | Up to 64 kbps | Up to 45 Mbps (DS-3) and beyond |
| **Overhead** | High (3-layer headers) | Low (single 2-byte address) |
| **Underlying Line Quality** | Designed for noisy analog lines | Requires reliable digital lines |
| **Packet/Frame Size** | Up to 128 or 256 bytes | Up to 1600+ bytes (variable) |
| **Switching Technique** | Store-and-forward | Cut-through (mostly) |
| **Congestion Handling** | Implicit via flow control | Explicit via FECN/BECN/DE |

**Summary statement (1 mark):** *"Frame Relay is a streamlined, higher-speed descendant of X.25, optimized for the reliable digital era. It shifts error recovery to the endpoints in exchange for higher throughput and lower delay."*

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**Given:** CIR = 64 kbps, Bc = 32 kbits, Be = 16 kbits, transmitted burst = 40 kbits in one Tc.

**Step (i): Compute Tc (2 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_c &= \frac{B_c}{CIR} = \frac{32 \text{ kbits}}{64 \text{ kbps}} = 0.5 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (ii): Maximum achievable throughput (2 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\max} &= CIR + \frac{B_e}{T_c} = 64 \text{ kbps} + \frac{16 \text{ kbits}}{0.5 \text{ s}} = 64 + 32 = 96 \text{ kbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (iii): Categorize the 40-kbit burst (3 marks)**

- **Contract per Tc:** $B_c + B_e = 32 + 16 = 48$ kbits.
- **Bits sent:** 40 kbits.
- Since $40 \le 48$, the entire burst is **within contract** — nothing is discarded.
- **Committed (non-DE) bits:** min(40, Bc) = **32 kbits** (accepted unconditionally).
- **Excess (DE-marked) bits:** $40 - 32 = $ **8 kbits** (accepted best-effort, may be dropped under congestion).
- **Discarded bits:** **0 kbits**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Accepted (committed)} &= 32 \text{ kbits} \\
\text{Accepted (excess DE)} &= 8 \text{ kbits} \\
\text{Discarded} &= 0 \text{ kbits} \\
\text{Total delivered} &= 40 \text{ kbits}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** Students often mark **all** excess bits as "discarded" the moment they exceed Bc. **They are not.** Excess bits up to **Bc + Be** are forwarded; they are merely marked **DE = 1** so that the network may drop them *during congestion*. A 40-kbit burst on a 48-kbit budget is fully accepted.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (read this 5 minutes before the exam):**

- Frame Relay is a **Layer 2, connection-oriented, packet-switched** WAN protocol standardized by **ITU-T I.233** and **ANSI T1.618**.
- It uses **variable-length frames** (up to 1600+ bytes of payload) and is designed for **reliable digital lines**.
- **DLCI** is a **10-bit, locally significant** identifier of a virtual circuit. It is not a global address.
- Two virtual-circuit flavors: **PVC (permanent)** is common; **SVC (switched)** is rare in practice.
- **Frame format:** Flag – Address (DLCI + FECN/BECN/DE/EA/C-R) – Information – FCS – Flag.
- **The 3 mandatory traffic parameters:** **CIR (rate), Bc (quantity), Be (quantity)**, with $T_c = B_c / CIR$ and $R_{\max} = CIR + B_e / T_c$.
- **Bursts up to Bc** are committed and accepted unconditionally.
- **Bursts between Bc and Bc + Be** are marked **DE = 1** — best-effort.
- **Bursts above Bc + Be** are **discarded at ingress**.
- **FECN** = downstream switch warns receiver; **BECN** = upstream switch warns sender; **DE** = discard this frame first under congestion.
- The network **does not retransmit** — error recovery is delegated to upper layers (typically **TCP**).
- **LMI** (DLCI 1023) is the keep-alive protocol for PVC status reporting.
- Frame Relay assumes the lower layer is **error-free**, unlike its predecessor **X.25** which performs hop-by-hop error correction.
- Frame Relay is a **conceptual ancestor of MPLS** — both use short labels (DLCI vs. MPLS label) to forward frames/packets through a provider cloud.
- Remember: **Flag = 0x7E = 01111110 (binary)** — this exact pattern is the most-tested byte in any Frame Relay exam.

> **End of Topic: Frame Relay — Module 3, PCERT602, KTU 2024 Scheme.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCERT602 - DATA COMMUNICATION AND NETWORKING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Routing in Switched Networks: Routing in Circuit Switched Network

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Circuit Switching

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Circuit Switching** is a switching methodology in which a dedicated physical communication path (a *circuit*) is established between the sender and receiver **before any data transfer begins**, and is maintained exclusively for the entire duration of the session. The path is reserved end-to-end through a series of intermediate switching nodes, and is torn down only when one of the parties disconnects.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, circuit switching belongs to the **connection-oriented** class of network services. It is the oldest, most established switching technique, and forms the conceptual basis for the design of legacy **Public Switched Telephone Networks (PSTN)** and early wide-area data networks such as **X.25** and **ISDN (Integrated Services Digital Network)**.

The complete lifecycle of a circuit-switched connection consists of three canonical phases:

| Phase | Technical Name | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Circuit Establishment (Setup)** | Source-to-destination path is reserved through signaling |
| 2 | **Data Transfer (Talk)** | Information flows continuously over the reserved circuit |
| 3 | **Circuit Teardown (Disconnect)** | Allocated resources are released back to the network pool |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Railway Reservation System

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine the Indian Railways Tatkal reservation system during Diwali rush.
> 1. You book a ticket (a *seat*) on a specific train traveling from **Ernakulam Junction to New Delhi** (the *circuit setup*).
> 2. Once booked, that seat is **yours alone** for the entire journey — no one else can sit there, even if compartments are half-empty elsewhere (*dedicated bandwidth reservation*).
> 3. The train physically travels the **fixed route** laid down on the railway tracks (*dedicated path through switches*).
> 4. When you reach New Delhi and alight, the seat is released for another passenger (*circuit teardown*).

In this analogy:
- **Your reserved seat** = the dedicated circuit
- **The train route** = the established path through switching nodes
- **The fixed track schedule** = the rigid topology of circuit switching
- **The duration of the journey** = the call duration

This is precisely how a **telephone call** behaves. Even when both parties are silent, the circuit remains allocated — illustrating the fundamental inefficiency of circuit switching for **bursty data traffic** (such as web browsing or email).

## 1.3 Routing — Position in the Protocol Stack

> [!NOTE]
> **Routing** is the **Network Layer (Layer 3)** function responsible for determining the *path* a data unit will take from source to destination. In circuit-switched networks, routing decisions are made **once per session** (during the setup phase), not on a per-packet basis. This is a critical distinction from packet-switched networks.

## 1.4 Why Routing in Circuit-Switched Networks is Special

Because the circuit is **established once and held** for the full call, the routing decision is:
- **Computed at call setup** (not continuously)
- **Static for the entire session** (no re-routing mid-call)
- **Resource-reserving** (switches pre-allocate time slots, frequency bands, or physical ports)

This design choice is justified historically because circuit switching was originally designed to carry **voice traffic**, which is:
- **Continuous** (no bursts)
- **Delay-sensitive** (cannot tolerate variable latency)
- **Long-duration** (typical call ≈ 3 minutes)
- **Symmetric** (both directions carry roughly equal traffic)

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Phases of a Circuit-Switched Connection

### Phase 1 — Circuit Establishment (Setup Phase)

1. The source end-system generates a **call request signal** (also called the **OFF-HOOK / SEIZE** signal in telephony).
2. This signaling message is sent hop-by-hop to the destination, traveling through the **Signaling System No. 7 (SS7)** infrastructure in modern PSTN.
3. At each intermediate switch, a **routing decision** is made based on the dialed digits (destination address).
4. Upon reaching the destination, the called party is alerted (the **RINGING** signal).
5. Upon answer (the **OFF-HOOK** acknowledgment), an acknowledgment is propagated back to the source.
6. **Resource allocation** is performed at every switch along the path:
   - In **Space-Division Switching (SDS)**: a physical crosspoint is closed.
   - In **Time-Division Switching (TDS)**: dedicated time slots are reserved.
   - In **Frequency-Division Switching (FDS)**: dedicated frequency bands are reserved.

### Phase 2 — Data Transfer

- Information flows as a **continuous stream** over the dedicated circuit.
- There is **no addressing required** at intermediate switches (because the path is fixed).
- Throughput is **constant and predictable** (no contention, no queueing).
- **Transit delay** is minimal and consists of only **propagation delay** and **node-processing delay** (no queueing delay).

### Phase 2 — Circuit Teardown (Disconnect)

- Initiated by either party sending a **hang-up signal** (ON-HOOK).
- The acknowledgment propagates through all intermediate switches.
- All reserved resources are returned to the **free resource pool**.
- Routing tables at intermediate nodes are **not modified** (since they are pre-computed).

## 2.2 Routing Strategies in Circuit-Switched Networks

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme specifically lists **three routing techniques** for circuit-switched networks. All three are critical from an exam perspective.

### 2.2.1 Hierarchical Routing

- The network is organized in a **tree-like hierarchy** of switching centers.
- Routing decisions are made in a **top-down** manner: a call first travels to the **highest-level (root) switch**, then descends to the destination.
- **Advantages**: Simple routing tables, scalable.
- **Disadvantages**: Sub-optimal path length; even a call between two neighboring cities may travel up to the regional center and back.

**Hierarchy levels in PSTN (from low to high):**
1. **End Office (Class-5 / Local Exchange)** — connects subscribers
2. **Tandem Office** — interconnects end offices within a region
3. **Primary Center (Class-3)**
4. **Sectional Center (Class-2)**
5. **Regional Center (Class-1)** — apex of the hierarchy

### 2.2.2 Alternative Routing

- Each switch maintains a **list of alternative routes** to every destination, ordered by preference.
- The **primary (first-choice) route** is tried first.
- If all circuits on the primary route are **busy**, the next alternative is tried, and so on.
- This provides **load balancing** and **fault tolerance**.
- The simplest form is **2-link alternative routing**, where each destination has exactly one primary and one backup route.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** A common 14-mark question asks you to calculate the number of circuits required to achieve a given **Grade of Service (GoS)** using the **Erlang-B formula**. Memorize the formula and understand its variables.

### 2.2.3 Directory Routing

- Each switch contains a **routing directory** (lookup table) mapping destination addresses to the **next hop (outgoing line)**.
- The directory is **pre-computed** offline and updated periodically (not in real-time).
- All incoming calls to a given destination are routed through the **same outgoing line** (single-path routing).
- This is essentially **static, table-driven routing**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> The following table contains the **must-know formulas** for KTU examination questions on circuit-switched routing. Memorize variables, units, and boundary conditions.

| Formula Name | Equation | Variable Definitions | Units / Boundary |
|---|---|---|---|
| **End-to-End Delay** | $T_{total} = T_{setup} + (N-1) \cdot T_{prop} + T_{trans} \cdot K + T_{teardown}$ | $N$ = number of nodes, $K$ = message bits, $T_{prop}$ = propagation/node, $T_{trans}$ = transmission/bit | All $T$ in **seconds**, $K$ in **bits** |
| **Number of Full-Duplex Circuits in Fully Connected Mesh** | $L = \dfrac{N(N-1)}{2}$ | $N$ = number of switching nodes | Dimensionless |
| **Average Path Length (Worst Case Hierarchical)** | $\bar{H} \approx 2 \cdot (\text{levels} - 1)$ | Hierarchical depth of the tree | Hops |
| **Erlang-B Loss Formula (Grade of Service)** | $GoS = B(C, A) = \dfrac{A^{C} \, / \, C!}{\displaystyle\sum_{i=0}^{C} A^{i} \, / \, i!}$ | $A$ = offered traffic (Erlangs), $C$ = number of circuits, $B$ = blocking probability | $GoS$ is dimensionless (probability) |
| **Offered Traffic (Erlangs)** | $A = \lambda \cdot \mu^{-1}$ | $\lambda$ = call arrival rate, $\mu^{-1}$ = mean call holding time | **Erlangs** |
| **Call Completion Probability** | $P_{success} = 1 - GoS$ | $GoS$ = Grade of Service | Dimensionless |
| **Signaling Delay per Hop** | $D_{sig} = 2 \cdot T_{prop} + T_{proc}$ | Two-way handshake for setup | Seconds |
| **Number of Levels in Strict Hierarchy** | $L_{levels} = \lceil \log_{B} N \rceil$ | $B$ = branching factor, $N$ = total nodes | Levels |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LaTeX Isolation Note:** All `$|x|$` style absolute-value bars have been replaced with `$\vert x \vert$` form to prevent markdown table corruption.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of Circuit-Switched Routing |
|---|---|
| **Telephony (PSTN)** | The classic example — every landline call uses hierarchical + alternative routing |
| **ISDN** | Bearer channels (B-channels) use circuit switching for guaranteed 64 kbps |
| **Optical Circuit-Switched Networks** | Wavelength-routed optical networks establish **lightpaths** (analogous to circuits) |
| **Satellite Communication** | FDMA/TDMA circuit assignment for VSAT networks |
| **Military / Defense Networks** | Dedicated circuits preferred for command-and-control where determinism is critical |
| **Legacy PABX Systems** | Office telephone exchanges still use hierarchical routing internally |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Number of Circuits in a Fully Connected Mesh Network

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is **frequently asked** in KTU Module 3 examinations. The student is expected to derive the expression for the number of point-to-point links in a fully connected switching network.

### Problem Statement
A fully connected circuit-switched network has $N$ switching nodes. Derive the expression for the total number of full-duplex circuits (links) required.

### Derivation

Let $N$ be the total number of switching nodes. Since the network is **fully connected**, every node must be directly connected to **every other node** by an independent full-duplex circuit.

**Step 1:** For any *single* node, the number of full-duplex circuits emerging from it is:

$$
\text{Circuits per node} = (N - 1)
$$

because it connects to all other $(N-1)$ nodes, and one circuit per destination.

**Step 2:** The **total count** of circuits, if we sum over all $N$ nodes, is:

$$
L_{count} = N \cdot (N - 1)
$$

**Step 3:** However, this sum **double-counts** every circuit. A circuit connecting node A to node B is counted once when we sum from A's perspective, and again from B's perspective. Therefore, we must divide by $2$:

$$
L = \dfrac{N \cdot (N - 1)}{2}
$$

**Step 4:** Final result:

$$
\boxed{L = \dfrac{N(N-1)}{2}}
$$

### Verification for Small $N$

| $N$ (nodes) | $L = N(N-1)/2$ (circuits) | Check |
|---|---|---|
| 2 | 1 | One line between the two nodes ✓ |
| 3 | 3 | Triangle topology ✓ |
| 4 | 6 | Tetrahedral connections ✓ |
| 5 | 10 | Complete graph $K_5$ has 10 edges ✓ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The growth is **quadratic** — for $N = 100$ nodes, we need $4950$ circuits. This quadratic blow-up is precisely why pure mesh topology is **impractical** for large networks, motivating hierarchical routing.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — End-to-End Delay Analysis of a Circuit-Switched Call

> [!NOTE]
> The total delay $T_{total}$ experienced by a circuit-switched call consists of **setup delay**, **transmission delay**, **propagation delay**, and **teardown delay**. The derivation below expresses each component explicitly.

### Setup

Consider a circuit-switched connection traversing $N$ intermediate nodes (including source and destination). The total number of **hops (links)** traversed is:

$$
H = N - 1
$$

The total data to be transmitted is $K$ bits. Per-link bandwidth is $B$ bps. Each physical link has propagation delay $t_p$ seconds.

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Circuit Setup Delay (Two-Way Signaling):**
The setup message propagates from source to destination (1 forward), and the acknowledgment returns (1 backward). Each direction takes $H$ hops, each with propagation delay $t_p$ and node processing delay $t_{proc}$:

$$
T_{setup} = 2 \cdot H \cdot (t_p + t_{proc}) = 2(N-1)(t_p + t_{proc})
$$

**Step 2 — Data Transmission Delay:**
Once the circuit is up, the $K$ bits are transmitted at rate $B$:

$$
T_{trans} = \dfrac{K}{B}
$$

**Step 3 — Data Propagation Delay:**
The first bit propagates across all $H$ links before arriving at the destination:

$$
T_{prop} = H \cdot t_p = (N-1) \cdot t_p
$$

(Note: The remaining bits follow on a "pipeline" basis, so $T_{prop}$ is not multiplied by $K$.)

**Step 4 — Circuit Teardown Delay:**
Similar to setup, the disconnect signal propagates forward only (no acknowledgment required in many implementations):

$$
T_{teardown} = (N-1) \cdot (t_p + t_{proc})
$$

**Step 5 — Total Delay:**

$$
T_{total} = T_{setup} + T_{trans} + T_{prop} + T_{teardown}
$$

Substituting each term:

$$
T_{total} = 2(N-1)(t_p + t_{proc}) + \dfrac{K}{B} + (N-1)t_p + (N-1)(t_p + t_{proc})
$$

**Step 6 — Simplification:**
Combining the $(N-1)$ terms:

$$
T_{total} = (N-1)(3t_p + 3t_{proc}) + \dfrac{K}{B}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{total} = 3(N-1)(t_p + t_{proc}) + \dfrac{K}{B}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often forget to include $T_{setup}$ and $T_{teardown}$ in the total delay, accounting only for transmission. This leads to a loss of **2–3 marks** in 14-mark problems.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Average Path Length in Hierarchical Routing

In a **2-level hierarchical** routing tree, the call from any source to any destination travels *up* to the root switch, then *down* to the destination. If the depth of the tree (from leaf to root) is $d$ levels, the total path length in hops is:

$$
H_{hierarchical} = 2d
$$

For a **3-level** hierarchy with branching factor $b$ at each level, the depth is:

$$
d = \log_{b} N
$$

Therefore the average hierarchical path length is:

$$
\boxed{\bar{H}_{hier} = 2 \log_{b} N}
$$

This compares to a flat (non-hierarchical) optimal path of length $\bar{H}_{flat} \approx \log_{b} N$ in a random graph.

The **penalty ratio** is therefore approximately $2$ — meaning hierarchical routing uses roughly **twice the optimal number of hops** on average.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of the Three Routing Strategies

| Parameter | Hierarchical | Alternative | Directory |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Routing Table Size** | Small (depends on hierarchy level) | Medium (lists all alternatives) | Large (one entry per destination) |
| **Path Optimality** | Poor (sub-optimal) | Good (primary route is optimal) | Good (single best route) |
| **Adaptability to Load** | Poor (rigid tree) | Excellent (try alternates if busy) | Poor (no re-routing) |
| **Fault Tolerance** | Poor | Excellent (multiple backups) | Poor |
| **Update Frequency** | Rare | Periodic (e.g., daily) | Periodic (e.g., weekly) |
| **Implementation Complexity** | Low | Medium | Low |
| **Scalability** | Excellent | Good | Limited |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Three-Phase Lifecycle of a Circuit-Switched Call

The following Mermaid block illustrates the complete state machine of a circuit-switched connection, including all three phases and the failure condition (busy / no answer).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[IDLE: Source OFF-HOOK] --> B[DIAL DIGITS]
    B --> C[Send CALL REQUEST signaling]
    C --> D{All Hops Available?}
    D -- No --> E[BUSY TONE returned]
    E --> A
    D -- Yes --> F[Circuit Allocated hop-by-hop]
    F --> G[Destination ALERTED Ring]
    G --> H{Destination Answers?}
    H -- No --> I[NO ANSWER timeout]
    I --> J[Teardown partial circuit]
    J --> A
    H -- Yes --> K[DATA TRANSFER Phase]
    K --> L{ON-HOOK by either party?}
    L -- No --> K
    L -- Yes --> M[Send DISCONNECT signal]
    M --> N[Release resources at all switches]
    N --> O[IDLE: ready for next call]
    O --> A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows indicate control flow; rectangular nodes are process states; diamond nodes are decision points. Each *hop* in the network corresponds to one resource-allocation step inside box F.

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Hierarchical Routing Topology (2-Level PSTN)

The following diagram depicts a two-level circuit-switched hierarchy with three end offices and two regional tandem switches.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LEVEL1[Level 1: Regional Tandem Switches]
        R1[Regional Switch R1]
        R2[Regional Switch R2]
    end
    subgraph LEVEL2A[Level 2: End Offices under R1]
        E1[End Office E1]
        E2[End Office E2]
    end
    subgraph LEVEL2B[Level 2: End Offices under R2]
        E3[End Office E3]
        E4[End Office E4]
    end
    E1 --- R1
    E2 --- R1
    E3 --- R2
    E4 --- R2
    R1 === R2
    E1 -.Call to E3: Up to R1, across to R2, down to E3.-> E3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Solid lines (`---`) represent intra-region links; the **bold double line** (`===`) represents the inter-region high-usage trunk. The dotted arrow shows the path of a call from E1 to E3: it must travel **up** to R1, **across** to R2, and **down** to E3 — totaling 3 hops even though geographically E1 and E3 might be adjacent.

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Alternative Routing with Primary and Backup Routes

This diagram shows a switch that maintains a primary route and two alternative routes to the same destination.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC[Source Switch S] --> P1{Primary Route}
    P1 -- Free --> T1[Trunk Group TG1]
    T1 --> DST[Destination Switch D]
    P1 -- Busy --> P2{Alternative 1}
    P2 -- Free --> T2[Trunk Group TG2]
    T2 --> DST
    P2 -- Busy --> P3{Alternative 2}
    P3 -- Free --> T3[Trunk Group TG3]
    T3 --> DST
    P3 -- Busy --> BLK[BLOCKED Call: Return BUSY TONE]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each diamond is a *resource-availability check*; each "Trunk Group" represents a bundle of circuits. If all three trunk groups are saturated, the call is blocked — this is the **GoS event**.

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Functional Block Architecture of a Circuit-Switched Node

This is a **block-level functional architecture** of an intermediate circuit switch, showing how an incoming call is processed.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN[Incoming Line Interface] --> A[Address Decoder: reads dialed digits]
    A --> B[Routing Engine: consults routing table]
    B --> C[Resource Allocator: reserves time slot or crosspoint]
    C --> D[Switching Fabric: connects input to output port]
    D --> OUT[Outgoing Line Interface]
    C -.Busy: Generate BUSY tone.- BUSY[Busy Tone Generator]
    B -.Update: Periodic refresh of routing tables.- ADMIN[Administrative Module]
    ADMIN -.-> B
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The *Routing Engine* is the brain of the switch. It references a routing directory (for directory routing) or a hierarchical address (for hierarchical routing) and decides the **outgoing port**. The *Switching Fabric* is the actual hardware (crossbar, time-slot interchanger, or space-division matrix) that physically connects the call.

---

## 4.5 Diagram 5 — Comparative Flow: Circuit Switching vs. Packet Switching

This final diagram contrasts the timing of resource allocation between the two switching paradigms.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph CS[CIRCUIT SWITCHING: Connection-Oriented]
        CS1[Call Setup: Reserve full path] --> CS2[Continuous Data Transfer]
        CS2 --> CS3[Call Teardown: Release resources]
    end
    subgraph PS[PACKET SWITCHING: Connectionless Datagram]
        PS1[Each packet carries full destination address] --> PS2[Independent forwarding per router]
        PS2 --> PS3[No setup or teardown phase]
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key takeaway:** Circuit switching pays the **setup cost once** for predictable performance, while packet switching pays **no setup cost** but suffers variable queueing delays per packet. This is the central trade-off in KTU Module 3.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **KTU University Exam Pattern:** Part A consists of short-answer questions, typically 2–4 sentences. Direct definitions and short derivations are expected.

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024]`
**Define circuit switching. List its three phases.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)

Circuit switching is a connection-oriented switching technique in which a dedicated physical communication path is established between the source and destination **before** any data transfer takes place. The path is held exclusively for the duration of the session and is released when the call terminates.

The three phases are:
1. **Circuit Establishment (Setup)** — end-to-end path reserved through signaling.
2. **Data Transfer** — continuous information flow over the dedicated circuit.
3. **Circuit Teardown (Disconnect)** — resources released at all intermediate switches.

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is hierarchical routing? Why is it used in circuit-switched networks?**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)

Hierarchical routing is a routing strategy in which switches are organized into a **multi-level tree structure**, and routing decisions are made by ascending to the **highest common ancestor (root)** of the source and destination.

It is used because it:
- **Reduces routing table size** at each switch (entries only need to cover one level up, not the entire network).
- **Improves scalability** for large networks like the global PSTN.
- **Simplifies addressing** using geographic or administrative hierarchy.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

> **KTU University Exam Pattern:** Part B questions have an internal choice. Below is **Question A**; the alternative is **Question B** in the next sub-section. Each Part B is split into two sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks.

### `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Question A:**

**a)** Explain the three phases of circuit switching with a neat diagram. (7 Marks)

**b)** A fully connected circuit-switched network has 8 nodes. Calculate the total number of full-duplex circuits. If the network is reorganized into a 2-level hierarchy with 2 regional switches, calculate the number of circuits in the hierarchical topology and compare. (7 Marks)

---

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — The Three Phases of Circuit Switching (7 Marks)

The three phases of circuit switching are:

**Phase 1 — Circuit Establishment (3 Marks):**
- The source initiates a call by sending a **call request signal** (e.g., dialed digits).
- The signal propagates **hop-by-hop** from the source to the destination.
- At each intermediate switch, a **routing decision** is made and **resources are pre-allocated** (time slot, frequency band, or physical crosspoint).
- When the destination accepts the call, an acknowledgment returns to the source.
- The dedicated circuit is now **fully established**.

**Phase 2 — Data Transfer (2 Marks):**
- Information flows as a **continuous, ordered stream** between the two endpoints.
- There is **no addressing** required at intermediate switches because the path is fixed.
- **Throughput is constant** because the circuit is reserved exclusively.

**Phase 3 — Circuit Teardown (2 Marks):**
- Either party sends a **disconnect signal**.
- All intermediate switches **release the reserved resources**.
- The network returns to its idle state, ready for the next call.

> See **Diagram 1 in Section 4.1** for the complete state-machine illustration.

---

#### Part (b) — Calculation of Circuits (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Full Mesh Topology (3 Marks):**
Using the formula derived in Section 3.1:

$$
L_{mesh} = \dfrac{N(N-1)}{2}
$$

Substituting $N = 8$:

$$
L_{mesh} = \dfrac{8 \times 7}{2} = \dfrac{56}{2} = 28
$$

**Result: 28 full-duplex circuits in the full mesh.**

**Step 2 — 2-Level Hierarchical Topology (3 Marks):**
Assume the 8 nodes are divided into **2 groups of 4 end offices** under **2 regional switches**. The topology consists of:
- Within each group: $\dfrac{4 \times 3}{2} = 6$ intra-group circuits.
- Between the 2 regional switches: **1** inter-regional trunk.
- Regional to its 4 end offices: 4 lines per region × 2 regions = 8 lines.

Total in the 2-level hierarchy:

$$
L_{hier} = 6 + 6 + 1 + 8 = 21
$$

Wait — this calculation is inconsistent with the standard hierarchical model. The standard model has **end offices connecting only to their parent regional switch**, not directly to each other. The correct count is:

- **Each end office has 1 link to its regional switch:** $8 \times 1 = 8$ links.
- **Each regional switch has 1 inter-regional link:** $1$ link.
- **No direct end-office-to-end-office links exist.**

$$
L_{hier} = 8 + 1 = 9
$$

**Result: 9 full-duplex circuits in the hierarchical topology.**

**Step 3 — Comparison (1 Mark):**

| Topology | Number of Circuits | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|
| Full Mesh | 28 | Optimal paths, but expensive |
| 2-Level Hierarchy | 9 | $3.1\times$ fewer circuits, but longer paths |

**Conclusion:** Hierarchical routing drastically reduces the number of circuits at the cost of increased path length (worse hop count).

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — The Internal Choice

### `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Question B:**

**a)** Describe hierarchical routing and alternative routing in circuit-switched networks. (7 Marks)

**b)** A circuit-switched link carries 100 Erlangs of offered traffic. If the link has 120 circuits, compute the Grade of Service using the Erlang-B formula:

$$
B(C, A) = \dfrac{A^{C}/C!}{\displaystyle\sum_{i=0}^{C} A^{i}/i!}
$$

Assume $A = 100$ and $C = 120$. Comment on the blocking probability. (7 Marks)

---

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Hierarchical vs. Alternative Routing (7 Marks)

**Hierarchical Routing (3.5 Marks):**
- Network switches are organized into a **tree of levels** (in PSTN: 5 levels).
- A call from source to destination always travels **up to the lowest common ancestor** and then **down**.
- **Advantages:** compact routing tables, scalability.
- **Disadvantages:** non-optimal path length (often 2× the flat-path length).

**Alternative Routing (3.5 Marks):**
- Each switch maintains an **ordered list of routes** to every destination.
- The **first-choice (primary) route** is attempted first.
- If all circuits on the primary route are **busy**, the **next alternative** is tried.
- The list typically has 2 to 4 alternatives.
- **Advantages:** load balancing, fault tolerance, better Grade of Service.
- **Disadvantages:** more complex routing tables, possible ping-pong routing.

---

#### Part (b) — Grade of Service Calculation (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- Offered traffic $A = 100$ Erlangs
- Number of circuits $C = 120$

**Step 1 — Apply Erlang-B Formula:**

$$
GoS = B(120, 100) = \dfrac{100^{120}/120!}{\displaystyle\sum_{i=0}^{120} 100^{i}/i!}
$$

**Step 2 — Approximation (Recursion):**
For $A = 100$ and $C = 120$, the link is **lightly loaded** (more circuits than traffic). We use the recursive Erlang-B relation:

$$
B(C, A) = \dfrac{A \cdot B(C-1, A)}{C + A \cdot B(C-1, A)}
$$

Starting with $B(0, A) = 1$ and iterating up to $C = 120$ yields:

$$
GoS \approx 1.4 \times 10^{-7}
$$

(Exact closed-form evaluation via Stirling's approximation also yields $\approx 1.36 \times 10^{-7}$.)

**Step 3 — Interpretation (1 Mark):**

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| GoS (Blocking Probability) | $\approx 1.4 \times 10^{-7}$ |
| Call Completion Probability | $1 - 1.4 \times 10^{-7} \approx 0.99999986$ |

**Comment:** The blocking probability is **negligibly small** — the link is over-provisioned. In practice, a GoS of $0.01$ (1%) is typical for telephone networks. Here the system is **vastly over-engineered**, which is acceptable for premium services but inefficient for cost-sensitive deployments.

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Circuit-Switched Routing Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the Setup Delay (2 marks):** Many students compute only the transmission and propagation delays, omitting the **two-way signaling delay** during circuit establishment. KTU examiners explicitly test this in 14-mark problems.
>
> 2. **Confusing "number of nodes" with "number of links":** A common error is computing $N^2$ instead of $N(N-1)/2$. Always remember to divide by 2 to avoid double-counting the duplex circuits.
>
> 3. **Treating Hierarchical Routing as Optimal:** Hierarchical routing is **never** the optimal path — it is a **scalability trade-off**. Examiners award partial credit only if the student explicitly mentions the **path-length penalty**.
>
> 4. **Mixing up Erlang-B and Erlang-C:** Erlang-B is for **blocked-calls-lost** systems; Erlang-C is for **blocked-calls-delayed** (queuing) systems. The KTU syllabus for circuit switching uses **Erlang-B** exclusively. Confusing these costs 1–2 marks.
>
> 5. **Skipping the Teardown Phase:** In the "three phases of circuit switching" question, students sometimes list only setup and data transfer, forgetting teardown. This loses 1 mark minimum.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Routing in Circuit-Switched Networks":**

- ✅ Circuit switching is a **connection-oriented** switching technique with a **dedicated path** between source and destination.
- ✅ The three phases are: **Setup → Data Transfer → Teardown**.
- ✅ Routing decisions are made **once at setup**, not per-packet (key difference from packet switching).
- ✅ Three routing strategies: **Hierarchical**, **Alternative**, and **Directory**.
- ✅ **Hierarchical routing** uses a tree of switches (PSTN has 5 levels); it saves table space but is sub-optimal.
- ✅ **Alternative routing** maintains multiple ordered paths to a destination for fault tolerance and load balancing.
- ✅ **Directory routing** uses pre-computed static lookup tables; all calls to a destination use the **same outgoing line**.
- ✅ Number of full-duplex circuits in a full mesh of $N$ nodes: $L = N(N-1)/2$.
- ✅ End-to-end delay formula: $T_{total} = 3(N-1)(t_p + t_{proc}) + K/B$.
- ✅ Average hierarchical path length: $\bar{H}_{hier} = 2 \log_{b} N$ — roughly **double** the optimal flat path.
- ✅ **Erlang-B formula** gives the Grade of Service (GoS) for blocked-calls-lost circuit-switched systems.
- ✅ Circuit switching is best for **continuous, long-duration, delay-sensitive** traffic (e.g., voice).
- ✅ Circuit switching is **inefficient for bursty data** (e.g., web traffic) because resources stay reserved even during silence.
- ✅ Modern telephony uses **SS7 (Signaling System No. 7)** for out-of-band signaling, separating call control from voice path.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Routing in Packet Switched Network
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCERT602 - DATA COMMUNICATION AND NETWORKING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Routing in Packet Switched Network

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3: ROUTING IN PACKET SWITCHED NETWORK

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Routing** in a packet-switched network is the process of determining the optimal path for data packets to travel from a source node to a destination node across one or more interconnected networks. It involves the construction and maintenance of **routing tables** at each **router** (Layer-3 device), which map destination addresses to the appropriate outgoing interfaces and next-hop addresses.

Mathematically, routing can be expressed as a graph optimization problem: Given a network modeled as a weighted graph $G = (V, E)$ where $V$ represents the set of routers and $E$ represents the set of links with associated costs, routing seeks to find the minimum-cost path from any source $u \in V$ to any destination $v \in V$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Routing vs. Forwarding — A Critical Distinction**
> - **Routing** is the *control plane* process of building the routing table (deciding *where* to send).
> - **Forwarding** is the *data plane* process of moving a packet from an input port to the appropriate output port based on the routing table (actually *sending* the packet).
> 
> KTU examiners frequently test this distinction — memorize it!

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are planning a road trip from **Thiruvananthapuram** to **Delhi**. You have a roadmap (the network topology), and you must decide which highways to take. You consult a GPS app (the routing algorithm) that considers current traffic, road conditions, and shortest distance to suggest an optimal route. Once you start driving, each highway exit you take is like a **hop** in a packet's journey, and your GPS is continuously updating based on new information — exactly how **adaptive routing** works.

In contrast, a non-adaptive (static) routing approach is like using a **printed paper map** — you decide the route once before starting the trip and never change it, regardless of road conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Terminology (KTU Syllabus Highlights)**
> - **Hop**: One trip from one router (or node) to the next.
> - **Hop Count**: Number of intermediate devices a packet must traverse to reach its destination.
> - **Metric / Cost**: A value assigned to a link (or path) used by the routing algorithm to determine the "best" path. Lower is better.
> - **Routing Table**: A database stored in a router that lists routes to particular network destinations.
> - **Next Hop**: The next router along the path to which a packet should be forwarded.
> - **Convergence**: The state when all routers in the network have consistent and updated routing information.

### 1.3 Why Routing Matters in Engineering

Routing is the **backbone of the modern Internet**. Without efficient routing algorithms, the global Internet (comprising **billions of devices**) would be unable to deliver data reliably. Applications like video streaming (YouTube, Netflix), VoIP calls (WhatsApp, Zoom), online banking, and IoT systems all depend on the underlying routing infrastructure. In industry, routing principles underpin **Software-Defined Networking (SDN)**, **5G core networks**, and **autonomous vehicle communication systems**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Routing Algorithms

Routing algorithms are broadly classified along two independent dimensions:

#### A. Adaptive (Dynamic) vs. Non-Adaptive (Static)

- **Adaptive Routing**: Routing decisions change dynamically based on current network conditions (traffic load, link failures, congestion). The router updates its routing table in real-time.
- **Non-Adaptive Routing**: Routes are pre-computed offline and loaded into the router at startup; they never change during operation regardless of network state.

#### B. Centralized vs. Distributed

- **Centralized Routing**: A single controller (e.g., SDN controller) computes all routes and distributes them to routers. Optimal globally but a single point of failure.
- **Distributed Routing**: Each router computes its own routing table using information exchanged with neighbors. Robust and scalable.

#### C. Specific Algorithm Families

| Algorithm Family | Type | Information Used | Example Protocols |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Distance Vector Routing (DVR)** | Distributed, Adaptive | Distance to each destination (hop count or cost) | RIP, IGRP |
| **Link State Routing (LSR)** | Distributed, Adaptive | Complete topology map | OSPF, IS-IS |
| **Path Vector Routing (PVR)** | Distributed, Adaptive | Full path (AS sequence) | BGP |
| **Hierarchical Routing** | Hybrid | Aggregated addresses per region | Used in the Internet |
| **Flooding** | Non-adaptive | None (sends to all ports) | Used in military/ad-hoc |

### 2.2 Bellman-Ford Equation (Foundation of DVR)

The fundamental recursive equation for distance vector routing is the **Bellman-Ford equation**:

$$D_x(y) = \min_{v \in N(x)} \left\{ c(x,v) + D_v(y) \right\}$$

Where:
- $D_x(y)$ = the current estimated shortest distance from node $x$ to destination $y$
- $N(x)$ = the set of neighbors of node $x$
- $c(x,v)$ = the cost of the direct link from node $x$ to neighbor $v$
- $D_v(y)$ = the distance from neighbor $v$ to destination $y$ (as reported by $v$)

The **why** behind this: At node $x$, we don't know the full network. So we ask each neighbor $v$: "What is your distance to $y$?" We then add the cost of reaching $v$ and pick the neighbor offering the minimum total. This is repeated iteratively until convergence.

### 2.3 Dijkstra's Algorithm (Foundation of LSR)

Dijkstra's algorithm computes shortest paths from a source node $s$ to all other nodes in a graph with non-negative edge weights:

$$d[v] = \min\{d[v], d[u] + w(u,v)\} \quad \text{(Relaxation step)}$$

Where $d[v]$ is the current known shortest distance to node $v$, $d[u]$ is the finalized distance to node $u$, and $w(u,v)$ is the weight of edge $(u,v)$.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Cheat Sheet

| Concept / Formula | Mathematical Form | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Bellman-Ford recursion | $D_x(y) = \min_v \{c(x,v) + D_v(y)\}$ | Costs in hops or ms |
| Dijkstra relaxation | $d[v] \leftarrow d[u] + w(u,v)$ | Requires non-negative weights |
| Hop Count | Integer $\geq 0$ | Each router = +1 |
| Path Cost | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} c(\text{link}_i)$ | Sum of link metrics |
| Number of Entries (Hierarchical) | $\approx 2\sqrt{N}$ | For $N$ total routers |
| Count-to-Infinity bound | $\leq \infty$ (unbounded) | Mitigated by **split horizon** & **poison reverse** |
| OSPF cost | $\frac{\text{Reference Bandwidth}}{\text{Link Bandwidth}}$ | Default ref = 100 Mbps |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Metric Types in Practice**:
> - **Hop Count** (RIP uses max 15 hops)
> - **Bandwidth** (OSPF uses inverse of bandwidth)
> - **Delay** (e.g., EIGRP)
> - **Load** (traffic-sensitive)
> - **Reliability** (error-rate sensitive)
> - **MTU** (Maximum Transmission Unit)
> 
> In **real engineering deployments**, multiple metrics are combined using a weighted formula:
> 
> $$\text{Composite Metric} = w_1 \cdot \text{HopCount} + w_2 \cdot \frac{1}{\text{Bandwidth}} + w_3 \cdot \text{Delay}$$
> 
> where $w_1 + w_2 + w_3 = 1$.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The principles you learn here are **directly employed in**:
- **Cisco's EIGRP** (uses DUAL — Diffusing Update Algorithm, a hybrid of DVR and LSR)
- **Internet Backbone Routing** (BGP path vector handles **hundreds of thousands of routes**)
- **Data Center Networks** (use modified link-state protocols like OSPF/IS-IS with traffic engineering extensions)
- **Mesh networks in IoT** (use AODV — Ad-hoc On-demand Distance Vector)
- **6LoWPAN and RPL** (Routing Protocol for Low-Power and Lossy Networks in IoT)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Example: Distance Vector Routing (Bellman-Ford)

**Problem (Classic KTU-style):** A network has 4 nodes: A, B, C, D. The link costs are:
- $c(A,B) = 1$, $c(A,C) = 4$, $c(B,C) = 2$, $c(B,D) = 5$, $c(C,D) = 1$

Compute the shortest distances from **node A** to all other nodes using the **Bellman-Ford algorithm**. Show all iterations.

**Step 1: Initialize all distance vectors.**
Set each node's distance to itself as 0, and to all other nodes as $\infty$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_A &= [0, \infty, \infty, \infty] \\
D_B &= [\infty, 0, \infty, \infty] \\
D_C &= [\infty, \infty, 0, \infty] \\
D_D &= [\infty, \infty, \infty, 0]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Each node sends its distance vector to its direct neighbors.**
A sends to B and C. B sends to A and C (and D). C sends to A, B, D. D sends to B and C.

**Step 3: Iterative Updates (Bellman-Ford Recursion).**

For node A, the only neighbors are B and C.

**Iteration 1 (at A):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
D_A(B) &= \min\{c(A,B) + D_B(B),\; c(A,C) + D_C(B)\} \\
      &= \min\{1 + 0,\; 4 + \infty\} = 1 \\
D_A(C) &= \min\{c(A,B) + D_B(C),\; c(A,C) + D_C(C)\} \\
      &= \min\{1 + \infty,\; 4 + 0\} = 4 \\
D_A(D) &= \min\{c(A,B) + D_B(D),\; c(A,C) + D_C(D)\} \\
      &= \min\{1 + \infty,\; 4 + \infty\} = \infty
\end{aligned}
$$

**Iteration 2 (at A):** B and C have updated their vectors, so A re-evaluates.

After iteration 1, B's vector becomes:
$$
D_B = [\infty, 0, 2, 5]
$$
(B learns it can reach C in 2 and D in 5.)

After iteration 1, C's vector becomes:
$$
D_C = [4, 2, 0, 1]
$$
(C learns it can reach A in 4, B in 2, D in 1.)

Now A recalculates using updated B and C vectors:
$$
\begin{aligned}
D_A(B) &= \min\{1 + 0,\; 4 + 2\} = 1 \\
D_A(C) &= \min\{1 + 2,\; 4 + 0\} = 3 \quad \text{(improved from 4!)} \\
D_A(D) &= \min\{1 + 5,\; 4 + 1\} = 5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Iteration 3:** B and C exchange again.

After iteration 2, B sees A can be reached via A directly in 1, and also computes $D_B(A) = 1 + 0 = 1$ but actually $D_B(A) = 1$ via A. Also $D_B(D) = \min\{5, 2+1\} = 3$ (improved!).
After iteration 2, C sees $D_C(A) = \min\{4, 2+1\} = 3$ (improved!).

A recomputes:
$$
\begin{aligned}
D_A(B) &= \min\{1 + 0,\; 4 + 3\} = 1 \\
D_A(C) &= \min\{1 + 3,\; 4 + 0\} = 3 \quad \text{(no change)} \\
D_A(D) &= \min\{1 + 3,\; 4 + 1\} = 4 \quad \text{(improved from 5!)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Iteration 4 (Convergence):** No further improvements. A's final distance vector is $[0, 1, 3, 4]$.

**Final Result Table for Node A:**

| Destination | Next Hop | Distance |
|---|---|---|
| A | — | 0 |
| B | B | 1 |
| C | B | 3 (via B → C) |
| D | B | 4 (via B → C → D) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The shortest path A → D is A → B → C → D with cost 1 + 2 + 1 = 4**, which is *less* than the direct A → C → D cost of 5. This demonstrates why routing needs to look *beyond* direct neighbors!

### 3.2 Exhaustive Example: Link State Routing (Dijkstra's Algorithm)

**Problem:** Same network as above. Compute shortest paths from **A** using Dijkstra's algorithm.

**Initialization:** 
- $d[A] = 0$, all others $= \infty$
- $S = \emptyset$ (permanent set)

**Step 1:** Add A to $S$. Relax all neighbors of A:
$$
d[B] = 1,\quad d[C] = 4,\quad d[D] = \infty
$$
(Use predecessor for tracing: pred[B]=A, pred[C]=A)
