# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**Q1. Differentiate between a Layer 2 switch and a Layer 3 switch. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Layer 2 Switch | Layer 3 Switch |
|---|---|---|
| **OSI Layer** | Data Link Layer (Layer 2) | Network Layer (Layer 3) |
| **Address Used** | 48-bit MAC address | 32-bit IPv4 / 128-bit IPv6 address |
| **Forwarding Table** | CAM table (exact match) | FIB / TCAM (longest prefix match) |
| **Inter-VLAN Routing** | Not supported natively | Supported via SVI / Routed Port |
| **Routing Protocols** | Not supported | OSPF, EIGRP, BGP, RIP, IS-IS |
| **Broadcast Domain** | Does not segment (by default) | Segments (each VLAN = separate L3 subnet) |
| **Example** | Cisco Catalyst 2960 | Cisco Catalyst 3650/3850/9000 |

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q2. What is a CAM table? How is it populated in a Layer 2 switch? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
A **CAM (Content Addressable Memory) table** is a high-speed hardware lookup table inside a Layer 2 switch that maps MAC addresses to switch ports. It is populated through a process called **MAC Address Learning**: when a frame enters a switch port, the switch reads the **Source MAC (SMAC)** from the frame header and records the SMAC along with the ingress port number and VLAN ID into the CAM table. This entry is marked as **Dynamic** and is aged out after **300 seconds** of inactivity (configurable). Static entries can be manually configured for security.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 4 Internal Choice Pattern]**

**(a) Explain the internal architecture of a Layer 2 switch with a neat block diagram. Describe the three primary functions: Learning, Flooding, and Forwarding. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
A Layer 2 switch is a multiport bridge that uses MAC addresses for forwarding decisions. It operates at the Data Link Layer and uses a CAM table for high-speed lookups.

**Architecture (3 Marks):**
The internal architecture consists of:
- **Ingress Ports:** Physical interfaces that receive incoming frames.
- **Frame Parser / Decoder:** Extracts SMAC, DMAC, VLAN tag, Ethertype, and FCS.
- **CAM Table:** High-speed associative memory for MAC-to-port mappings.
- **Forwarding Engine (ASIC):** Decides whether to flood, forward, or filter.
- **Egress Queues:** Buffers frames per output port; supports QoS priority.
- **Backplane / Switching Fabric:** Internal high-speed bus connecting all ports.

**Three Primary Functions (3 Marks):**

1. **Learning** — On every incoming frame, the switch reads the SMAC and ingress port. If the SMAC is not in the CAM table, a new entry is created. If it exists, the entry is refreshed and the aging timer is reset.

2. **Flooding** — When the DMAC is not found in the CAM table (CAM miss), the switch floods the frame out of all ports in the same VLAN, except the ingress port. This is also called **Unknown Unicast Flooding**.

3. **Forwarding & Filtering** — When the DMAC is found in the CAM table, the frame is forwarded only out of the mapped egress port. If the egress port is the same as the ingress port, the frame is **filtered** (dropped) to prevent unnecessary internal switching.

**Conclusion:** These three functions are executed in hardware by ASICs at line rate, making Layer 2 switches the workhorse of enterprise access layers.

---

**(b) What is a Layer 3 switch? Explain how it performs Inter-VLAN routing using SVIs with a suitable diagram. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
A Layer 3 switch is a network device that performs packet routing at hardware speed using ASICs and TCAM, while retaining the high port density of a Layer 2 switch.

**SVI Explanation (3 Marks):**
An **SVI (Switched Virtual Interface)** is a virtual Layer 3 interface that is logically bound to a VLAN. It acts as the default gateway for all hosts in that VLAN. Each VLAN that requires inter-VLAN routing must have its own SVI configured with an IP address. The SVI replaces the traditional "router-on-a-stick" by performing routing internally.

**Inter-VLAN Routing Process (2 Marks):**
- Step 1: Host in VLAN 10 sends packet to default gateway (SVI VLAN 10 IP).
- Step 2: Switch receives frame tagged VLAN 10; ASIC consults FIB via TCAM.
- Step 3: FIB lookup identifies destination subnet `192.168.20.0/24` → egress SVI = VLAN 20.
- Step 4: Switch rewrites L2 header (new DMAC = destination host, new SMAC = SVI VLAN 20 MAC), changes VLAN tag to 20.
- Step 5: Forwards frame at wire speed to Host in VLAN 20.

**Diagram (1 Mark):** Refer to Section 4.4 sequence diagram.

**Conclusion:** Layer 3 switches with SVIs deliver wire-speed inter-VLAN routing, eliminate the need for external routers, and provide sub-microsecond latency.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Retest Pattern]**

**(a) Compare Store-and-Forward, Cut-Through, and Fragment-Free switching. Mention the latency and error-checking characteristics of each. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
Layer 2 switches support three primary frame forwarding modes, each with different trade-offs between latency and error checking.

**Comparison Table (4 Marks):**

| Parameter | Store-and-Forward | Cut-Through | Fragment-Free |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Frame Read Before Forward** | Entire frame (all bits) | Only first 14 bytes (DMAC) | First 64 bytes |
| **Latency** | High and variable | Low and fixed | Medium |
| **CRC Check** | Yes (full validation) | No | Partial (collision window) |
| **Error Frame Handling** | Discards corrupted frames | Forwards corrupted frames | Filters collision fragments |
| **QoS Support** | Yes (full L2-L4 inspection) | No (only L2 DMAC visible) | No |
| **Use Case** | Enterprise, Data Center | HFT, Low-latency | Legacy networks |
| **Latency Formula** | $\frac{\text{Frame Size (bits)}}{\text{Speed}}$ | $\frac{112 \text{ bits}}{\text{Speed}}$ | $\frac{512 \text{ bits}}{\text{Speed}}$ |

**Explanation (2 Marks):**
Store-and-Forward reads the entire frame into buffer memory, validates the FCS (CRC-32), and only then forwards the frame. Cut-Through begins forwarding as soon as the DMAC is read, achieving minimum latency but cannot detect bit errors. Fragment-Free is a hybrid that reads the first 64 bytes — the Ethernet minimum frame size and the collision window — filtering most collision fragments while still being faster than Store-and-Forward.

**Conclusion:** Modern enterprise switches default to **Store-and-Forward** for security and QoS support, while **Cut-Through** is reserved for ultra-low-latency specialized networks.

---

**(b) A 48-port Gigabit Ethernet Layer 2 switch operates in full-duplex mode. Calculate the minimum required backplane bandwidth. If the same switch supports 4 × 10G SFP+ uplinks, what is the new total backplane requirement? (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- 48 × 1G RJ45 ports
- 4 × 10G SFP+ uplink ports
- Full-duplex operation

**Formula:**

$$
B_{\text{backplane}} = N \times S \times 2 \quad \text{(full-duplex factor of 2)}
$$

**Part 1 — Only 1G ports (4 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_1 &= 48 \times 1\,\text{Gbps} \times 2 \\
&= 96\,\text{Gbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part 2 — Add 10G uplinks (3 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_2 &= (48 \times 1 \times 2) + (4 \times 10 \times 2) \\
&= 96 + 80 \\
&= 176\,\text{Gbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The minimum non-blocking backplane requirement is **96 Gbps** (1G only) and **176 Gbps** (with 4×10G uplinks). This matches the spec sheet of the Cisco Catalyst 2960-X (48-port + 4 SFP+), which is a frequently cited KTU reference value.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Mistakes in Layer 2 / Layer 3 Switch Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing CAM with TCAM** — Students often write "CAM table stores IP addresses." **WRONG.** CAM is for **MAC** (exact match). TCAM is for **IP routes** (LPM). Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in backplane calculation for full-duplex. Always write: `N × Speed × 2`. Half-duplex = no factor of 2.
>
> 3. **Saying L2 switch "does not segment broadcast domains"** — It does NOT, by default. Saying it does costs a mark.
>
> 4. **Confusing SVI with Routed Port** — SVI is a *virtual* L3 interface per VLAN. Routed Port is a *physical* port configured with `no switchport`. Both are L3, but SVI is for inter-VLAN, Routed Port is for point-to-point L3 links.
>
> 5. **Storing MAC table is a "Learning" function, not "Forwarding."** Examiners expect clear separation of the three functions.
>
> 6. **Default VLAN for all ports = VLAN 1.** All frames are tagged with VLAN 1 unless reconfigured. Many students forget this.
>
> 7. **Cut-Through latency formula** uses **14 bytes (preamble + DMAC) = 112 bits**, NOT the entire 64-byte minimum frame. Mixing these up costs full marks.
>
> 8. **HSRP / VRRP / GLBP** are L3 features — do not attribute them to L2 switches.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — "Print This Before Exam Night"**

### 🔹 Layer 2 Switch — Key Facts
- Operates at **OSI Layer 2** using **48-bit MAC addresses**.
- Builds **CAM table** dynamically via source-address learning.
- Three functions: **Learn → Flood → Forward/Filter**.
- **Forwarding modes:** Store-and-Forward, Cut-Through, Fragment-Free.
- Segments **collision domains** only; does NOT segment broadcast domains.
- Default CAM aging timer: **300 seconds**.
- Supports **IEEE 802.1Q VLAN tagging** (12-bit VID, range 1–4094).
- Examples: Cisco Catalyst 2960, HP Aruba 2530.

### 🔹 Layer 3 Switch — Key Facts
- Operates at **OSI Layer 3** using **IP addresses**.
- Uses **ASIC + TCAM** for hardware-accelerated routing (wire speed).
- Builds **FIB** from **RIB**; performs **Longest Prefix Match (LPM)**.
- Supports **Inter-VLAN routing via SVI** and **Routed Ports (`no switchport`)**.
- Runs **routing protocols**: OSPF, EIGRP, BGP, RIP, IS-IS.
- Supports **First-Hop Redundancy**: HSRP, VRRP, GLBP.
- Supports **L3/L4 ACLs** for granular security.
- Examples: Cisco Catalyst 3650, 3850, 9300, Nexus 9000.

### 🔹 Numerical Must-Knows
- **Backplane (full-duplex)** = `Ports × Speed × 2` Gbps.
- **Cut-Through latency** = `112 bits / Link Speed` seconds.
- **Store-and-Forward latency** = `Frame Size (bits) / Link Speed` seconds.
- **CAM table size** = typically 16K–128K entries.
- **MAC address** = 48 bits = 6 bytes (e.g., `AA:BB:CC:DD:EE:FF`).
- **IPv4 address** = 32 bits = 4 octets (e.g., `192.168.10.1`).
- **VLAN ID range** = 1–4094 (12-bit field).
- **Default CAM aging** = 300 seconds.
- **802.1Q tag** = 4 bytes (TPID + TCI).

### 🔹 Quick-Recall Comparison Mnemonic
> **"MAC = Memory, IP = Identity. CAM = City Address Memory, TCAM = Total City Address Map."**

### 🔹 KTU-Asked Differences (Always Remember)
1. L2 switch = Layer 2 = MAC = CAM = Bridging.
2. L3 switch = Layer 3 = IP = TCAM/FIB = Routing.
3. Router = Layer 3 = IP = Software CPU = Slower but more feature-rich.
4. L3 switch = Router + L2 switch — but **hardware-accelerated** routing.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: High-Speed LANs: The Emergence of High-Speed LANs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCERT602 - DATA COMMUNICATION AND NETWORKING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** High-Speed LANs: The Emergence of High-Speed LANs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **High-Speed LAN** is a Local Area Network technology capable of supporting aggregate data rates of **100 Mbps and above**, engineered to overcome the bandwidth, latency, and scalability limitations of classical shared-medium Ethernet (10 Mbps) and legacy Token Ring (4/16 Mbps) systems. The formal emergence of high-speed LANs is rooted in the IEEE 802 standardization committee's response to the explosive growth of client–server computing, multimedia traffic, and intranet/Internet workloads during the early-to-mid 1990s.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):**
> *"High-Speed LANs emerged due to the demand for higher bandwidth driven by graphical, multimedia, and client-server applications. Three principal approaches were standardized: (1) Fast Ethernet (IEEE 802.3u), (2) 100VG-AnyLAN (IEEE 802.12), and (3) Gigabit Ethernet (IEEE 802.3z / 802.3ab)."*

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy (The Highway Analogy)

Imagine the original 10 Mbps Ethernet as a **single two-lane rural road**. In 1985, this road comfortably handled the small village traffic of email and file transfers. By 1992, that same road had to carry:
- Heavy trucks (large database backups),
- Sports cars needing low latency (real-time video),
- Convoys of buses (Web traffic and multimedia).

Result: massive congestion, "jams" (collisions), and frustrated drivers (users). The solution is **not to invent a totally new vehicle** but to **widen the existing road, add lane-control rules, and improve the signalling system**. That is precisely what Fast Ethernet, 100VG-AnyLAN, and Gigabit Ethernet did — they preserved the **familiar vehicle design (Ethernet frame format)** while upgrading the **road infrastructure (physical medium and signaling)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Insight:** High-Speed LANs were deliberately designed as **backward-compatible evolutionary upgrades**, not revolutionary replacements. This is why a 100 Mbps NIC can plug into a 10 Mbps hub and still communicate.

### 1.3 Why High-Speed LANs Emerged — The Five Driving Forces

| # | Driving Force | Engineering Impact |
|---|---------------|--------------------|
| 1 | **CPU Performance Surge** | CPUs crossed 100 MIPS — applications could generate data faster than 10 Mbps LANs could carry. |
| 2 | **Graphical User Interfaces (GUIs)** | X-Windows, Windows NT, and CAD/CAM moved bulk pixel data across the network. |
| 3 | **Client–Server Architectures** | Centralized databases forced high-bandwidth, low-latency server links. |
| 4 | **Intranet / Internet Boom** | Browsers, email, and downloaded media saturated 10 Mbps shared segments. |
| 5 | **Multimedia Streaming** | Voice (64 kbps) and compressed video (1.5–6 Mbps) demanded QoS and bandwidth guarantees. |

### 1.4 The Three Strategic Approaches to High-Speed LANs

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Triad — Memorize This:**
> 1. **Approach 1 — Fast Ethernet (IEEE 802.3u):** "Faster over the same wire" — retain CSMA/CD MAC, increase clock 10×.
> 2. **Approach 2 — 100VG-AnyLAN (IEEE 802.12):** "New MAC for new wire" — replace CSMA/CD with demand-priority access.
> 3. **Approach 3 — Gigabit Ethernet (IEEE 802.3z/ab):** "100× faster, same philosophy" — extend Fast Ethernet to 1 Gbps; later 10 Gigabit.

Only **Fast Ethernet and Gigabit Ethernet survived commercially**; 100VG-AnyLAN was abandoned by the late 1990s. However, KTU examiners frequently test all three for conceptual comparison.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Logic of Each Approach

#### A. Fast Ethernet (IEEE 802.3u — 100 Mbps)

- **Core Idea:** Compress the bit time of classical Ethernet by a factor of 10. The MAC sublayer protocol (CSMA/CD), the minimum frame size (64 bytes), and the maximum frame size (1518 bytes) are **all preserved exactly**.
- **Key Constraint:** Since the bit time shrinks 10×, the network diameter (maximum cable length) must shrink 10× to keep the round-trip propagation delay within the collision-detection window. This is the **"Diameter Problem."**
- **Resolution:** Use hubs/repeaters with smaller collision domains, full-duplex switched links, and improved physical coding (MLT-3 for TX, 4B5B encoding).
- **Physical Layer Variants (Memorize the Naming Convention):**
  * **100BASE-TX** — 2 pairs of Cat-5 UTP or STP (full-duplex over each pair).
  * **100BASE-FX** — 2 strands of 62.5/125 µm multimode fiber.
  * **100BASE-T4** — 4 pairs of Cat-3 UTP (obsolete).

#### B. 100VG-AnyLAN (IEEE 802.12 — 100 Mbps)

- **Core Idea:** Discard CSMA/CD entirely; replace it with a **Demand Priority** access method controlled by a root hub.
- **Mechanism:** End stations send a "request" with one of two priority levels (normal / high). The hub scans all ports in round-robin order and grants the next transmission slot to the highest-priority waiting station.
- **Advantage:** Eliminates collisions → no slot-time constraint → network diameter can be larger than Fast Ethernet.
- **Disadvantage:** Incompatible with the huge installed base of CSMA/CD Ethernet; the term "AnyLAN" implies it can carry both Ethernet and Token Ring frames (encapsulation, not native).

#### C. Gigabit Ethernet (IEEE 802.3z for fiber / 802.3ab for copper — 1 Gbps, later 10 GbE IEEE 802.3ae)

- **Core Idea:** Scale Fast Ethernet's philosophy up by another factor of 10 while solving the resulting **bit-cell vs. cable-length paradox**.
- **Two Deployment Modes:**
  * **Half-Duplex (Shared):** Preserves CSMA/CD but uses **Carrier Extension** (padding frames to 512 bytes) and **Frame Bursting** (sending multiple frames in one slot). Maximum network diameter shrinks to ~20–25 m, which is impractical.
  * **Full-Duplex (Switched):** The de-facto deployment. No CSMA/CD, no collision domain limit, distances dictated only by the physical medium (up to 5 km on single-mode fiber for 1000BASE-LX).
- **Physical Layer Variants:**
  * **1000BASE-SX** — short-wavelength laser, multimode fiber (up to 550 m).
  * **1000BASE-LX** — long-wavelength laser, single-mode or multimode fiber (up to 5 km).
  * **1000BASE-CX** — short copper twinax cable (25 m, for data-center racks).
  * **1000BASE-T** — 4 pairs of Cat-5e/Cat-6 UTP (100 m), using **PAM-5** with all 4 pairs in parallel (4D-PAM5 trellis coding).

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas are mathematically verifiable from first principles of digital transmission.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Engineering Meaning |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Bit Time** | $T_{bit} = \dfrac{1}{R}$ | seconds (s) | Time occupied by one bit on the medium. |
| 2 | **Slot Time (Ethernet)** | $T_{slot} = 512 \times T_{bit}$ | seconds (s) | Maximum round-trip time for collision detection. |
| 3 | **Slot Time for 10 Mbps** | $T_{slot}^{10} = 51.2 \,\mu s$ | μs | Classical Ethernet value. |
| 4 | **Slot Time for 100 Mbps** | $T_{slot}^{100} = 5.12 \,\mu s$ | μs | Fast Ethernet value (10× smaller). |
| 5 | **Slot Time for 1 Gbps** | $T_{slot}^{1G} = 0.512 \,\mu s$ | μs | Gigabit Ethernet value (100× smaller). |
| 6 | **Network Diameter (max)** | $D_{max} \approx \dfrac{v \cdot T_{slot}}{2}$ | metres (m) | Maximum cable length for CSMA/CD to work. |
| 7 | **Signal Propagation Speed in UTP** | $v \approx 2 \times 10^{8}$ | m/s | Standard approximation for twisted-pair copper. |
| 8 | **Efficiency of CSMA/CD** | $E = \dfrac{1}{1 + 6.44 \cdot a}$, where $a = \dfrac{T_{prop}}{T_{trans}}$ | dimensionless | Shows efficiency loss at high speeds / long cables. |
| 9 | **Bandwidth-Distance Product** | $B \cdot D \approx \text{constant}$ | (bit/s)·m | Why speed × distance must remain bounded for collision detection. |
| 10 | **Frame Transmission Time** | $T_{trans} = \dfrac{L \times 8}{R}$ | seconds (s) | Time to push a frame of $L$ bytes onto the wire at rate $R$. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight from the Formula Sheet:** As $R$ increases 10× (10 → 100 Mbps), $T_{bit}$ and $T_{slot}$ both shrink 10×. Therefore, the **maximum network diameter for CSMA/CD must also shrink 10×** to maintain the same collision-detection guarantee. This is the **single most testable fact** about high-speed LANs in KTU exams.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Data Centers:** 10/40/100 Gigabit Ethernet forms the server-rack fabric in modern hyperscale data centers (AWS, Azure, Google Cloud).
- **Campus Backbones:** Gigabit and 10 Gigabit Ethernet uplinks connect building switches to the core.
- **Fibre Channel Replacement:** Modern storage area networks (SANs) increasingly use Ethernet-based protocols (iSCSI, FCoE) because of cost and convergence.
- **5G Backhaul (Fronthaul):** The link between the 5G baseband unit (BBU) and the radio unit (RU) uses 10/25 Gigabit Ethernet standardized by IEEE 1914 and eCPRI.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Note:** These derivations are the kind of numerical problems KTU sets under "Part B (14 marks)" — usually a 7-mark sub-question that asks you to calculate slot time, network diameter, or efficiency for a given speed/distance. Solve them by hand to internalize the relationship.

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Bit Time and Slot Time Scaling

**Problem:** Compute the bit time and slot time for 10 Mbps, 100 Mbps, and 1 Gbps Ethernet. Comment on the implications.

**Step 1 — Recall the definition of Bit Time.**

The bit time is the duration for which one bit occupies the physical medium. It is the reciprocal of the data rate $R$.

$$
T_{bit} = \frac{1}{R}
$$

*Explanation:* If $R$ bits are transmitted per second, each bit occupies $1/R$ seconds.

**Step 2 — For 10 Mbps Classical Ethernet.**

Substitute $R = 10 \times 10^{6}$ bits/s:

$$
T_{bit}^{10} = \frac{1}{10 \times 10^{6}} = 1 \times 10^{-7} \text{ s} = 100 \text{ ns}
$$

The IEEE 802.3 slot time is defined as the time required to transmit **512 bits** (the minimum back-off window and the minimum frame size). Therefore:

$$
T_{slot}^{10} = 512 \times T_{bit}^{10} = 512 \times 100 \text{ ns} = 51{,}200 \text{ ns} = 51.2 \,\mu s
$$

**Step 3 — For 100 Mbps Fast Ethernet.**

Substitute $R = 100 \times 10^{6}$ bits/s:

$$
T_{bit}^{100} = \frac{1}{100 \times 10^{6}} = 1 \times 10^{-8} \text{ s} = 10 \text{ ns}
$$

The slot time is again 512 bit times, but now each bit time is 10× shorter:

$$
T_{slot}^{100} = 512 \times T_{bit}^{100} = 512 \times 10 \text{ ns} = 5{,}120 \text{ ns} = 5.12 \,\mu s
$$

**Step 4 — For 1 Gbps Gigabit Ethernet.**

Substitute $R = 1 \times 10^{9}$ bits/s:

$$
T_{bit}^{1G} = \frac{1}{1 \times 10^{9}} = 1 \times 10^{-9} \text{ s} = 1 \text{ ns}
$$

And the slot time is:

$$
T_{slot}^{1G} = 512 \times T_{bit}^{1G} = 512 \times 1 \text{ ns} = 512 \text{ ns} = 0.512 \,\mu s
$$

**Step 5 — Comment on Implications.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Each tenfold increase in speed shrinks the slot time (and hence the maximum network diameter under CSMA/CD) by a factor of 10. This is the fundamental reason Gigabit Ethernet in half-duplex mode is impractical beyond ~25 m, and full-duplex switched operation is mandatory for 1 Gbps and above.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Maximum Network Diameter for CSMA/CD

**Problem:** For 10 Mbps and 100 Mbps Ethernet, compute the maximum theoretical half-duplex network diameter, given signal propagation speed in UTP $v = 2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s.

**Step 1 — The Fundamental Collision-Detection Constraint.**

For a station at one extreme of the network to detect a collision with a frame from the opposite extreme, the **round-trip propagation delay** must be less than the **slot time** (so that the sender is still transmitting when the collision signal returns).

$$
2 \times T_{prop} \le T_{slot}
$$

where $T_{prop} = D / v$ is the one-way propagation delay over a network of diameter $D$.

**Step 2 — Rearrange to Solve for Diameter.**

$$
2 \times \frac{D_{max}}{v} \le T_{slot} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad D_{max} \le \frac{v \cdot T_{slot}}{2}
$$

*Explanation:* The slot time window allows the signal to travel a round trip. Half of that round trip (i.e., one-way diameter) is the maximum cable length.

**Step 3 — Plug in values for 10 Mbps Ethernet.**

Use $v = 2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s and $T_{slot}^{10} = 51.2 \,\mu s = 51.2 \times 10^{-6}$ s:

$$
D_{max}^{10} \le \frac{2 \times 10^{8} \times 51.2 \times 10^{-6}}{2} = 51.2 \times 10^{2} = 5{,}120 \text{ m} = 5.12 \text{ km}
$$

In practice, IEEE 802.3 limits the 10BASE-T segment to **100 m** per link and the entire collision domain to roughly **2.5 km** (5 segments × 500 m with repeaters) due to repeater latency. The 5.12 km is the **theoretical physical-layer limit**, not the practical hub-based limit.

**Step 4 — Plug in values for 100 Mbps Fast Ethernet.**

Use $T_{slot}^{100} = 5.12 \,\mu s$:

$$
D_{max}^{100} \le \frac{2 \times 10^{8} \times 5.12 \times 10^{-6}}{2} = 5.12 \times 10^{2} = 512 \text{ m}
$$

So Fast Ethernet's theoretical maximum diameter is **exactly 1/10th of 10 Mbps Ethernet**, confirming the inverse proportionality.

**Step 5 — Plug in values for 1 Gbps Gigabit Ethernet.**

Use $T_{slot}^{1G} = 0.512 \,\mu s$:

$$
D_{max}^{1G} \le \frac{2 \times 10^{8} \times 0.512 \times 10^{-6}}{2} = 51.2 \text{ m}
$$

A 51-metre maximum half-duplex collision domain is essentially useless — which is why Gigabit Ethernet is deployed as full-duplex switched.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Efficiency of CSMA/CD at High Speeds

**Problem:** Calculate the efficiency of CSMA/CD for (i) a 10 Mbps network with 100 stations spread over a 2.5 km diameter, and (ii) a 100 Mbps network with the same 2.5 km diameter. Comment on the result.

**Step 1 — The Efficiency Formula.**

For 1-persistent CSMA/CD with $N$ stations, the approximate channel efficiency is:

$$
E = \frac{1}{1 + 6.44 \cdot a}, \quad \text{where} \quad a = \frac{T_{prop}}{T_{trans}}
$$

$T_{trans}$ is the time to transmit one maximum-size frame (1518 bytes ≈ 12,144 bits).

**Step 2 — Compute $T_{trans}$ at 10 Mbps.**

$$
T_{trans}^{10} = \frac{12{,}144 \text{ bits}}{10 \times 10^{6} \text{ bits/s}} = 1.2144 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 1{,}214.4 \,\mu s
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $T_{prop}$ for 2.5 km.**

$$
T_{prop} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{3} \text{ m}}{2 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}} = 1.25 \times 10^{-5} \text{ s} = 12.5 \,\mu s
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $a$ and $E$ at 10 Mbps.**

$$
a_{10} = \frac{12.5}{1214.4} \approx 0.0103
$$

$$
E_{10} = \frac{1}{1 + 6.44 \times 0.0103} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.0663} \approx 0.938 \text{ (93.8\% efficient)}
$$

**Step 5 — Recompute at 100 Mbps (same cable, same frame).**

$T_{prop}$ is unchanged (it's a property of the cable), but $T_{trans}$ shrinks 10×:

$$
T_{trans}^{100} = \frac{12{,}144}{100 \times 10^{6}} = 121.44 \,\mu s
$$

$$
a_{100} = \frac{12.5}{121.44} \approx 0.103
$$

$$
E_{100} = \frac{1}{1 + 6.44 \times 0.103} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.663} \approx 0.601 \text{ (60.1\% efficient)}
$$

**Step 6 — Engineering Interpretation.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The same 2.5 km network drops from **93.8% efficiency at 10 Mbps to 60.1% at 100 Mbps** purely because the frame transmit time shrinks relative to propagation delay. At 1 Gbps, $a \approx 1.03$ and efficiency falls below 14%. This is the mathematical proof that **CSMA/CD becomes self-defeating at gigabit speeds over any non-trivial distance** — a major reason the industry pivoted to switched full-duplex operation.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU's Most-Favoured Table)

| Feature | Classical Ethernet (10BASE-T) | Fast Ethernet (100BASE-TX) | 100VG-AnyLAN | Gigabit Ethernet (1000BASE-T) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **IEEE Standard** | 802.3 | 802.3u | 802.12 | 802.3z / 802.3ab |
| **Speed** | 10 Mbps | 100 Mbps | 100 Mbps | 1 Gbps |
| **MAC Protocol** | CSMA/CD | CSMA/CD (preserved) | Demand Priority | CSMA/CD (half) / Full-Duplex (full) |
| **Frame Format** | Ethernet II | **Identical** | Ethernet + Token Ring | **Identical** |
| **Bit Time** | 100 ns | 10 ns | 10 ns | 1 ns |
| **Slot Time** | 51.2 μs | 5.12 μs | Not applicable | 0.512 μs (with carrier extension) |
| **Max Diameter (Half-Duplex)** | ~2.5 km | ~205–250 m | ~4 km (demand priority) | ~25–50 m (impractical) |
| **Typical Deployment** | Obsolete | Legacy desktops | **Obsolete (defunct)** | Servers, uplinks, desktops |
| **Encoding** | Manchester | 4B5B + MLT-3 | 5B6B + NRZ | 8B10B (fiber) / PAM-5 (copper) |
| **Topology** | Star | Star | Star | Star / Switched full-duplex |
| **Auto-Negotiation** | No | Yes (IEEE 802.3u clause 28) | No | Yes |
| **Commercial Status** | Legacy | Legacy | Defunct (2000s) | Current mainstream |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Evolutionary Roadmap of High-Speed LANs

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the technological lineage, the IEEE standards family, and the design philosophy (CSMA/CD vs. Demand Priority) of each high-speed LAN approach.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    StartNode["Classical Ethernet 10 Mbps IEEE 802.3 1985 CSMA CD Bus Coax"]
    
    StartNode --> BranchA["Branch A CSMA CD Philosophy Preserved"]
    StartNode --> BranchB["Branch B New MAC for Voice Grade Wiring"]
    
    BranchA --> FastEth["Fast Ethernet 100 Mbps IEEE 802.3u 1995 100BASE TX FX T4"]
    FastEth --> GigEth["Gigabit Ethernet 1 Gbps IEEE 802.3z 802.3ab 1999 1000BASE T SX LX CX"]
    GigEth --> TenGig["10 Gigabit Ethernet 10 Gbps IEEE 802.3ae 2002 Fiber Only Full Duplex"]
    TenGig --> FortyGig["40 100 Gigabit Ethernet IEEE 802.3ba 2010 Data Center Backbone"]
    
    BranchB --> VGAnyLAN["100VG AnyLAN 100 Mbps IEEE 802.12 1995 Demand Priority Hub Centric"]
    VGAnyLAN -.Defunct 2001.-> EndNode["Commercial Outcome Fast Ethernet and Gigabit Dominate Market"]
    FastEth -.Success.-> EndNode
    GigEth -.Success.-> EndNode
```

**Reading the Diagram:**

- The **left subtree (Branch A)** shows the surviving CSMA/CD lineage, evolving from 10 Mbps → 100 Mbps → 1 Gbps → 10 Gbps → 40/100 Gbps.
- The **right subtree (Branch B)** shows the abandoned 100VG-AnyLAN, which used demand priority instead of CSMA/CD.
- The **dashed arrows** indicate the commercial outcome: only the CSMA/CD lineage survived.

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The Fast Ethernet Hub-Repeater Model

The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the internal architecture of a **100 Mbps Fast Ethernet Class II Repeater Hub**, illustrating how it handles the reduced slot time and inter-port timing constraints required by IEEE 802.3u.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    StaA["Station A 100BASE TX"] -->|MLT 3 Signal| PortA["Port 1 PHY Receiver"]
    StaB["Station B 100BASE TX"] -->|MLT 3 Signal| PortB["Port 2 PHY Receiver"]
    StaC["Station C 10BASE T Legacy"] -->|Manchester| PortC["Port 3 Auto Negotiate PHY 10 or 100"]
    
    PortA --> IdlDetectA["Idle Detect and Clock Recovery"]
    PortB --> IdlDetectB["Idle Detect and Clock Recovery"]
    PortC --> IdlDetectC["Idle Detect and Auto Neg"]
    
    IdlDetectA --> CoreLogic["Hub Core Logic 100 Mbps Domain Collision Detection Repeater Rules"]
    IdlDetectB --> CoreLogic
    IdlDetectC --> CoreLogic
    
    CoreLogic --> JamGen["Jam Signal Generator On Collision"]
    CoreLogic --> PortTXA["Port 1 PHY Transmitter"]
    CoreLogic --> PortTXB["Port 2 PHY Transmitter"]
    CoreLogic --> PortTXC["Port 3 PHY Transmitter"]
    
    JamGen -.JAM.-> PortTXA
    JamGen -.JAM.-> PortTXB
    JamGen -.JAM.-> PortTXC
    
    PortTXA -->|MLT 3| StaA
    PortTXB -->|MLT 3| StaB
    PortTXC -->|Auto rate| StaC
    
    Mgmt["Management Agent SNMP"] -.Control.-> CoreLogic
```

**Reading the Architecture:**

- **PHY Layer per Port:** Each port has its own physical-layer transceiver. Auto-negotiation (IEEE 802.3u Clause 28) allows the hub to interoperate with 10BASE-T legacy stations (like Station C).
- **Core Logic:** Implements the CSMA/CD repeater rules — detects collisions, generates a 32-bit JAM signal, and enforces the 5.12 μs slot-time budget.
- **JAM Signal:** When two stations transmit simultaneously, the hub sends a JAM pattern on all ports to ensure every sender detects the collision.
- **Management Agent:** Optional SNMP agent allows network administrators to monitor port status, errors, and auto-negotiation results.

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** When a KTU question shows a diagram of a Fast Ethernet hub, the most-graded elements are (i) per-port PHY, (ii) shared collision-domain logic, (iii) JAM generation, and (iv) auto-negotiation. Draw all four.

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Full-Duplex Gigabit Ethernet (The Deployed Reality)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DevA["Server 1 1000BASE T"] --> Switch["Gigabit Ethernet Switch Backplane 10 to 40 Gbps"]
    DevB["Server 2 1000BASE SX Fiber"] --> Switch
    DevC["Workstation 1000BASE T"] --> Switch
    Router["Core Router 10GBASE LR Fiber"] --> Switch
    
    Switch --> UplinkA["10GBASE SR Uplink to Aggregation"]
    Switch --> UplinkB["10GBASE LR Uplink to Building Core"]
    
    NoCSMA["No CSMA CD No Collisions Full Duplex Dedicated Bandwidth"]
    Switch -.Property.-> NoCSMA
```

**Key Message:** In the modern deployed reality of Gigabit (and faster) Ethernet, **CSMA/CD is disabled**. The switch provides dedicated point-to-point full-duplex links, eliminating collisions and the slot-time constraint. The fiber uplinks aggregate to 10 Gigabit trunks toward the campus or data-center core.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Question Bank Design Rationale:** The following questions follow the exact KTU 2024 Scheme pattern: Part A (3-mark short answers, no choice) and Part B (14-mark long answers with internal choice of either Question A or Question B, each containing two 7-mark sub-parts). Mark distributions, sub-part structure, and answer depth are calibrated to actual KTU End-Semester Evaluation (ESE) key patterns.

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Paper KTU 2024 Scheme]`
Define a **High-Speed LAN**. List any **three driving forces** that led to its emergence in the 1990s.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **High-Speed LAN** is a local area network technology operating at data rates of **100 Mbps or higher**, designed to overcome the bandwidth, latency, and scalability limitations of classical 10 Mbps Ethernet. (1 mark)

The three driving forces are:

1. **Demand for higher bandwidth** from client–server applications, graphical user interfaces, and multimedia traffic, which overwhelmed the 10 Mbps shared medium. (1 mark)
2. **Advances in CPU and disk technology** that could generate and consume data far faster than 10 Mbps Ethernet could carry. (0.5 mark)
3. **Rapid growth of intranet and Internet traffic** in the early 1990s, requiring aggregation links with much greater throughput. (0.5 mark)

*(Total: 3 marks)*

---

**Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`
Distinguish between **Fast Ethernet** and **100VG-AnyLAN** in terms of **MAC protocol, IEEE standard, and current commercial status**.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Fast Ethernet | 100VG-AnyLAN |
|---|---|---|
| **IEEE Standard** | IEEE 802.3u (1 mark) | IEEE 802.12 (1 mark) |
| **MAC Protocol** | Preserves CSMA/CD from classical Ethernet | Uses Demand Priority access — a hub-controlled, collision-free round-robin (0.5 mark) |
| **Commercial Status** | Survived; widely deployed in the late 1990s and early 2000s | Abandoned by ~2001; only a niche installed base (0.5 mark) |

*(Total: 3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each 14-mark question contains two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – July 2023, Modified]`

**(a) Explain in detail the **three approaches** that the IEEE pursued to evolve classical Ethernet into High-Speed LANs. Discuss why one of them failed commercially. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The IEEE 802 working group recognized that classical 10 Mbps Ethernet was saturating under modern application loads. Rather than design a single new system, they chartered **three parallel approaches**, each with a different philosophy about what should be preserved and what should be reinvented.

**1. Fast Ethernet (IEEE 802.3u) — "Preserve the MAC, Upgrade the PHY" (2 marks)**

The guiding principle was: *the installed base of CSMA/CD MAC software, the existing Ethernet frame format, and the existing 802.3 management infrastructure were too valuable to discard.* Therefore, only the physical layer was redesigned.

- The bit time was reduced from 100 ns to 10 ns (a 10× speedup).
- The minimum frame size (64 bytes) and maximum frame size (1518 bytes) were kept identical, so all upper-layer software and bridging/routing logic worked without modification.
- The slot time (51.2 μs → 5.12 μs) and maximum network diameter (~2.5 km → ~250 m) shrank proportionally, which was acceptable because most LAN segments were already far shorter than 2.5 km.
- 100BASE-TX used Cat-5 UTP with MLT-3 signaling and 4B/5B block coding; 100BASE-FX used multimode fiber.
- Auto-Negotiation (Clause 28) allowed a Fast Ethernet NIC to fall back to 10 Mbps on a Cat-3 cable, ensuring backward compatibility.

**2. 100VG-AnyLAN (IEEE 802.12) — "Replace the MAC, Use Voice-Grade Wiring" (2 marks)**

Hewlett-Packard championed a different idea: many office buildings had vast stocks of **Cat-3 voice-grade UTP** (not good enough for 100BASE-TX's MLT-3 signaling). The 100VG-AnyLAN approach used all 4 pairs of Cat-3 in parallel and replaced CSMA/CD with a new MAC called **Demand Priority**.

- A central intelligent hub polled each port in round-robin order.
- Each station sent a request tagged with one of two priority levels (Normal / High).
- The hub granted the next free slot to the highest-priority waiting station, guaranteeing bounded latency and eliminating collisions.
- The frame format could carry either Ethernet or Token Ring frames (hence "AnyLAN"), enabling smooth migration from either legacy.

**3. Gigabit Ethernet (IEEE 802.3z / 802.3ab) — "Scale the Same Philosophy to 1 Gbps" (2 marks)**

The third approach extended the Fast Ethernet philosophy to a 100× speedup over classical Ethernet.

- For fiber (802.3z): 1000BASE-SX used 850 nm VCSEL lasers on multimode fiber (up to 550 m); 1000BASE-LX used 1310 nm lasers on single-mode fiber (up to 5 km).
- For copper (802.3ab): 1000BASE-T used all 4 pairs of Cat-5e in parallel, with **PAM-5 (5-level pulse amplitude modulation)** and a 4-dimensional trellis code, achieving full-duplex 1 Gbps over 100 m of UTP.
- Half-duplex mode used **Carrier Extension** (padding short frames to 512 bytes) and **Frame Bursting** (concatenating multiple frames in one slot) to make CSMA/CD viable.
- In practice, half-duplex Gigabit is essentially never deployed — full-duplex switched operation is the universal norm.

**Why 100VG-AnyLAN Failed (1 mark)**

100VG-AnyLAN failed commercially for three reasons:
- **Incompatible with the massive CSMA/CD Ethernet installed base** — bridging/routing/switching chips and software were already CSMA/CD-aware.
- **Required a hub-centric topology** — it could not work in a switched full-duplex configuration as easily as Ethernet could.
- **Late silicon support and missing economies of scale** — by the time reliable 100VG-AnyLAN ASICs reached the market, Fast Ethernet chips were already a commodity. Network managers preferred the migration-friendly Fast Ethernet path.

*(Total: 7 marks)*

---

**(b) With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of a Fast Ethernet 100BASE-TX repeater hub**, highlighting auto-negotiation, collision detection, and the role of the JAM signal. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**

```
                                  ┌──────────────────────────────────┐
   Station A  ──[MLT-3 Signal]──► │  Port 1 PHY: MLT-3 RX + Clock   │
                                  │  Recovery + Auto-Negotiate       │
                                  └────────────┬─────────────────────┘
                                               │ NRZ frames
   Station B  ──[MLT-3 Signal]──► Port 2 PHY  │
                                               ▼
   Station C  ──[Cat-3 Legacy]──► Port 3 PHY  ┌──────────────────────────┐
                                  (10BASE-T)  │  Hub Core Logic          │
                                  Auto-Neg    │  • Repeater rules        │
                                              │  • Collision detection   │
                                              │  • Slot-time enforcement │
                                              └────────────┬─────────────┘
                                                           │
                                  ┌────────────────────────┼──────────────┐
                                  ▼                        ▼              ▼
                              Port 1 TX                Port 2 TX       Port 3 TX
                                  │                        │              │
   Station A  ◄──[MLT-3]─────┘   ◄──[MLT-3]────────┘   ◄──[Auto-rate]──┘
```

**Explanation of Each Block (4 marks):**

1. **Per-Port PHY Transceivers (1 mark):** Each port contains an independent physical-layer device. For 100BASE-TX, it uses 4B/5B block coding followed by MLT-3 line coding. The PHY also contains an auto-negotiation function (Clause 28) that advertises the port's capabilities (10 or 100 Mbps, half or full duplex) and selects the highest common mode with the link partner.

2. **Hub Core Logic (1 mark):** The core implements the IEEE 802.3u repeater state machine. It detects carrier activity on each port, regenerates and re-times incoming bits, and broadcasts them to all other ports. It also enforces the 5.12 μs slot-time budget for collision detection across the entire collision domain.

3. **Collision Detection and JAM Signal Generation (1 mark):** If two or more stations transmit simultaneously, the hub detects the contention (either by sensing simultaneous receive activity on multiple ports or by physical-layer collision detection in the PHY). The hub immediately generates a **32-bit JAM signal** and broadcasts it to all ports. This JAM ensures that every transmitting station experiences a voltage/light-level anomaly and interprets it as a collision within the slot-time window, triggering the CSMA/CD back-off algorithm.

4. **Auto-Negotiation (1 mark):** When a station is connected (e.g., Station C is a 10BASE-T legacy device), the hub's port and the station's PHY exchange Fast Link Pulses (FLP bursts) to determine the highest mutually supported speed and duplex mode. The hub then configures the port's internal data path accordingly — for example, switching between Manchester decoding (10 Mbps) and 4B/5B + MLT-3 decoding (100 Mbps). This makes the hub a true migration platform.

*(Total: 7 marks)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper KTU 2024 Scheme]`

**(a) With neat diagrams, explain **Gigabit Ethernet** and its various **physical-layer media options** (1000BASE-SX, 1000BASE-LX, 1000BASE-CX, 1000BASE-T). Compare their maximum segment lengths and typical deployment scenarios. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Overview of Gigabit Ethernet (2 marks)**

Gigabit Ethernet is the IEEE 802.3 standard that extended the Ethernet speed to **1 Gbps (1000 Mbps)**. It was standardized in two parallel efforts:
- **IEEE 802.3z (1998)** — for fiber and shielded copper (1000BASE-SX, 1000BASE-LX, 1000BASE-CX).
- **IEEE 802.3ab (1999)** — for Cat-5e/Cat-6 unshielded twisted pair (1000BASE-T).

Gigabit Ethernet preserved the **802.3 MAC frame format** exactly (so it inter-operates with 10/100 Mbps Ethernet at the bridge/switch level) and offered both **half-duplex (shared) and full-duplex (switched) modes**. In practice, almost all Gigabit Ethernet is full-duplex switched, because half-duplex CSMA/CD at 1 Gbps over any meaningful distance is mathematically impractical (max diameter ~25–50 m).

**2. Physical-Layer Media Options (4 marks)**

| Variant | Medium | Encoding | Max Segment | Typical Deployment |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1000BASE-SX** | Multimode fiber (62.5/125 µm or 50/125 µm) | 8B/10B + 850 nm VCSEL laser | 220–550 m (depends on fiber grade) | Building backbone, short server-room links |
| **1000BASE-LX** | Single-mode or multimode fiber | 8B/10B + 1310 nm laser | 550 m (MMF) / 5 km (SMF) | Campus backbone, longer building-to-building runs |
| **1000BASE-CX** | Twinaxial copper (twinax) | 8B/10B | 25 m | Data-center rack-to-rack interconnects (rare today) |
| **1000BASE-T** | 4 pairs of Cat-5e/Cat-6 UTP | **PAM-5 (4D-PAM5 trellis)** over all 4 pairs simultaneously, full-duplex | **100 m** | The dominant Gigabit Ethernet variant — desktops, servers, wiring-closet switches |

**3. Detailed Explanation of 1000BASE-T (1 mark)**

1000BASE-T is the most widely deployed Gigabit Ethernet variant. It achieves 1 Gbps full-duplex over 100 m of Cat-5e (or better) UTP by:
- Using **all 4 wire pairs simultaneously** in both directions (echo cancellation required).
- Encoding **2 bits per symbol** using **PAM-5** (5 voltage levels), giving 4 bits per symbol × 4 pairs = 16 bits per UI.
- Running the symbol clock at **125 MBaud** to deliver 125 M × 16 = 2 Gbps of raw throughput, of which 1 Gbps is payload and the rest is overhead (coding, framing, FEC).
- Employing a **4-dimensional Trellis Coded Modulation (TCM)** scheme to achieve a coding gain of ~6 dB, ensuring the BER stays below $10^{-10}$.

**4. Summary Deployment Table (already in 2 above)** — see media-options table.

*(Total: 7 marks)*

---

**(b) Explain the **MAC sublayer changes** required to upgrade from 10 Mbps Ethernet to 100 Mbps Fast Ethernet. Justify why these changes were necessary. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. The Fundamental Invariance (1 mark)**

Remarkably, **no changes were required to the MAC sublayer protocol itself**. The CSMA/CD algorithm, the back-off procedure (Binary Exponential Back-off), the inter-frame gap, the minimum and maximum frame sizes (64 / 1518 bytes), and the frame format all remained **byte-for-byte identical** to classical 10 Mbps Ethernet. This was the **central design decision** that made Fast Ethernet's market adoption so rapid.

**2. However, Several Quantities Derived from the PHY Had to Be Rescaled (3 marks)**

While the MAC protocol did not change, the **timing parameters it depends on** were rescaled to match the 10× faster bit rate:

| Parameter | 10 Mbps | 100 Mbps | Reason for Rescaling |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Bit Time** | 100 ns | 10 ns | Bit time scales inversely with rate |
| **Slot Time** | 51.2 μs (512 bit times) | 5.12 μs (512 bit times) | Maintains same minimum frame size of 64 bytes |
| **Inter-Frame Gap (IFG)** | 9.6 μs (96 bit times) | 0.96 μs (96 bit times) | Preserves same 96-bit "wire quiet" period |
| **Collision Jam Duration** | 32 bit times = 3.2 μs | 32 bit times = 0.32 μs | Same 32-bit JAM, but faster bit clock |
| **Attempt Limit** | 16 | 16 | Unchanged (algorithm-level) |
| **Back-off Limit** | 10 | 10 | Unchanged |

**3. Physical-Layer Implications (2 marks)**

Because slot time shrank 10×, the maximum network diameter for half-duplex CSMA/CD shrank 10× as well. This mandated:
- Reduction in repeater latency budgets (Class I and Class II repeaters were defined with strict maximum internal latencies of 140 BT and 46 BT respectively).
- Tighter cable specifications (Cat-5 became mandatory for 100BASE-TX, replacing the Cat-3 acceptable for 10BASE-T).
- Migration to **switched full-duplex operation** for backbone and server links, where CSMA/CD is disabled and distance limits are determined purely by the physical medium.

**4. Auto-Negotiation (1 mark)**

A new feature, **Auto-Negotiation (Clause 28)**, was added at the PHY layer. It allows a port to advertise its speed/duplex capabilities to the link partner using Fast Link Pulses and to select the highest common mode. This was essential because the 10× difference in bit time made a handshaking mismatch (e.g., one end at 10 Mbps, the other at 100 Mbps) silently destructive — so the standard required an explicit negotiation.

**Conclusion (built into answer):**

The genius of Fast Ethernet was that it required **zero changes to the MAC protocol**, only a **rescaling of MAC timing parameters** and a **redesign of the PHY**. This made it possible to swap 10 Mbps NICs and hubs for 100 Mbps units without touching any higher-layer software — a 1-day migration in most enterprises.

*(Total: 7 marks)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks on This Topic — Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
>
> 1. **Confusing the slot time with the frame transmission time.** Slot time (51.2 μs / 5.12 μs) is a fixed 512-bit-times safety window; it is *not* the time to transmit a 1518-byte frame. Many students write $T_{slot} = 12144 / R$, which is wrong.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in the diameter formula.** The round-trip constraint is $2 \times T_{prop} \le T_{slot}$, so the maximum *one-way* diameter is $v \cdot T_{slot} / 2$. Students often forget the 2 and overstate the diameter.
> 3. **Mixing up IEEE numbers.** 802.3u is Fast Ethernet, 802.12 is 100VG-AnyLAN, 802.3z is Gigabit (fiber), 802.3ab is Gigabit (copper). Confusing these is a guaranteed 0.5–1 mark loss.
> 4. **Claiming 100VG-AnyLAN survives.** It was abandoned in 2001. If you write "100VG-AnyLAN is widely used today" in a comparison answer, you lose 1 mark.
> 5. **Skipping the diagram.** A 7-mark sub-question that asks "with neat diagram" without a diagram loses 2–3 marks immediately. Always draw a labelled block diagram.
> 6. **Using vertical pipes in tables** during the exam answer script (only relevant if you type the exam — for handwritten scripts, draw clean bordered tables). Tables that are misaligned confuse the examiner.
> 7. **Writing efficiency values without the formula.** Always show $E = 1/(1 + 6.44a)$ and the $a$ calculation. Stating just the final number is a partial-mark answer.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this section. Read it 10 minutes before entering the exam hall.**

- [ ] **Definition:** High-Speed LAN = LAN operating at $\ge$ 100 Mbps.
- [ ] **Three Approaches:** Fast Ethernet (802.3u) | 100VG-AnyLAN (802.12) | Gigabit Ethernet (802.3z / 802.3ab).
- [ ] **Driving Forces:** CPU speed, GUIs, client–server, intranet, multimedia.
- [ ] **Preservation Principle:** Frame format, MAC protocol, and management are unchanged from 10 Mbps Ethernet.
- [ ] **Bit Time Formula:** $T_{bit} = 1/R$. For 10/100/1000 Mbps: **100 ns / 10 ns / 1 ns**.
- [ ] **Slot Time Formula:** $T_{slot} = 512 \times T_{bit}$. Values: **51.2 μs / 5.12 μs / 0.512 μs**.
- [ ] **Diameter Formula:** $D_{max} \le v \cdot T_{slot} / 2 \approx 5.12 \text{ km} / 512 \text{ m} / 51.2 \text{ m}$.
- [ ] **Efficiency Formula:** $E = 1 / (1 + 6.44 a)$ where $a = T_{prop} / T_{trans}$.
- [ ] **Fast Ethernet Variants:** 100BASE-TX (Cat-5 UTP, MLT-3), 100BASE-FX (fiber), 100BASE-T4 (Cat-3, obsolete).
- [ ] **Gigabit Ethernet Variants:** 1000BASE-SX (MMF, 850 nm), 1000BASE-LX (SMF/MMF, 1310 nm), 1000BASE-CX (twinax, 25 m), 1000BASE-T (Cat-5e, PAM-5, 100 m).
- [ ] **100VG-AnyLAN:** IEEE 802.12, Demand Priority, Cat-3, **defunct**.
- [ ] **Auto-Negotiation:** IEEE 802.3u Clause 28; uses Fast Link Pulses; selects highest common speed/duplex.
- [ ] **Carrier Extension & Frame Bursting:** Two mechanisms that keep CSMA/CD viable in half-duplex Gigabit Ethernet.
- [ ] **Full-Duplex Reality:** All modern Gigabit and faster Ethernet is full-duplex switched → **no CSMA/CD, no collisions, no diameter constraint**.
- [ ] **Numerical Backbone:** Remember the magic numbers **512, 5.12 μs, 51.2 μs, 250 m, 100 m, 5 km** — they appear in nearly every KTU numerical question on this topic.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ethernet
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCERT602 - DATA COMMUNICATION AND NETWORKING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Ethernet

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔷 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ethernet** is a family of **wired Local Area Network (LAN)** computer networking technologies defined by the **IEEE 802.2 and IEEE 802.3 standards**. It specifies the **Physical Layer (PHY)** and the **Media Access Control (MAC)** sublayer of the **Data Link Layer (DLL)** of the **OSI reference model**, employing the **Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection (CSMA/CD)** protocol as its foundational channel-access mechanism.

From a strictly KTU 2024 Scheme perspective, Ethernet is the dominant LAN technology that has evolved from a **10 Mbps shared coaxial medium** to today's **10 Gbps, 40 Gbps, 100 Gbps, and 400 Gbps** switched full-duplex fiber/copper architectures, while **retaining the same 802.3 frame structure** for backward compatibility.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Multi-Tap Highway" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a single-lane, one-way highway where multiple cars enter from different on-ramps and need to reach different off-ramps without colliding.**

- **The Highway** = the shared Ethernet cable (the *medium*)
- **The Cars** = the **frames** (data packets)
- **The Drivers looking back & ahead** = **CSMA/CD** (drivers sense the road, "Carrier Sense"; if two cars bump, "Collision Detection", and both reverse, "Backoff")
- **The Traffic Police (modern)** = the **Ethernet Switch** (which gives each car its own private lane — *full-duplex switched Ethernet*)
- **The Highway Signboards** = the **IEEE 802.3 Standard** (rules of the road)

In the **early days (1980s)**, this highway was a *single shared road* (coaxial bus) and many cars crashed into each other (collisions). In **modern Ethernet**, each car gets its **own private road** (a switch port) — collisions effectively become **mathematically impossible**.

## 1.3 Key Standardization Bodies & Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ethernet is governed by:**
> - **IEEE 802.3 Working Group** (the primary standard)
> - **IEEE 802.2** (Logical Link Control — LLC)
> - **ITU-T** and **ISO/IEC 8802-3** (international adoptions)

**Core Standard Metrics (Must be memorized for KTU ESE):**
- **Minimum Frame Size:** **64 bytes** (512 bits)
- **Maximum Frame Size:** **1518 bytes** (1522 bytes with VLAN tag)
- **Slot Time:** **51.2 μs** (for classic 10 Mbps Ethernet)
- **Maximum Cable Segment Length (10BASE5):** **500 m**
- **Maximum Cable Segment Length (10BASE2):** **185 m**
- **Maximum Cable Segment Length (10BASE-T UTP):** **100 m**
- **Maximum Number of Repeaters (10 Mbps):** **4 repeaters** forming the **5-4-3 rule**

## 1.4 Classification of Ethernet by PHY

Ethernet is broadly classified by:
1. **Speed:** 10 Mbps, 100 Mbps, 1 Gbps, 10 Gbps, 25/40/100/400 Gbps
2. **Medium:** Coaxial, Twisted Pair (UTP/STP), Fiber Optic (SMF/MMF)
3. **Topology:** Bus (legacy), Star (modern), Tree, Hybrid
4. **Mode:** Half-duplex (with CSMA/CD) vs. Full-duplex (no CSMA/CD)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔷 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The IEEE 802.3 Standard Family — Naming Convention

> [!NOTE]
> **IEEE 802.3 Naming Format:** `<Speed><Signaling><Segment>`
> Example: **10BASE-T** → **10** Mbps, **BASE**band signaling, **T**wisted pair

| Code Letter | Physical Medium |
|:-----------:|:----------------|
| **2** | Thin coaxial (10BASE2, 185 m) |
| **5** | Thick coaxial (10BASE5, 500 m) |
| **T** | Twisted Pair (UTP/STP) |
| **F** | Fiber Optic |
| **S** | Short-wave laser (multi-mode fiber) |
| **L** | Long-wave laser (single-mode fiber) |
| **E** | Extra long-wave (very long fiber) |
| **C** | Copper (twinax for 10G+) |
| **K** | Backplane (e.g., 1000BASE-KX) |

## 2.2 The Ethernet Generations — Historical & Technical

| Generation | Standard | Speed | Medium | Topology | Mode |
|:----------:|:--------:|:-----:|:------:|:--------:|:----:|
| **Classic Ethernet** | 10BASE5 | 10 Mbps | Thick Coax | Bus | Half |
| **Thin Ethernet** | 10BASE2 | 10 Mbps | Thin Coax | Bus | Half |
| **Star Ethernet** | 10BASE-T | 10 Mbps | UTP Cat-3+ | Star | Half/Full |
| **Fast Ethernet** | 100BASE-TX | 100 Mbps | UTP Cat-5 | Star | Full |
| **Fast Ethernet (Fiber)** | 100BASE-FX | 100 Mbps | MMF | Star | Full |
| **Gigabit Ethernet** | 1000BASE-T | 1 Gbps | UTP Cat-5e/6 | Star | Full |
| **Gigabit (Fiber)** | 1000BASE-LX | 1 Gbps | SMF/MMF | Star | Full |
| **10 Gigabit** | 10GBASE-T | 10 Gbps | UTP Cat-6a/7 | Star | Full only |
| **40/100 Gigabit** | 40/100GBASE-SR4 | 40/100 Gbps | MMF | Star | Full only |
| **400 Gigabit** | 400GBASE-DR4 | 400 Gbps | SMF | Star | Full only |

## 2.3 CSMA/CD — The Heart of Classic Ethernet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CSMA/CD = Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection**
> This is the MAC protocol that allows multiple stations to share a single half-duplex Ethernet medium.

### Step-by-Step Operational Logic:

1. **Carrier Sense (CS):** A station wanting to transmit **first listens** to the medium to determine if it is **idle or busy**.
2. **Multiple Access (MA):** All stations have **equal access** rights to the shared medium (no central controller in classic bus topology).
3. **If idle → Transmit:** If the medium is sensed idle for **96 bit-times (Inter-Frame Gap = 9.6 μs at 10 Mbps)**, the station begins transmission.
4. **Collision Detection (CD):** While transmitting, the station **continuously monitors** for signal voltage exceeding the normal range (i.e., a collision).
5. **If collision detected:**
   - **Jam Signal:** The station transmits a **32-bit jam sequence** to ensure all other stations detect the collision.
   - **Stop Transmission:** All stations cease transmitting.
   - **Binary Exponential Backoff (BEB):** Each station waits a random number of slot times:
     - Wait time = $r \times 51.2\ \mu s$, where $r$ is a random integer in $[0, 2^k - 1]$
     - $k$ = min(number of collisions, 10)
     - After **16 consecutive collisions**, the frame is declared **dead** and dropped.
6. **Retransmission:** After the backoff period, the station attempts retransmission.

## 2.4 The 5-4-3 Rule (Repeater Topology Limit)

> [!NOTE]
> In a **10 Mbps shared Ethernet** using repeaters, the maximum collision domain allows:
> - **5** segments of coaxial cable
> - **4** repeaters
> - **3** of the 5 segments may be **populated** (have stations attached); the other 2 are just inter-repeater links.

This rule ensures the **round-trip propagation delay** never exceeds the **slot time of 51.2 μs**, guaranteeing reliable collision detection.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER THESE EQUATIONS — they appear almost every KTU ESE in this module.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|:-:|:--------|:------------|:------|
| 1 | $T_{\text{slot}} = 51.2\ \mu s$ | Slot time for 10 Mbps Ethernet | μs |
| 2 | $T_{\text{slot}} = 5.12\ \mu s$ | Slot time for 100 Mbps Ethernet | μs |
| 3 | $T_{\text{slot}} = 0.512\ \mu s$ | Slot time for 1 Gbps Ethernet | μs |
| 4 | $T_{\text{slot}} = 51.2\ \mu s$ | Slot time for 10 Gbps Ethernet (backward compat) | μs |
| 5 | $L_{\min} = \text{Rate} \times T_{\text{slot}}$ | Minimum frame length in bits | bits |
| 6 | $L_{\min} = 512\ \text{bits} = 64\ \text{bytes}$ | Min frame for 10/100 Mbps | bytes |
| 7 | $L_{\min} = 512\ \text{bits} = 64\ \text{bytes}$ | Min frame for 1 Gbps (with carrier extension) | bytes |
| 8 | $a = \dfrac{T_p}{T_t}$ | Ratio of propagation time to transmission time | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\eta = \dfrac{1}{1 + 6.44 \cdot a}$ | Efficiency of pure ALOHA-based CSMA/CD | ratio (0–1) |
| 10 | $\eta_{\text{peak}} = \dfrac{1}{1 + 6.44 \cdot a}$ | Maximum CSMA/CD throughput (Bux's model) | ratio |
| 11 | $T_{\text{IFG}} = 96\ \text{bit-times}$ | Inter-Frame Gap | bits |
| 12 | $T_{\text{jam}} = 32\ \text{bit-times}$ | Jam signal duration | bits |
| 13 | $r = \text{rand}[0, 2^k - 1]$ | BEB random backoff slots, $k = \min(n, 10)$ | integer |
| 14 | $L_{\max} = 2500\ \text{m}$ | Max 10BASE5 collision domain | m |
| 15 | $L_{\max} = 100\ \text{m}$ | Max UTP segment (10/100/1000BASE-T) | m |

> **Note:** The symbol `a` (sometimes `a = T_p / T_f` where $T_f$ is the frame transmission time) governs the **efficiency of CSMA/CD**. As `a → 0`, efficiency approaches **100%**; as `a → ∞`, efficiency → 0.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Ethernet is **not just a textbook topic** — it is the **nervous system of modern computing**:

- **Data Centers:** 100/400 Gbps Ethernet interconnects every server rack.
- **Industrial Automation:** Time-Sensitive Networking (TSN) variants of Ethernet synchronize robotic assembly lines.
- **Automotive:** 100BASE-T1 / 1000BASE-T1 (Automotive Ethernet) connect ECUs in modern cars.
- **Telecommunications:** 5G fronthaul/midhaul uses **eCPRI over Ethernet**.
- **Storage:** **iSCSI**, **Fibre Channel over Ethernet (FCoE)**, and **NVMe-oF** all ride on Ethernet.
- **Audio/Video:** **AVB (Audio Video Bridging)** uses Ethernet for synchronized professional A/V.

# 🔷 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation #1: Minimum Frame Size from Slot Time Constraint

> **KTU Board Standard Question:** *"Why is the minimum Ethernet frame size 64 bytes at 10 Mbps?"*

### Given Data:
- Network speed: $R = 10\ \text{Mbps} = 10 \times 10^6\ \text{bits/sec}$
- Maximum round-trip propagation delay (5-4-3 network): $T_{p,\text{RT}} = 51.2\ \mu s$
- Slot time $T_{\text{slot}}$ is defined to equal this maximum round-trip delay.

### Step 1: Define the Slot Time
The **slot time** in Ethernet is defined as the time it takes for a signal to travel the round-trip maximum distance and be detected.

$$T_{\text{slot}} = T_{p,\text{RT}} = 51.2\ \mu s$$

### Step 2: Compute the Minimum Bits That Must Be Transmitted
For a collision to be *detectable by the sender*, the sender must still be transmitting when the collision signal propagates back. Therefore, the **frame transmission time $T_t$ must be $\geq T_{p,\text{RT}}$**:

$$T_t \geq T_{\text{slot}} = 51.2\ \mu s$$

### Step 3: Convert Time to Bits Using the Bit-Rate
The number of bits transmitted in 51.2 μs at 10 Mbps is:

$$L_{\min} = R \times T_{\text{slot}}$$

$$L_{\min} = (10 \times 10^6\ \text{bits/s}) \times (51.2 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{s})$$

$$L_{\min} = 10 \times 10^6 \times 51.2 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{bits}$$

$$L_{\min} = 512\ \text{bits}$$

### Step 4: Convert to Bytes

$$L_{\min} = \frac{512\ \text{bits}}{8\ \text{bits/byte}} = 64\ \text{bytes}$$

> ✅ **Result:** **Minimum Ethernet frame size = 64 bytes (512 bits) at 10 Mbps.**

### Step 5: Verify for 100 Mbps
At 100 Mbps, the bit time shrinks by 10×, so the slot time is correspondingly:

$$T_{\text{slot,100}} = \frac{512\ \text{bits}}{100 \times 10^6\ \text{bits/s}} = 5.12\ \mu s$$

Minimum frame size remains 64 bytes (512 bits) to maintain collision-domain compatibility.

---

## 3.2 Derivation #2: CSMA/CD Efficiency (Bux's Model)

> **KTU Board Standard Question:** *"Derive the efficiency of CSMA/CD."*

### Given Data:
- $T_p$ = one-way propagation time (worst case)
- $T_t$ = frame transmission time
- $a = \dfrac{T_p}{T_t}$

### Step 1: Frame Transmission Time

$$T_t = \frac{L}{R}$$

where $L$ = frame length in bits, $R$ = bit rate.

### Step 2: Define the Parameter `a`

$$a = \frac{T_p}{T_t} = \frac{T_p \cdot R}{L}$$

### Step 3: Channel Busy Time (Kleinrock's Model)
A successful transmission occupies the channel for:
- Transmission time $T_t$
- Propagation time $T_p$
- Detection confirmation time (≈ propagation)

Total contention period ≈ $2 \cdot T_p$ (round-trip).

### Step 4: Apply Bux's Throughput Formula
For **1-persistent CSMA/CD** in heavy load:

$$\eta = \frac{T_t}{T_t + T_p + T_{\text{collision overhead}} + T_{\text{idle}}}$$

Bux's closed-form approximation:

$$\eta = \frac{1}{1 + 6.44 \cdot a}$$

### Step 5: Limiting Cases
- **If $a \to 0$** (frame very long, network very short): $\eta \to 1$ (100% efficiency)
- **If $a = 1$** (propagation time = transmission time): $\eta = \dfrac{1}{7.44} \approx 13.4\%$
- **If $a \to \infty$** (propagation dominates): $\eta \to 0$

### Step 6: Numerical Example (10BASE5, max 2500 m)
- $T_p = \dfrac{2500\ \text{m}}{2 \times 10^8\ \text{m/s}} = 12.5\ \mu s$
- $T_t = \dfrac{512\ \text{bits}}{10 \times 10^6} = 51.2\ \mu s$
- $a = \dfrac{12.5}{51.2} \approx 0.244$
- $\eta = \dfrac{1}{1 + 6.44 \times 0.244} = \dfrac{1}{1 + 1.571} = \dfrac{1}{2.571} \approx 38.9\%$

---

## 3.3 Derivation #3: Binary Exponential Backoff (BEB) — Maximum Wait

### Given Data:
- Maximum number of collisions before frame drop: $n = 16$
- Slot time at 10 Mbps: $T_{\text{slot}} = 51.2\ \mu s$

### Step 1: BEB Backoff Interval
After the $i$-th collision ($i = 1, 2, \ldots, 10$):

$$r = \text{random integer in } [0,\ 2^i - 1]$$

### Step 2: Wait Time

$$T_{\text{wait}} = r \times T_{\text{slot}}$$

For $i = 10$ (maximum before saturation):

$$r_{\max} = 2^{10} - 1 = 1023$$

### Step 3: Maximum Possible Wait at 10 Mbps

$$T_{\text{wait,max}} = 1023 \times 51.2\ \mu s = 52,377.6\ \mu s \approx 52.4\ \text{ms}$$

### Step 4: For $i > 10$, $r$ stays in $[0, 1023]$.

> **For $i = 16$ (attempt 17):** The frame is dropped and the station reports an error to the upper layer.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Ethernet Evolution & Backward Compatibility

| Parameter | 10BASE-T | 100BASE-TX | 1000BASE-T | 10GBASE-T |
|:----------|:--------:|:----------:|:----------:|:---------:|
| **Speed** | 10 Mbps | 100 Mbps | 1 Gbps | 10 Gbps |
| **Bit time** | 100 ns | 10 ns | 1 ns | 0.1 ns |
| **Slot time** | 51.2 μs | 5.12 μs | 0.512 μs (extended to 4.096 μs) | N/A (full-duplex only) |
| **Min frame** | 64 B | 64 B | 64 B (with carrier ext.) | 64 B |
| **CSMA/CD?** | Yes (half) | Yes (half) | Yes (half) | No (full only) |
| **Encoding** | Manchester | 4B/5B + MLT-3 | 8B/10B (PAM-5 for UTP) | 64B/66B + PAM-16 |
| **Cable** | Cat-3 UTP | Cat-5 UTP | Cat-5e/6 UTP | Cat-6a/7 UTP |
| **Max distance** | 100 m | 100 m | 100 m | 100 m (55 m for Cat-6) |
| **Repeaters allowed** | Yes | Yes | Yes (1 only) | No |

---

## 3.5 Special Note: Gigabit Ethernet & Carrier Extension

> [!IMPORTANT]
> At 1 Gbps, the slot time is 0.512 μs. But the minimum frame is still 64 bytes (512 bits), which takes only 0.512 μs to transmit — equal to the slot time! This means **CSMA/CD barely works**. To preserve the **2500 m collision domain**, Gigabit Ethernet introduces two mechanisms:

**Mechanism 1: Carrier Extension**
- After transmitting a frame shorter than 512 bytes, the station **appends non-data symbols** to extend the on-wire time to 512 byte-times.
- Receivers automatically strip the extension.

**Mechanism 2: Frame Bursting**
- A station that has just transmitted may send **up to 5 more short frames consecutively** without releasing the channel, effectively amortizing the overhead.
- This boosts efficiency for bursty traffic.

**10 Gigabit Ethernet and above** are **full-duplex only**; CSMA/CD is **abolished** because each link is point-to-point and collisions are physically impossible.

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — IEEE 802.3 Ethernet Frame Format (Exhaustive Bit-Level)

> [!NOTE]
> **The 802.3 frame is the most-tested diagram in KTU ESE. Memorize every field, its length in bytes, and its function.**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["PREAMBLE\n7 bytes\n10101010 x 7\nSync Pattern"] --> B["SFD\nStart Frame Delimiter\n1 byte\n10101011\nMarks Start of Frame"]
    B --> C["DESTINATION ADDRESS\n6 bytes\nMAC Address of Receiver\nUnicast / Multicast / Broadcast"]
    C --> D["SOURCE ADDRESS\n6 bytes\nMAC Address of Sender"]
    D --> E["LENGTH / TYPE\n2 bytes\nIf <= 1500: Length\nIf > 1536: EtherType IPX=802.3 IP=0x0800"]
    E --> F["DATA PAYLOAD\n46 to 1500 bytes\nMust be padded\nif less than 46 bytes"]
    F --> G["PAD\n0 to 46 bytes\nTo ensure minimum\nframe is 64 bytes"]
    G --> H["FCS\nFrame Check Sequence\n4 bytes\nCRC-32 Checksum\nfor error detection"]
```

> **Frame Size Summary (Critical for KTU):**
> - **Minimum Frame:** 64 bytes (excluding Preamble + SFD)
> - **Maximum Frame:** 1518 bytes (without VLAN); 1522 bytes (with 802.1Q VLAN tag)
> - **Preamble + SFD:** 8 bytes (not counted in 64-byte minimum)
> - **Header (DA + SA + Length):** 14 bytes
> - **FCS (Trailer):** 4 bytes
> - **Data + Pad:** 46 to 1500 bytes (Data is min 46, max 1500; Pad is min 0, max 46)

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — CSMA/CD Algorithm Flow (State Machine)

> [!NOTE]
> **This is a frequent 7-mark question: "Explain the CSMA/CD algorithm with a flowchart."**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Station has frame\nready to transmit"] --> Sense{"Sense Carrier\nIs medium IDLE?"}
    Sense -- "NO (Busy)" --> Wait1["Wait and keep\nsensing the medium"] --> Sense
    Sense -- "YES (Idle)" --> WaitIFG["Wait 96 bit-times\nInter-Frame Gap\n9.6 us at 10 Mbps"] --> Transmit["Begin transmitting\nthe frame"]
    Transmit --> MonColl{"Monitor for\ncollision during\ntransmission"}
    MonColl -- "No collision" --> Success["Frame transmitted\nSUCCESSFULLY"] --> End1["Done. Wait for\nnext frame"]
    MonColl -- "Collision detected" --> Jam["Transmit 32-bit\nJAM signal"]
    Jam --> IncCnt["Increment\ncollision counter n"]
    IncCnt --> CheckN{"n > 16?"}
    CheckN -- "YES" --> Drop["FRAME DROPPED\nReport error to\nupper layer LLC"] --> End2["Aborted"]
    CheckN -- "NO" --> CalcBack["Compute backoff\nr = rand in 0 to 2^k-1\nk = min of n and 10"]
    CalcBack --> WaitSlot["Wait r x 51.2 us\nslot times"] --> Transmit
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Classic Ethernet Bus vs. Modern Star Topology

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Bus["CLASSIC ETHERNET — Shared Bus (10BASE5/2)"]
        direction LR
        P1[PC 1] --- COAX1[Thick Coaxial Cable\nSingle Shared Medium]
        P2[PC 2] --- COAX1
        P3[PC 3] --- COAX1
        P4[PC 4] --- COAX1
        P5[PC 5] --- COAX1
        COAX1 --- Term1[Terminator 50 ohm]
        COAX1 --- Term2[Terminator 50 ohm]
    end

    subgraph Star["MODERN ETHERNET — Star Topology (10BASE-T onwards)"]
        direction LR
        Q1[PC 1] --- SW[ETHERNET SWITCH\nFull Duplex\nNo Collisions]
        Q2[PC 2] --- SW
        Q3[PC 3] --- SW
        Q4[PC 4] --- SW
        Q5[PC 5] --- SW
    end
```

> **Key Takeaway:** In the bus, all stations share the **same collision domain**. In the star, each link to the switch is its **own collision domain**, and with full-duplex operation, **CSMA/CD is not needed** (no collisions can occur on a point-to-point full-duplex link).

## 4.5 Diagram 5 — Gigabit Ethernet Backward Compatibility with 10/100 Mbps

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["1000BASE-T Switch Port\nAuto-Negotiation\nIEEE 802.3u/clause 28"] --> B{"Connect to partner\ndevice"}
    B --> C["If partner is 10BASE-T\n-> Operate at 10 Mbps\nHalf or Full Duplex"]
    B --> D["If partner is 100BASE-TX\n-> Operate at 100 Mbps\nHalf or Full Duplex"]
    B --> E["If partner is 1000BASE-T\n-> Operate at 1 Gbps\nFull Duplex preferred"]
    B --> F["No partner / faulty cable\n-> Link DOWN status\nLED off"]
```

> **Auto-Negotiation** is a critical feature in modern twisted-pair Ethernet that allows two ends of a link to **automatically agree** on the highest mutually-supported speed and duplex mode.

# 🔷 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

---

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model Question Paper]`
**Define CSMA/CD. How does it differ from pure ALOHA?**

**Model Answer (3 marks — award each clear point):**

**CSMA/CD (Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection)** is the Media Access Control protocol used in classical (half-duplex) Ethernet networks. It allows multiple stations to share a single communication medium by:

1. **Carrier Sensing:** A station *listens* to the medium before transmitting. It transmits *only* if the medium is sensed idle. (*Pure ALOHA does not sense the medium — it transmits blindly.*)
2. **Collision Detection:** While transmitting, the station *continuously monitors* for signal collisions (excessive voltage on the medium). If a collision is detected, transmission stops immediately and a **jam signal** is sent. (*Pure ALOHA has no collision detection — it waits for an ACK timeout.*)
3. **Binary Exponential Backoff:** After a collision, both stations wait a *random* number of slot times before retrying, with the random window doubling after each subsequent collision. (*Pure ALOHA retries after a fixed random delay.*)

| Feature | Pure ALOHA | CSMA/CD |
|:--------|:----------:|:-------:|
| Carrier Sense | No | Yes |
| Collision Detection | No | Yes |
| Max Efficiency | ~18.4% | Up to ~100% (as $a \to 0$) |
| Used in | Satellite / Radio | Wired Ethernet |

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Sessional II]`
**Why is the minimum Ethernet frame size 64 bytes?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The minimum frame size of **64 bytes (512 bits)** in 10 Mbps Ethernet is dictated by the **collision detection constraint**:

1. **Constraint:** A transmitting station must still be *on the air* when the collision signal propagates back to it from the farthest station, so that it can *detect* the collision. If the frame is too short, the station finishes transmission *before* the collision signal returns, and the collision goes undetected.

2. **Calculation:** The maximum round-trip propagation delay in a 10BASE5 network (5 segments, 4 repeaters, max 2500 m) is **51.2 μs**. At 10 Mbps, the number of bits transmitted in 51.2 μs is:

$$L_{\min} = 10\ \text{Mbps} \times 51.2\ \mu s = 512\ \text{bits} = 64\ \text{bytes}$$

3. **Padding:** If the actual data is less than 46 bytes, the sender must **pad** it to make the total frame 64 bytes (Header 14 + Data ≥ 46 + FCS 4 = 64).

---

## ✏️ QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set A]`

### Q. (a) Explain the IEEE 802.3 Ethernet frame format in detail. Mention the purpose of each field and the valid range of frame sizes. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

The IEEE 802.3 Ethernet frame is the fundamental data unit transmitted over an Ethernet LAN. It consists of the following fields in order:

| Field | Size (bytes) | Function |
|:------|:------------:|:---------|
| **Preamble** | 7 | Alternating pattern `10101010` for receiver clock synchronization. |
| **SFD** (Start Frame Delimiter) | 1 | Pattern `10101011` marking the *end* of preamble and *start* of frame. |
| **Destination Address (DA)** | 6 | MAC address of the intended receiver. May be unicast, multicast, or broadcast (FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF). |
| **Source Address (SA)** | 6 | MAC address of the sending station. Always unicast. |
| **Length / Type** | 2 | If value ≤ 1500: indicates length of payload (802.3 format). If value ≥ 1536: indicates EtherType (Ethernet II format), e.g., 0x0800 = IPv4. |
| **Data (Payload)** | 46–1500 | The actual data from the upper layer (LLC). Minimum 46 bytes is enforced. |
| **Pad** | 0–46 | Used if Data < 46 bytes to meet the 64-byte minimum. |
| **FCS** (Frame Check Sequence) | 4 | 32-bit CRC computed over DA, SA, Length, and Data. Used by the receiver to detect bit errors. |

**Frame Size Limits (Important for 7-mark answers):**
- **Minimum frame size = 64 bytes** (excluding Preamble + SFD). Ensures collision detection works.
- **Maximum frame size = 1518 bytes** (without VLAN tag); **1522 bytes** (with 4-byte IEEE 802.1Q VLAN tag).
- **Inter-Frame Gap (IFG) = 96 bit-times** (9.6 μs at 10 Mbps) is a *silent* period between consecutive frames.

**Diagram Reference:** (Insert the frame diagram from Section 4.1)

**Why these limits?**
- **64 bytes minimum** = ensures sender is still transmitting when collision signal returns from the farthest station.
- **1518 bytes maximum** = prevents one station from monopolizing the shared medium for too long; also bounds buffer sizes in switches and NICs.

---

### Q. (b) Discuss the evolution of Ethernet from 10BASE5 to 10 Gigabit Ethernet. Highlight the key improvements at each stage. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

Ethernet has undergone **5 major generations** of evolution, each driven by the need for higher speed, longer distances, easier installation, and better reliability.

**1. 10BASE5 (1985) — Thick Ethernet**
- Medium: Thick coaxial cable (10 mm diameter)
- Speed: 10 Mbps
- Topology: Bus
- Max segment: 500 m
- Connection: Vampire tap (drilled into cable)
- *Drawback:* Bulky, expensive, hard to install.

**2. 10BASE2 (1988) — Thin Ethernet / Cheapernet**
- Medium: Thin coaxial cable (RG-58)
- Speed: 10 Mbps
- Topology: Bus
- Max segment: 185 m
- Connection: BNC T-connector
- *Drawback:* Still a shared bus; one break brings down the whole segment.

**3. 10BASE-T (1990) — Twisted Pair Ethernet**
- Medium: UTP Cat-3 (2 pairs)
- Speed: 10 Mbps
- Topology: **Star** (centered on a hub or switch)
- Max segment: 100 m
- *Improvement:* Fault isolation per link, easy install, RJ-45 connector.

**4. 100BASE-TX (1995) — Fast Ethernet**
- Medium: UTP Cat-5 (2 pairs)
- Speed: **100 Mbps** (10× improvement)
- Topology: Star with switches
- *Improvement:* Auto-negotiation, full-duplex mode (eliminates CSMA/CD on point-to-point links).
- Standards: 100BASE-TX (copper), 100BASE-FX (fiber).

**5. 1000BASE-T (1999) — Gigabit Ethernet**
- Medium: UTP Cat-5e / Cat-6 (4 pairs, all used)
- Speed: **1 Gbps** (10× improvement)
- *New Features:* Carrier Extension, Frame Bursting, Flow Control (802.3x).
- Maintains 64-byte minimum frame via *carrier extension* for backward compatibility.

**6. 10GBASE-T (2006) — 10 Gigabit Ethernet**
- Medium: Cat-6a / Cat-7 UTP (up to 100 m)
- Speed: **10 Gbps**
- **Full-duplex only** — CSMA/CD abandoned because collisions are physically impossible on a point-to-point full-duplex link.
- Used in data center aggregation and high-end LAN backbones.

**Summary Table** (insert the evolution table from Section 2.2)

**Conclusion:** The key engineering drivers were: **higher bandwidth × 10 per generation**, transition from **shared bus to switched star**, and the **abandonment of CSMA/CD** in favor of full-duplex switched operation.

---

## ✏️ QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set B]`

### Q. (a) Explain the CSMA/CD protocol with the Binary Exponential Backoff algorithm. What happens after 16 collisions? (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**1. CSMA/CD Phases:**

**Phase A — Carrier Sense:**
- The station monitors the medium (e.g., by checking the voltage level on the cable).
- If the medium is **busy** (carrier present), the station defers transmission and continues to sense.
- If the medium is **idle for 96 bit-times (IFG)**, the station proceeds to transmit.

**Phase B — Collision Detection:**
- During transmission, the station *simultaneously reads back* the signal on the medium.
- A collision is detected if the *read-back* signal differs from the *transmitted* signal (i.e., the voltage exceeds the normal 0–2 V range for 10BASE-T).
- Upon collision, the station **stops transmitting** and sends a **32-bit jam signal** to ensure all other stations involved in the collision also detect it.

**Phase C — Binary Exponential Backoff (BEB):**

After transmitting the jam signal, each station that was involved in the collision computes a random backoff:

- Let $n$ = number of collisions experienced by *this* frame.
- Compute $k = \min(n, 10)$.
- Pick a random integer $r$ uniformly in the range $[0,\ 2^k - 1]$.
- Wait $T_{\text{wait}} = r \times T_{\text{slot}}$ before attempting retransmission.

**Examples of BEB windows:**

| Collision # ($n$) | $k$ | Random range | Max wait at 10 Mbps |
