|:------------------:|:---:|:------------:|:--------------------:|
| 1 | 1 | 0 or 1 | 51.2 μs |
| 2 | 2 | 0 to 3 | 153.6 μs |
| 3 | 3 | 0 to 7 | 358.4 μs |
| 10 | 10 | 0 to 1023 | 52,377.6 μs |
| 11 to 16 | 10 | 0 to 1023 | 52,377.6 μs (frozen) |

**2. What happens after 16 collisions?**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If a frame experiences **16 consecutive collisions** (i.e., $n > 16$), the station **abandons the frame** and reports an error to the **Logical Link Control (LLC)** sublayer. The frame is *dropped*. The higher layer (e.g., TCP) is then responsible for retransmission.

The rationale: 16 consecutive collisions indicate persistent network congestion or a physical fault (e.g., a broken cable, a faulty NIC, or a duplex mismatch). Continuing to retry would only worsen the problem.

**3. Flowchart Reference:** (Insert the CSMA/CD flow diagram from Section 4.2)

---

### Q. (b) Explain Switched Ethernet and Full-Duplex Ethernet. Why is CSMA/CD not needed in full-duplex mode? (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**1. Switched Ethernet:**

In a switched Ethernet network, the central device is an **Ethernet switch** (a multiport bridge) instead of a **hub**.

- **Hub (legacy):** A hub is a *physical-layer* repeater. It broadcasts every received bit out *all* other ports. All ports share the *same collision domain* and the *same bandwidth*. CSMA/CD is mandatory.

- **Switch (modern):** A switch is a *data-link layer* device. It uses the **Destination MAC address** in each frame to look up a forwarding table (MAC address table) and forwards the frame *only* to the correct output port. Each port is its **own independent collision domain**.

**Benefits of Switched Ethernet:**
- **Dedicated bandwidth per port** (no sharing).
- **Parallel forwarding** (multiple simultaneous conversations).
- **Micro-segmentation** (each port = 1 collision domain).
- **Store-and-forward** or **cut-through** switching modes for QoS.

**2. Full-Duplex Ethernet:**

In **full-duplex mode**, a station can **transmit and receive simultaneously** over the same link.

- Requires a **switch** (not a hub) at the other end and **separate transmit and receive channels** (which is naturally the case in twisted-pair with separate pairs, and in fiber with separate strands).
- **No collisions** are possible because transmitting on TX and receiving on RX are *physically independent*.
- Bandwidth is **effectively doubled** (e.g., 100 Mbps full-duplex = 100 Mbps TX + 100 Mbps RX = 200 Mbps aggregate).

**3. Why CSMA/CD is not needed in full-duplex:**

CSMA/CD exists to *resolve* collisions on a *shared, half-duplex* medium. In full-duplex point-to-point links:

| CSMA/CD Requirement | Full-Duplex Reality |
|:---------------------|:--------------------|
| Shared medium | Dedicated point-to-point link |
| Multiple stations can transmit simultaneously → collisions possible | Only **one** transmitter on each end of the link → collisions **impossible** |
| Carrier Sense needed to avoid collision | Other end is *always* "available" to send — no need to sense |
| Collision Detection needed to recover | Collisions cannot occur → no need to detect |

> ✅ **Therefore, IEEE 802.3x explicitly disables CSMA/CD when Auto-Negotiation establishes full-duplex mode.** This is why 10 Gigabit Ethernet is full-duplex *only* — there is no CSMA/CD defined for it.

**4. Flow Control in Full-Duplex:**

Instead of CSMA/CD, full-duplex Ethernet uses **PAUSE frames** (IEEE 802.3x) for flow control — the receiver can send a PAUSE frame to ask the sender to stop temporarily.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost KTU Students 1–3 Marks Each:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the IFG:** Students often calculate the minimum frame as 64 bytes *including* the preamble. **The 64-byte minimum EXCLUDES the 8-byte Preamble + SFD.** Loss: 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing the slot time values:** 51.2 μs is for **10 Mbps** only. For 100 Mbps, it is **5.12 μs**; for 1 Gbps, it is **0.512 μs** (extended to 4.096 μs). Loss: 1 mark.
> 3. **Not mentioning "why 64 bytes":** Just writing "the minimum frame size is 64 bytes" without explaining the *collision detection constraint* (sender must still be transmitting when collision signal returns) is incomplete. Loss: 1 mark.
> 4. **Writing "BEB" without the formula:** You must state $r = \text{rand}[0, 2^k - 1]$ and $k = \min(n, 10)$. Just writing "random backoff" is not enough. Loss: 1 mark.
> 5. **Mixing 802.3 and Ethernet II frames:** In 802.3, the Length/Type field carries the *length* if value ≤ 1500; in Ethernet II, it carries the *EtherType* if value ≥ 1536. Don't confuse them. Loss: 1 mark.
> 6. **Missing "carrier extension" in Gigabit Ethernet:** A 7-mark question on Gigabit Ethernet *must* mention carrier extension and frame bursting to be considered complete. Loss: 2 marks.
> 7. **Wrong efficiency formula:** The Bux approximation is $\eta = 1/(1 + 6.44a)$. Some students write $\eta = 1/(1 + 5a)$ — that is the simplified Tanenbaum model. KTU's recommended answer uses **Bux's formula**. Loss: 1 mark.
> 8. **Ignoring the 5-4-3 rule:** For any classic Ethernet (10 Mbps) question, mention the 5-4-3 rule (5 segments, 4 repeaters, 3 populated) to score full marks.

---

## 5.4 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for Last-Minute Review:**

### 🔑 Must-Memorize Numbers
- **Min frame:** **64 bytes** (excl. preamble) = 512 bits
- **Max frame:** **1518 bytes** (1522 with VLAN)
- **Slot time @ 10 Mbps:** **51.2 μs**
- **Bit time @ 10 Mbps:** **100 ns** (0.1 μs)
- **IFG:** **96 bit-times** = 9.6 μs @ 10 Mbps
- **Jam signal:** **32 bit-times**
- **Max retry:** **16 collisions** then drop
- **BEB cap:** $k = \min(n, 10)$, range $r \in [0, 1023]$
- **5-4-3 rule:** 5 segments, 4 repeaters, 3 populated

### 🔑 Must-Memorize Concepts
- **CSMA/CD = Carrier Sense + Multiple Access + Collision Detection**
- **BEB:** doubles the random window after every collision up to 10, then freezes
- **Carrier Sense:** listen before transmit; wait IFG if busy
- **Collision Detection:** monitor while transmitting; send 32-bit jam if detected
- **Efficiency:** $\eta = 1/(1 + 6.44a)$ where $a = T_p / T_t$
- **Manchester encoding** for 10 Mbps; **4B/5B + MLT-3** for 100BASE-TX; **8B/10B + PAM-5** for 1000BASE-T; **64B/66B + PAM-16** for 10GBASE-T
- **Full-duplex = no CSMA/CD, no collisions, PAUSE frames for flow control**
- **Gigabit Ethernet uses carrier extension + frame bursting** to maintain 64-byte minimum
- **10 Gbps and above are full-duplex only**

### 🔑 Must-Memorize Format
- **802.3 Frame:** Preamble (7) | SFD (1) | DA (6) | SA (6) | Length/Type (2) | Data (46–1500) | Pad (0–46) | FCS (4)
- **FCS = CRC-32** computed over DA + SA + Length + Data

### 🔑 Must-Memorize Evolution
- **10BASE5 (thick coax, 500 m) → 10BASE2 (thin coax, 185 m) → 10BASE-T (UTP, 100 m, star) → 100BASE-TX (Fast Ethernet, Cat-5) → 1000BASE-T (Gigabit, Cat-5e) → 10GBASE-T (10 Gig, Cat-6a)**

### 🔑 Key Differences to Remember
- **Hub vs. Switch:** Hub = shared collision domain; Switch = isolated collision domains
- **Half vs. Full Duplex:** Half = CSMA/CD required; Full = CSMA/CD disabled
- **Bus vs. Star:** Bus = single shared cable; Star = central hub/switch with point-to-point links
- **Coax vs. UTP vs. Fiber:** Coax is legacy/obsolete; UTP is dominant for ≤10 Gbps; Fiber is used for ≥10 Gbps and long distances
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Token Ring
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCERT602 - DATA COMMUNICATION AND NETWORKING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Token Ring # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned) **Token Ring** is a Local Area Network (LAN) protocol standardized as **IEEE 802.5**, operating at the **Medium Access Control (MAC) sublayer** of the Data Link Layer (Layer 2) of the OSI reference model. It is a deterministic, token-passing media access control technique in which a small control packet called a *token* circulates continuously around a logical ring topology, granting the station currently possessing it the exclusive right to transmit data onto the shared medium for a bounded time interval. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Scheme High-Yield Definition** > Token Ring is a **deterministic, contention-free** LAN access method where a **3-byte token frame** circulates unidirectionally around a logical ring. A station can transmit **only when it captures the free token**, ensuring that **no collisions** occur under normal operation. ## 1.2 Key Standardized Parameters (Must Memorize) | Parameter | Value / Standard | |-----------|------------------| | IEEE Standard | **IEEE 802.5** | | Topology | **Logical Ring, Physical Star** | | Token Size | **3 bytes** | | Media | Twisted Pair (originally 150 Ω STP) | | Max Stations per Ring | **250** | | Data Rate | **4 Mbps / 16 Mbps / 100 Mbps** | | Access Method | **Token Passing (Deterministic)** | | Direction of Flow | **Unidirectional** | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Talking Stick" > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Intuition: The Council of Chiefs** > Imagine a tribal council where members sit in a circle. A ceremonial **talking stick** is passed from person to person, one direction only. **Only the person holding the stick may speak.** When finished, they pass the stick to their neighbor. If someone has nothing to say, they simply pass it along immediately. This guarantees that: > - **No two people talk at once** (no collisions). > - **Everyone gets a fair turn** (bounded waiting time). > - **Order is preserved** (deterministic access). > > In Token Ring, the **talking stick = the free token**, the **council members = the stations (workstations/MAUs)**, and the **circle = the logical ring**. ## 1.4 Why Token Ring? The Core Problem It Solves In contention-based protocols like **CSMA/CD (Ethernet IEEE 802.3)**, as network load increases, **collision probability rises quadratically**, crushing throughput. Token Ring solves this by **physically preventing simultaneous access** — performance degrades gracefully and remains bounded even at high load, making it ideal for **real-time industrial and mission-critical applications**. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Logic of Token Passing — Step-by-Step The Token Ring protocol operates through a precisely orchestrated sequence: 1. **Initialization (Ring Purge):** On power-up, one station is elected as the **Active Monitor (AM)**, which initializes the ring and purges any stray data frames. 2. **Idle State — Token Circulation:** A special control frame called the **Free Token** (3 bytes) circulates continuously around the logical ring in one direction. 3. **Token Capture (Decision Point):** A station with data ready to transmit **waits until it receives the free token**, then *seizes* it by setting the **T bit (Token bit) = 1**, transforming it into the start of a data frame preamble. 4. **Data Transmission:** The station transmits its full data frame onto the ring immediately after the captured token. 5. **Frame Circulation & Reception:** The frame travels around the ring. **The destination station copies the frame**, sets the **A bit (Address-recognized) and C bit (Frame-copied)** flags, and forwards the frame onward. 6. **Frame Removal (Source Stripping):** The **originating station removes (strips)** its own frame from the ring when it returns, and **releases a new free token** downstream. 7. **Acknowledgment:** The returning frame's A and C bits inform the sender whether the destination was active and whether the frame was successfully received. > [!TIP] > **Critical KTU Concept:** In **Classical Token Release**, the free token is released *only after* the sender receives its own frame back. In **Early Token Release (ETR)** used in 16 Mbps Token Ring, the free token is released *immediately after* transmission, allowing multiple frames on the ring simultaneously — a major throughput boost. ## 2.2 Token Ring Frame Formats (IEEE 802.5) ### 2.2.1 Token Frame (3 bytes) $$\text{Token Frame} = \underbrace{1\text{ byte}}_{\text{SD}} + \underbrace{1\text{ byte}}_{\text{AC}} + \underbrace{1\text{ byte}}_{\text{ED}}$$ - **SD (Start Delimiter):** `JK0JK000` — signals frame beginning, uses differential Manchester violation patterns. - **AC (Access Control):** Contains the **T bit**, **PPP (Priority bits)**, and **RRR (Reservation bits)**. - **ED (End Delimiter):** Marks token end. ### 2.2.2 Data Frame Format $$\text{Data Frame} = \text{SD} + \text{AC} + \text{FC} + \text{DA} + \text{SA} + \text{Data} + \text{FCS} + \text{ED} + \text{FS}$$ | Field | Size | Function | |-------|------|----------| | **SD** | 1 byte | Start Delimiter (J,K symbol violations) | | **AC** | 1 byte | Access Control (T-bit, Priority, Reservation) | | **FC** | 1 byte | Frame Control (data vs. control frame) | | **DA** | 6 bytes | Destination MAC Address | | **SA** | 6 bytes | Source MAC Address | | **Data** | ≤ 4500 bytes | Upper-layer payload (LLC) | | **FCS** | 4 bytes | Frame Check Sequence (CRC-32) | | **ED** | 1 byte | End Delimiter (also includes I-bit) | | **FS** | 1 byte | Frame Status (A-bit, C-bit, reserved) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **A-bit (Address Recognized)** and **C-bit (Frame Copied)** together form the **Frame Status (FS)** field. They are the only **non-CRC-checked** fields in the frame — used because they are modified en route by the destination. ## 2.3 Priority Access Mechanism (The PPP / RRR System) The **Access Control (AC) byte** implements an **8-level priority system (0–7)**: $$\text{AC byte} = \underbrace{PPP}_{\text{3 bits Priority}} + \underbrace{T}_{\text{1 bit Token}} + \underbrace{RRR}_{\text{3 bits Reservation}}$$ - **PPP bits:** Current priority of the token. - **T bit:** `0` = Free Token, `1` = Data frame follows. - **RRR bits:** A station with a high-priority frame can *reserve* the next token by setting RRR to its desired priority. **Priority Rules:** - A station with a frame of priority **P** can seize a free token **only if `P ≥ PPP`** of the current token. - A station may **raise PPP** to a higher value (using the RRR reservation) so its data gets the next token. ## 2.4 Ring Management — Active Monitor (AM) > [!NOTE] > Every Token Ring LAN has exactly **one Active Monitor** and one or more **Standby Monitors**. The AM is elected via a process called **Claim Token** when the ring initializes. **Responsibilities of the Active Monitor:** | Function | Mechanism | |----------|-----------| | **Master Clock** | Provides the ring's primary timing reference | | **Token Watchdog** | Detects lost/orphaned tokens; inserts a new free token if none seen in time | | **Frame Purging** | Strips continuously circulating frames (due to sender failure) | | **Beaconing** | Initiates diagnostic frames to locate ring breaks | If the AM fails, standby monitors initiate a **Claim Token** process, and a new AM is elected within seconds. ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield for Board Exam) > [!TIP] > **Memorize these formulas verbatim for numerical problems in KTU ESE.** | # | Formula / Term | Symbol | Definition / Unit | |---|----------------|--------|-------------------| | 1 | **Walk Time** (1 bit) | $T_w$ | Time for **1 bit** to travel the entire ring circumference (seconds) | | 2 | **Ring Latency** (total) | $T_L$ | $T_L = N \cdot T_s + T_w$ (seconds), where $N$ = stations, $T_s$ = 1-bit station delay | | 3 | **Token Holding Time (THT)** | $T_{THT}$ | Maximum time a station may hold the token (default ≈ **10 ms** in 4 Mbps) | | 4 | **Token Rotation Time (TRT)** | $T_{TRT}$ | Time for one complete cycle of the token around the ring | | 5 | **Maximum Frame Transmission Time** | $T_{max}$ | $T_{max} = \dfrac{L_{max}}{R}$ where $L_{max}$ = max frame length, $R$ = data rate | | 6 | **Maximum Token Rotation Time** | $T_{max\_TRT}$ | $T_{max\_TRT} = N \cdot T_{THT}$ (if all N stations transmit) | | 7 | **Effective Throughput (Classical)** | $S$ | $S = \dfrac{T_{data}}{T_{cycle}}$ (frames/sec) | | 8 | **Ring Bit Length** | $B_L$ | $B_L = D \cdot 5 \mu s/km$ (typical: 1 bit per 5 μs of cable @ 4 Mbps) | | 9 | **Propagation delay per km** | $t_p$ | $\approx 5\,\mu s/km$ for STP | | 10 | **TTRT (Timed Token)** | $T_{TTRT}$ | Target Token Rotation Time — guarantees bounded access delay | ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility | Application Domain | Why Token Ring is Used | |--------------------|------------------------| | **Industrial Process Control** | Deterministic, bounded latency (TTRT) — critical for SCADA / PLC networks | | **Avionics & Aerospace** | Token Bus (IEEE 802.4, descendant) used in aircraft; deterministic behavior mandatory | | **Legacy IBM SNA Networks** | IBM invented Token Ring in 1984; dominated enterprise networks in the 1990s | | **Fiber Distributed Data Interface (FDDI)** | Direct descendant — uses dual-counter-rotating token rings at **100 Mbps** over fiber | | **Embedded & Real-Time Systems** | Predictable worst-case delay unlike CSMA/CD's unbounded collision recovery | > [!NOTE] > While Ethernet won the LAN wars commercially, **Token Ring's deterministic principles live on** in IEEE 802.4 (Token Bus), FDDI, and modern real-time Ethernet variants (e.g., TTEthernet, Profinet IRT). # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Token Rotation Time (TRT) for a Loaded Ring ### Problem Setup Consider a Token Ring with **$N$** active stations. Each station has data to send. The **Token Holding Time (THT)** per station is fixed. Derive the **Maximum Token Rotation Time**. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Time contributed by one station.** When station $i$ captures the token, it transmits for a duration not exceeding $T_{THT}$. Therefore, station $i$ contributes a maximum of $T_{THT}$ seconds of ring occupancy. $$\text{Time per station} = T_{THT} \quad \text{(seconds)}$$ **Step 2 — Time contributed by all $N$ stations.** Since the token must visit all $N$ stations sequentially (worst case — all have data): $$T_{data\_total} = N \cdot T_{THT}$$ **Step 3 — Adding the ring latency (propagation + station bit delays).** The token must also physically propagate through the ring and incur 1-bit delay at each station: $$T_L = N \cdot T_s + T_w$$ where $T_s$ = 1-bit station latency, $T_w$ = 1-bit walk time. **Step 4 — Total Maximum Token Rotation Time.** The total time for one complete cycle of the token is the sum of all stations' transmission time plus the ring latency: $$\begin{aligned} T_{TRT,\max} &= T_{data\_total} + T_L \\ T_{TRT,\max} &= N \cdot T_{THT} + N \cdot T_s + T_w \end{aligned}$$ **Final Result (boxed formula):** $$\boxed{\,T_{TRT,\max} = N \cdot (T_{THT} + T_s) + T_w\,}$$ ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Walk Time ($T_w$) from Cable Length ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Identify propagation speed.** For a typical shielded twisted pair (STP) used in 802.5, the signal propagation speed is approximately $2 \times 10^8$ m/s (≈ 2/3 the speed of light in vacuum). **Step 2 — Propagation delay per kilometer.** $$t_p = \frac{1\,\text{km}}{2 \times 10^5\,\text{km/s}} = 5\,\mu s/km$$ This is the **standardized KTU value** of $t_p \approx 5\,\mu s/\text{km}$. **Step 3 — Total propagation delay over cable of length $D$ km.** $$T_{prop} = D \cdot t_p = 5D \quad (\mu s)$$ **Step 4 — Express in terms of bit duration (Walk Time).** Walk Time is conventionally expressed as the time for **1 bit** to traverse the ring: $$T_w = D \cdot t_p \quad (\mu s)$$ At a data rate of $R$ bps, the bit duration is $T_b = 1/R$ seconds. The number of "bits on the ring" purely from cable is: $$\text{Bit Length} = D \cdot t_p \cdot R \quad \text{(bits)}$$ ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Throughput Efficiency ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Define useful data transmitted per cycle.** Suppose each of the $N$ stations transmits a single frame of length $L$ bits during one token rotation: $$T_{useful} = \frac{N \cdot L}{R} \quad \text{(seconds)}$$ **Step 2 — Define total cycle time.** $$T_{cycle} = T_{TRT,\max} = \frac{N \cdot L}{R} + T_L$$ **Step 3 — Define efficiency as ratio of useful time to total cycle time.** $$\begin{aligned} \eta &= \frac{T_{useful}}{T_{cycle}} \\ \eta &= \frac{\dfrac{N \cdot L}{R}}{\dfrac{N \cdot L}{R} + T_L} \\ \eta &= \frac{1}{1 + \dfrac{R \cdot T_L}{N \cdot L}} \end{aligned}$$ **Final Result:** $$\boxed{\,\eta = \dfrac{N \cdot L}{N \cdot L + R \cdot T_L}\,}$$ **Interpretation:** As $N \cdot L$ (total data per cycle) increases, efficiency $\eta \to 1$. As ring latency $T_L$ increases, efficiency drops — this is why **Early Token Release (ETR)** improves efficiency by reducing effective cycle time. ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style) > [!NOTE] > **Sample Question:** A Token Ring LAN has **10 stations**, each with a **1-bit latency**. The ring cable is **500 m** long, and the data rate is **4 Mbps**. The Token Holding Time (THT) is **10 ms**. Compute: > (a) Ring Latency $T_L$ > (b) Maximum Token Rotation Time $T_{TRT,\max}$ > (c) Comment on whether the ring is "efficient". ### Solution **Given:** - $N = 10$ stations - $T_s = 1\text{ bit} / 4\,\text{Mbps} = 0.25\,\mu s$ (station latency) - $D = 500\,\text{m} = 0.5\,\text{km}$ - $R = 4 \times 10^6\,\text{bits/s}$ - $T_{THT} = 10\,\text{ms} = 10{,}000\,\mu s$ **Step (a) — Ring Latency $T_L$:** $$\begin{aligned} T_w &= D \cdot t_p = 0.5 \times 5\,\mu s = 2.5\,\mu s \\ N \cdot T_s &= 10 \times 0.25\,\mu s = 2.5\,\mu s \\ T_L &= N \cdot T_s + T_w = 2.5 + 2.5 = 5\,\mu s \end{aligned}$$ **Step (b) — Maximum Token Rotation Time:** $$\begin{aligned} T_{TRT,\max} &= N \cdot (T_{THT} + T_s) + T_w \\ &= 10 \times (10{,}000 + 0.25) + 2.5 \\ &= 10 \times 10{,}000.25 + 2.5 \\ &= 100{,}002.5 + 2.5 \\ &= 100{,}005\,\mu s \\ &\approx 100.005\,\text{ms} \end{aligned}$$ **Step (c) — Efficiency Comment:** The ring latency ($5\,\mu s$) is negligible compared to total data time ($\approx 100\,\text{ms}$). Therefore, the ring is **highly efficient** (efficiency $\approx 1.0$). $$\eta = \frac{100{,}000}{100{,}005} \approx 0.99995 = 99.995\%$$ ## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Token Ring vs. CSMA/CD) | Performance Metric | Token Ring (802.5) | Ethernet (802.3 CSMA/CD) | |--------------------|-------------------|---------------------------| | Access Method | Deterministic, token-based | Contention-based, probabilistic | | Collision Possible? | **No** (by design) | **Yes** (statistical) | | Throughput under High Load | **Remains stable & high** | **Degrades sharply** | | Throughput under Low Load | Lower (must wait for token) | Higher (immediate access if idle) | | Maximum Access Delay | **Bounded** (TTRT) | **Unbounded** (theoretical worst case) | | Frame Size | Variable, ≤ 4500 bytes | 64–1518 bytes | | Cable Length Limit | ~250 m per MAU (with repeaters) | ~100 m (UTP) / 2.5 km (coax) | | Fairness | **Guaranteed** (round-robin) | Not guaranteed (capture effect possible) | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Token Ring Logical Topology with Token Circulation ```mermaid flowchart LR node1["Station 1
Sender"] --> node2["Station 2
Idle"] node2 --> node3["Station 3
Idle"] node3 --> node4["Station 4
Destination"] node4 --> node1 nodeToken1((Free Token)) -. "circulates" .-> node1 nodeToken2((Frame F1)) -. "travels" .-> node4 classDef active fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 classDef idle fill:#E0E0E0,stroke:#666,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef token fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class node1,node4 active class node2,node3 idle class nodeToken1,nodeToken2 token ``` **Interpretation:** The logical ring is **unidirectional**. Station 1 currently holds the **free token** and is about to transmit **Frame F1** to Station 4. Stations 2 and 3 are in idle forwarding mode. ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Token Ring Frame Passing Sequence (Time-Domain State Machine) ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Idle: Ring Powered On Idle --> TokenCirculating: Active Monitor Inserts Free Token state "Token Circulating" as TC { [*] --> WaitForToken WaitForToken --> CheckQueue: Token Arrives CheckQueue --> UseToken: Data Ready CheckQueue --> ForwardToken: No Data } state "Transmission Phase" as TP { [*] --> CaptureToken CaptureToken --> SendDataFrame SendDataFrame --> FrameTravels FrameTravels --> DestinationCopies DestinationCopies --> ReturnsToSender } TokenCirculating --> TransmissionPhase: Captured by Sender TransmissionPhase --> TokenCirculating: Source Strips Frame, Releases New Token TC --> [*]: Ring Failure, Beacon Process ``` **Interpretation:** Shows the **state machine of a station** in a Token Ring — from idle waiting, through token capture, to data transmission and release of a new free token. ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Active Monitor Election & Ring Recovery ```mermaid flowchart TD start(["Ring Boot"]) --> claim[All Stations Send CLAIM_TOKEN Frames] claim --> battle{Highest MAC Wins} battle --> am[That Station Becomes Active Monitor] am --> monitor{AM Functional?} monitor --> yes[Provide Master Clock, Watch Token] monitor --> no[AM Fails or Token Lost] no --> purge[Standby Monitors Detect Absence] purge --> purgeAction[Purge Ring, Insert New Free Token] purgeAction --> monitor yes --> heartbeat[Send AMP Frames Periodically] heartbeat --> monitor classDef monitorNode fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px classDef recoveryNode fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px class am,monitor,yes,heartbeat monitorNode class no,purge,purgeAction recoveryNode ``` **Interpretation:** Illustrates the **self-healing ring management** — the Active Monitor is elected via a contention-based Claim Token process, and recovery is automatic if the AM fails. ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Priority & Reservation Logic Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA["Station Receives Free Token
AC Byte: PPP T RRR"] nodeB{Data Ready?} nodeC["Forward Token Unchanged
No Action"] nodeD{Frame Priority P >= PPP?} nodeE["Capture Token
Set T = 1
Transmit Frame"] nodeF["Cannot Capture Now
But: Set RRR = max RRR, P
Forward Token"] nodeG["Sender Strips Frame
Sets RRR in Next Token
Releases Free Token"] nodeH{PPP_new >= My P?} nodeI["Capture Reserved Token
Transmit My Data"] nodeA --> nodeB nodeB -- "No" --> nodeC nodeB -- "Yes" --> nodeD nodeD -- "Yes" --> nodeE nodeD -- "No" --> nodeF nodeE --> nodeG nodeG --> nodeH nodeH -- "Yes" --> nodeI nodeH -- "No" --> nodeC classDef decision fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:2px classDef action fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px class nodeB,nodeD,nodeH decision class nodeC,nodeE,nodeF,nodeG,nodeI action ``` **Interpretation:** The **priority/reservation protocol** allows a high-priority station to pre-empt lower-priority ones by setting the RRR bits, ensuring that the next free token carries an elevated PPP value. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why Mermaid Block Diagrams Instead of Physical Drawings?** > Token Ring's physical wiring involves a **MAU (Multistation Access Unit)** with a star-shaped physical layout, but its **logical operation is a ring**. Mermaid's flow-chart/graph syntax is ideally suited to capture this **logical** view — which is what the KTU examiner tests. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A Questions (Each 3 Marks) --- ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **Define Token Ring. List any four features of IEEE 802.5 Token Ring LAN.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1 mark):** Token Ring is a **deterministic LAN access protocol standardized as IEEE 802.5**, in which a special 3-byte control packet called a *free token* circulates unidirectionally around a logical ring. A station can transmit only when it captures the free token, thereby preventing collisions. **Four Features (2 marks — ½ mark each):** 1. **Logical ring, physical star** topology using MAUs. 2. **Token passing access** — contention-free, deterministic. 3. **Supports 4 / 16 / 100 Mbps** data rates. 4. **IEEE 802.5 standard** with up to 250 stations per ring. 5. **Priority mechanism** using 3-bit PPP / 3-bit RRR fields. 6. **Active Monitor** for ring management and token recovery. *(Any four well-explained features = 2 marks)* --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Explain the function of the Active Monitor in a Token Ring network.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Active Monitor (AM)** is a designated station in a Token Ring LAN responsible for **ring management and integrity**. Its key functions are: 1. **Master Clock Source (1 mark):** Provides the primary timing reference for all stations on the ring, ensuring synchronized bit-level operation. 2. **Token Watchdog (1 mark):** Detects lost or orphaned tokens. If no token is observed within a defined time window (e.g., ~10 ms for 4 Mbps), the AM purges the ring and injects a new free token. 3. **Frame Purging & Beaconing (1 mark):** Strips continuously circulating "orphan" frames caused by a failed sender, and initiates **beaconing** — diagnostic frames to locate and isolate ring breaks. > The AM is elected via the **Claim Token** process at ring initialization, and another monitor takes over automatically if the AM fails. --- ## 5.2 PART B Questions (Each 14 Marks) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice Format --- ### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *Choose A or B* **[KTU University Exam — July 2024]** **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **operation of Token Ring LAN** under **IEEE 802.5**, clearly describing the role of the **free token, token capture, frame circulation, and source stripping**. Discuss the difference between **Classical Token Release** and **Early Token Release (ETR)**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A Token Ring LAN has **15 stations**. The propagation delay of the cable is **5 μs/km**, and the total ring cable length is **2 km**. The data rate is **16 Mbps**, and each station introduces a **1-bit delay**. The Token Holding Time is **15 ms**. Compute: (i) Ring Latency $T_L$ (ii) Maximum Token Rotation Time $T_{TRT,\max}$ (iii) Comment on the ring's efficiency. **(7 marks)** --- ### **Model Solution — Question A** #### Part (a) — Operation of Token Ring (7 marks) **Free Token Circulation (1 mark):** A 3-byte free token circulates unidirectionally around the logical ring. It contains only SD, AC, and ED fields. The T-bit in the AC byte is `0`, indicating a free (available) token. **Token Capture (1.5 marks):** When a station with data ready receives the free token, it **inverts the T-bit to 1**, effectively converting the token into the preamble of a data frame. The station then appends the rest of the data frame fields (FC, DA, SA, Data, FCS, ED, FS) and transmits. **Frame Circulation & Reception (1.5 marks):** The frame propagates around the ring. Each station inspects the **Destination Address (DA)**. The **destination station copies the frame into its buffer**, sets the **A-bit and C-bit** in the Frame Status field, and forwards the frame onward. **Source Stripping (1 mark):** The **originating station** recognizes its own frame (by SA) when it returns, **strips it from the ring**, and releases a new free token downstream. **Classical vs. Early Token Release (2 marks):** | Aspect | Classical Token Release | Early Token Release (ETR) | |--------|------------------------|----------------------------| | New token released | Only after sender's frame returns & is stripped | **Immediately after frame transmission** (frame still on ring) | | Max frames on ring | **1** | **Multiple** (limited by ring latency) | | Throughput @ 16 Mbps | Limited | **Significantly higher** | | Used in | 4 Mbps Token Ring | **16 Mbps and 100 Mbps Token Ring** | **Diagram (1 mark):** *(Draw the logical ring with 5 stations, show the free token circulating, then show the data frame being transmitted, the destination copying it, and the source stripping & releasing a new token. A Mermaid / flowchart version is acceptable.)* --- #### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks) **Given:** - $N = 15$ stations - $D = 2\,\text{km}$, $t_p = 5\,\mu s/\text{km}$ - $R = 16\,\text{Mbps} = 16 \times 10^6\,\text{bits/s}$ - $T_s = 1\,\text{bit} / R = 1 / (16 \times 10^6) = 0.0625\,\mu s$ - $T_{THT} = 15\,\text{ms} = 15{,}000\,\mu s$ **(i) Ring Latency (2 marks):** $$\begin{aligned} T_w &= D \cdot t_p = 2 \times 5 = 10\,\mu s \\ N \cdot T_s &= 15 \times 0.0625 = 0.9375\,\mu s \\ T_L &= N \cdot T_s + T_w = 0.9375 + 10 = 10.9375\,\mu s \end{aligned}$$ **(ii) Maximum Token Rotation Time (3 marks):** $$\begin{aligned} T_{TRT,\max} &= N \cdot (T_{THT} + T_s) + T_w \\ &= 15 \times (15{,}000 + 0.0625) + 10 \\ &= 15 \times 15{,}000.0625 + 10 \\ &= 225{,}000.9375 + 10 \\ &= 225{,}010.9375\,\mu s \\ &\approx 225.011\,\text{ms} \end{aligned}$$ **(iii) Efficiency Comment (2 marks):** The ring latency $T_L \approx 10.94\,\mu s$ is **negligible** compared to the total data time ($\approx 225\,\text{ms}$). $$\eta = \frac{225{,}000}{225{,}010.94} \approx 0.99995 = 99.995\%$$ **Conclusion:** The ring operates at near-maximum efficiency. With 16 Mbps rate and ETR, this design is well-suited for **high-throughput deterministic applications**. --- ### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice* **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **(a)** Explain the **IEEE 802.5 Token Ring frame format** in detail. Describe the function of each field, with emphasis on the **Access Control (AC)** byte and the **Frame Status (FS)** byte. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare **Token Ring (IEEE 802.5)** and **Ethernet (IEEE 802.3)** in terms of access method, collision behavior, performance under load, and fairness. State two real-world applications where Token Ring's deterministic access is preferred. **(7 marks)** --- ### **Model Solution — Question B** #### Part (a) — Frame Format (7 marks) **Complete Frame Format (3 marks — diagram with field labels):** $$\text{Frame} = \text{SD} + \text{AC} + \text{FC} + \text{DA} + \text{SA} + \text{Data} + \text{FCS} + \text{ED} + \text{FS}$$ | Field | Size | Function | |-------|------|----------| | SD | 1 byte | Start Delimiter — J,K symbol violation patterns | | AC | 1 byte | Access Control — T-bit, PPP, RRR | | FC | 1 byte | Frame Control — distinguishes data vs. control | | DA | 6 bytes | Destination MAC Address | | SA | 6 bytes | Source MAC Address | | Data | ≤ 4500 bytes | LLC payload | | FCS | 4 bytes | CRC-32 error detection | | ED | 1 byte | End Delimiter — also includes Intermediate bit (I) | | FS | 1 byte | Frame Status — A-bit, C-bit, reserved | **Access Control (AC) Byte Deep Dive (2 marks):** The AC byte has the structure: **`PPP-T-RRR`** - **T bit:** `0` = Free Token; `1` = Data frame follows the AC byte. - **PPP (Priority, 3 bits):** Token's current priority level (0–7). A station may only capture the token if its frame priority ≥ PPP. - **RRR (Reservation, 3 bits):** A station with a higher-priority frame can "reserve" the next free token by writing its priority into RRR. The sender, when releasing the next token, copies the RRR value into the PPP field. **Frame Status (FS) Byte Deep Dive (2 marks):** The FS byte contains three flag bits (each duplicated for reliability — not CRC-protected): - **A bit (Address Recognized):** Set to `1` by the destination when it sees its own address. - **C bit (Frame Copied):** Set to `1` when the destination successfully copies the frame. - **r bits:** Reserved. > The A and C bits tell the **sender** whether the frame was received and acknowledged. Together with the strip-on-return mechanism, this implements a **single-bit acknowledgment** for delivery confirmation. --- #### Part (b) — Comparison (7 marks) **Comparison Table (4 marks):** | Parameter | Token Ring (802.5) | Ethernet (802.3) | |-----------|--------------------|-------------------| | **Access Method** | Token passing (deterministic) | CSMA/CD (probabilistic) | | **Collisions** | None (by design) | Yes — detected & retried | | **Performance at High Load** | Stable, bounded | Degrades (throughput collapse) | | **Performance at Low Load** | Lower (wait for token) | Higher (immediate if idle) | | **Fairness** | Guaranteed (round-robin) | Not guaranteed (capture effect) | | **Max Access Delay** | Bounded by TTRT | Theoretically unbounded | | **Topology** | Logical ring, physical star | Bus / Star | | **Max Frame Size** | 4500 bytes | 1518 bytes | **Real-World Applications (3 marks — 1.5 marks each):** 1. **Industrial Process Automation (e.g., SCADA, PLC Networks):** Token Ring's **bounded TTRT** ensures that critical control signals reach their destination within a guaranteed time window — essential for safety systems in chemical plants, power grids, and manufacturing lines. 2. **Avionics & Aerospace Control Buses:** Aircraft systems (e.g., ARINC 629, a Token Bus derivative) use token-passing because **deterministic delay** is non-negotiable for flight control, engine monitoring, and navigation data — collision-induced delays are unacceptable. *(Other valid answers: Token Bus in factory automation, FDDI in backbone networks, real-time financial trading systems.)* --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ Where KTU Students Typically Lose Marks in Token Ring Questions:** > > 1. **Forgetting the 1-bit station delay** ($T_s$): Many students compute $T_L$ as only the cable propagation delay $T_w$, completely missing the $N \cdot T_s$ term. This causes a wrong answer in numerical problems. > > 2. **Confusing T-bit meanings:** The T-bit in the AC byte is `0` for **free** token and `1` for **busy** (data) — not the other way around. Mixing this up is a guaranteed 1-mark loss. > > 3. **Misstating ETR:** "Early Token Release" releases the token **after transmission, NOT after the frame returns** — the frame is *still on the ring* when the new token is released. Many students incorrectly say "before transmission". > > 4. **Confusing A-bit and C-bit:** A-bit = "I see my address"; C-bit = "I successfully copied the frame into buffer". They are NOT the same. > > 5. **Wrong formula for efficiency:** The efficiency formula is $\eta = N \cdot L / (N \cdot L + R \cdot T_L)$ — students often forget to multiply $T_L$ by the data rate $R$ when converting time to bit-units. > > 6. **Calling Token Ring a "ring topology" without qualification:** The KTU answer must specify **"logical ring, physical star"**. A purely "ring topology" answer is incomplete. > > 7. **Ignoring the Active Monitor:** Any Token Ring question worth >5 marks *will* ask about ring management. Memorize the AM's three functions: clock, watchdog, purger. ## 5.4 Topic Recap — High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist > [!TIP] > **Print this section and revise the night before the exam.** - [ ] Token Ring = **IEEE 802.5**, MAC sublayer, **deterministic**, **collision-free** - [ ] Topology = **Logical Ring, Physical Star** (uses MAUs) - [ ] **Free Token** = 3 bytes: SD + AC + ED; T-bit = `0` - [ ] **Data Frame** = 9 fields, max data = **4500 bytes** - [ ] **AC byte = PPP-T-RRR**; T = token/busy, PPP = priority, RRR = reservation - [ ] **FS byte** has **A-bit** (address recognized) and **C-bit** (frame copied) — not CRC-checked - [ ] **Classical Token Release:** new token only after frame returns (1 frame on ring) - [ ] **Early Token Release (ETR):** new token released right after transmission (multiple frames on ring) - [ ] **Active Monitor** = master clock + token watchdog + frame purger - [ ] **Claim Token** process = AM election; **Beaconing** = fault isolation - [ ] Ring Latency formula: $\boxed{T_L = N \cdot T_s + T_w}$ - [ ] Max TRT formula: $\boxed{T_{TRT,\max} = N \cdot (T_{THT} + T_s) + T_w}$ - [ ] Walk time per km: $t_p = 5\,\mu s/\text{km}$ (STP) - [ ] Station bit delay: $T_s = 1/R$ seconds - [ ] Efficiency: $\eta = \dfrac{N \cdot L}{N \cdot L + R \cdot T_L}$ - [ ] Token Ring **wins on** determinism, bounded delay, fairness, high-load performance - [ ] Token Ring **loses on** low-load latency, cost, complexity, commercial market share - [ ] **FDDI** = dual-counter-rotating token rings at **100 Mbps** over fiber (direct descendant) - [ ] Token Ring is preferred in **industrial control, avionics, real-time SCADA** systems

topics.title: Fibre Channel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCERT602 - DATA COMMUNICATION AND NETWORKING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fibre Channel

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fibre Channel (FC)** is a high-speed, lossless, **gigabit-per-second (Gbps)** networking technology standardized by the **American National Standards Institute (ANSI)** under the **T11 committee**. It is primarily designed to provide a deterministic, low-latency transport mechanism for **Storage Area Networks (SANs)**, interconnecting servers, disk arrays, tape libraries, and other storage peripherals.

The standard defines a layered architecture (FC-0 through FC-4) that supports both **channel networking** (dedicated, high-throughput) and **networked storage** (packetized, switched) operations. Fibre Channel operates over both optical fiber and copper media, supporting current line rates of **1, 2, 4, 8, 16, 32, 64, and 128 Gbps**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Spelling Note:** "Fibre" is intentionally spelled in the **British (ISO) form** (with "re" instead of "er") because the technology was developed under international ISO/IEC standards, not American IEEE conventions. It does **NOT** mean it uses only fiber — it also runs on copper (Twinax) cables.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine the **airport luggage conveyor system**:

- Every suitcase (data frame) is placed on a dedicated high-speed belt.
- The belts never jam, never lose a bag, and have **priorities** (first-class, business, economy).
- There are multiple belt designs: a **single private belt** (point-to-point), a **shared circular belt** (arbitrated loop), and a **massive hub with switches directing bags to the right carousel** (switched fabric).

Similarly, Fibre Channel creates a **deterministic, lossless "data conveyor belt"** where storage traffic is delivered in a guaranteed order, with no retransmission-induced delays, and no packet loss. This is why it dominates enterprise SANs where **milliseconds of latency matter** (e.g., banking transactions, database clusters, virtual machine migrations).

### 1.3 Why Fibre Channel Exists — The Problem It Solves

Traditional Ethernet and TCP/IP networks were designed for **best-effort delivery**. When used for storage (via iSCSI or NFS), they suffer from:

1. **Packet loss** triggering TCP retransmissions → unpredictable latency.
2. **No bandwidth guarantee** — competing traffic can starve storage I/O.
3. **CPU overhead** for TCP/IP stack processing in servers.

Fibre Channel was engineered to deliver **channel-like performance** (the speed of a direct SCSI bus) with the **flexibility of a network** (switches, scalability, distance extension). It provides **in-order, lossless delivery with credit-based flow control**, making it the gold standard for mission-critical storage.

### 1.4 Key Standard Bodies and Documents

| Standard Body | Document / Standard | Scope |
|---|---|---|
| ANSI T11 | FC-PI (Physical Interface) | FC-0 layer |
| ANSI T11 | FC-FS (Framing and Signaling) | FC-1, FC-2 layers |
| ANSI T11 | FC-AL (Arbitrated Loop) | Loop topology |
| ANSI T11 | FC-SW (Switched Fabric) | Switched topology |
| INCITS | FC-GS (Generic Services) | FC-3 services |
| INCITS | FC-4 | Protocol mapping (SCSI, IP) |

> [!TIP]
> For KTU exams, remember that Fibre Channel is defined by **ANSI T11**, not by IEEE or IETF — a common trap question.

### 1.5 Fibre Channel vs. Competing Technologies (At a Glance)

| Feature | Fibre Channel | iSCSI (over Ethernet) | InfiniBand | FCoE |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Base Protocol | Native FC | SCSI over TCP/IP | Native IB | FC over Ethernet |
| Typical Speed | 16/32/64 Gbps | 10/25/100 Gbps | 40/100/200 Gbps | 25/50/100 Gbps |
| Lossless | Yes (native) | No (TCP retransmit) | Yes (native) | Yes (with PFC/CEE) |
| Typical Use | Enterprise SAN | Mid-market SAN | HPC, AI clusters | Converged LAN/SAN |
| Cable | Fiber / Copper | Cat6a / Fiber | Copper / Fiber | Cat6a / Fiber |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five-Layer FC Architecture (FC-0 to FC-4)

Fibre Channel is built as a **five-layered stack**, each layer performing a distinct function. Understanding this hierarchy is the **single most important KTU Module-4 topic** for marks.

#### FC-0: Physical Layer
- Defines the **physical media**: multimode fiber (MMF), single-mode fiber (SMF), and copper twin-axial cable.
- Specifies **transceivers** (e.g., SFP, SFP+, QSFP) and their optical/electrical characteristics.
- Defines **wavelengths** (850 nm for SR, 1310 nm for LR, 1550 nm for ER).
- Sets **distance limits** (e.g., 150 m for OM3 MMF at 8 Gbps, 10 km for SMF at 16 Gbps).
- Establishes the **bit clock** — the raw bit rate.

#### FC-1: Transmission Protocol
- Implements **8b/10b encoding** (for speeds ≤ 8 Gbps) or **64b/66b encoding** (for 16 Gbps and above, sometimes called "16GFC+").
- The 8b/10b scheme maps every 8 data bits into a 10-bit transmission code (called a **transmission character**) to guarantee:
  - **DC balance** (equal number of 1s and 0s over time).
  - **Sufficient transitions** for clock recovery.
  - **Special control characters** (K28.5 comma) for frame delineation.
- Provides **byte synchronization** and **link-level error detection**.

#### FC-2: Framing and Signaling (The Heart of FC)
This is the **most complex and examinable layer**. It defines:

1. **Frames**: Variable-size (up to 2148 bytes) data units.
2. **Sequences**: A series of related frames forming one I/O operation.
3. **Exchanges**: One or more sequences forming a complete command (e.g., SCSI read).
4. **Classes of Service** (1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7) — discussed below.
5. **Flow control** via **Buffer-to-Buffer Credits (BBC)** and **End-to-End Credits (EE_Credit)**.
6. **Fabric Login (FLOGI)**, **Port Login (PLOGI)**, and **Process Login (PRLI)**.

#### FC-3: Common Services
- Provides services that span multiple N_Ports (node ports) on a single node.
- Examples: **Striping** (distributing data across multiple links for higher throughput), **Hunt Groups** (multiple ports sharing one address), and **Multicast**.

#### FC-4: Upper Layer Protocol Mapping
- Maps upper-layer protocols onto FC's lower layers.
- **FC-4 = SCSI**: The dominant use — **Fibre Channel Protocol (FCP)** carries SCSI commands and data. This is what makes FC a storage transport.
- **FC-4 = IP**: Allows IP packets to ride over FC (FC-IP).
- **FC-4 = FICON**: IBM's mainframe channel protocol over FC.
- **FC-4 = VI**: Virtual Interface for cluster interconnects.
- **FC-4 = NVMe over FC (FC-NVMe)**: Modern use for low-latency flash storage.

### 2.2 The Three Fibre Channel Topologies

#### A. Point-to-Point (FC-P2P)
- **Simplest topology**: exactly two devices connected directly.
- **No switching, no sharing**.
- **Maximum bandwidth** (full duplex).
- **Drawback**: not scalable.
- Used for direct server-to-storage connections.

#### B. Arbitrated Loop (FC-AL / FC-AL-2)
- **Up to 126 devices** connected in a **logical ring** (physically often a hub-based star, but logically a loop).
- Only **one pair of devices** can communicate at a time (hence "arbitrated" — they must win arbitration).
- **Half-duplex** operation.
- An **L_Port** (Loop Port) participates in the loop.
- Used in legacy, low-cost SANs.

#### C. Switched Fabric (FC-SW / FC-SW-2)
- **The dominant topology** in modern data centers.
- Uses one or more **Fibre Channel switches** (e.g., Brocade, Cisco MDS).
- Supports **up to ~16 million** addressable devices (24-bit address space: 16M fabric addresses, 16M Nx_Ports).
- **Concurrent communication**: multiple pairs can talk simultaneously (non-blocking switch fabric).
- Each port is an **N_Port** (Node Port) or **F_Port** (Fabric Port).
- **Bandwidth**: full duplex per port.

### 2.3 Classes of Service (KTU Frequently Asked)

| Class | Type | Acknowledgment | Bandwidth | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Class 1** | Dedicated connection | Yes | 100% reserved | Real-time, deterministic |
| **Class 2** | Connectionless | Yes (ACK) | Multiplexed | General storage traffic |
| **Class 3** | Connectionless (datagram) | **No ACK** | Multiplexed | Bulk data transfer (most common) |
| **Class 4** | Fractional bandwidth | Yes | Reserved % | Video / guaranteed bandwidth |
| **Class 5** | Multicast | No | Multiplexed | Broadcasting to multiple ports |
| **Class 6** | Multicast with ACK | Yes (to originator) | Reserved | Reliable multicast |
| **Class 7** | Unidirectional connection | No | 100% reserved | Dedicated unidirectional streams |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In practice, **Class 3 dominates** enterprise SANs (≈95%+ of traffic), with Class 2 used for control frames and Class F used internally by the fabric.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **exam-ready**. Master these to score full marks on derivations and numerical problems.

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Unit | Description |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **8b/10b Coding Efficiency** | $\eta_{8b10b} = \dfrac{8}{10} = 0.8$ | (dimensionless) | Ratio of data bits to transmitted bits |
| 2 | **64b/66b Coding Efficiency** | $\eta_{64b66b} = \dfrac{64}{66} \approx 0.9697$ | (dimensionless) | Used at 16 Gbps and above |
| 3 | **Effective Data Rate at N Gbps (8b/10b)** | $R_{eff} = N \times 0.8$ | Gbps | Useful data rate excluding encoding overhead |
| 4 | **Effective Data Rate at N Gbps (64b/66b)** | $R_{eff} = N \times \dfrac{64}{66}$ | Gbps | Used in 16G/32G/64G FC |
| 5 | **Frame Payload Range** | $0 \le P \le 2112$ | bytes | Max 2112 bytes (2048 data + 64 header) |
| 6 | **Max Devices in FC-AL Loop** | $N_{max} = 126$ | devices | Address 0 = FL_Port, 127 = AL_PA map |
| 7 | **Fabric Address Space** | $2^{24} = 16{,}777{,}216$ | ports | 24-bit address in switched fabric |
| 8 | **Round-Trip Latency Budget** | $T_{RTT} = \dfrac{2 \times D}{v_{fiber}}$ | seconds | $D$ = distance, $v_{fiber} \approx 2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s |
| 9 | **Buffer-to-Buffer Credit** | $BBC \ge \left\lceil \dfrac{R \times T_{prop}}{L} \right\rceil$ | credits | $R$ = data rate, $L$ = frame size |
| 10 | **Frame Error Check (CRC)** | CRC-32 | bits | 32-bit polynomial check on every frame |

> **Real-world utility:** Formula 9 is critical for SAN engineers sizing a link. For example, at **16 Gbps** over **10 km of single-mode fiber**, propagation delay ≈ 100 µs; frame size = 2112 bytes = 16,896 bits. So:
> $$ BBC \ge \left\lceil \dfrac{16 \times 10^{9} \times 100 \times 10^{-6}}{16896} \right\rceil = \lceil 94.7 \rceil = 95 \text{ credits} $$
> This is why long-distance FC links require switches with deep buffer credits.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Effective Throughput with 8b/10b Encoding

**Problem Statement (typical KTU Part-B style):**
*Calculate the effective data throughput of a 4 Gbps Fibre Channel link that uses 8b/10b encoding, and the number of full-size frames (2112-byte payload) that can be transmitted per second.*

#### Step 1 — Identify the given parameters
We are given:
- Line (raw) bit rate: $R_{line} = 4 \text{ Gbps} = 4 \times 10^{9} \text{ bits/second}$
- Encoding scheme: 8b/10b
- Frame payload: $L_{payload} = 2112 \text{ bytes} = 2112 \times 8 \text{ bits} = 16896 \text{ bits}$

#### Step 2 — Recall the 8b/10b overhead
In 8b/10b encoding, every 8 bits of data are encoded as 10 bits on the wire. This means the **effective data rate is reduced by 20%**.

$$
R_{eff} = R_{line} \times \eta_{8b10b}
$$

#### Step 3 — Substitute the values

$$
R_{eff} = 4 \times 10^{9} \times \frac{8}{10}
$$

$$
R_{eff} = 4 \times 10^{9} \times 0.8
$$

$$
R_{eff} = 3.2 \text{ Gbps}
$$

#### Step 4 — Compute frames per second
We need to determine how many frames of payload 2112 bytes can be sent per second at this effective data rate.

$$
f_{fps} = \frac{R_{eff}}{L_{payload}}
$$

$$
f_{fps} = \frac{3.2 \times 10^{9} \text{ bits/s}}{16896 \text{ bits/frame}}
$$

$$
f_{fps} = 189{,}393.94 \text{ frames/s}
$$

#### Step 5 — Apply framing overhead
The 2112 bytes is the **payload only**. A complete FC frame consists of:
- **Start of Frame (SOF)** delimiter: 4 bytes
- **Frame Header**: 24 bytes
- **Payload**: 2112 bytes (Data Field)
- **CRC**: 4 bytes
- **End of Frame (EOF)** delimiter: 4 bytes

$$
L_{total} = 4 + 24 + 2112 + 4 + 4 = 2148 \text{ bytes/frame}
$$

$$
L_{total} = 2148 \times 8 = 17184 \text{ bits/frame}
$$

#### Step 6 — Recompute frames per second using the line rate (because the SOF/header/EOF are also transmitted)

$$
f_{fps}^{line} = \frac{R_{line}}{L_{total} + L_{encode}}
$$

We must first apply 8b/10b to the entire frame, not just payload. So the *transmitted bits per frame* become:

$$
L_{trans} = 2148 \times 8 \times \frac{10}{8} = 21480 \text{ bits/frame}
$$

$$
f_{fps}^{line} = \frac{4 \times 10^{9}}{21480}
$$

$$
f_{fps}^{line} = 186{,}221.97 \text{ frames/s}
$$

#### Step 7 — Final Result

$$
\boxed{R_{eff} = 3.2 \text{ Gbps}, \quad f_{fps}^{line} \approx 186{,}222 \text{ frames/second}}
$$

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Buffer-to-Buffer Credit (BBC) Calculation

**Problem Statement:**
*A Fibre Channel link operates at 8 Gbps, uses 8b/10b encoding, has a round-trip propagation delay of 50 microseconds, and transmits full-size frames of 2148 bytes. Calculate the minimum Buffer-to-Buffer Credits required for the link to remain fully utilized.*

#### Step 1 — List the given quantities
- $R_{line} = 8 \text{ Gbps} = 8 \times 10^{9} \text{ bits/s}$
- $T_{RTT} = 50 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}$
- $L_{frame} = 2148 \text{ bytes} = 2148 \times 8 = 17184 \text{ bits}$

#### Step 2 — Compute the effective data rate (account for 8b/10b)

$$
R_{eff} = 8 \times 10^{9} \times \frac{8}{10} = 6.4 \times 10^{9} \text{ bits/s}
$$

#### Step 3 — Use the BBC formula
The link must have enough credits outstanding to keep the wire busy during one round-trip. The number of frames that "fit" in flight is:

$$
BBC_{min} = \left\lceil \frac{R_{line} \times T_{RTT}}{L_{frame}^{trans}} \right\rceil
$$

where $L_{frame}^{trans}$ is the **transmitted (encoded) frame length** in bits.

#### Step 4 — Compute transmitted frame size

$$
L_{frame}^{trans} = 2148 \text{ bytes} \times 8 \text{ bits/byte} \times \frac{10}{8}
$$

$$
L_{frame}^{trans} = 17184 \times 1.25 = 21480 \text{ bits}
$$

#### Step 5 — Substitute and solve

$$
BBC_{min} = \left\lceil \frac{8 \times 10^{9} \times 50 \times 10^{-6}}{21480} \right\rceil
$$

$$
BBC_{min} = \left\lceil \frac{400{,}000}{21480} \right\rceil
$$

$$
BBC_{min} = \left\lceil 18.62 \right\rceil
$$

$$
\boxed{BBC_{min} = 19 \text{ credits}}
$$

#### Step 6 — Verification and intuition
- A 19-credit buffer means 19 full frames can be in flight before an R_RDY (receiver-ready) acknowledgment returns.
- This keeps the 8 Gbps link fully saturated without retransmission.
- In practice, SAN engineers **provision 25–30% more credits** (≈ 24–25) for safety margin and inter-switch links (ISLs) with multiple hops.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Layer-to-Real-World Mapping

| FC Layer | Real-World Equivalent | Function | KTU Marker Tip |
|---|---|---|---|
| **FC-0** | Ethernet PHY / cable | Carries raw bits over fiber or copper | Know speed/distance table |
| **FC-1** | Ethernet MAC (partial) | Encoding/decoding, link integrity | 8b/10b is the key buzzword |
| **FC-2** | IP + TCP lite | Framing, sequencing, flow control | Master the **frame format** |
| **FC-3** | OS kernel helper | Striping, hunt groups, multicast | Often a 1-mark question |
| **FC-4** | Application protocol | Maps SCSI / IP / NVMe onto FC | "FCP = SCSI over FC" is the magic phrase |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram 1: The Five-Layer FC Architecture (Mermaid Block Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeApp["APPLICATION LAYER  Upper protocols  SCSI  IP  NVMe  FICON"]:::app
    nodeFC4["FC 4  Protocol Mapping  SCSI to FCP  IP to FC IP  NVMe to FC NVMe"]:::fc4
    nodeFC3["FC 3  Common Services  Striping  Hunt Groups  Multicast"]:::fc3
    nodeFC2["FC 2  Framing and Signaling  Frames  Sequences  Exchanges  Class of Service  Flow Control"]:::fc2
    nodeFC1["FC 1  Transmission Protocol  8b 10b Encoding  64b 66b Encoding  Byte Sync  Link Errors"]:::fc1
    nodeFC0["FC 0  Physical Layer  Fiber SMF MMF  Copper Twinax  SFP SFPplus  Wavelength 850 1310 1550 nm"]:::fc0

    nodeApp --> nodeFC4 --> nodeFC3 --> nodeFC2 --> nodeFC1 --> nodeFC0

    classDef app fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef fc4 fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef fc3 fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef fc2 fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#9d174d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef fc1 fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#4338ca,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef fc0 fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#991b1b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> The arrows represent the **service primitives** passed from higher to lower layers. Each layer offers services to the one above and consumes services from the one below — a strict layering principle.

### 4.2 Diagram 2: FC Topologies Comparison (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph p2p["POINT TO POINT  2 devices  Full duplex  Highest bandwidth  No scalability"]
        p2pA["Server"]:::srv
        p2pB["Storage Array"]:::str
    end

    subgraph loop["ARBITRATED LOOP  up to 126 devices  Half duplex  Shared loop  L Port"]
        loopHub["FC AL Hub"]:::hub
        loopA["Server 1"]:::srv
        loopB["Server 2"]:::srv
        loopC["Storage 1"]:::str
        loopD["Storage 2"]:::str
    end

    subgraph fabric["SWITCHED FABRIC  up to 16 million ports  Full duplex per port  N Port  F Port"]
        sw1["FC Switch 1"]:::sw
        sw2["FC Switch 2"]:::sw
        fabA["Server 1"]:::srv
        fabB["Server 2"]:::srv
        fabC["Storage 1"]:::str
        fabD["Storage 2"]:::str
        fabE["Tape Library"]:::str
    end

    p2pA --- p2pB

    loopHub --- loopA
    loopHub --- loopB
    loopHub --- loopC
    loopHub --- loopD

    sw1 --- sw2
    sw1 --- fabA
    sw1 --- fabB
    sw2 --- fabC
    sw2 --- fabD
    sw2 --- fabE

    classDef srv fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af,color:#000
    classDef str fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,color:#000
    classDef hub fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#a16207,color:#000
    classDef sw fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#9d174d,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Diagram 3: FC-2 Frame Format (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeSOF["SOF  4 bytes  Delimiter"]:::hd
    nodeH1["Frame Header  R_CTL 24 bytes  D_ID  S_ID  Type  F_CTL  SEQ_ID  DF_CTL  SEQ_CNT  OX_ID  RX_ID  Parameter"]:::hd
    nodeData["Data Field  0 to 2112 bytes  Optional headers  Logical Unit Number  Initiator Task Tag"]:::data
    nodeCRC["CRC  4 bytes  Polynomial 0x04C11DB7"]:::hd
    nodeEOF["EOF  4 bytes  Delimiter"]:::hd

    nodeSOF --> nodeH1 --> nodeData --> nodeCRC --> nodeEOF

    classDef hd fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#3730a3,color:#000
    classDef data fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e,color:#000
```

| Field | Size | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| SOF (Start of Frame) | 4 bytes | Marks frame start; varies by class |
| Frame Header | 24 bytes | Routing, sequence, exchange IDs |
| Data Field | 0–2112 bytes | SCSI payload or other upper-layer data |
| CRC | 4 bytes | 32-bit cyclic redundancy check |
| EOF (End of Frame) | 4 bytes | Marks frame end |

### 4.4 Diagram 4: Flow Control with Buffer-to-Buffer Credits

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Sender
    participant Receiver
    Note over Sender,Receiver: Both sides initialize with N BBC credits
    Sender->>Receiver: Frame 1 (uses 1 credit)
    Sender->>Receiver: Frame 2 (uses 1 credit)
    Sender->>Receiver: Frame 3 (uses 1 credit)
    Receiver-->>Sender: R_RDY (returns 1 credit)
    Sender->>Receiver: Frame 4 (uses 1 credit)
    Note over Receiver: Receiver buffer can hold N frames max
    Note over Sender: Sender stops when credits exhausted
    Note over Sender: Sender resumes when R_RDY received
```

> The **R_RDY primitive** is a single "receiver-ready" signal that the receiver sends back per credit replenished. This is a **hop-by-hop** mechanism (each switch port pair has its own BBC), enabling **lossless end-to-end** behavior.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
>
> **Q1.** What is Fibre Channel? List any two features that distinguish it from Ethernet.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Fibre Channel is a high-speed, lossless, switched networking technology standardized by ANSI T11, primarily used to interconnect storage devices in Storage Area Networks (SANs). It operates at gigabit speeds (1 to 128 Gbps) over fiber or copper.
> **Two distinguishing features (any two):**
> 1. **Lossless delivery via credit-based flow control** — Ethernet uses best-effort with potential packet loss and TCP retransmissions; FC guarantees in-order delivery with no drops.
> 2. **Dedicated bandwidth and class-of-service guarantees** — FC offers Classes 1, 2, 4, 6, 7 with reserved bandwidth; Ethernet is best-effort (QoS is best-effort, not guaranteed).
> 3. **Channel + network hybrid design** — FC supports both dedicated point-to-point and large switched fabrics (up to 16M devices); Ethernet primarily uses shared/switched topologies.
> *(Any two clearly stated — 1 mark each; definition 1 mark.)*

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
>
> **Q2.** Briefly explain the role of the FC-2 layer in the Fibre Channel architecture.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The **FC-2 layer** is the **Framing and Signaling** layer of the Fibre Channel architecture. Its key functions are:
> 1. **Frame construction and delineation** — defines the SOF, header, payload, CRC, and EOF of an FC frame (max 2148 bytes).
> 2. **Sequences and exchanges** — groups related frames into sequences and sequences into exchanges to represent higher-level operations.
> 3. **Classes of service** — supports Classes 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, defining the connection and acknowledgment behavior.
> 4. **Flow control** — manages **Buffer-to-Buffer Credits (BBC)** for lossless delivery.
> *(3 marks distributed across FC-2 functions: framing 1 mark, sequences 1 mark, flow control 1 mark.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 — Model Paper Pattern]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **five-layer Fibre Channel architecture (FC-0 to FC-4)**. Mention the function of each layer in detail. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Layer | Full Name | Function | Example / Detail |
|---|---|---|---|
| **FC-0** | Physical Interface | Defines the physical media, transceivers, connectors, and electrical/optical characteristics | Fiber (MMF/SMF), copper twinax; SFP/SFP+ modules; 850/1310/1550 nm wavelengths; bit clocking |
| **FC-1** | Transmission Protocol | Provides 8b/10b (≤8 Gbps) or 64b/66b (16G+) encoding, byte synchronization, link-level error detection | 20% encoding overhead in 8b/10b; K28.5 comma for alignment |
| **FC-2** | Framing and Signaling | The heart of FC: defines frames (max 2148 bytes), sequences, exchanges, classes of service, login procedures, and flow control | SOF/EOF delimiters, 24-byte header, BB Credits, FLOGI/PLOGI |
| **FC-3** | Common Services | Services spanning multiple N_Ports on a single node | Striping, Hunt Groups, Multicast |
| **FC-4** | Protocol Mapping | Maps upper-layer protocols onto FC | FCP (SCSI), FC-IP, FC-NVMe, FICON, VI |

*Refer to the block diagram in Section 4.1 for the layered architecture (1 mark for diagram, 6 marks for the table).*

**(b)** Explain the **three Fibre Channel topologies** — Point-to-Point, Arbitrated Loop, and Switched Fabric. Compare them with respect to maximum devices, bandwidth, and typical use. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Point-to-Point (FC-P2P) (2 marks):**
- Direct connection between exactly two devices (e.g., a server and a storage array).
- **No switching required**; each port is an **N_Port**.
- **Full duplex** with maximum bandwidth (entire link dedicated).
- **Disadvantage**: not scalable; isolates two devices only.
- **Use**: Direct server-to-array connections in small deployments or as a back-up link.

**2. Arbitrated Loop (FC-AL) (2 marks):**
- **Up to 126 devices** in a logical ring (often physically wired as a hub-based star).
- Only **one pair communicates at a time** — others wait for arbitration.
- **Half-duplex** operation; uses **L_Ports**.
- **Disadvantage**: contention reduces effective bandwidth; loop initialization required.
- **Use**: Legacy low-cost SANs, small workgroup storage.

**3. Switched Fabric (FC-SW) (2 marks):**
- The **modern, dominant topology** using one or more FC switches (Brocade, Cisco MDS).
- **Up to 16 million addressable devices** (24-bit address space).
- **Concurrent communication**: multiple pairs exchange data simultaneously (non-blocking fabric).
- **N_Ports** (node) and **F_Ports** (fabric) form the edges; **E_Ports** interconnect switches.
- **Use**: Enterprise data centers, hyperscale SANs.

**Comparison Table (1 mark):**

| Feature | Point-to-Point | Arbitrated Loop | Switched Fabric |
|---|---|---|---|
| Max devices | 2 | 126 | ~16 million |
| Bandwidth | Full duplex, dedicated | Half duplex, shared | Full duplex, concurrent |
| Port type | N_Port | L_Port | N_Port / F_Port |
| Cost | Lowest | Low | Higher (switches) |
| Scalability | None | Limited | Highest |
| Typical use | Direct attach | Legacy SAN | Enterprise SAN |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** What is meant by **Class of Service** in Fibre Channel? Explain **Class 1, Class 2, and Class 3** services with their key properties. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1 mark):** A *Class of Service* in Fibre Channel defines the **connection type, acknowledgment behavior, and bandwidth guarantee** for a frame's journey from source to destination. Each Class of Service represents a different trade-off between reliability, latency, and bandwidth efficiency.

**Class 1 — Dedicated Connection (2 marks):**
- Establishes a **dedicated, full-bandwidth connection** between two N_Ports before data exchange.
- Frames are delivered **in order**, with **no interference** from other traffic.
- Connection remains until torn down.
- **Use case**: Deterministic, real-time applications (high-speed replication, real-time video).
- **Drawback**: Wastes bandwidth when idle; complex setup.

**Class 2 — Connectionless with Acknowledgment (2 marks):**
- **No dedicated connection**; frames are **multiplexed** with traffic from other pairs.
- **Each frame is acknowledged** (ACK_1 or ACK_0) by the destination.
- Multiplexed but reliable — provides notification of delivery.
- **Use case**: Control traffic, login procedures (FLOGI, PLOGI, PRLI use Class 2).
- **Note**: Rarely used for data; mainly for FC control frames.

**Class 3 — Connectionless Datagram (2 marks):**
- **No dedicated connection**; **no acknowledgment** at FC-2 level.
- Pure **datagram service** with **multiplexed, best-effort** delivery.
- Lossless delivery is **assured by flow control (BB Credits)**, not by ACKs.
- **Most common class in modern SANs** (≈95% of all storage traffic).
- **Use case**: All SCSI data transfers via FCP (Fibre Channel Protocol) for SCSI.

---

**(b)** With a neat diagram, describe the **Fibre Channel Frame Format** at the FC-2 layer. Explain the role of each field. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

> *(Refer to the diagram in Section 4.3.)*

A Fibre Channel frame consists of five fields, transmitted in this order:

| Field | Size | Function | Marks |
|---|---|---|---|
| **SOF (Start of Frame)** | 4 bytes | Delimiter marking the beginning of the frame. The exact bit pattern indicates the **class of service**. Examples: SOFc1 (Class 1), SOFi3 (Class 3 Initiate), SOFn3 (Class 3 Normal). | 1 |
| **Frame Header** | 24 bytes | Contains 11 fields for routing and protocol control: **R_CTL** (routing control), **D_ID** (destination address, 24-bit), **S_ID** (source address, 24-bit), **Type** (e.g., FCP), **F_CTL** (frame control flags), **SEQ_ID**, **DF_CTL**, **SEQ_CNT** (sequence count), **OX_ID / RX_ID** (originator/receiver exchange IDs), **Parameter**. | 3 |
| **Data Field** | 0 to 2112 bytes | Carries the upper-layer payload (e.g., SCSI CDB + data, IP packet, NVMe command). May include optional headers. | 1 |
| **CRC (Cyclic Redundancy Check)** | 4 bytes | 32-bit CRC using polynomial **0x04C11DB7** (same as Ethernet/HDLC). Detects any bit errors over the frame. | 1 |
| **EOF (End of Frame)** | 4 bytes | Delimiter marking frame end; EOF also encodes the class. EOFdt = "End Frame — Data Terminate", EOFa = "Abort", etc. | 1 |

> **Total frame size (max) = 4 + 24 + 2112 + 4 + 4 = 2148 bytes (5 marks complete; 2 marks for diagram accuracy and clarity).**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (KTU 2024 Valuation Pattern):**
>
> 1. **Spelling trap**: Writing "Fiber Channel" (with "er") instead of **"Fibre Channel"** costs a mark in definitions. Always use the British ISO spelling.
> 2. **Layer-function confusion**: Mixing up FC-1 (encoding) and FC-2 (framing). FC-1 is 8b/10b; FC-2 is frames and sequences.
> 3. **Topology-port mismatch**: Forgetting that **L_Ports** are for loops and **N_Ports/F_Ports** are for fabric. Saying "Arbitrated Loop uses N_Ports" will lose marks.
> 4. **Class 3 misunderstanding**: Saying "Class 3 is unreliable." Class 3 is **unacknowledged but lossless** — flow control (BB Credits) guarantees no drops. Reliability is preserved; only per-frame ACKs are absent.
> 5. **Unit confusion**: Writing "Gbps" for 8b/10b effective rate without noting the 20% encoding penalty.
> 6. **Skipping diagrams**: For 7-mark questions on architecture/frame format, **a diagram is mandatory**. A text-only answer typically loses 1–2 marks.
> 7. **Confusing FC-P2P with FC-AL "point-to-point"**: A 2-device loop is NOT a P2P link; P2P is a direct connection with no loop or switch.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist — Master These Before the Exam:**

- **Fibre Channel** = ANSI T11 standard, gigabit-speed, **lossless**, used in **SANs**.
- **Five layers**: FC-0 (physical) → FC-1 (encoding: 8b/10b or 64b/66b) → FC-2 (frames, sequences, exchanges, classes, flow control) → FC-3 (common services) → FC-4 (SCSI/IP/NVMe mapping).
- **8b/10b** = 20% overhead → efficiency = 0.8. **64b/66b** ≈ 3% overhead → efficiency ≈ 0.9697.
- **Topologies**: P2P (2 devices), FC-AL (≤126, half-duplex loop), FC-SW (≤16M, full-duplex fabric) — modern SANs use FC-SW.
- **Port types**: **N_Port** (node), **F_Port** (fabric switch port), **E_Port** (inter-switch), **L_Port** (loop), **G_Port** (generic, auto-detects).
- **Class of Service**: Class 1 (dedicated, ACK), Class 2 (multiplexed, ACK), Class 3 (multiplexed, **no ACK** — most common), Class 4 (fractional BW), Class 5 (multicast), Class 6 (reliable multicast), Class 7 (unidirectional).
- **Frame format**: SOF(4) + Header(24) + Data(0–2112) + CRC(4) + EOF(4) = **max 2148 bytes**.
- **Flow control**: **Buffer-to-Buffer Credits (BB Credits)** prevent loss; formula $BBC \ge \lceil R \cdot T_{RTT} / L_{frame} \rceil$.
- **FCP = SCSI over FC**, **FC-NVMe = NVMe over FC**, **FC-IP = IP over FC**, **FICON = mainframe channel over FC**.
- **Speeds to remember**: 1G, 2G, 4G, 8G (use 8b/10b), 16G, 32G, 64G, 128G (use 64b/66b).
- **Distinguishing FC from Ethernet**: FC is **lossless + credit-based**; Ethernet is best-effort + retransmission-based. FC has **deterministic latency**; Ethernet does not.
- **Login sequence**: **FLOGI** (fabric) → **PLOGI** (port-to-port) → **PRLI** (process-to-process, e.g., SCSI initiator-target).
- **Fibre Channel was designed for storage, not general networking** — its dominance in enterprise SANs comes from its **determinism, lossless behavior, and SCSI heritage**.

> **Final Exam Mantra:** *"Fibre Channel is the conveyor belt that never jams, never loses a suitcase, and delivers them in order — every time."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Connection-Oriented Transport Protocol Mechanisms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCERT602 - DATA COMMUNICATION AND NETWORKING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Connection-Oriented Transport Protocol Mechanisms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Connection-Oriented Transport Protocol Mechanisms** refer to the suite of operational procedures employed by the **Transmission Control Protocol (TCP)** — the dominant connection-oriented transport-layer protocol in the TCP/IP suite — to establish, maintain, and terminate a reliable, ordered, and error-checked byte-stream communication session between two end systems across an unreliable Internet Protocol (IP) network.

As per the KTU 2024 syllabus, the core mechanisms include:

1. **Connection Establishment** via the **3-Way Handshake** (SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK).
2. **Connection Termination** via the **4-Way Handshake** (FIN → ACK → FIN → ACK).
3. **Reliable Data Transfer** using sequence numbers, cumulative acknowledgments (ACKs), and retransmission timeouts (RTO).
4. **Flow Control** using the **Sliding Window Protocol** with the *Receiver Window (rwnd)* field.
5. **Error Control** through checksums, acknowledgments, and retransmissions.
6. **Congestion Control** using mechanisms such as **Slow Start**, **Congestion Avoidance**, **Fast Retransmit**, and **Fast Recovery**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme):** The module emphasizes the *mechanisms* (i.e., how the protocol *works internally*), not the API or socket programming. Focus on **state transitions, sequence/ack number arithmetic, and window management**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — A Phone Call

Imagine a telephone conversation versus dropping a letter in a postbox.

- A **letter (UDP — connectionless)** is fire-and-forget: you drop it, hope it arrives, and have no idea if the receiver is even listening. There is no setup, no acknowledgment, and no graceful goodbye.
- A **phone call (TCP — connection-oriented)**, however, follows a strict protocol:
  1. **Dial & Ring (SYN):** You initiate a call; the network rings the other party's phone.
  2. **Receiver Picks Up & Greets (SYN-ACK):** The other party answers, confirms the line is open, and establishes a two-way channel.
  3. **You Acknowledge (ACK):** Both sides confirm they can hear each other, and the conversation begins.
  4. **Conversation (Data Transfer):** If one party speaks too fast, the other says *"Slow down, I can't keep up"* (**Flow Control**). If words are missed, the listener says *"Pardon?"* and the speaker repeats (**Error Control / Retransmission**).
  5. **Goodbye (FIN):** *"I have to go now"* → *"Okay, goodbye"* → *"Goodbye"* → *"Take care."* The line is then released.

> [!NOTE]
> TCP is **byte-stream oriented**, not message oriented. It treats data as a continuous sequence of bytes, numbered individually. This is critical for understanding its reliability mechanisms.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (Bold for Emphasis)

- **Maximum Segment Size (MSS):** Typically **1460 bytes** (1500-byte Ethernet MTU − 20-byte IP header − 20-byte TCP header).
- **Default Receiver Window:** Often **16 bits**, allowing up to **65,535 bytes** in flow control (extended via Window Scale option).
- **Sequence Number Space:** **32 bits** (wraps around after 2³² − 1 bytes).
- **Port Number Space:** **16 bits** (range **0–65535**).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The TCP Segment Header — The Heart of the Mechanism

The TCP header (minimum **20 bytes**, maximum **60 bytes** with options) is where all connection-oriented mechanisms are encoded. Understanding each field is mandatory for KTU exams.

| Field | Size (bits) | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Source Port | 16 | Sender's application port |
| Destination Port | 16 | Receiver's application port |
| **Sequence Number** | 32 | Byte-offset of the **first byte** in this segment |
| **Acknowledgment Number** | 32 | Next expected byte from the other side (cumulative ACK) |
| Data Offset | 4 | Header length in 32-bit words (5–15) |
| **Flags (Control bits)** | 9 | URG, ACK, PSH, RST, SYN, FIN (plus NS, CWR, ECE) |
| **Window Size (rwnd)** | 16 | Receiver's available buffer space in bytes |
| Checksum | 16 | 16-bit one's complement checksum over header + data + pseudo-header |
| Urgent Pointer | 16 | Offset from seq num to urgent data (if URG=1) |
| Options | 0–320 | MSS, Window Scaling, Timestamps, SACK |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **six classical control flags** you must memorize: **SYN, ACK, FIN, RST, PSH, URG**. In KTU problems, you will often be asked to identify the flag setting in a state transition.

## 2.2 The 3-Way Handshake — Connection Establishment

Before any data is exchanged, TCP performs a 3-step synchronization:

| Step | Direction | Flags | Sequence Number | Acknowledgment Number | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Client → Server | **SYN** | `seq = x` (ISN_C) | — | Client requests connection; proposes initial sequence number |
| 2 | Server → Client | **SYN, ACK** | `seq = y` (ISN_S) | `ack = x + 1` | Server agrees, sends its own ISN, and ACKs client's SYN |
| 3 | Client → Server | **ACK** | `seq = x + 1` | `ack = y + 1` | Client confirms; connection is **ESTABLISHED** |

**Why three steps and not two?**
- A **2-way handshake** cannot guarantee that both sides have actually opened the connection, because old duplicate SYN packets (from a delayed previous session) could falsely "open" a new connection.
- The 3-way handshake ensures **both sides synchronize their ISNs** AND **confirm bidirectional reachability**.

## 2.3 The 4-Way Handshake — Connection Termination

TCP uses a **half-close** model, so termination requires 4 steps:

| Step | Direction | Flags | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Active Closer → Passive Closer | **FIN, ACK** | "I have no more data to send" |
| 2 | Passive Closer → Active Closer | **ACK** | Acknowledges the FIN |
| 3 | Passive Closer → Active Closer | **FIN, ACK** | "I also have no more data" |
| 4 | Active Closer → Passive Closer | **ACK** | Final acknowledgment; both sides enter **TIME_WAIT** |

> [!NOTE]
> After Step 4, the connection enters the **TIME_WAIT** state for **2 × MSL (Maximum Segment Lifetime)** — typically **2 × 2 minutes = 4 minutes** — to ensure that any delayed segments are destroyed before the connection fully closes.

## 2.4 Flow Control — The Sliding Window Protocol

TCP uses a **variable-size sliding window** to allow the sender to transmit multiple segments before receiving an ACK, while ensuring the receiver is not overwhelmed.

**Key Variables:**

- `rwnd` (Receiver Window): the size of the receiver's buffer, advertised in every ACK.
- `cwnd` (Congestion Window): the sender's internal estimate of network capacity.
